Title: Waqar Younis Resigns as Pakistan Coach Amid Disappointing Results

LAHORE - In a surprising turn of events, Waqar Younis has tendered his resignation as the head coach of the Pakistan cricket team, three months ahead of the expiration of his contract. The decision comes just a day after Shahid Afridi stepped down as the T20 captain.

The resignations follow Pakistan's underwhelming showings in the Asia Cup held in Bangladesh and the World T20 tournament in India. The team managed to secure victories in only three out of the eight matches they contested across both tournaments, failing to progress beyond the initial rounds. It has been over ten months since Pakistan last tasted victory in any format, having won a 2-Test series against England in the UAE back in October 2019. Since then, the team has suffered defeats in the ODI and T20 series against England, followed by losses in the T20 and ODI series against New Zealand.

In his resignation letter submitted to PCB chairman Shahryar Khan and the board's executive committee, Waqar cited the team's recent poor performances as the primary reason for his decision. It marks his second tenure as head coach, having previously held the position in 2010-11 before stepping down due to personal reasons.

Despite the disappointing results, the Pakistan Cricket Board (PCB) has yet to announce a replacement for Waqar or a new captain following Afridi's resignation. The search for fresh leadership is expected to begin in earnest soon.

text:
Kolkata, India - West Indies' mercenary squad in the World Twenty20 will aim to unleash their Caribbean flair against a fearless England team in Sunday's final, as both sides strive for the honor of becoming the first to claim a second title in this prestigious tournament.

Despite nearly boycotting the event due to a contractual dispute with their board, Darren Sammy and his troops have effortlessly made their way to the Kolkata final, showcasing the same skill and bravado that has earned them worldwide admiration. If Twenty20 cricket is indeed about power and knowledge of destructive potential, then Chris Gayle's 47-man army, who heavily rely on Gayle's ability to breach opponents' defenses, certainly have the tools for the job. Marlon Samuels, Lendl Simmons, and Andre Russell have taken their cue from their illustrious teammates and made a name for themselves over the past month.

"We said before the tournament that Chris is under pressure," Sammy admitted after the thrilling semifinal win. "But he's got us here, so we're happy with him." The West Indies side, however, are far from invincible.

England have displayed two contrasting aspects of their game in recent matches, suffering an embarrassing defeat to qualifiers Afghanistan before silencing a boisterous home crowd as they knocked out favorites India. The England team that West Indies will face on Sunday is not the same one they convincingly defeated by six wickets in the group stage last month. Eoin Morgan's side have gradually found their rhythm and the right combinations throughout the tournament since that defeat. They demonstrated courage against a high-flying Afghanistan, showed resilience to hold their nerve against Sri Lanka, and stunned the previously undefeated New Zealand with a near-flawless performance.

Their batsmen, particularly Jason Roy, Joe Root, and Jos Buttler, have batted with a freedom that has been refreshing to watch. Their catching has also been nothing short of exceptional. Equally impressive has been their death bowling. Ben Stokes appeared to possess ice in his veins during the final over against Sri Lanka, thwarting Angelo Mathews' audacious attempt at pulling off a sensational victory as England secured their place in the last four. Against New Zealand, Stokes and Chris Jordan successfully contained the Black Caps during the final four overs, conceding only 20 runs and claiming five wickets, restricting them to a total that England chased down with 17 balls to spare. Morgan has freed his batsmen from the weight of expectation, and it is evident in their performance.

"That's the way a lot of us play," Roy said of England's aggressive batting after his 44-ball innings of 78 set the foundation for victory over New Zealand. "The whole team has got that kind of license, that kind of opportunity to go out there and do what they want and be free with their skills."

text: on. A female lies on her back, calling like crazy. Males rush in one after another, a dozen or more in a row. There’s lots of thrashing around, churning water. And plenty of visible whale penises ‘about as long as this cabin’, Mayo said, spreading his arms wide. The only trouble is that he and others have seen the whales going at it at the ‘wrong’ times of year, considering that calves are born December through March. Which raises all kinds of questions: whether estimates of their gestation period are off, whether females might be able to store sperm and become pregnant at some later point, whether the whales might mate just for kicks. When it comes to whales, Hunt said: ‘There’s no other animal on the planet where we just know that little about basic reproduction.’ In 2012, as research in keratin-based tissues percolated in labs around the world, Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian with North Slope Borough in Barrow, Alaska, who had been sending Hunt and Rolland bowhead poop for years, contacted the pair to suggest applying the keratin approach to baleen. ‘This suddenly got really exciting as soon as we realised we might be able to get a pregnancy signature out of the baleen,’ Hunt said. ‘There’s really been no way to reconstruct the history of an individual whale.’ Hunt, Rolland and several colleagues began with a pilot study published in 2014 of bowhead whales killed by native Alaskan hunters. Bowheads were a logical species to start with because these subsistence hunts are the one sustainable, fresh, reliable source of baleen, which you can get only from a dead whale. Also, bowheads, which are right whales’ closest relatives, have the longest- and slowest-growing baleen. An adult’s plate appears to capture 25 years of growth, longer than any other keratin-based tissue that has been studied. The team was able to detect the stress hormone cortisol and the reproductive hormone progesterone in the baleen, and could clearly distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. Since baleen grows continuously, it doesn’t produce annual rings like a tree does; instead, the researchers used existing estimates of baleen growth rates as a chronometer for their samples. Best of all, when they looked by intervals at older baleen from mature females, they found changes in progesterone that neatly matched bowheads’ estimated gestation length. The baleen seemed to document past pregnancies. What they needed to cross-check the technique was baleen from whales with known pregnancy histories. That’s where Stumpy and another ship-struck female North Atlantic right whale nicknamed Staccato came in. Both whales had a nearly 30-year-long file of regular sightings, including two recent pregnancies. And the carcasses of both happened to be found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to collect plates of their baleen and tuck it away. ‘They’re really valuable plates,’ Hunt said. Sure

text:
Walking through San Marco, it was the way people held their hands that struck me. This mirroring created a sense of deep tranquility, like stepping into a new environment. It felt as if I'd entered a tangible medium  something I could immerse myself in, something that could transform the ambiance of a room, making everything seem sweeter, cooler, calmer, brighter than before. To greet the world with love, tenderness, and joy  this, I believe, is what Angelico's paintings suggest faith allows you to do.

Two thoughts crossed my mind then. The first was that I hadn't truly grasped the art of painting until that afternoon. What makes painting great, I realized, is its ability to create an atmosphere around itself, one that can affect you generations later. The second was that I hadn't fully contemplated the values of Angelico's world, hadn't decided to adopt them for myself. Instead, it felt like I was viewing things under a new light  the serene, radiant light of Angelico's paintings. It occurred to me that this is what faith means  not a set of beliefs you hold to be true or rules you follow, but an atmosphere you inhabit, one that alters your perception of the world, your understanding of its essence.

San Marco itself hinted at this connection. After all, wasn't that what the friars' daily lives were about  what those special rituals and practices were for? They were the tools used to cultivate and maintain an atmosphere of faith  that unique way of existing in the world. When I look at The Last Judgment now, it's not the promise of an afterlife or the idea of a just, orderly universe that moves me. It's the depiction of a world where everything matters, where everything has weight and significance. This, it seems to me, is the allure of faith: it imbues things with a profound, intrinsic worth.

I find myself drawn to a particular detail in the painting  a small angel in the garden of the blessed, tenderly embracing a friar who has just risen from his grave. To greet the world as the angel greets the friar, with love, tenderness, and joy  this, Angelico's paintings seem to suggest, is what faith empowers you to do. The question for a secular person is how, in the absence of faith, one can come to experience the world that way. Is it possible to maintain a sense of the sacred, even without belief? My experience at San Marco suggests an answer. We may not share the friars' beliefs. But perhaps, by adopting the practices they used to nurture their faith  by placing ourselves in situations where the sacred might be encountered  we can cultivate our own sense of the profound and meaningful.

Title: A Romantic Evening at Cheuvront's - A Hidden Gem!

I recently took my date to this stunning restaurant that I simply cannot recommend enough - Cheuvront's. It's the perfect spot for a romantic night out, and trust me, it definitely sets the mood. The interior is modern yet intimate, with an urban vibe that's just dark enough to create a mysterious allure.

We started off our evening by ordering a bottle of wine from their impressive selection. The sommelier was more than happy to help us choose a wine to pair with our meal. To accompany the wine, we also opted for a cheese plate and a pate plate, allowing the staff to select cheeses and pates that complemented our chosen wine.

The cheese plate consisted of three exquisite cheeses, each more delicious than the last. It was served with fresh bread, some crunchy nuts, and tart dried cranberries - a perfect balance of flavors and textures. The pate plate was equally impressive, with two rich and creamy pates that seemed designed to be enjoyed together.

The food and drink were exceptional, and the service was friendly, if a bit slow on this particular evening. I believe our waitress must have been giving us some space to savor our meal and conversation. After finishing off the wine and indulging in the cheeses and pates, we both left feeling completely satisfied - no room for dessert!

Overall, it was an outstanding dining experience. My date raved about Cheuvront's all evening long, praising everything from the food to the atmosphere. It goes without saying that I'll be paying more attention to Kristi's recommendations in the future. If you're looking for a memorable night out, I highly recommend giving Cheuvront's a try!

text: are in their power Unlike some other recent thinkers such as Jason Stanley, Latour argues that these movements are only superficially like early 20th-century fascism. Rather, they represent a novel political order that is based on climate-change denial, where wealthy elites aim to create gated communities and escape routes by deregulation and disenfranchisement. All the while, they try (in vain) to realise themselves in things that seem ultimately unfulfilling and empty: superyachts, short trips into space or into the deep sea, and buying up entire islands. By influencing and subverting the democratic process, they try to encourage deregulation so as to pull more and more resources toward themselves. Realising (at some level) that this is not sustainable, they retreat into increasingly remote fantasies such as TESCREAL (an ideological bundle of -isms: transhumanism, extropianism, singularitarianism, cosmism, rationalism, effective altruism and longtermism). It’s promoted by philosophers at the University of Oxford such as Nick Bostrom, Hilary Greaves and William MacAskill. They envisage a future where humanity will transform itself into a posthuman state (facilitated by so-called ‘liberal’ eugenics and AI), colonise the accessible Universe, and plunder our ‘cosmic endowment’ of resources to produce astronomical amounts of ‘value’ (for an overview, see Émile Torres’s recent essay for Salon). The happiness of these future posthumans, most of whom would be digital, justifies neglecting current-day problems. ‘For the purposes of evaluating actions,’ Greaves and MacAskill write, ‘we can in the first instance often simply ignore all the effects contained in the first 100 (or even 1,000) years, focusing primarily on the further-future effects. Short-run effects act as little more than tie-breakers.’ The TESCREAL world leaves little scope for the diversity of expression of being human: the joyful, vulnerable and diverse ways of being in, for instance, Traveller and Roma communities, Indigenous societies, and more. Why do the wealthiest people seek to actively deny the climate crisis rather than address it? The philosopher Beth Lord, drawing on Spinoza, argues that they are in the grip of bad emotions. Normally, our emotions help us seek out what is good for us and avoid what is bad. We have three basic affects: joy, sadness and desire. Desire is an expression of the conatus: we seek things that bring us joy and avoid things that bring us sadness. Overall, this aids our self-preservation. However, because of the complex ways in which our emotions intermingle, it is possible to be mistaken in them and to desire things that really do not help us to realise ourselves. Seeking prestige, fame and wealth seems like it will help us realise ourselves but, actually, we are gripped by them and are in their power. While these misconceptions are prominent among the wealthiest elites, we see them in everyone. The ethicist Eugene Chislenko argues that we might all be climate crisis deniers in some sense. BRIDGETOWN, BARBADOS  In a groundbreaking move for cricket, the upcoming tri-series involving West Indies, South Africa, and Australia will be played entirely under floodlights, marking the first time all matches in such a series are scheduled for nighttime. The West Indies Cricket Board (WICB) made the announcement on Saturday.

This momentous series will see 10 one-day internationals taking place during the night, with the opening match set for June 3rd. Roland Holder, WICB Operations manager, described the series as a significant event featuring three highly appealing teams and some of the world's top players. He expressed excitement about their readiness to deliver captivating cricket under the lights, considering they are accustomed to playing in such conditions across various global venues.

Fans will appreciate this format, as day-night matches have proven to enhance spectator experience, Holder noted. International cricket is set to return to Guyana for the first time in two years, coinciding with the country's 50th anniversary of independence. The initial fixtures will take place in Guyana, followed by three matches in St Kitts, and concluding with the remaining group games and final at the Kensington Oval in Barbados.

The announcement arrives at a time when South Africa has expressed reservations about Australia's proposal to host a Test match on their upcoming tour under floodlights.

text: This place is absolute perfection. From the setting to the service to the food, I loved every moment of it. The only way I found out about this place was through Yelp. You seriously are my trusty sidekick. From the previous reviews I was a little worried that it might be packed, but then I remembered it was a Monday night so no worries. Walking in the restaurant, it is quite small, but just absolutely adorable. It really does feel like an upscale country home. Wood lines the whole restaurant and they have mini chandeliers hanging over every table. Even the touches of place setting are just perfect. Our server was Gina and she was a complete sweetheart. We started off with the Bohemian Sidecar and the Cucumber Vesper. Both were absolutely delightful! Then we moved on to appetizers, because while getting drunk is fun, I was really excited for the food. We had the steak tartare and mussels in the saffron and white wine sauce. I loved the steak tartare with the potato chips and fried egg on top. Put fried egg on top of anything and I'm sold. Put fried egg on top of raw steak and I'm in love! The mussels came out just as we were finishing up the steak tartare, perfect timing! The mussels were equally amazing if not more, and I am so sad that they only give one piece of bread! Well that, along with the bread and butter. But that still wasn't enough for me! I wanted to soak up all of that saffron sauce goodness! After Gina cleared that out of our way, she brought out the glass of Bordeaux and Rhone blend. I hate when I don't have a drink in front of me, and she made sure that never happened. Then the entrees came. The pork shoulder with the bacon for my bf, and the stuffed chicken with the pome puree and mushrooms for me. Again, absolute perfection on both accounts! I loved the mushrooms that were slightly charred and crispy, with the sauce and the puree and the chicken with goat cheese. I'm getting hungry again just writing about it. My bf loved his, but I still maintain that mine was better. And both of these entrees were incredibly reasonably priced at $15 and $17. Could life get any better? Well apparently it could! After we polished off our entrees, then came dessert and after dinner drinks! We had the souffle which was amazing with the creme anglaise. And the berries with the creme fraiche. Heaven in my mouth! We finished that off with the chocolate martini and the pear tini. This review cannot do this place justice. This is truly a unique experience that is far above any of the restaurants in Scottsdale. Please go in and experience this for yourself, you will not be disappointed!

ISLAMABAD  The Pakistan International Airlines (PIA) is set to add a second Airbus A330 aircraft to its Premier Service, which was launched on August 14 this year, on the London route next month, according to official sources. All flights to London will now be operated using an Airbus A330 as part of the Premier Service.

The sources went on to confirm that a third Airbus is being planned for inclusion in the Premier fleet in February 2017. Furthermore, the PIA plans to expand this service to other suggested international routes such as Toronto, New York, Birmingham, and Beijing.

At present, the Premier Service flights from Islamabad to London are operated on Monday, Wednesday, and Sunday, while flights from Lahore to London take place on Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday. In terms of return flights, those departing from London for Islamabad operate on Tuesdays, Saturdays, and Sundays, whereas those from London to Lahore fly on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays.

The aircraft boasts 38 business class seats and 269 economy class seats, along with LCD screens, flight entertainment featuring 250 channels, and a flat-bed service for business passengers. Additionally, a complimentary limousine service is being offered to business class passengers at London's Heathrow Airport for destinations within a 25-kilometer radius.

Currently, the PIA fleet consists of 38 aircraft, including 11 Airbus A320s, five Airbus A310s, 11 Boeing 777s, six ATR 42s, and five ATR 72s. Efforts are being made to grow the fleet further, with hopes that its size will reach 42 by the end of the current financial year.

Shortly after he and the Jamaican-born cultural theorist Stuart Hall founded the New Left Review in 1960, he left in high dudgeon as devotees of theoretical history took over the editorial board. He particularly objected to the journal’s adoption of a ‘Third Worldism’ in which ‘the entire “West”… stands impeached by its complicity with colonialism,’ as he wrote in his (only recently published) resignation letter in 1963 – the year that The Making of the English Working Class was published. Affirming the identity of interests of peoples across the alleged West-Third World divide, EP addressed the judgments of Frantz Fanon’s The Wretched of the Earth (1961), which, like Tagore and Gandhi earlier, cautioned forcefully against ‘mimicry’ of a West whose culture and institutions were manifestly morally and practically bankrupt, with Europe headed ‘into the abyss’. Sympathetically conceding that the text’s context made it ‘not only comprehensible but inevitable’, EP nevertheless fervently contested the conclusion that the ‘West’ had ‘nothing to offer’. His effort in his work to recover alternative ‘English’ values – radical humanistic ones – was partly in a spirit of defiant redemption. ‘For us,’ he explained, ‘the “European game” can never be finished’: ‘If “our” tradition has failed … then it is for us to put it in repair’ rather than hastily conclude that ‘the humanist values discovered in the West are corrupted beyond recall’. text:
MUMBAI: India's cotton exports in the 2016/17 season are projected to decline by approximately 28% compared to the previous year, reaching around 5 million bales. This significant drop is due to Pakistan, one of India's major buyers, potentially cutting its purchases in half as a result of escalating tensions and improvements in its own domestic production, according to industry and government officials.

Pakistan's reduced imports from the world's leading cotton producer are expected to benefit countries such as Brazil, the United States, and several African nations in increasing their own exports. Pakistan may import between 1 million and 1.5 million bales during the 2016/17 season, a substantial decrease from the 2.7 million bales purchased in the same period last year, as stated by India's Textile Commissioner Kavita Gupta during a press briefing on Monday.

Gupta attributed the reduction to an improvement in Pakistan's cotton production, but industry sources suggested that tensions between the two nations are also playing a role. "Pakistan still requires imports, but Pakistani buyers are increasingly looking towards Brazil and the U.S.," said Pradeep Jain, a ginner based in Jalgaon, Maharashtra's western state.

Typically, the world's largest cotton consumer begins importing from September onwards. However, exporters have reported a significant drop in inquiries over the past few weeks. In 2015/16, Pakistan surpassed Bangladesh to become India's primary cotton buyer, accounting for around 40% of India's exports. As of now, India has contracted only 500,000 bales for export due to weak demand from international buyers, as per Dhiren Sheth, president of the Cotton Association of India. By this time last year, Indian traders had already agreed on contracts to export 1 million bales, according to dealers.

India's cotton output in the 2016/17 season is expected to increase by around 3.8% compared to the previous year, reaching approximately 35.1 million bales. This forecasted rise is primarily due to increased yields resulting from favorable monsoon rains and fewer pest issues, as Gupta explained during her press briefing: "This year, despite lower acreage, we are anticipating higher per-hectare yields on account of good monsoon rains and reduced impact from pests."

(1 Indian bale = 170 kg)

text:
In the vast expanse of our world, both condors soaring majestically across the skies and whooping cranes gracefully dancing in wetlands have faced their own set of challenges. At the US Geological Survey Patuxent Wildlife Research Center in Maryland, whooping cranes are taught the basics of survival - eating, drinking, and flocking - by humans donning something between a burka and a beekeeping suit, with crane puppet heads attached to their hands. Even pandas have been cared for by humans dressed as panda mascots. While these animals may not cause the chaos that adolescent condors do, they can still develop peculiar behaviors of their own. For instance, puppet-reared whooping cranes struggle with incubating their eggs in the wild; often abandoning their nests just days before hatching. John French, research manager at Patuxent and head of the crane program, has a few theories as to why these cranes face issues with parenting. One idea is that they're sensitive to black flies, which swarm nesting cranes in the wild, but were spared from during their upbringing in relative comfort. Another theory is that crucial information about the breeding process was missed during their puppet-parented childhood. When I asked French what this missing piece might be, he admitted, 'I have no idea.' This conundrum ensures a continual need for more puppet-rearing, which seems paradoxical in nature.

This peculiar method of conservation is mirrored by the growing de-extinction movement, where one wonders if these puppet-reared condors and whooping cranes are living out a tragic version of Plato's cave allegory - born into an artificial world, only to be thrust into the harsh, complex reality. Lacking the necessary tools for survival outside their carefully managed environments, they inevitably struggle. It's challenging to fathom why keepers thought this hands-on approach would foster psychologically healthy animals. However, like all intensive conservation programs, puppet-rearing was never truly about the animals themselves.

The primary goal of any captive breeding program is to establish self-sustaining populations of wild animals that can survive and thrive independently from human intervention. Yet, the underlying motivation for their existence often stems from a deep-seated human guilt. We drove the condor, the whooping crane, the panda, and countless other species to the brink of extinction; therefore, we feel compelled to save them. This sentiment typically translates into an obsessive focus on the most adorable or symbolic of these endangered species, which either remain in captivity or are released back into the very world that nearly exterminated their ancestors. This approach, while well-intentioned, is flawed and beguiling, as exemplified by the de-extinction movement's ambitious plans to resurrect extinct species such as the passenger pigeon.

According to Kripke, when a proper name is introduced, it’s typically attached directly to an individual. There might be a baptism in which we say: ‘By “Aristotle” we’ll mean him [pointing].’ Our intention is that the name should then pick out that person in any actual or possible situation, including situations where they happen not to fit the descriptions we associate with the name. Consider a possible situation in which Aristotle goes in for gardening, while Aristotle’s brother is the person taught by Plato and who teaches Alexander. That didn’t happen, but it might have happened. Kripke points out that if ‘Aristotle’ just means ‘the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander’, then ‘Aristotle’ would pick out the wrong person in that possible situation. Indeed, it seems that the actual situation could turn out to be one in which Aristotle has few if any of the properties we associate with him. Historians could conceivably discover that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for the achievements of others – including being taught by Plato and teaching Alexander – and somehow got away with it. But if that’s true, then the name ‘Aristotle’ can’t just mean ‘the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander’. For then it would be a trivial truth, not a potentially contestable historical fact, that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke argues that many common nouns, such as ‘gold’, ‘water’ and ‘tiger’, also refer directly, rather than by way of some sort of mental checklist. We define ‘gold’ by saying: ‘Gold is that substance’ (pointing to a sample) and allow the world, rather than any associated description, to determine the conditions under which something qualifies as a sample of gold. The most famous illustration of how the name of a substance is supposed to function in this way is provided not by Kripke, but by Putnam, another leading proponent of the ‘theory of direct reference’. Putnam asks us to imagine a Twin Earth – just like our Earth – which contains doppelgängers of us humans. The only difference between the two Earths is that on Twin Earth the clear, thirst-quenching, etc liquid that fills the oceans, lakes and rivers is not the chemical substance H2O, but another substance – XYZ. Suppose it’s 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered. On both Earths, the inhabitants call their liquid ‘water’. We have untrammelled access to our own minds, so the argument goes, and this immediacy of introspection means that we are rarely wrong about ourselves. This is known as the ‘privileged access’ view of self-knowledge, and has been dominant in philosophy in various guises for much of the 20th century. René Descartes relied on self-reflection in this way to reach his conclusion ‘I think, therefore I am,’ noting along the way that: ‘I know clearly that there is nothing that can be perceived by me more easily or more clearly than my own mind.’ An alternative view suggests that we infer what we think or believe from a variety of cues – just as we infer what others think or feel from observing their behaviour. This suggests that self-knowledge is not as immediate as it seems. For instance, I might infer that I am anxious about an upcoming presentation because my heart is racing and my breathing is heavier. But I might be wrong about this – perhaps I am just feeling excited. This kind of psychological reframing is often used by sports coaches to help athletes maintain composure under pressure. The philosopher most often associated with the inferential view is Gilbert Ryle, who proposed in The Concept of Mind (1949) that we gain self-knowledge by applying the tools we use to understand other minds to ourselves: ‘The sorts of things that I can find out about myself are the same as the sorts of things that I can find out about other people, and the methods of finding them out are much the same.’ Ryle’s idea is neatly summarised by another New Yorker cartoon in which a husband says to his wife: ‘How should I know what I’m thinking? I’m not a mind reader.’ Many philosophers since Ryle have considered the strong inferential view as somewhat crazy, and written it off before it could even get going. The philosopher Quassim Cassam, author of Self-knowledge for Humans (2014), describes the situation: Philosophers who defend inferentialism – Ryle is usually mentioned in this context – are then berated for defending a patently absurd view. The assumption that intentional self-knowledge is normally immediate … is rarely defended; it’s just seen as obviously correct. But if we take a longer view of history, the idea that we have some sort of special, direct access to our minds is the exception, rather than the rule. For the ancient Greeks, self-knowledge was not all-encompassing, but a work in progress, and something to be striven toward, as captured by the exhortation to ‘know thyself’ carved on the Temple of Delphi. text:
Samuel Beckett, as he delved deeper into prose fiction, found himself increasingly free to dismantle conventional expectations of what a novel should be. By the time he penned his major postwar novels  Molloy, Malone Dies, and The Unnamable  published between 1951 and 1953 in French and later adapted into English  he had fully embraced the chaos. Molloy's narrative is told by a man who questions whether he's penning a diary or addressing an audience. He frequently finds himself disconnected from his body, grappling with a fading sense of self and reality. He views himself as a contemplative, someone who, in true Schopenhauerian fashion, has renounced the will to live: "My life, my life, now I speak of it as if it's over, now as a joke that continues, and it isn't, for at the same time it's over and it goes on. Is there any tense for that?" As the trilogy unfolds, each subsequent narrator confesses to being the author of the previous one. The stories crumble, reveal their fictitious nature, yet we seem no closer to discovering the author's presence. It appears then that Beckett's experiments with quietism in the 1930s culminated in an aesthetic characterized by disarray, confusion, and powerlessness, shaping his most acclaimed novels. "I work with impotence, ignorance," Beckett told one interviewer, adding, "I don't think impotence has been fully explored before." Yet, what about the initial preoccupation? The desire to escape suffering and pain? The quest for redemption or some form of salvation? "I always took refuge in the innermost place of human weakness and humility," Beckett admitted.

In his later years, Beckett occasionally seemed eager to distance himself from soteriological systems. In 1986, three years before his passing, he told an interviewer that unlike Schopenhauer, Leopardi, and certain 'eastern thinkers', he didn't propose a 'way out' or a 'hope of an answer' or a 'solution'. Death, he said grimly, was the only solution. However, it's worth noting that, for the quietists such as Molinos, the purest beatitude  and perhaps even the truest salvation  was found in the renunciation of hope for salvation. The theologian William Inge called this 'the mystic paradox' in a book on Christian mysticism that Beckett read and took notes on in the 1930s.

text:
Sam Cooke, a man who shaped his own career, but lived with the complexities of being a black star. In the latter part of 1963, he and his bandmates attempted to check into a hotel in Shreveport, Louisiana, only to be denied. They made a scene, were arrested, and imprisoned. The song "A Change is Gonna Come" was recorded in February 1964 during a session produced by Hugo Peretti and Luigi Creatore. It was subtly included on Cookes album Ain't That Good News (1964). As chronicled by Guralnick in Dream Boogie, following the session, Cooke played the song for Bobby Womack, his protg. Womack said the song sounded like death. On December 22, 1964, "A Change Is Gonna Come" was finally released as a single. Eleven days earlier, Cooke had been shot and killed at a Los Angeles motel, in what was later ruled a justifiable homicide. The song didn't do exceptionally well commercially; it peaked at number 31 on the Billboard chart in 1965. However, it became an anthem of the Civil Rights movement and remains a powerful symbol of that era of protest. It was Cookes last, and perhaps most enduring, song.

When art leans too heavily towards rhetoric or sloganeering, it often falls flat. "A Change Is Gonna Come" is an unusual protest song - it's not raw, angry, or street-level. The dramatic orchestration suggests glitzy nightclubs and movie palaces, rather than barricades and lunch-counter sit-ins. Cooke's voice, pure as ever, glides through the strings and timpanis like a well-mixed martini slips down your throat. The arrangement, by Ren Hall, builds and crests atop an obvious sense of its own significance. This is a grand song, we're told through the production. Yet, when Cooke sings, he does so in a quiet, personal first person, narrating everyday scenes of injustice. Each verse culminates in a simple chorus where Cooke repeats the title line: 'A change is gonna come.' The promise of change resonates in the way he stretches out the words 'long' and 'know', how he twirls the phrase: It's been a long, long time comin', but I know-oh-oo-oh A change is gonna come, Oh yes it will. It's both a prayer and a warning.

One of the challenges in creating successful art about social change is that people experience injustices as individuals, not as movements or policies or messages. When art strays too far from this reality and veers towards rhetoric or sloganeering, it often loses impact. What makes "A Change Is Gonna Come" resonate isn't Cookes vocal abilities or the timing of its release. Its political power lies in its personal touch. Cooke retreated from the royal 'we' and the broad metaphoric strokes that folk singers like Dylan used to rally their audiences to the cause. He didn't call for a march in Alabama to fight for rights, but instead sang about his own experiences: "I was born by the river in a little tent, oh, and just like the river I began to spend, my life in mournin', still searchin' for that day... A change is gonna come."

This dovetails with a cognitive-emotional phenomenon called ‘psychic numbing’, which the psychologist Paul Slovic describes as the inability to appreciate losses of life as they become larger. The importance of saving one life is great when it is the first, or only, life saved, but diminishes marginally as the total number of lives saved increases. It’s common for people to remember themselves swimming from a point above themselves, for example. Why this is the case is a bit of a puzzle. Lisa Libby and her colleagues found a clue when they discovered that taking a different perspective on oneself allows a person to represent different aspects of a situation. When we represent ourselves in our own memories, we focus on contextual factors, such as the larger significance of actions and events, the larger setting, and how we and our actions appear to others. But when we recall an event from the point of view we experienced it from, the details of the immediate environment are clearly represented, as are our bodily reactions and our feelings, and the where and when of the event. In interpersonal conflict, it’s easy to detect large differences in how a situation is constructed. The social psychologist Roy Baumeister has found (perhaps predictably) that, when it comes to ordinary wrongdoing such as breaking promises or betraying secrets, perpetrators minimise the importance of what they’ve done, whereas victims maximise it. But a more surprising difference is that victims find the perpetrator’s intentions unfathomable, describing them as ‘incoherent, contradictory, arbitrary, or senseless’. Today, philosophy finds itself thriving, yet it's important to acknowledge certain challenges that have surfaced within the realm of Cold War philosophy. Historically, philosophers have delved into areas such as logic, metaphysics, and epistemology, shying away from political discourse. However, Cold War philosophy has some evident flaws on a philosophical level. Take its conception of ethics for instance  it's quite distinct from traditional ethical theories that emphasize the integration of the individual into a larger moral framework. Instead, Cold War philosophy champions individualism and moral neutrality, favoring Gordon Gekko's famous mantra of "Greed is good."

Another point of contention is what I refer to as 'disidentification.' In essence, every choice we make implies the existence of an alternative; otherwise, there would be no choice. However, if we identify ourselves too closely with any given option at the outset, we become incapable of choosing anything but that option, as making a different choice would essentially mean losing our identity and possibly even compromising our integrity. This is not problematic when it comes to making everyday decisions such as selecting a toothpaste brand or a political candidate. But when this rational decision-making process becomes the guiding principle for all aspects of life  as is the case with Cold War philosophy  it can lead us to abandon our own identities.

For instance, if I'm grappling with choosing my religion between Catholicism and Hinduism, I must distance myself from both faiths to make an objective decision. This 'disidentification' becomes a necessity when adhering to Cold War philosophy's insistence on taking this approach in all things. At its extreme, the moral agent is expected to be disconnected from everything, leaving them with no fundamental identity beyond that of a rational chooser  someone who prioritizes transitivity and completeness in their preferences before making a decision based on utility. That's a rather shallow identity, you might agree.

We are all defined by certain characteristics that we refuse to let go under any circumstances. What else is there to live for? The prevalence of rational choice theory, coupled with the flaws inherent in Cold War philosophy, seems to indicate that the root of the problem lies in its claim to universal validity and singular relevance  a claim inherited from Allen.

That’s why it rarely applies to the psychoactive substances that we see as part of normal life, whether caffeine in the west, coca in the Andes, or ayahuasca in the Amazon. During the first years of the 20th century, opium, morphine and cocaine became less socially acceptable, rather as tobacco has in our era. Their use was now viewed through the prism of medical harm, and their users correspondingly started to seem feckless or morally weak. The drugs themselves became, in a sense, ‘legal highs’: not technically prohibited but retreating into the shadows, available only under the counter or from those in the know. And then, once their sale was formally banned in the years around the Great War, ‘drugs’ became a term with legal weight: a specified list of substances that were not merely medically dangerous or culturally foreign, but confined to the criminal classes. The banning of drugs occasioned strikingly little public debate, certainly compared with the prohibition of alcohol in the US. Then again, the ‘drug problem’ was pretty marginal at that point, and confined to subcultures (ethnic, bohemian, criminal) without a public voice. The only organised resistance to this new language of condemnation came from the pharmaceutical industry, concerned that its legitimate trade was being tarnished by unfortunate associations. What’s now the American Pharmacists Association, pressured by its major corporate sponsors such as Johnson &amp; Johnson, complained about the casual use of terms such as ‘drug evil’, ‘drug fiend’ and ‘drug habit’, and lobbied newspapers to specify the drugs in question as ‘narcotics’ or ‘opiates’. But ‘drugs’ was too vague and too useful to replace with more precise terms. It conveyed not simply particular chemicals, but a moral position on the use of them by certain people and for certain purposes. This position was eventually enshrined in the legal frameworks that emerged to prohibit them. The 1961 UN Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, the founding document of the international drug laws, is unique among UN conventions in using the word ‘evil’ to describe the problem it seeks to address. Legislators celebrated the 1961 Convention as the culmination of a 50-year battle to prohibit drugs, a battle that had begun at the Hague Opium Conference of 1911. Yet with hindsight, 1961 was the moment at which the consensus around the evils of drugs began to fracture. An adventurous postwar generation, the first to be raised as truly global consumers, was awakening to the realisation that alcohol was not the world’s only intoxicant. A Spanish court has requested that Neymar and his father stand trial over allegations surrounding the footballer's contentious transfer to FC Barcelona, potentially mirroring a similar legal battle faced by fellow teammate Lionel Messi. The prosecution claims that Neymar's 2013 move to the Catalan giants was undervalued and involved irregularities in the contract between the player and the club, limiting other clubs' ability to bid for his services and impacting the transfer fee.

The original deal, valued at 57.1 million ($64.9 million) by Barcelona, saw 40 million paid to the N&N company owned by Neymar's father, with Santos - where Neymar began his career - receiving 17.1 million, of which 6.8 million was given to DIS. However, Spanish authorities believe that the actual transfer amount was closer to 83.3 million, with the remaining funds reportedly going to Neymar and his family, as well as Santos.

Former Barcelona president Sandro Rosell resigned in 2014 over the affair, and both the club and Rosell were charged with tax fraud related to the signing. Barcelona made a voluntary tax payment of 13.5 million at the time. Despite these developments, no trial has yet taken place.

In response to media scrutiny, Neymar and his father have previously defended themselves, with Neymar telling Brazilian TV station Globo in February that they should be given the opportunity to prove any allegations. "Before making false claims that we hid something, they need to provide proof," he stated. "My father handles the financial aspects of my career, but when you see someone you care about suffering, it hurts."

Spanish prosecutors have called for Neymar, his father Neymar da Silva Santos, Rosell, and FC Barcelona itself to be tried on charges of corruption. If the case proceeds to trial, Neymar faces a potential six-year prison sentence and fines. In addition, Rosell, FC Barcelona, Neymar's father, and Brazilian businessman Jos Maria Campomar, who also played a role in the transfer, are being charged with fraud.

Neymar's mother, Nadine, could potentially face charges as well due to her alleged financial gains from the alleged fraudulent transfer. Both Neymar and his father testified before a judge in Spain in February as part of the ongoing investigation into the transfer, while investigators in Brazil also opened proceedings against the duo over the same case in the same month. The Brazilian federal prosecution service accused them of forging contracts to reduce their tax liability. However, these tax fraud charges were later dismissed by a judge, pending completion of the tax authorities' investigation.

Hey there! So, yes, I'm a professor at Yale University, but let me tell you - I'm no genius. When I first mentioned to my four grown kids that I was going to teach a new course on genius, they thought it was the funniest thing ever. 'You? You're no genius! You're a plodder.' And they were right. So here I am, some dozen years later, still teaching a successful course on genius at Yale, and I even wrote an Amazon Book of the Year selection, The Hidden Habits of Genius (2020). How did that happen? Well, I guess I had the 'boldness of ignorance' that Nikola Tesla talked about.

I started my professional life trying to be a concert pianist, back in the days of the Cold War. The United States was then trying to beat the Soviet Union at every game. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a 23-year-old pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition, which was like the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Brooklyn's Bobby Fischer defeated Boris Spassky in chess. Because I had shown an interest in music and was also tall with huge hands, they thought I would be the next Cliburn. Although our family wasn't wealthy, my parents managed to provide me with a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, DC. Soon enough, I was sent off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music, where every opportunity was presented to me. And I had a strong work ethic - by the age of 21, I had clocked in around 15,000 hours of focused practice (Mozart only needed 6,000 to get to master-composer and performer level).

But within two years, I could see that I would never earn a dime as a concert pianist. I had everything going for me except one thing: I lacked musical talent. No special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no absolute pitch - all things very necessary to a professional performer. 'If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach' - that's the mantra of conservatoires like the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day in the same studio teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists? My intuition was to find a larger arena in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn to become a college professor and researcher of music history - a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I found employment at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the 'three Bs:' Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms.

But the most captivating composer I ran into there was an M: Mozart. My interest in him grew with the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a while, it seemed like the entire world was obsessed with this funny, passionate guy.

text:
The rugged, red-veined rocks of Bohusln in western Sweden are home to one of Europe's highest concentrations of Bronze Age art. During a recent October visit, I had the privilege of witnessing these ancient carvings hidden away from the modern world, nestled where the North Sea would have once lapped at the shore, around 2,500 to 4,000 years ago. These petroglyphs were painted bright red for tourists, resembling a gigantic child's drawing with wobbly lines and vibrant colors. But the raw energy of the scene surpassed any sense of elegance it might have lacked. Archers pursued majestic stags amidst fleets of longships that seemed to flow seamlessly into processions of bulls and towering giants teetering precariously on spindly legs. The boundaries between land, sea, human, and animal blurred as they ebbed and flowed before my eyes.

Bronze Age rock art in Bohusln, Sweden, is believed to depict the enactment of a ritual. As I stood there, I couldn't help but try to piece together some forgotten epic story, like solving a Bronze Age cartoon. We may never know why these markings were made or what they meant to say. The blocky figures offer no clues about where this tale might have begun or ended. Yet, perhaps the tangled quality of the petroglyphs is their true message.

The longships suggest a culture deeply intertwined with the sea, while the abundance of animals hints at a strong bond with nature. Similar sites on the Cornish and Iberian peninsulas indicate that cultural influences traveled along what would later be called the 'whale roads' - the open waters connecting Bronze Age Scandinavia to the rest of Europe for trade and conflict.

We find ourselves in a similarly intricate age, where our consumption of fossil fuels, nitrogen fertilizers, smartphone cobalt, antibiotics, and plastic entwine us with distant times and places. The world of the Anthropocene is woven from this dense network of relationships, each of which leaves its mark on the future.

Two hundred generations have come and gone since these petroglyphs were chiseled into the granite, yet they remain vibrant and immediate, a testament to an enduring presence. In their silence, they pose thought-provoking questions: How do we communicate with the deep future through the currents of time and matter? Are our deliberate symbols what leave the most lasting impressions or are our unintentional traces more enduring?

I reside in Edinburgh, on the edge of the North Sea. I've come to view these waters as an Anthropocene laboratory, a place that allows us to catch a glimpse of the long-term consequences of how we live today. Its past offers insights into our future. As the Pangea supercontinent was splitting apart 200 million years ago...

Three separate analyses of stereotype-threat research, however, have concluded that this effect is at best very weak and perhaps entirely nonexistent. Overall, the evidence simply does not support the idea that stereotypes exert a significant influence on the behaviour of the stereotyped in this way. Where does this leave de Beauvoir’s version of the existentialist idea that internalised gender stereotypes shape the person you become? Her theory does seem to predict that the association between mathematics and maleness should influence the mathematical achievements of women overall. But it does not predict that the stereotype has the kind of influence that the stereotype-threat experiments look for. De Beauvoir’s theory is not that being reminded of the stereotype will temporarily lower a woman’s mathematical abilities. Rather, it is that internalised stereotypes shape the goals we pursue in childhood, adolescence and adult life. De Beauvoir argues that, because we all internalise the stereotypes of gender from a young age, and because we develop interests and experience according to our expectations of ourselves and of one another, those stereotypes shape whom we become. The analyses that show stereotype threat to have little or no influence on behaviour are consistent with de Beauvoir’s theory of how stereotypes shape our lives. These analyses have indeed found a gender disparity at the highest levels of performance on mathematics tests. It is not at all clear why there is such a disparity. Could it be the effect of gender stereotypes shaping the abilities that people develop, as de Beauvoir suggests? LONDON - Former South African cricketer Steve Elworthy has been appointed as the Managing Director for the organization of the 2019 Cricket World Cup, which will be held in England and Wales. The 51-year-old, who made 39 appearances for The Proteas in one-day internationals and played in four Tests after being capped at the age of 32, brings a wealth of experience to the role having previously served as tournament director for major events such as the 2007 and 2009 World Twenty20 competitions, as well as the 2013 Champions Trophy held in England and Wales - a competition he is also responsible for organizing again next year.

Tom Harrison, CEO of the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB), praised Elworthy's appointment, stating that his track record of delivering fan-friendly tournaments has earned him widespread admiration not just within cricket but across the sports industry as a whole. "Elworthy possesses an outstanding ability to deliver well-received tournaments, and we are confident that he will do an exceptional job in organizing both the 2017 and 2019 World Cups," said Harrison.

Elworthy, who had an unsuccessful stint with Lancashire County Cricket Club in 1996 and was dropped on the morning of a one-day final appearance, expressed his excitement for what he described as the biggest challenge of his career to date. "Since I retired from professional cricket in 2003, I have been given numerous opportunities within cricket administration, but managing the ICC Cricket World Cup 2019 is the pinnacle of those experiences," said Elworthy. The tournament is set to begin in May 2019.

text:
Ren Descartes's dog, Monsieur Grat (or 'Mister Scratch'), was a frequent companion on the philosopher's contemplative strolls and deserved his affectionate gaze. Yet, for the most part, Descartes didn't hold nonhuman animals in high regard when it came to their inner lives. In a letter from 1646, he wrote, "Animals do not speak as we do because they have no thoughts." Followers of Descartes still argue that consciousness is a uniquely human trait, potentially facilitated by our ability to communicate and organize memories, sensations, and plans over time. On this view, nonhuman animals are merely sophisticated automatons with preprogrammed behaviors that react to specific stimuli. Insects like bees and ants often symbolize the mechanical approach to animal nature. Researchers have long known that these creatures must possess a diverse range of behaviors in order to construct their intricate dwellings, defend against threats, and care for their young. Yet many still see bees and ants as little more than reflex machines, devoid of an understanding of the world or the ability to foresee even the immediate future. It's believed that, without external stimuli or internal triggers like hunger, an insect's mind is dormant. Insects appear to be philosophical zombies: hypothetical beings reliant on routines and reflexes alone, without any self-awareness. But maybe we're mistaken in attributing machine-like qualities to insects because we understand machinery  while our understanding of even the simplest brains is still fragmentary and imperfect.

However, recent findings suggest that consciousness-like phenomena may not be limited to humans or great apes but could also exist among insects. Not all this evidence comes from experiments designed explicitly to explore consciousness; some has lain hidden in scientific literature for decades, even centuries, without its significance being recognized. Based on such evidence, several biologists (Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney) and philosophers (Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra) now suggest that consciousness-like phenomena may not have evolved as recently as we thought. Instead, they could be evolutionarily ancient, arising during the Cambrian era around 500 million years ago. At its roots, consciousness might be an adaptation that helped animals survive and thrive. In essence, we think it was a way for early life forms to better understand their environment, make decisions, and navigate the world around them. Of course, this is still a contentious issue, and much more research is needed before we can definitively answer whether insects are conscious or not. But the evidence is mounting, and it's worth considering the possibility that these tiny creatures might have inner lives just as rich and complex as our own.

text:
In Orwell's 1984, Winston's rebellion is a quiet, personal act - sneaking away from the ever-watchful screens to jot down thoughts in his hidden diary, just like a teenager craving solitude with her headphones blasting music in her locked bedroom. That is, until he meets Julia. It isn't until romance enters the picture that Winston's rebellion truly comes alive - dangerously so. It's almost as if adolescence is the only time real freedom can start. 1984 holds a special place not for its political commentary, but for its portrayal of the inner turmoil of growing up. The book's enduring appeal lies in its insight into the futility of teenage defiance. Every teenager hates this truth - many even dislike the ending - but it lingers because they know deep down it's true.

In Orwell's contemporary work, Animal Farm (1945), the animals we empathize with experience their ultimate betrayal: the pigs taking up residence in their former masters' house. However, in 1984, it's Winston who ultimately gives in. He comes to love Big Brother because, like adult society, Big Brother has mastered the manipulation of language, power, and thought to preserve its system. We're born into this world, and we lose before we even begin. It's a grim, perhaps cynical perspective - but it resonates with teenagers who see their own lives mirrored in 1984's pages. A dystopian novel with a happy ending might be more entertaining to read, but the book you pass down to your children is the one that first opened your eyes to reality. When a child asks for the same book over and over again, they are essentially asking to learn, to confront what they must accept. As adults, we do the same thing. Books act as therapists, using imagination to unearth secrets we didn't know we harbored. A child may not realize the implications of naming a doll 'mummy' or 'daddy', or having them argue. Similarly, we often fail to recognize the revelations we make when we push a book into someone else's hands. Maybe the best-seller list, devoid of fleeting sensations and dopamine highs, is a reflection of the collective consciousness. Perhaps it's trying to tell us what we need to learn, what we are coming to terms with. There's one thing I am sure of: I hope my daughter doesn't develop the same attachment to The Carrot Seed as I did.

These catastrophes had been made possible by the progress of science and technology, progress that had brought the very existence of our world into jeopardy. The advent of the nuclear age had transformed peace into the perpetual preparation for war, and, in an interesting reversal of Carl von Clausewitz’s dictum that war is a continuation of politics by other means, threatened to cancel politics altogether by ensuring the mutual destruction of the belligerents. Anders deplored tacticians’ and politicians’ collective blindness, their unconsciousness in seeking to instrumentalise the threat of annihilation for political purposes – a gamble that relied on their very ignorance. In the early 1940s, he wrote (translations from the French my own): None of us has a knowledge commensurate with what an atomic war could be … which means that, in this field, no one is competent and that the apocalypse is therefore, by essence, in the hands of the incompetent. The modern use of nuclear power had blurred the distinction between civilian and military, and rendered the possibility of disaster omnipresent. A threshold had been irredeemably crossed when mankind deliberately hung a sword over its head and created the conditions for its self-annihilation. For Anders, the disasters of the 20th century were simply the logical outcome of a pernicious process that had already been underway for many years, involving the gradual exclusion of mankind from all production processes – and, ultimately, from the world created by those processes. As the will to deepen my attention for chess was dying, a part of myself was dying, too If we can’t concentrate, we will not be able to enjoy the state of consciousness – called flow – that is part of the chess experience. Flow was conceived and popularised by the Hungarian-American psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, and it is a mental state characterised by intense absorption, loss of self-consciousness, goal-related feedback from the world and an altered sense of time. Flow experiences are deeply rewarding, and they arise when our skill level and challenge level are optimally matched; too little challenge and we get bored, too much and we feel anxious. Chess is a great way to access flow, yet – as a lodestar for living – flow has limitations. Mostly, it describes a quality of consciousness, not a method for obtaining it. Ultimately, flow is not a virtue but a form of pleasure. While flow is a desirable state of mind, promoting it might not lead to desirable qualities of character; just as likely it could yield an atomised society of sophisticated hedonists with gaming addictions and virtual-reality sickness. Unlike attention or flow, concentration prompts an awareness of mood, even a commitment to meaning, and an appreciation for method. As a young player, I leant on various methods to deepen concentration, including taking long walks before games and listening to favourite music tracks. They worked mostly because the purpose of the concentration was never in doubt. However, in early December 2008, in a generic hotel room in Palma, Mallorca, I vividly remember trying to prepare for a game while feeling unusually displaced from myself. As generations of American football coaches have put it: ‘The will to win is not as important as the will to prepare to win.’ And I noticed that I had lost that. It felt as if the underlying motivational vector for concentration had collapsed; and, as the will to deepen my attention for chess was dying, a part of myself was dying, too. The will to sustain the identity that perpetuated my desire to win had gone, and I knew that it was time to concentrate on what the game symbolised rather than on the game itself. Chess is an arena where system meets psyche, and the world needs a way to make better sense of the nature of that encounter today. To make good chess moves, you need to see the whole position in all its reverberating dynamism. But you also need to see your own mind, and know its power and limitations; the personal is indeed political, and vice-versa. text: of development of a distinction between self and world. Another objection needs to be addressed before we credit too many animals, and only animals, with consciousness. This is the fact that much human behaviour depends on subconscious processing, of which we are often unaware. Our actions in the world rely to a surprising extent on stimuli we haven’t consciously noticed. Moreover, the experience of ‘volition’ has been found to follow our actions after a time-lag, rather than preceding them or being simultaneous with them. Some have interpreted this to mean that consciousness has no effect on behaviour and is purely ‘epiphenomenal’. Instead, maybe the brain collects and weighs current environmental stimuli and data from memory, computes the best behavioural option, and makes the choice for us by initiating an action. If consciousness is causally ineffective, the argument that animals need it for living is unavailable. Or perhaps what we need consciousness for is fully automated in them. However, these arguments do not diminish the case for consciousness being widespread in the animal kingdom. Despite the wonders of unconscious processing, it’s obvious that no human being can nourish herself, escape predation, reproduce, engage in a social life or find the way to a new destination when she is not conscious of a world outside her own body. Although there are impressive examples of ‘blindsight’ – where subjects with a damaged visual cortex can make visual discriminations better than a mere ‘guess’ – the blindsighted are not totally unconscious. Of course, sleeping and damaged brains are not doing their usual job of collecting, weighing and computing. But there is no reason to think that consciousness could be ‘subtracted’ from a brain that’s doing its usual job successfully. It is the organism with a working brain and consciousness that normally faces the challenges of the world. Consciousness is an evolutionary invention like wings or lungs. It is useful to us; it’s therefore most likely to be useful to other organisms with traits deeply homologous to ours. They share with us the difficulties of moving, probing the environment, remembering, predicting the future and coping with unforeseen challenges. If the same behavioural and cognitive criteria are applied as to much larger-brained vertebrates, then some insects are likely to qualify as conscious agents – with no less certainty than cats or Descartes’ dog.

But how might the octopus structure its representation of time? If embodied interactions with our surroundings ground human metaphors for time, one can only imagine the metaphors an octopus might use. Given it has equal competence interacting with objects behind or in front of its body, past events could be, metaphorically speaking, just as manipulable as future ones. Expressions like ‘let’s put the past behind us’ would be meaningless for the octopus. Moreover, given the octopus can change its visual perspective of its surroundings by swimming upward, all events in time – the past, present and future – could be surveyed from one metaphorical wide-view vantage point, allowing for the identification of patterns in events that occur across long periods time. If humans were to adopt octopus-inspired metaphors for time, our relationship to our personal past might become more meaningful. It would allow our past to be causally linked to the present. It might allow our attention to shift away from the culturally conditioned obsession with future-focused progress. It might allow historical ideas to be excavated and used as sources of innovation for everything from medicine to politics. And it might allow a more expansive conceptualisation of events that can be considered meaningful, all due to metaphors of time that are free from constraints. One way of considering how this might be possible is through research on the narratives we form about our lives. According to narrative psychologists, a stable sense of self depends on a unified narrative that structures life experiences into a coherent story, connected in time. Traumatic experiences can rupture a narrative, resulting in dissociative identity disorder in which a person adopts a different personality altogether following an experience that is too difficult to integrate. Treatment for dissociative identity disorder involves flexibly moving through the past and future – analogous to how the octopus moves through space – to first rebuild the individual’s disrupted past sense of self, and then shift focus toward a desired future self. Cultural theorists have written about dissociative identity disorder scaling up to the level of society, following periods of mass trauma such as war. In 1945, Carl Jung wrote that the central task of the German people was to confront the collective guilt associated with the atrocities their country had just committed during the Second World War in order to move forward, towards a more liberal future. Similarly, in the modern-day United States, writers like Ta-Nehisi Coates lay bare historic injustices committed against African Americans, arguing that the only way the country can address racial inequality now and move forward is to acknowledge and repair past wrongs. text:
The root cause of the current environmental crisis, which threatens to devastate vast parts of the biosphere, Indigenous communities worldwide, and even Western technological civilization itself, lies in a mindset that treats nature as mere means to an abstract end. While some longtermists may not have been as explicit as Bostrom, there's a noticeable tendency to view the natural world as utilitarian beings - objects to be used for someone else's benefit, with no intrinsic value of their own. MacAskill and his colleague, for instance, assert that effective altruism (EA) and longtermism are 'welfarist in nature,' meaning they aim primarily to promote wellbeing rather than protect biodiversity or preserve natural beauty.

This perspective, however, is problematic. Every issue we face seems to stem from a lack of restraint in our technological advancement, not an overabundance. Just as concerning is the longtermist insistence that we must constantly strive for more powerful technologies, despite the fact that the primary threat to human existence today comes precisely from these very technologies. In Ord's words, 'without significant efforts to protect humanity, there is a strong reason to believe that the risk will be higher in this century, and increasing with each passing century of technological progress.' Similarly, Bostrom acknowledged in 2012 that most existential risks in the near future stem from human activity - specifically, potential breakthroughs in technology that could drastically enhance our ability to manipulate the world around us or our own biology. As we gain more power, so too will the potential consequences of our actions - intended and unintended, positive and negative.

On this view, the only solution is more technological development, even if it's the riskiest path forward. But isn't this reasoning flawed? If we truly want to ensure our survival, shouldn't we be cautious about developing dangerous new dual-use technologies? If more technology means greater risk - as history and future projections both confirm - then perhaps the only way to achieve 'existential security' is to slow down or even halt technological innovation altogether.

Longtermists offer a solution to this predicament: the so-called 'value-neutrality thesis.' This idea posits that technology itself is morally neutral, just a tool. The NRA's slogan 'Guns don't kill people, people do,' succinctly conveys this message - the consequences of technology, good or bad, beneficial or harmful, are determined by the user, not the object itself. As Bostrom put it in 2002, 'we should not blame civilization or technology for imposing big existential risks.' Ord echoes this sentiment, stating that 'the problem is not so much the technology as it is the way we use it.'

However, this argument doesn't quite hold up. Technology is not morally neutral; it can be used for good or ill. By focusing solely on its potential benefits, we risk ignoring the very real dangers it presents. Instead, we should strive to develop technologies responsibly and with caution, acknowledging their dual-use nature and the potential harm they could cause. Only then can we hope to navigate the uncertain future ahead.

As The New York Times reported in 2012, there has been a huge decline in the percentage of working-class children with married parents over the past three decades. But the available alternatives – like solo parenting, serial monogamy, or forming joint households with friends or siblings – are deeply stigmatised and don’t offer much financial stability. A feminism based on the Liberation Ethic would question the very foundations of our work and family lives. It would attack the ‘masculine’ obsession with narrowly defined profit and productivity. It would demand generous social welfare programmes and part-time jobs with good pay, interesting work, and room for advancement. It would help people transform marriage to work for them – or create different kinds of relationships that suit them better. It would ditch the false dichotomy of dependence and independence and acknowledge that, in a complex human society, we are all necessarily interdependent. Above all, it would argue not that women should live more like men, but that everyone, regardless of gender, should live more like they want to. Real equality won’t come from cajoling men or women into doing work we’d rather not do – at home or at the office. It will come from offering us all better lives.

text:
Isaac Berlin, a renowned thinker and writer, held an almost unmatched status as both an intellectual and moralist in the Western world during the latter half of the 20th century. He passed away on November 5, 1997. Shortly after his demise, Henry Hardy, his editor and friend, described him as "not only the most remarkable and delightful, but also, in so many ways, the wisest, the surest in judgment, and the best."

Berlin's seminal essay on liberty, much like many of his essays that originated from lectures, begins by differentiating between two concepts of political freedom. The first, known as 'negative liberty', refers to the extent to which I am free from interference by other individuals or authorities. Being negatively free doesn't guarantee freedom from physical or psychological constraints; it merely signifies the level of freedom from human intrusion or coercion, such as being allowed to live without excessive political involvement. This contrasts with 'positive liberty', a term used because it denotes the ability to accomplish something rather than just being freed from something: for example, the freedom to run for parliament. At first glance, one might think that we are presented with an overly simplistic distinction between two sides of the same coin. However, Berlin refuses to accept this notion, acknowledging that both concepts appear to be at "no great logical distance from each other." What makes his account unique is that he treats each concept realistically and historically, demonstrating not only a substantively significant  rather than just logically valid  distinction between negative and positive liberty, but also the potential harm of failing to recognize its significance.

Essentially, Berlin flips the script by making theory more grounded in reality instead of the other way around. This bold move places history and realism at the forefront of understanding these concepts. And what history and our current experiences teach us is that negative and positive liberty cater to legitimate human needs and ideals. Negative freedom has been the cornerstone of political liberalism, with its emphasis on individuals being allowed to act independently as long as their actions do not unduly harm others. Similarly, positive liberty has played a central role in emancipatory theories of politics, from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism. In this context, Berlin illustrates that one of the major insights of modern history and contemporary reality is just how disastrous the concept of positive liberty can be when misunderstood or misapplied.

Consider our extensive knowledge or boundless imagination  these qualities might have inherent worth, granting us unique rights, such as the right to not be eaten without consent. Knowledge, acquired in the way humans typically learn, is only possible for reflective, rational beings. As for creative imagination, many believed that excessive rational thinking led to the horrors of World War I, prompting a focus on creativity over logic, as in Andr Breton's Surrealist Manifesto (1924). But what is human creative imagination? Do animals possess such capacity? Perhaps a pet dog can imagine going for a walk. However, this is not the same as the human ability to create art, literature, or revolutionize scientific theories, or envision new ways of living. Only a reflective, rational mind can wield this kind of creativity.

So, it appears that many aspects of humans that seem unique and valuable, potentially deserving of rights, are underpinned by rationality. With this understanding in mind, let's examine the minds of our closest relatives  apes and monkeys. Do they share our rational capabilities? The jury is still out on this one. Researchers disagree, with some suggesting that these creatures can reason or think in a manner akin to humans.
