This does not mean that all of our mini-c moments are going to blossom, of course. But they definitely will not blossom if they are suppressed or dismissed. Beghetto and I kept the term ‘little-c’ to represent everyday creativity. In our model, little-c is when you keep working at mini-c. You share that thought, that rough draft, that working idea. You can get advice and feedback and then keep working, revising and improving. A plot twist might develop into a complete story. An improvised tool might become a basic prototype. At little-c, other people start to appreciate and recognise your creativity. Little-c could be a meal you make for friends that’s your own twist on an old favourite, or a song that you record and share with a small group of listeners online. How do you know if you’ve gone from mini-c to little-c? There’s no checklist to fill out. Certainly, though, when you realise that you are not only sharing your efforts in a particular area with others, but that they are truly enjoying your work, you’re on your way. A lot of people stop at little-c in an area that interests them – and that is absolutely fine. My grandma enjoyed painting and took some classes. She painted for fun and to give to her loved ones. More than 20 years after her death, I have two paintings of colourful flowers on my living room walls. They do not have the precision or skill you might expect from a painting in an art museum. They are not blazingly original; they’re flowers. But they are pretty and they make me think of her, and there is creative worth in that. In other cases, people keep plugging away. Maybe their creative efforts are part of their job, or a personal passion that continues to inspire them. Whatever the reason, with extensive practice over time comes creative expertise, which is the level we call ‘Pro-c’. This is the point where the work begins to have an impact in some way not just among a small circle of friends or in the local community, but on a wider scale. Pro-c is when your efforts are published, produced, recorded or manufactured and distributed broadly to many people. text: of things, men veil the injustice of their superior position and thus cannot, on pain of contradiction, acknowledge the rational constitution of the female sex. When the impasse is so deeply intrenched, how is the cycle of coercion to be broken? The problem is no simpler when considered from the standpoint of women: Surrounded by trivialities from youth onwards, tied up with trinkets, chastened by coercion, and held back by an idleness that feels like comfort, how can, how should, a woman’s mind penetrate through this fourfold fog and find the light?This is no rhetorical question. Given that true progress cannot be made while one half of the human race lies in chains, Holst’s answer is that the fate of the human vocation lies in the ‘higher intellectual development’ of woman. Her text lays down two fundamental principles of woman’s higher development. First, higher development does not stem from an impure source, such as the vain desire to shine (the only motive that her male interlocutors seem to be able to imagine for a woman’s desire for higher learning). It stems ‘from the only true source: humankind’s duty to develop all its powers and to contribute to the wellbeing of the whole as an active member.’ Second, higher intellectual development is ‘entirely free’. A woman must not be confined to gendered elementary books but allowed to follow her genius wherever it leads. Holst contends that the crown of a woman’s higher intellectual development is ‘the philosophy of history’. The philosophy of history is neither a collection of facts nor a matter of a priori speculation. It consists in ‘tracing the course that human inclinations and passions have taken at all times’ and investigating ‘the course of the gradual development of the predispositions and capacities of the human mind from the first stage of culture to its zenith.’ The philosopher of history considers the conditions and institutions in which Enlightenment is advanced and frustrated, and thereby comes ‘to feel their value from a philosophical perspective’. Holst’s goal is not to reject existing institutions but to redefine the normative definition of woman within them Higher intellectual development thus enlivens women to their position as full participants in the human vocation, and thereby empowers them to understand why they presently take a subordinate social role. By grasping their historical standpoint at the zenith of culture, women discover that departing from the state of nature was always nature’s intention. Nature determined that human beings should not remain locked in violent contagion but ‘develop all of their powers’: We only want to be free to fulfil the first duty of humankind, which is to train all of one’s powers in the most beautiful harmony to the highest perfection. We share this duty equally, and it is as much our responsibility as it is that of men. Before we are man or woman, male or female

text:
In today's world, it seems that we thrive on discord. We are wired in such a way that we need a bit of friction to keep us spiritually alert and intellectually stimulated. And who better to provide that necessary discomfort than the contrarian thinkers? John Stuart Mill, in his book On Liberty (1859), actually praised eccentricity! He argued that it's these 'eccentrics' who contribute bold perspectives, fresh insights, and new ideas that keep our world spinning. As he wrote, "Eccentricity has always abounded when and where strength of character has abounded; and the amount of eccentricity in a society has generally been proportional to the amount of genius, mental vigor, and moral courage which it contained."

It's this redeeming 'eccentricity' that contrarians embody. Their ideas are sharp and unique because they refuse to be confined within the boundaries set by any group, whether explicitly or implicitly defined. Excluded as they often are, they have a bird's eye view of how herding, marginalization, and exclusion operate. They no longer fear the consequences of voicing their unconventional views because they have nothing left to lose.

In essence, contrarians are what 'public intellectuals' should strive to be  unyielding critics of society  but unfortunately, in practice, few live up to this ideal. It's the fervor of their dissent, the intensity of their language, and their deep-seated commitment that make them formidable figures. They are not just provocateurs seeking attention or a dramatic performance; they are individuals driven by intellectual duty and inner conviction.

The unique composition of contrarian minds, their innate skepticism of anything authoritative or established, their iconoclasm, and their radical separation from the society in which they were born, all contribute to their access to a higher truth than most societies can bear to hear. They don't care for trends, authorities, or societal hierarchies, and are impatient with the rituals of the establishment. By severing ties with their tribe, they gain an unobstructed view of reality. Their dissent doesn't just free them; it gives them a new perspective.

Spinoza, for instance, didn't truly find his philosophical footing until he was expelled from his community. Even though he was already exceptionally learned, his exclusion opened his eyes to truths that might have remained hidden within the confines of his tribe. In essence, it takes a bit of friction and a dash of eccentricity to truly see things as they are.

text:
Growing up with a mixed lineage, my sisters and I were blessed with the privilege of multiple identities. A diverse genetic pool coursed through our veins, and we felt entitled to claim ownership over any piece of that family history that resonated with us. Boz, however, had no known family tree. His father worked at Trusham Quarry, his grandfathers were both laborers on the farm, and delving deeper into the past only brought us to the foggy tales of our ancestors: a great-grandmother who bred hens that folks from distant counties came to buy; a great-grandfather who knocked out a gypsy boxer at the Okehampton fair. Boz took for granted his lineage, believing they were all laborers who worked the land for meager wages, stretching back through time to peasants and serfs, seemingly unchanging. To him, this was an inevitable and honorable way of life.

I found myself drawn to Boz, despite our stark differences. He was unlike my own father, and I accepted his appreciation for manual labor on the land and around animals at face value. That is until one day, while driving through that region, I spotted a large Union Jack flag waving by the house of the pig farm where Boz worked. As I passed, I could see the ominous row of low buildings  these weren't fields for the pigs to roam but rather, a factory.

Boz's father was a bully, known to dish out physical abuse not just to his sons (Boz being the eldest of three) but also to his wife when he drank excessively, which happened more frequently as time went by. I remember Boz visiting my house occasionally, seething with anger that he rarely spoke of. I grew to recognize that particular mood  it always stemmed from his father and Boz didn't wish to discuss it.

In those days, women seldom left abusive marriages  the only exception being my own mother, who had done so less for escape than for excitement. Children were powerless in the face of a domineering husband. I learned bits and pieces about Boz's father from others. He had reportedly gotten into a dispute with a neighbor. Instead of involving the council or police, he opted to settle matters through fisticuffs. However, the neighbor did call the authorities, leading to Boz's father's arrest and eventual suspended sentence.

When we first moved to this village, my father visited house after house to introduce himself. Boz's father refused to see him  his mother had to explain that a previous rector had 'upset' him as a boy, leaving a lasting resentment.

MEXICO CITY  FIFA unveiled the timeline for its revamped bidding process for the 2026 World Cup on Tuesday, as Gianni Infantino's reformed FIFA Council held its inaugural meeting since it replaced the former executive committee under changes enacted in February. The strategic council, which is responsible for making key decisions, announced that prospective host nations must comply with criteria related to human rights, sustainable event management, and environmental protection. Any bidders falling short of "technical requirements" will be disqualified, according to FIFA.

Infantino was elected as FIFA president in February, and one of his primary pledges was expanding the number of teams in the finals from 32 to 40. However, this proposal is met with strong opposition from Europe's biggest clubs. FIFA revealed that decisions on the number of participating teams, the tournament format, eligible confederations for bidding, and the possibility of joint bids are expected by October. Joint bids were previously prohibited in 2011, but Infantino has expressed his desire to promote them, potentially distributing the tournament across several countries within a specific region.

FIFA outlined that a consultation process detailing new standards for bidders will run until May 2017, followed by a preparation phase extending until December 2018, during which countries must submit their bids. The evaluation of these bids is slated to take place from January 2019 to February 2020, with the final decision set for three months later. FIFA was compelled to reform its bidding process following the December 2010 vote that awarded the 2018 and 2022 tournaments to Russia and Qatar respectively, an election subject to ongoing criminal investigation by Swiss authorities. The preparations of the 2022 host Qatar have also been tainted by allegations of abuse towards migrant workers within the construction industry. Qatar maintains it is addressing the issue.

Under the revised statutes, the hosts will be chosen by FIFA's 209 members at its annual congress. However, the council will still compile a shortlist of three candidates. Previously, hosting rights were determined by the executive committee. FIFA announced that Gibraltar and Kosovo's membership applications would be presented to the Congress on Friday, with both countries eligible to participate in the 2018 World Cup qualifying competition if accepted. Benin was suspended immediately due to legal interference within its football federation, while a decision not to suspend Greece was confirmed by the council. FIFA Council member Fernando Sarney commended Infantino's leadership on Tuesday. "Things are getting calmer at FIFA," said the Brazilian representative to reporters. "Gianni is focusing on football, he is traveling and speaking with people in an effort to bring football back into our discussions, as before we spoke about everything but football."

The remnants of our meals were still scattered on the table, which I assumed would signal to the waitstaff that we were finished eating, but apparently not subtle enough.

While the food was mediocre at best, if I had paid full price for it and experienced the service we did, I would have walked out without a second thought. The games here can be entertaining if they're operational, but given our experience with the staff, I might not notice them next time.

In conclusion, stick to the games if you're coming here - just make sure they're working first! This place is convenient for me and my friends to meet up, but I can't recommend it for food or service.

text:
It seems like we're living in a time where the future of humanity feels uncertain, with dire predictions of catastrophe filling our newspapers and social media feeds. Videos of raging wildfires, devastating floods, and hospitals overwhelmed by COVID-19 patients dominate our timelines. Activists from groups like Extinction Rebellion are taking drastic measures to save the planet, and a survey found that more than half of people believe there's a 50% or greater chance that our way of life could end within the next hundred years.

While apocalypticism isn't a new concept - people have been predicting the end times for millennia - the situation today is different. The potential threats we face now are based on scientific evidence presented by experts in fields like climatology, ecology, epidemiology, and more. We know that climate change poses a severe threat to civilization. We know that biodiversity loss and the current mass extinction could lead to sudden, irreversible, catastrophic shifts in global ecosystems. A nuclear war could darken the skies for years or even decades, causing widespread crop failure. And while the exact origins of SARS-CoV-2 are still debated, it's clear that advances in synthetic biology will soon allow bad actors to design pathogens far more deadly and contagious than anything natural evolution could produce.

Some philosophers and scientists are also warning us about emerging threats like machine superintelligence, molecular nanotechnology, and stratospheric geoengineering, which look just as formidable. These concerns have led many scholars to agree with Stephen Hawking's assessment in The Guardian in 2016 that "we are at the most dangerous moment in the development of humanity." Lord Martin Rees estimates that we only have a 50/50 chance of making it to 2100. Noam Chomsky argues that the risk of annihilation is currently higher than it has ever been in human history. And Max Tegmark believes that "it's probably going to be within our lifetimes...that were either going to self-destruct or get our act together."

Consistent with these grim predictions, the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists set their iconic Doomsday Clock to just 100 seconds before midnight (or doom) in 2020 - the closest it's ever been since the clock was first created in 1947. Over 11,000 scientists from around the world have also signed an article stating "clearly and unequivocally that planet Earth is facing a climate emergency," and without an "immense increase of scale in endeavors" to address these issues, our future looks bleak.

text:
In the heart of Germany, during the late 18th century, a small town named Jena stood out as a beacon of intellectual freedom. One professor, speaking of this place, remarked, "Here, we have complete liberty to think, teach, and write." Compared to other regions, censorship was less stringent, and the range of subjects that could be explored was vast. As Jena's most distinguished resident, the playwright Friedrich Schiller, put it, "The professors here are almost entirely independent." This freedom drew thinkers, writers, and poets who faced persecution in their homelands to this open and relatively free city.

Schiller himself had sought refuge in Jena after being arrested for his revolutionary play The Robbers (1781) in the Duchy of Wrttemberg. On a given day, it wouldn't be uncommon to find more renowned authors, philosophers, and poets strolling through Jena's streets than in a larger city over an entire century. Among them were the tall and gaunt-looking Schiller (who required a drawer full of rotten apples for his writing), the obstinate philosopher Fichte, who centered himself in his work, and the young scientist Alexander von Humboldt  the first to predict harmful human-induced climate change.

The brilliant Schlegel brothers, Friedrich and August Wilhelm, both writers and critics with pens as sharp as French guillotines, lived in Jena, as did the young philosopher Friedrich Schelling, who redefined the relationship between the individual and nature, and G W F Hegel, who would become one of the most influential philosophers in the Western world. This group of extraordinary thinkers was united by a shared obsession with the free self  a rare freedom indeed during an era of absolutism.

In Jena lived the formidable and spirited Caroline Michaelis-Bohmer-Schlegel-Schelling. Despite bearing the names of her father and three husbands, she refused to live by social conventions. The young poet Novalis, a former student of Jena, often visited his friends from his nearby estate in Weienfels during the winter months. One could even catch a glimpse of Germany's most celebrated poet, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, as he skated on the river  his bulging belly held together by a flowery waistcoat. As an older and more renowned figure, Goethe served as something like a benevolent godfather to the younger generation. He was inspired, even rejuvenated, by their new ideas, while they idolized him in return.

These great thinkers drew students from across Germany and Europe to Jena. Starstruck at seeing so many famous poets and philosophers sitting together at concerts in Zur Rose tavern, they couldn't believe their eyes when seemingly all of Germany's greatest minds squeezed into one room during parties. Each of these intellectual giants lived a life worth recounting, but the fact that they all came together at the same time in the same place is even more remarkable. That's why I call them the 'Jena Set' in my book Magnificent Rebels (2022). In an age when most of Europe was still held in shackles, Jena offered a taste of liberation and intellectual exploration that resonates even today.

In a small fishing village, a female North Atlantic right whale lies on her side, her body floating limply in the icy water. Her calls echo through the stillness, a desperate plea for help that brings in a flurry of curious males. One after another, they arrive, their powerful tails slicing through the waves as they attempt to reach her. It's mating season, but something doesn't seem right. The whales are behaving oddly, and this has left scientists scratching their heads.

"There's no other animal on Earth where we know so little about its basic reproduction," says marine biologist Dr. John Hunt, his voice tinged with frustration. "We can't even figure out why these whales are mating at the wrong times of year."

The question that looms large is whether these whales might be storing sperm for later use or if they mate just for fun. Dr. Hunt and his team have been trying to answer this question through the study of a unique tissue found in whales: baleen.

In 2014, Dr. Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian from Barrow, Alaska, suggested applying the keratin approach to baleen. "It suddenly got really exciting when we realized we might be able to get a pregnancy signature out of the baleen," said Dr. Hunt.

The team began their research with bowhead whales, whose subsistence hunts are the only sustainable and reliable source of baleen. The slow-growing baleen plates of these whales, which capture 25 years of growth, proved to be an excellent starting point for their study.

By analyzing the tissues for stress hormones like cortisol and reproductive hormones like progesterone, they were able to distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. The team then used existing estimates of baleen growth rates as a chronometer for their samples.

As they looked at older baleen from mature females, they found changes in progesterone that neatly matched the estimated gestation length of bowhead whales. It seemed that the baleen was documenting past pregnancies.

To cross-check their technique, the team needed baleen samples from whales with known pregnancy histories. That's where Stumpy and another North Atlantic right whale nicknamed Staccato came in. Both whales had a nearly 30-year-long file of regular sightings, including two recent pregnancies. And fortunately, their carcasses happened to be found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to collect plates of their baleen for future study.

"They're really valuable samples," said Dr. Hunt. "With these, we can finally start to unlock some of the secrets of these mysterious creatures."

Their fear of falling may seem the same as ours looking on from safety, but it's not the fall they dread; it's the flames. And those standing outside, shouting 'Don't!' and 'Hang on!', can never truly understand the terror that drives such a choice. The suicidal person is not misguided but rather facing choices beyond our comprehensionchoices only understood by those who also feel the flames slowly consuming them.

text: If you ask philosophically minded researchers – in the Anglophone world at least – why it is that science works, they will almost always point to the philosopher Karl Popper (1902-94) for vindication. Science, they explain, doesn’t presume to provide the final answer to any question, but contents itself with trying to disprove things. Science, so the Popperians claim, is an implacable machine for destroying falsehoods. Popper spent his youth in Vienna, among the liberal intelligentsia. His father was a lawyer and bibliophile, and an intimate of Sigmund Freud’s sister Rosa Graf. Popper’s early vocations draw him to music, cabinet making and educational philosophy, but he earned his doctorate in psychology from the University of Vienna in 1928. Realising that an academic post abroad offered escape from an increasingly antisemitic Austria (Popper’s grandparents were all Jewish, though he himself had been baptised into Lutheranism), he scrambled to write his first book. This was published as Logik der Forschung (1935), or The Logic of Scientific Discovery, and in it he put forward his method of falsification. The process of science, wrote Popper, was to conjecture a hypothesis and then attempt to falsify it. You must set up an experiment to try to prove your hypothesis wrong. If it is disproved, you must renounce it. Herein, said Popper, lies the great distinction between science and pseudoscience: the latter will try to protect itself from disproof by massaging its theory. But in science it is all or nothing, do or die. Karl Popper, 1987. Photo by Süddeutsche Zeitung/AlamyPopper warned scientists that, while experimental testing might get you nearer and nearer to the truth of your hypothesis via corroboration, you cannot and must not ever proclaim yourself correct. The logic of induction means that you’ll never collect the infinite mass of evidence necessary to be certain in all possible cases, so it’s better to consider the body of scientific knowledge not so much true as not-yet-disproved, or provisionally true. With his book in hand, Popper obtained a university position in New Zealand. From afar, he watched the fall of Austria to Nazism, and commenced work on a more political book, The Open Society and its Enemies (1945). Shortly after the war, he moved to the UK, where he remained for the rest of his life. For all its appealing simplicity, falsification was quickly demolished by philosophers, who showed that it was an untenable way of looking at science. In any real experimental set-up, they pointed out, it’s impossible to isolate a single hypothetical element for disproof. Yet for decades, Popperianism has nonetheless remained popular among scientists themselves, in spite of its potentially harmful side-effects. Why should this be? text: divine grace, the unmerited love of God for sinful humanity. Certain modern synonyms for the death-stroke at first glance seem to support this interpretation: Gnadenstoss in German ‘the jab of grace’, for example, but it does not appear to be medieval. Coup de grâce, also not medieval, means a gracious blow, understood as a death-stroke, as does mercy-stroke in English, a usage not attested before the 18th century. Since modern usages cannot establish the truth of this theory, an opening exists for a competing theory – and one, I think, far more persuasive than the preceding. It is this. There is no evidence that knights called their daggers misericords before the crusades. If this silence in the texts mirrors reality, then it is likely that, when knights began seeking blessings for the weapons they wielded in the holy wars, they did so because the ostensible purpose of the expeditions was to bring relief for – mercy to – fellow Christians. Their mission was to protect and succour those reportedly suffering under or resisting the oppression of non-believers, especially Muslims in the eastern Mediterranean. This would also explain why so many of these weapons exhibit engraved crosses on their handles and blades. For the ‘cross’, as a monastic writer noted centuries before this strange co-mixture of the lethal and the compassionate had emerged, ‘was made the Sun of Justice for us that we might be illuminated by its mercy.’ As with swords, prized knives received names from their warrior owners, who had engravers inscribe them with various devotional formulas. One such warrior’s knife dating from the late 13th century bears the extraordinary inscription ‘AMOR VINCIT OMNIA’ – ‘love,’ perhaps the name of the knife, ‘conquers all.’ At one level, the inscription alludes to the Constantinian vision, In hoc signo vinces. At another, in my opinion, it suggests that the warrior who authorised the engraving was construing righteous warfare, and therefore the use of this sanctified weapon, as an act of love. A festive commemoration of Muhammad Ali's life continued on Wednesday in his hometown of Louisville, where the "I Am Ali" Festival celebrated the iconic boxer's legacy by sharing stories and engaging children through arts and crafts. Craig Davidson, a 45-year-old Louisville resident, brought his 15-year-old sons to pay their respects. "As a kid their age, I grew up watching his fights," Davidson said. "I knew about his stance during the Vietnam War. I just wanted them to know more about him."

Ali, whose boxing prowess, charisma, and vocal opposition to the Vietnam War made him one of the world's most influential figures in the turbulent 1960s and '70s, passed away last Friday at an Arizona hospital due to septic shock. He was 74 years old. Preparations were underway for the final days of mourning, including a public memorial service on Friday at the KFC Yum! Center. Fans lined up outside the arena earlier in the day to secure tickets for the service, with at least 14,000 distributed after officials released them early due to the long queue.

The final service will feature notable figures such as former U.S. President Bill Clinton and comedian Billy Crystal. Actor Will Smith, who portrayed Ali in a 2001 film, and former heavyweight champion Lennox Lewis will serve as pallbearers. The festival took place at the Kentucky Center for the Performing Arts, with activities geared towards children. These included music, dance, art, and educational workshops celebrating Ali's life. Children had the opportunity to create butterfly and bee masks, symbolizing Ali's famous quote about his boxing style: "Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee. The hands can't hit what the eyes can't see."

"Today is about creating a connection with Muhammad Ali for these children," Louisville Mayor Greg Fischer explained in an interview on Wednesday. "As they grow older, they can reflect on this day, ensuring his legacy continues across multiple generations." The festival began with the release of 30 Monarch butterflies into the air, reminding everyone that before winning the boxing heavyweight title or claiming an Olympic gold medal, Ali was a young boy from Louisville whose bicycle had been stolen. Upon turning to the police for help, he discovered his love for boxing.

It's worth acknowledging a nuanced difference when discussing the nature of labor conditions in the Persian Gulf, but it's important to remember that this doesn't negate the fact that these societies can still be rightfully categorized as slave societies. A discerning observer might question: 'But what about the definition of slavery?' The work relationships prevalent in the Persian Gulf aren't reminiscent of the property-based relations described by Finley, where slaves were owned and traded like commodities in markets and other circumstances. Yet, we must also concede that contemporary human relationships can exhibit characteristics strikingly similar to chattel slavery, as defined by sociologist Orlando Patterson as 'social death'. This is when the social and legal disadvantage of a laborer is so severe that it effectively renders them enslaved, regardless of whether the legal system officially recognizes their ownership.

One additional point worth noting is the correlation between homicide and slavery observed by Davis and legal scholar Robert Cover. When we examine historical accounts of slave transportation across various routes such as the transatlantic, trans-Saharan, Silk Road, and Indian Ocean slave trades, we see that death rates often exceeded 30%. In modern Persian Gulf societies, we witness high rates of homicide and unexplained deaths among low-status migrant laborers. The most alarming evidence can be found in reports about the welfare of Nepalese workers, particularly those working on construction sites for the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar. A recent report indicates that at least 1,400 Nepali workers have died on these sites since work commenced, with approximately 150 deaths per year continuing to occur. The International Labour Organization has also published a report highlighting an alarmingly high death rate among otherwise healthy Nepalese migrant workers, particularly in Saudi Arabia, Oman, Bahrain, and Kuwait. The death rate in Saudi Arabia was significantly higher than the death rate in Malaysia, with almost the same number of deaths occurring in Malaysia despite greater numbers of Nepalis traveling there for work. Similar statistics can be found for other Persian Gulf countries. The estimated death rate for Nepalese workers in Saudi Arabia between 2008 and 2015 was 2.26 per 1,000 workers, while the rates for Bahrain, Oman, and Lebanon were even higher at 2.60, 2.40, and 2.86 respectively. These rates should be contrasted with lower death rates in Malaysia (1.72) and South Korea (1.61). It seems that the death rate for Nepalese migrant workers in the Persian Gulf has remained consistent over time, and a significant number of these deaths remain unexplained. Many of the unexplained deaths are young women.

text: If I shout Checkmate! during a game of gin rummy, I will cause complete bafflement The problem with the strong juicer view is that, like non-cognitivism, it’s implausible as an account of how religious language is generally used. When the religious person says: God exists, and atheists respond by pointing to the depth of evil in the world, the religious person typically tries to account for the evil by offering theodicies – explanations for the evil (This evil – war – is a result of acting freely. That evil is there to build our characters) – or by appealing to mystery (For all we know, God exists and has good reason to allow these evils). If what the religious person meant were entirely metaphorical, such religious responses would make as much sense as my responding to Mary by pointing out that the evidence that Jane is not a glowing body of ignited gas and that Tom lacks wings is less than decisive, or by insisting that for all we know Jane literally is a glowing body of ignited gas. A variant of the strong juicer view starts with the Wittgensteinian thought that our use of language is embedded in certain ‘language-games’ and ‘forms of life’. Suppose that during a game of gin rummy I shout Checkmate! Given that ‘checkmate’ has its home in an entirely different game bound by very different rules to gin rummy, my remark will likely result in complete bafflement. Indeed, it’s not clear that I would have succeeded in saying anything at all. Some suggest that, similarly, utterances such as Jesus rose from the dead have their home in religious language-games, and when they are placed in different language-games, bound by different rules (eg, in a science lab or academic history class), they too are stripped of content. But then such utterances offer nothing for the scientist in her lab or the historian in her academic class to dispute. Scientific and historical criticism of religious belief is impossible. Again, this variant strong juicer view is implausible as an account of how religious language is generally used. The religious do themselves regularly use God exists, Jesus rose from the dead and so on in scientific and historical contexts – using fine-tuning arguments for God’s existence, and historical arguments for the resurrection. And even if it is true that talk of God’s existence and Jesus’ resurrection have their home within a religious form of life to which non-believers fail to belong, it does not follow that those non-believers cannot reasonably contradict and reject such beliefs. After all, accusations made during the 17th-century Salem witch trials had their home in a religious form of life to which contemporary atheists fail to belong, but that doesn’t preclude contemporary atheists contradicting and indeed refuting 17th-century religious accusations of witchcraft. text:
In reading Boethius' Consolation, it becomes apparent that readers are keenly aware of a work's genre, and this knowledge shapes their expectations regarding how its author intends it to be understood. By crafting the Consolation in alternating prose and verse, Boethius signaled that his work belongs to the Menippean satire genre, which traditionally mocks figures of authority. As Joel Relihan demonstrated in Ancient Menippean Satire (1993), this genre often depicts the failures of those in power. Therefore, readers of the Consolation might expect that Philosophy's teachings would not be treated with complete seriousness.

Relihan goes on to develop a reading diametrically opposed to the straightforward interpretation in The Prisoner's Philosophy (2007). According to Relihan, Philosophy fails to provide consolation for the Prisoner, and this failure reveals Boethius' critique of human reasoning. The underlying message is that Christian faith alone offers the true consolation, which the imprisoned Boethius mistakenly sought from Philosophy.

This interpretation treats all the Consolation's philosophical discourse as mere rhetoric, created by Boethius the author solely to demonstrate its inadequacy and deliver the main message indirectly. However, it is difficult to accept this notion. Would Boethius, who dedicated his life to philosophy, really have treated arguments so callously? Moreover, why would he elaborate on an intricate argument at the end of the work about divine prescience and contingency, arguably his finest piece of reasoning, if his goal were merely to show the inadequacy of philosophy rather than its ability to console?

Although Relihan's reading raises crucial questions, it ultimately proves unconvincing. A more reasonable perspective is that Boethius intended for Philosophy's arguments to be taken seriously and made a genuine effort to articulate well-reasoned perspectives through her voice. At the same time, he wanted readers to recognize the limitations of such reasoning by creating tensions within the argumentative structure of the Consolation as a whole.

Three main areas of tension run throughout the Consolation: human happiness, providence and the suffering of the good, and human freedom. Over an extended period, Philosophy presents a nuanced view of human happiness that values the true goods of sufficiency, respect, power, enduring fame, and joy while acknowledging the genuine worth of some goods of fortune, such as loved ones.

However, starting from the middle of the work, Philosophy's initial, easier remedies give way to a more complex understanding of human happiness. This complexity lies in accepting that some goods of fortune have value but recognizing that seeking many of the things people typically pursue - riches, high office, kingdoms, public praise, and sensual pleasures - is a misguided attempt to obtain the true goods of happiness. The true goods, which cannot be taken away from someone in the Prisoner's position, are essential to happiness, but some goods of fortune, like loved ones, are also accepted as valuable.

Despite presenting a thoughtful and nuanced understanding of human happiness, Boethius still wanted readers to question the limitations of such reasoning by creating tensions within the argumentative structure of the Consolation.

He called out her name. She turned, and smiled. Fukuji now saw who the man was, too. He had been in love with Fukuji’s wife before Fukuji had married her. Both had died in the tsunami. Fukuji’s wife called to him, over her shoulder: ‘I am married now, to this man.’‘But don’t you love your children?’ Fukuji cried out in reply. His wife paused at that, and began to sob. Fukuji looked sadly at his feet for a moment, not knowing what more to say. When he looked up, the woman and the man had drifted away. People struggled to reach loved ones, first by phone and later by searching through the debris left behind as the waters receded. Some went on looking for weeks, months, even years, as the toll of the dead and missing rose towards 20,000. Survivors of the disaster soon began seeing and feeling ghostly presences. text:
In this exploration, I delve into a synthesis that draws upon the wisdom of African ethnophilosophy while tackling the questions posed by non-African philosophies. The term 'ethnophilosophy' gained prominence through the African philosopher Paulin Hountondji, who used it in a critical light to describe the philosophical pursuit that aims to recognize traditional African viewpoints as philosophically significant and, for some African scholars, a distinctive African addition to humanity's philosophical legacy.

While Hountondji was correct in rejecting ethnophilosophy as representative of substantive African philosophy, he overlooked its potential as a rich source of African thought. Western philosophers like Thaddeus Metz at the University of Johannesburg, who have ventured into African philosophy, have greatly benefited from ethnophilosophical resources. Ethnophilosophy, comprising African traditional worldviews and scholarly writings on traditional African phenomena, has provided me with the foundational ideas for what I call 'consolation philosophy.'

From the monistic worldview of the Idoma people of Central Nigeria, I took the concept of mood as the essence of things. From Senghor, I borrowed the idea of the melancholic being, and built a comprehensive philosophy of human existence around it. From African philosophers such as Placide Tempels, Asouzu, Ramose, and Chimakonam, I took the idea of an interconnected universe filled with missing links  a cosmic picture echoed by Julius Nyerere and Senghor in their descriptions of the African extended-family system. Consolation philosophy is African philosophy, but I believe its principles are universally applicable, engaging the interests of Europeans, Americans, Indians, Chinese, and others alike.

My intellectual journey has continued to unfold, enriched by my interactions with fellow African philosophers, Western analytic and continental philosophers, and my growing curiosity about Oriental philosophies. As an African philosopher, my personal struggle is finding a philosophical balance  navigating the waters between the challenges of tradition and the allure of modernity.

text: About 4,200 years ago, the area we now call southern Iraq was rocked by revolts. The once-independent Sumerian city states had been brought under one rule by the legendary king Sargon of Akkad. Over the course of what modern historians call the Old Akkadian period, the reign of Sargon and his successors reshaped the newly conquered cities in countless ways: old nobles were demoted and new men brought to power, old enemies were defeated and new standards of statecraft imposed. The Sumerian world grew much bigger and richer, but also more unstable. Discontent with the new empire festered, provoking a steady stream of uprisings as the cities attempted to regain their independence. One such revolt is depicted in a fascinating poem known as ‘The Exaltation of Inana’. Besides being a poetic masterpiece in its own right, ‘The Exaltation’ bears the distinction of being the first known work of literature that was attributed to an author whom we can identify in the historical record, rather than to an anonymous tradition or a fictional narrator. The narrator of the poem is Enheduana, the high priestess of the city of Ur and the daughter of Sargon. According to ‘The Exaltation’, she was cast into exile by one of the many revolts that plagued the Old Akkadian Empire. Tablets inscribed with The Exaltation of Inana in three parts. Old Babylonian period, c1750 BCE. Courtesy of the Yale Babylonian Collection We do not know for sure whether the poem was written by the historical Enheduana herself, as a literary retelling of a real event, or by a later poet writing in her name, in the ancient version of a historical fiction that was meant to celebrate the famous high priestess. What we do know is that the poem conveys a sense of what it would have been like to live through a period of profound turbulence, whether as a personal account or an echo preserved in the cultural memory of later periods. That is one reason why the works attributed to Enheduana – which, besides ‘The Exaltation’, comprise four other poems – continue to speak to us, millennia after their composition. As the world grows increasingly unstable, we have much to learn from this ancient priestess. The poems do not merely register the reality of an historic upheaval – they go one step further by turning that instability into a cosmic insight, an occasion to reflect on what the world is really like. They contain, compressed within their often-cryptic verses, the germ of an ancient philosophy of change. If the poems seek to turn radical change from a transient political phenomenon into a universal principle, they do so primarily by exalting the goddess Inana, better known by her Babylonian and Assyrian name Ishtar. The choir, a group of men chained together and dressed in prison uniforms, were singing about Tunisia’s former political prisoners — more than 3,000 at recent count, incarcerated and horribly abused by the former regime; now released, but adrift and unsupported, they are struggling to re-enter society. text:
In the realm of events, we find chains and cascades of occurrences, shrouded in language's shadows. According to Koselleck, this is what we call 'history'. It's not driven by reason or a predetermined purpose as some might suggest, but rather it flows from a series of contingent events shaped by formal oppositions such as before and after, above and below, inner and outer.

History, in essence, lacks inherent meaning or direction. However, through careful analysis, we can start to make sense of its complexities and contradictions. The grid Koselleck proposed for understanding history - his theory of history - influences how historians approach their work, shaping their perspectives based on factors like time (contemporary or retrospective), social or political position, and connection to the community being studied.

For instance, a contemporary historian may view events through different lenses than someone recounting them later as an eyewitness or a narrator. Similarly, a historian's position in the social or political hierarchy can impact their interpretation of events, whether they are aligned with the winners or losers of a conflict. And finally, a historian's affiliation with the community being studied - be it political, religious, social, or economic - influences how objectively they can analyze the events.

Koselleck believed that there is a fundamental difference between his theory of history and hermeneutics (or Michel Foucaults discourse analysis). He argued that not all experiences can be encapsulated by language, especially when intense emotions like friendship and enmity, power and subjugation, or gender inequality come into play. As he stated in a lecture honoring his teacher Hans-Georg Gadamer in 1985, these intense divisions create events that resist definition, leaving every word, sentence, or speech feeling inadequate.

It was this resistance to language that intrigued Koselleck as both a historian and theorist. Some of his most profound essays delve into the boundaries of language. For example, 'Terror and Dream', originally penned as an afterword for the German edition of Charlotte Beradt's The Third Reich of Dreams (1966; English translation 1968), is a compelling exploration of these limits. In this essay, subtitled Methodological Remarks on the Experience of Time during the Third Reich, Koselleck posits that dream protocols collected by Beradt provide a more authentic portrayal of life in Nazi Germany than any other historical source. Dreams, as pre-linguistic manifestations of terror, capture the reality of the Third Reich at this very moment, reflecting not just fear and humiliation from previous days or weeks but also the ongoing terror that was unfolding.

Over the past few years, the Kindle hardware design team has been focusing on creating the perfect digital reading device - a universal container for books if you will. However, I've recently found myself drifting away from purchasing digital books, despite my daily consumption of digital content on my phone.

The pile of unread books on my bedside table has always felt like a graveyard of good intentions, but the list of unread books on my Kindle feels more like a black hole of fleeting intentions. I realized this only recently when taking stock of both my physical and digital libraries. While I continue to acquire physical books at an alarming rate, particularly works of literary fiction, my interest in digital books has waned.

I split my time between New York and Tokyo, and each trip to the city means a visit to bookstores like McNally Jackson or Three Lives and Company. The allure of their curated front tables is too much to resist. I still love giving and receiving physical books as gifts. Yet, ironically, I spend hours every day reading articles and essays on my phone, sharing them effortlessly with others.

The romance between our smartphones and short-to-mid-length content feels almost natural at this point, given the convenience and ease of use they offer. This is part of the reason why venture capitalists have poured hundreds of millions of dollars into New York publishing startups like Vox, Vice, and Buzzfeed. The smartphone coupled with the open web provides a near-perfect container for distributing journalism at scale.

However, when it comes to digital books, I've found myself drawn back to print. Once bought by a reader, a book moves through a routine - it is read and underlined, dog-eared and scuffed, and most importantly, reread. Reading a book once feels like knowing it in passing; reading it over and over again deepens the relationship between the reader and the book. The bond between a reader and a book is measured not in hours or minutes but, ideally, in months and years.

As a consumer of digital books, I find the Kindle experience pitch-perfect - with a boundless catalogue, instant distribution, reasonable prices, and seamless integration into Amazon's ecosystem. But as a reader, I feel a sense of emptiness. All the consumption parts are great, but the tactile experience of holding a physical book, turning its pages, and marking it up is missing.

Amazon's dominance in the publishing industry is well-known, powering not just publishers but also hosting files and providing servers for many large companies around the world. The revenue generated from this business alone accounts for nearly $2 billion of Amazon's bottom line. So it's no surprise that they have built a seamless, efficient system for digital book distribution. But for me, there's something missing - a sense of connection, of intimacy, that I find in a physical book. Perhaps this is why I continue to buy and read physical books voraciously, despite my daily consumption of digital content on my phone.

**MEXICO CITY:** In a significant development, FIFA revealed the timeline for its postponed bidding process for the 2026 World Cup on Tuesday. This announcement was made during the inaugural meeting of FIFA's newly constituted FIFA Council, which replaced the former executive committee following reforms enacted in February. The Council, with a strategic mandate, stipulated that potential host nations must adhere to criteria concerning human rights, sustainable event management, and environmental protection. Nations failing to meet "technical requirements" would be disqualified, according to the statement.

Gianni Infantino, who was elected as FIFA president in February, has pledged to increase the number of teams in the finals from 32 to 40, a proposal that has garnered strong opposition from Europe's leading clubs. Decisions regarding the number of teams, tournament format, eligibility of continental confederations to bid, and whether to permit joint bids are expected by October. Infantino has expressed his intention to encourage joint bids, potentially distributing the event across several countries within a region.

FIFA outlined that a "consultation process" detailing new standards for potential hosts will run until May 2017, followed by a preparation phase lasting until December 2018, during which nations would submit their bids. The evaluation process will commence in January 2019 and continue until February 2020, with the final decision to be made three months later. FIFA was compelled to revise its bidding process following the controversial awarding of the 2018 and 2022 tournaments to Russia and Qatar respectively in December 2010. This vote is currently under investigation by Swiss authorities, while Qatar's preparations have been dogged by allegations of abuse of migrant workers within the construction industry. Qatar has asserted that it is addressing this issue.

Under the revised statutes, the hosts will be chosen by FIFA's 209 member nations at its annual Congress. However, the Council will still compile a shortlist of three candidates. Previously, hosting rights were decided by the old executive committee. FIFA also announced that Gibraltar and Kosovo's applications for membership would be presented to the Congress on Friday, and that both could participate in the 2018 World Cup qualifying competition if accepted. Benin was suspended with immediate effect due to legal interference in its football federation. The Council ratified a decision not to suspend Greece.

FIFA Council member Fernando Sarney praised Infantino's leadership on Tuesday, stating, "Things are calmer in FIFA now. Gianni is focusing on football, traveling and engaging with people to bring football back into our discussions because previously we talked about everything but football."

text: Arriving at the chess board is like entering an eagerly anticipated party. All my old friends are there: the royal couple, their associates, the reassuringly straight lines of noble infantry. I adjust them, ensuring that they are optimally located in the centre of their starting squares, an anxious fidgeting and tactile caress. I know these pieces, and care about them. They are my responsibility. And I’m grateful to my opponent for obliging me to treat them well on pain of death. In many ways, I owe chess everything. Since the age of five, the game has been a source of friendship, refuge and growth, and I have been a grandmaster for 20 years. The lifelong title is the highest awarded to chess players, and it is based on achieving three qualifying norms in international events that are often peak performances, combined with an international rating reflecting a consistently high level of play – all validated by FIDE, the world chess federation. There are about 1,500 grandmasters in the world. At my peak, I was just outside the world top 100, and I feel some gentle regret at not climbing even higher, but I knew there were limits. Even in the absence of a plan A for my life, chess always felt like plan B, mostly because I couldn’t imagine surrendering myself to competitive ambition. I have not trained or played with serious professional intent for more than a decade, and while my mind remains charmed by the game, my soul feels free of it. In recent years, I have worked in academic and public policy contexts, attempting to integrate our understanding of complex societal challenges with our inner lives, while also looking after my two sons. I miss many things about not being an active player. I miss the feeling of strength, power and dignity that comes with making good decisions under pressure. I miss the clarity of purpose experienced at each moment of each game, the lucky escapes from defeat, and the thrill of the chase towards victory. But, most of all, I miss the experience of concentration. I can still concentrate, of course, but not with the same reliability and intensity that a life of professional chess affords. In fact, from a distance, chess looks to me suspiciously like a socially permissible pretext to concentrate for several hours at a time. In The Island from the Day Before (1994), Umberto Eco composes a love letter that includes the line: ‘[O]nly in your prison does [my heart] enjoy the most sublime of freedoms’ – that could be said of chess, too, and the experience of concentration is what makes it possible. She wasn’t English, she wasn’t a scientist, hence she wasn’t right. Sent off, in compensation, to the Galápagos Islands to repeat the fieldwork that Charles Darwin had done a century before, Bateson found the inhabitants far more interesting than the fauna. Pleading with his father not to be ‘terribly disappointed’, he wrote to tell him he needed to ‘break with ordinary impersonal science’. He would study people, anthropology. Ten years later, on the opening page of Naven, he observed that, while the artist might capture the whole emotional tone or ethos of a society in a few pages, anthropologists restricted themselves to the mechanics of its organisation. But he would seek to link organisation and ethos. As a scientist, he would invade the territory of art; or do science the way an artist might. Thus Bateson, responding to the conflicting attitudes of his parents, found himself at the beginning of the movement that questioned the limitations of a traditional scientific approach, in particular the notion that the scientific observer is entirely separate from the objects of his or her study. But such thinking was ingrained in Western civilisation. ‘There are times,’ he wryly remarked ‘when I catch myself believing there is something which is separate from something else.’ Naven takes its title from a series of bizarre rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which bewildered Bateson for some years. text:
In a small town on the Arctic Ocean, a female bowhead whale lies still, her body floating gently in the icy waters. Each year, males come to her, drawn by an instinctive urge to mate. As they swim around her, their bodies thrash and churn the water, leaving behind a trail of splashes and questions.

The issue is that this encounter occurs at a time of year when calves are typically born (December through March). This leads to various theories: perhaps we have miscalculated the gestation period of these mammoth creatures, or maybe the females can store sperm for later fertilization, or even that the whales engage in mating for pleasure.

Dr. Hunt summed it up perfectly when he said, "There's no other animal on Earth where we know so little about basic reproduction." In 2012, a breakthrough came in the form of a suggestion by Dr. Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian from Barrow, Alaska. She proposed applying a method used to study keratin-based tissues to baleen.

The idea was thrilling because it meant we might be able to uncover the reproductive history of these whales, something that had never been done before. Dr. Hunt, along with Dr. Rolland and their colleagues, started their investigation with a study published in 2014, focusing on bowhead whales hunted sustainably by native Alaskans.

The reason for choosing bowheads was twofold: these subsistence hunts provide the only reliable source of baleen, which can only be obtained from a deceased whale, and bowheads, being the closest relatives to right whales, have the longest-growing baleen. An adult's plate records approximately 25 years of growth, longer than any other keratin-based tissue that has been studied.

Using this baleen, the team was able to detect cortisol, a stress hormone, and progesterone, a reproductive hormone. They could easily distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. The fact that baleen continues to grow made it an ideal tool for studying the whales' lives.

The team was particularly excited when they found changes in progesterone in older baleen from mature females that neatly matched bowheads' estimated gestation length. It seemed as if the baleen was a diary, chronicling the whales' pregnancies. However, to validate their method, they needed baleen from whales with known pregnancy histories.

Enter Stumpy and another North Atlantic right whale named Staccato. Both whales had extensive records of regular sightings, including two confirmed pregnancies. Remarkably, the carcasses of both were found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to preserve their baleen.

"They're really valuable samples," said Dr. Hunt. And so, the team began piecing together the reproductive history of these magnificent creatures, one plate at a time.

text:
In the vein of Upton Sinclair's exposs a century ago, we're once again tempted by the idea that we can have it all: indulgence and wellness. We crave authentic, mouthwatering, fresh grub, but we also yearn for sustainability and economic revival. It seems like we've found the perfect blend  a wholesome loaf brimming with self-righteous morsels of goodness. Michael Pollan encourages us to 'eat food', promising pleasure and wellness will follow. Yet, we're cautioned against flavors that are delightful yet "bad". Modern food technology is now the target, designing edibles that are literally irresistible. As Michael Moss pointed out in Salt, Sugar, Fat (2013), corporations have organized their operations around the pursuit of the 'hyperpalatable', aiming for a sensory 'bliss point'  delivering addictive taste while neglecting nutritional value. Those who savor these foods are ridiculed, their tastes criticized even as they are pitied for their choices, which carry the stigma of poor health.

The stakes of good taste feel higher than ever, dividing those whose virtuous choices promote health and longevity from those whose insatiable appetites lead them towards disease and decay. But promising health and wellbeing as rewards for individual good taste raises questions worth pondering. The food deemed 'good' (or 'real') is less technological, more 'natural', less 'processed'  and often more expensive. As Rachel Laudan reminds us, opting for less processed food means taking on more of the labor yourself  a shift towards domestic preparation, and often women. Contrary to Upton Sinclair's vision of flavor and health for all, good food is commonly linked with resources, access, and time to procure, prepare, and savor it. Good taste  and good health  remain luxury items, symbols of distinction, largely the domain of the fortunate or affluent. Moreover, food habits are portrayed predominantly as matters of individual choice (expressions of good or bad taste) and personal responsibility, rather than outcomes of intricate social and technological systems. Taste is vital, but it is not health.

The essay tries to highlight the paradox that we live in today where we yearn for both indulgence and wellness through food, but the food that is considered 'good' or 'real' is often expensive, time-consuming to prepare, and associated with wealth, making healthy eating a privilege rather than a right. The essay also points out how our perception of good taste and health is influenced by complex social and technological systems, but these factors are rarely acknowledged or discussed in popular discourse.

text:
The leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru embodied a dedication to some Pan-Asian principles that resonated strongly at the Bandung Conference of 1955. This gathering of 29 Asian and African nations, representing more than half the world's population, was the last significant manifestation of Asian unity, eventually overshadowed by Cold War tensions and nation-building projects. Pan-Islamism has ebbed and flowed throughout the past century, from Turkey and Egypt to Indonesia and Algeria. The concept of Muslim intellectualism and global solidarity among Muslims empowered many 20th-century nationalist leaders and movements. By the mid-1960s, most of the world's Muslims had shaken off European colonial rule. The Ottoman Caliphate was abolished by the Turkish Parliament in 1924, and by the 1950s, it was a largely forgotten relic. However, as we approach nearly two decades into the 21st century, Pan-Africanism and Pan-Asianism seem to have faded away while Pan-Islamism and the ideal of Muslim world unity persist. Why is this?

The answer lies in the latter stages of the Cold War. It was during the 1980s that a new Muslim internationalism emerged, as part of a growing political Islam. This wasn't a conflict between the ancient civilizational traditions of Islam and the West, nor a reassertion of original religious values. Instead, it was a new formation born from the Cold War. A Saudi-US alliance began promoting the idea of Muslim solidarity in the 1970s as an alternative to the secular Pan-Arabism of Egypt's Gamal Abdel Nasser, who allied with the Soviet Union. Any ideas of an 'Islamic' utopia would have faltered without the failures of many post-colonial nation-states and the subsequent disillusionment of many Muslims.

The notion that Pan-Islamism represents authentic, ancient, suppressed Muslim political values in rebellion against global Westernization and secularization was initially a paranoid fear of Western colonial officers, but it has more recently come mainly from Islamists. Western pundits and journalists have made mistakes by accepting Islamist claims about Islam's essential political values at face value. The kind of Islamism associated with Egypt's Muslim Brotherhood or Ruhollah Khomeini's Iran did not exist before the 1970s. None of the Indian Muslims who met with Wilson, nor the late Ottoman-era caliphs, were interested in enforcing Sharia law in their societies. None of them aimed to veil women. On the contrary, the first generation of Pan-Islamists was highly modernist: they advocated for women's liberation, racial equality, and cosmopolitanism. Indian Muslims, for example, were proud that the Ottoman Caliph had Greek and Armenian ministers and ambassadors. They also wanted to see Hindu and Muslim ministers and high-level officials appointed in their governments. None would have wished for or anticipated the separation of Turks and Greeks in Ottoman lands, Arabs and Jews in Palestine, or Muslims and Hindus in India. Only the rudimentary form of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism remains.

On account of the fact that you have to actually do the good thing to get the praise – and equally, you have to actually do the bad thing to get the punishment – people are more likely to follow through on their good, and not act upon their bad, intentions. This is a highly welcome feature of social existence, all things considered. The ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments encourages us to respect the sanctity of other persons Finally, and perhaps most interestingly of all, Smith thought that there was a special effect of the ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments: it predisposed us to be careful around other people. One last example: imagine you are walking along a path above a cliff, and you accidentally dislodge a boulder, which crashes down and kills the rock-climber below. You didn’t mean to do this – it was an accident! But the fact that you did do it matters enormously. You will be blamed by others, and will likely blame yourself too. (‘Why didn’t I look where I was going?!’) But the fact that we feel ourselves responsible even for the things that we didn’t mean to do is, Smith thinks, a very useful and desirable state of affairs, insofar as it encourages us to take care when we are acting in ways that could (inadvertently) harm others. Precisely because you know you’ll rightly be held responsible for the death of people below you, even if you only accidentally knock a rock onto them, you’re more inclined to take care where you tread when you go for a clifftop stroll. As Smith put it, the ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments in this regard encourages us to respect the sanctity of other persons: The happiness of every innocent man is, in the same manner, rendered holy, consecrated, and hedged round against the approach of every other man; not to be wantonly trod upon, not even to be, in any respect, ignorantly and involuntarily violated, without requiring some expiation, some atonement in proportion to the greatness of such undesigned violation. What has this got to do with the assassin’s knife, the guitar used by Hendrix, or the films that Spacey starred in? Like Smith, I cannot explain why our psychologies tend to transfer the guilt of an agent, or the history of what an object was used for, on to the past or future status of a thing itself. text:
Philosophy, much like learning to play the piano, involves a lot of practice and correcting elementary mistakes. It's an ongoing process that requires patience and perseverance over a considerable period of time. And guess what? We might be surprised to find that we've already made significant strides in our journey.

It seems that the worry about philosophy's progress should be put to rest, as many things we've accomplished and continue to do are exactly what's needed to make headway at an early stage. In fact, some of the very things that philosophers debating progress often mention as achievements, others might find underwhelming, become striking advancements in this new perspective. This realization serves as further evidence that philosophy is still maturing and growing. Progress is happening right before our eyes, we just need to adjust our frame of reference.

David Chalmers points out the discovery of errors: 'We've seen a lot of convergence on smaller theses like the one stating that knowledge isn't simply justified true belief... We're particularly skilled at agreeing on negative theses that rule out certain specific views.' It appears we're in the early stages of inquiry, much like Socrates was. We're still often discovering what we don't know, and when we do acquire knowledge, it's usually of a modest sort, as one would expect at this stage.

Gary Gutting, in his book 'What Philosophers Know,' emphasizes the knowledge that philosophers have achieved regarding distinctions  for instance, between causality and compulsion, knowledge and true opinion, logical and epistemic possibility, and meaning and reference. This is akin to playing some fine elementary piano tunes! The same goes for our understanding of basic options in philosophy. We've managed to distinguish and make useful categorical, propositional, quantifier, and modal forms of logic. We've experimented with various methods and approaches  dialectical, transcendental, skeptical, existentialist, phenomenological, analytic, and so on. We also have some basic theories, like decision theory and utilitarianism in ethics, as well as a multitude of finely crafted arguments that have been meticulously discussed and refined to be passed down to future generations.

In essence, if we're early-stage philosophers, then progress is indeed happening right under our noses. What's needed is an early-stage mental orientation, which encourages us to view our work as setting up later stages. While we may not expect breakthrough methods or lasting insights on big issues at this stage, we should focus on pioneering dispositions and elementary discoveries  like identifying important falsehoods and methodological dead ends  that will pave the way for more advanced philosophy in future generations. This is our responsibility. In early-stage philosophy, we're not quite sure where we're headed, but we're making progress all the same. The key is to adjust our perspective and embrace the journey.

text: Most of the time, getting a marriage licence is an administrative transaction like any other. The piece of paper, the legality of matrimony, is an errand, a bureaucratic checkmark. The real celebration comes later. The everyday realities of marriage arrive later still. But what happens when that order is reversed? When you’ve had the ceremony and the long years together but no piece of paper, because it wasn’t possible, because your society didn’t allow it? What happens when, suddenly, that sanction is overturned? On a cold, rainy night in Seattle last December, I saw such a quiet revolution in action. Several hundred couples — some of them couples who had already spent decades together, who had been ‘domestic partners’ for years, who had long since held grand but not legal weddings before their friends and families — lined up for the part of the matrimonial process that most couples do first: the marriage licence. The result, that night, was a strange mixture of beauty and bureaucracy. Couples kissed and cried as they spent hours in line and filled out paperwork. They carried flowers, tiaras, and signs declaring their years together — as well as lawn chairs, blankets, and thermos flasks. To pass the hours, they shared their stories of meeting each other and falling in love, reflecting with wonder or anger on the time when they thought this day would never come. Some could still hardly believe that, in just a few hours, at 12:01 am, Washington’s law extending the right of marriage to same-sex couples would go into effect. On one side it read, ‘20 years together.’ On the other: ‘Just married’ The law had actually passed the state legislature more than nine months earlier, but opponents tried to block it by demanding it be put to a popular vote. No state in the nation had ever directly voted in favor of gay marriage — in fact, citizens of 30 states have voted to write bans on same-sex marriage into their very constitutions. Washington’s opponents of gay marriage felt confident that the people would strike this law down, too. But Americans are rapidly changing their minds about gay marriage. In the decade prior to Washington’s law, according to the Pew Research Center, public opinion on legalising same-sex marriage moved from a majority in opposition (54 per cent) and a minority in support (35 per cent) to roughly equal numbers for and against. In 2012, support for marriage equality took a clear lead for the first time, with 48 per cent in favour, and 43 per cent against. And on November 6, voters chose to legalise same-sex marriage not only in Washington, but in Maine and Maryland as well. Issuing marriage licences is never quite like processing real estate documents, Jon Scherer, who does both for King County, Washington, told me. ‘Almost everybody who comes in to get a marriage

LONDON - Cricket history was rewritten on Monday at Trent Bridge, as Nottinghamshire and Northamptonshire shattered multiple records during an extraordinary one-day cup match. The day's action left statisticians scrambling to find a comparable performance anywhere in the world.

The offensive fireworks began immediately, with Nottinghamshire's Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb smashing a record-breaking 146 runs from just 97 deliveries for the opening partnership. Lumb himself went on to set new personal and county records with an astounding 184 runs. This helped propel Nottinghamshire to a monumental total of 445/8, the second-highest score ever in a List A match worldwide.

Despite this daunting target, Northamptonshire refused to surrender, amassing an impressive 425 in response - the third highest total for a team batting second. However, they ultimately fell short thanks to Rory Kleinveldt's stellar performance of 128 runs. The day's unprecedented action saw an astonishing 870 runs scored, surpassing the previous record of 867 set by Surrey and Glamorgan in 2002.

Following the thrilling encounter, a jubilant Lumb expressed his admiration: "It was a bit nerve-wracking at times, but what an incredible game of cricket! Never in doubt." He continued, "I was surprised they hung on as long as they did, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was simply outstanding. It was an unbelievable game. I don't think we could have done any more. I'm proud of all the boys."

Nottinghamshire's record-breaking opening stand surpassed the previous English best of 318, set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. Lumb and Wessels' performance marks the third instance of a 400+ partnership in List A cricket, following two other occasions in 2007. In these matches, runs were played over 40-60 overs.

You all have an interest in engaging in what philosophers call an epistemic division of labour (after epistēmē, ancient Greek for ‘knowledge’) whereby information is pooled. This is where, according to Edward Craig’s Knowledge and the State of Nature (1990), the concept of knowledge emerges to fill a need. It serves to flag good informants. This is no mean feat, because the property of being a good informant is not immediately observable like the property of being tall or dark-haired. There’s an art to identifying good informants on a given question. The concept of knowledge therefore needs to track a variety of indicator properties – properties that are observable and that correlate well with and hence indicate the property of being a good informant on a given issue. For example, being able to offer a justification for one’s opinion might serve as such an indicator property; or having a good track record on this type of issue; or standing in the right causal relation to the state of affairs in question (by having looked at the berries earlier today, for instance). The concept of knowledge you need is one that tracks these and other properties that reliably indicate a good informant. According to Craig’s pragmatic genealogy, then, the concept of knowledge is rooted in practical needs: its salient contribution to life in the State of Nature is that it renders you sensitive to the presence of people who know something you’re trying to find out. And as the genealogical story shows, this is a need deriving from needs so basic that we today are likely to share those needs in some form, so that, for us as well, the concept of knowledge earns its keep notably by helping us identify good informants. Though abstract and seemingly idle, the concept of knowledge thus turns out to be a true game-changer because it makes knowledge social: it transforms the solitary business of information acquisition into a joint enterprise, where we don’t have to see everything with our own eyes, but mutually rely on each other to pool far more information than any one of us could ever gather by themselves. For a community to pool information, it must cultivate accuracy and sincerity in its members Having come this far in the genealogy of the concept of knowledge, we can explore the same scenario further to make sense of the idea of truth as a value. The Latin word for ‘truth’, veritas, adorns the blazons of countless institutions, and yet it can seem that, strictly speaking, truth is just a property of certain sentences, and not really the kind of thing that can carry value.
