Resources are interconnected through competition; what one individual consumes can mean less for another. A pesticide or antibiotic attack wipes out all but those most resistant, favoring resistance over susceptibility. The survivors then flourish in numbers. New strategies are being developed to maintain both susceptible and resistant individuals within networks of cancer cells, pathogens, or agricultural pests. Integrated pest management and adaptive chemotherapy, for instance, aim to kill only some sensitive pests or cancer cells, leaving the rest to keep the resistant ones in check. These new control methods take into account the local interactions that govern these networks of pests or cells.

In social circles, the most popular girl is the most connected  all friendships seem to pass through her. Networks vary in shape based on the arrangement of links. The traditional hierarchical network, for example, follows a chain of command from the factory owner down to the workers. Evolution has produced phylogenetic tree-shaped networks, tracing ancestors and descendants from unicellular organisms to the vast bacterial tree or the branch of vertebrates that includes us mammals.

The tree and the fully connected network represent extremes in the spectrum of possible network shapes. Human social life can generate complex network shapes, as any girl who's been through middle school knows. Two girls are linked by public recognition as friends, with popular girls serving as hubs for many connections, some of whom have links to others. Loners or outcasts may not be connected at all. In between the hubs and the outsiders, there are those who are linked to someone within the inner circle but also those who are overlooked by the chosen ones.

A girl's position in this network can be described by how well-connected she is  her 'betweenness centrality.' It represents the number of connections that pass through her. The most popular girl is the most connected, with all bonds of friendship eventually passing through her. In my middle school, understanding betweenness centrality was so intricate and crucial that one girl kept a notebook to track daily changes in the links. By mapping out who was friends with whom, it became clear how friendships shifted over time.

Athenian citizenship played a crucial role in understanding the trial of Socrates. His allegiance to his city significantly impacted the course of events, as it was deeply intertwined with the core values of Athenian political culture - the relationship between public discourse and civic duty.

In essence, this tale revolves around citizenship engagement, albeit in a context distinct from contemporary U.S. discussions centered on recognition. Maximus of Tyre, a late Roman-era writer, recounted that Socrates remained stoically silent amidst Meletus' accusations. However, dissidents who challenge the legitimacy of political structures occasionally exercise a principled refusal to engage in legal proceedings. Despite this, Socrates acknowledged the court's authority. He engaged actively in his defense, albeit unconventionally. Given that he had accepted the benefits bestowed by the laws, how could he now question their validity?

Socrates did not challenge the trial's legitimacy; instead, he fulfilled his civic responsibilities. He was accountable for actions perceived as detrimental to the community. Plato's portrayal of Socrates' defense speech underscores his efforts to exonerate himself and demonstrate that he had served the community rather than harmed it. In fact, Socrates presented himself as a civic benefactor deserving of recognition. His contributions to Athens' social fabric, in Plato's view, warranted privileges such as free meals at the state hearth.

Plato's dialogues offer insights into Socrates' commitment to his city and provide a vivid depiction of Athenian political culture. In the dialogue "Crito," an imaginary conversation takes place between Socrates and the personified Laws of Athens, who express their concerns about Socrates' intention to evade punishment by fleeing the prison. These laws symbolized not only the written rules of the Athenian state but also its societal norms.

The Laws questioned Socrates about his dissatisfaction with them and when he discovered any flaws. They reminded him that it was through their guidance on marriage that he entered the world, nurtured him as a child, and educated him. Socrates acknowledged these contributions, affirming his respect for the laws that shaped his existence.

In summary, Socrates' trial offers valuable lessons about citizenship, civic duty, and respect for the law. Despite facing adversity, he chose to engage within the system rather than reject it entirely. His courage and integrity serve as an enduring testament to the power of active citizenship in a democratic society.

text:
In the later half of the 20th century, Sellars's philosophy played a significant role in shaping our understanding of Hegelian thought. One key aspect of his view was a semantic theory that set it apart from the mainstream at the time, allowing Sellars's followers to start making sense of Hegel's logic and reviving the philosophy of Hegel. Let's delve into Robert Brandom's recent 'non-metaphysical' interpretation of Hegel, focusing on his semantic theory since it's pivotal to our understanding of Hegelian thought.

The analytic tradition relied on a straightforward conception of semantics that distinguished syntax (the rules governing language structure) from semantics (the study of the meaning of linguistic expressions). Semantics was seen as the connection between language and the world, but Sellars argued against this notion. Instead, he proposed that the meaning of a linguistic expressionand also the meaning of a concept or a thoughtis primarily about its inferential relationships with other expressions or thoughts. In essence, semantics is more about word-word relations rather than word-world connections.

Sellars expanded the idea of inference to encompass both formally valid inferences, which depend on sentence structure alone, and material inferences, whose validity isn't guaranteed by the syntax of sentences. For example, while the inference from 'The ball is red' to 'The ball is colored' isn't formally valid, it's still a reasonable inference we often make in everyday life. Sellars's semantics also acknowledges that language-entry transitions (observation reports) and language-exit transitions (our actions based on stated intentions and desires) are crucial elements in determining meaning. Understanding one's beliefs and desires relies heavily on how they interact with the world, and other people.

As a result, the meaning of a word or sentence, and the meaning of a concept or thought, is determined by its position within a complex linguistic economy that includes observation reports, inferences, various speech acts, etc., all guided by norms of proper usage. This semantic approach is holistic, as there are no expressions whose content is independent of other expressions in the system.

Sellars's student Brandom built upon this idea, emphasizing that our concepts have a historythey develop connections based on their origins and context. Brandom used this view of semantics to interpret Hegel's conception of dialectic, incorporating not only material connections between concepts but also material exclusions: what a concept includes or excludes tells us much about the concept itself.

text:
Russell wasn't always easy to get along with, but he wasn't entirely dismissive of pragmatism. He admired Peirce, a fellow logician, and learned quite a bit about him in 1914 during his visit to Harvard for the Lowell Lectures. Peirce was a hot topic at the department while Russell was there, largely due to his untimely death during Russell's visit. Russell was asked to return to Harvard to teach a seminar on Peirce and edit the first volume of his posthumous papers, but the outbreak of World War I put a halt to those plans. Still, he remained interested in the man. His book, "The Analysis of Mind" (1921), delved into pragmatism's idea that belief is a disposition to act in a positive way.

Moreover, Frank Ramsey, a brilliant young philosopher who emerged on the Cambridge philosophical scene in the early 1920s, was a self-proclaimed pragmatist. He attributed his leanings to Russell's encouragement during his undergraduate years. In 1923, after reading the first volume of Peirce's posthumous papers, Ramsey's pragmatism gained even more strength and scope.

Notably, Ramsey made significant contributions to the philosophy of language, logic, and truth theory. He believed that instead of trying futilely to prove something like induction, we should validate it by demonstrating its necessity in practice. Additionally, he provided pragmatist or agent-centered treatments of probability, causality, and scientific laws. He argued, like Peirce, that belief is a habit of action, and used this idea to figure out how to measure partial belief and formalize choice. He was intrigued by Peirce's account of truth and was in the middle of writing a book on the subject when he died at just 26 years old.

Pragmatist ideas and concerns were woven throughout the analytic philosophy that dominated Oxford and Cambridge in the late 1920s. In 1929, the year before his death  and the year Wittgenstein returned to Cambridge after his self-imposed exile as a school teacher in Austria  Ramsey challenged Wittgenstein's Tractarian theory that language mirrors the world, and that anything unsayable in Wittgenstein's logical language was nonsense, sometimes of mystical significance.

Wittgenstein's later philosophy, as outlined in his "Philosophical Investigations" (1953) and other works, can also be seen as a form of pragmatism, with its emphasis on the primacy of practice and meaning as use. Wittgenstein himself acknowledged that he was heavily influenced by Ramsey.

In the following decades, Ramsey's and Wittgenstein's versions of pragmatism followed very different paths in British philosophy. Wittgenstein became particularly influential at Oxford, while Ramsey had a quiet but impactful presence in Cambridge. Generations of Cambridge philosophers rediscovered his insights piecemeal, often without understanding where he had originally derived them from. Nonetheless, pragmatist theses and arguments continued to shape analytic philosophy.

text:
It's important to consider the approach we should take when addressing the issue of lengthy sentences for Black individuals in our criminal justice system. Whether we lean towards 'levelling down' by reducing the sentences or 'levelling up' is a complex question, one that involves understanding if life sentences are ever truly just and appropriate. Those advocating for 'levelling up' can't assume their stance is the correct one, given that the very issue at hand is under debate. Here's one reason why 'levelling down' might be more fitting.

The US criminal justice system tends to treat Black and brown individuals less favorably than whites. It seems reasonable to suggest that the treatment of white individuals aligns more closely with our society's ideals of justice, as they are often perceived as the 'norm'. People of color, on the other hand, seem to be subjected to an unfair 'surplus penalty', while whites may not be getting off too easily. If this is true, then reducing penalties for Black individuals rather than increasing those for whites would move us closer to justice.

It's difficult to believe that someone should spend their entire life in prison without a solid reason. The only valid reasons would be if they pose an ongoing threat to public safety or if their actions deserve such a severe punishment. I've already argued that the majority of the over 200,000 individuals currently serving life sentences in the US do not pose a lifelong threat to public safety. Whether they should spend the rest of their lives behind bars depends solely on whether they deserve it. Deserving such a punishment would require them to be fully responsible for their criminal actions.

Responsibility is best understood as what it excludes - an individual who is not responsible has a partial or complete excuse. A complete excuse makes one wholly non-culpable, and this has required, since at least the 19th century, that one did not know what they were doing, did not know it was wrong, or could not control their actions due to an irresistible impulse. Under these definitions, most individuals serving life sentences probably are responsible for their actions. Growing up in environments of deprivation increases the likelihood that a person will commit crimes, but a broader understanding of responsibility is necessary.

For one thing, the insanity defense is a complete excuse that makes a person legally innocent (although they may still be institutionalized in a psychiatric facility). However, there are many more individuals whose responsibility for their actions might be reduced, even though it's not entirely absent. The Death Penalty Information Center reported in its 2021 report that all but one of the eleven people executed in 2021 had significant impairments, including evidence of mental illness, brain injury or damage, or an IQ within the intellectually disabled range, as well as chronic childhood trauma, neglect, and abuse. It's safe to assume that those sentenced to life also have extremely high rates of such impairments. Many have experienced abuse, neglect, and trauma.

We are used to thinking of athletics as a levelling force in society, the ultimate meritocratic arena wherein anyone, regardless of class or wealth, can run, throw, hit and sweat their way to fame and fortune as professional athletes. Youth sport is widespread in most modern developed societies, and mandatory gym classes provide a path to physical training and development across the socioeconomic spectrum. This state of affairs is dramatically different from the ancient Greek situation. Only Greek male citizens could train and compete in sports. Within this population, informal constraints limited participation even further. Most poorer Greeks operated close to a basic subsistence level and worked on small farms, so they did not have leisure time available for long training sessions in the gym, located far off in the city centres, let alone could they take months out of their schedule to train and compete at Olympia without incurring disastrous agricultural consequences at home. This means that vast swaths of people who lived in an ancient Greek city – women, local residents who were not born in Greece, foreigners, and poor men – were largely excluded from athletics. In turn, participation in sport was strongly emblematic of a certain political and socioeconomic in-group – relatively wealthy, Greek, citizen males. The Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged I now turn to the second key point, the cultural convention of athletic nudity. Athletes in ancient Greece were always expected to both train and compete naked. In fact, our modern word ‘gymnasium’ comes from the ancient Greek word gymnos, an adjective that means ‘naked’. Thus, while we don’t honour its etymology any more, the word’s original meaning was literally ‘naked place’. Why nude athletics? Answers to this question are elusive. Although many texts from the classical world survive, they have little to say about the meaning of nudity in society or art. The textual sources that do touch briefly on naked sport indicate that the Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged as such a central part of their culture. Thucydides and Plato attribute the invention of athletic nudity to the Spartans and the Cretans, respectively, and indicate that (from their point of view) it set the Greeks apart from non-Greeks as especially civilised. However, it is not clear from their discussions how, when or why this state of affairs might have arisen. The cultural convention of athletic nudity probably originated so far back in time from the point of view of these authors that they had no direct knowledge of its advent. text:
Rovers from Earth, painted with hues of dust swirling through the Martian winds, descend onto the red planet's surface at an alarming speed in aeroshells and parachutes. They land with a thud, protected by airbags and braking retrorockets, each equipped with cameras, spectrometers, antennas, and manipulator arms. Some of them remain there, while others rove the Martian landscape for years on end, searching for even the slightest trace of water - life's elusive ally.

Like lost travelers in a desert, these robots persistently seek out any sign of moisture. This strategy, dubbed 'following the water,' is based on the eerie harmony between water and life. On Earth, wherever we find water, life isn't far behind. It's believed that water plays a crucial role in biology, not just here but throughout the universe. Water, after all, is made of two of the most abundant elements in the cosmos - hydrogen and oxygen. In its liquid form, it's unmatched in its ability to meet life's needs. It facilitates energy transmission, nutrient delivery, and waste removal. It shapes proteins and other macromolecules, shields against harmful radiation, and retains warmth exceptionally well. And remarkably, unlike most substances in the universe, when water freezes, it expands instead of contracting - a protective, insulating layer that prevents oceans from freezing solid.

For Mars researchers, this 'following the water' strategy has been fruitful. Every mission sent to Mars in search of water has found some, and we now know that our neighboring planet was once warmer, wetter, and potentially more habitable. However, billions of years ago, things changed drastically as Mars cooled and lost most of its air and water, settling into a quiet senescence. But present-day Mars still holds an aquatic secret: a slumbering aquasphere frozen beneath the surface. This subterranean reservoir may occasionally stir, emerging to the surface in temporary briny flows.

NASA's next step in exploring Mars is to 'seek signs of life,' which officially began with the successful landing of Curiosity rover in the summer of 2012. While Curiosity can detect organic carbon in Martian rock and soil, it seems to be following the water too. So far, it has rolled through what appears to be an ancient dried-up riverbed, finding evidence suggesting that some of Mars's ancient water was drinkable.

However, whether there is anything on Mars worth drinking remains a question that Curiosity isn't well-equipped to answer. But it should have plenty of time to try, thanks to its nuclear power source.

LONDON - Gold prices dipped slightly on Tuesday as global equities rallied following a reduction in political uncertainty within the U.K., along with increased hopes for additional economic stimulus measures. This positive sentiment subsequently diminished demand for safe-haven assets, including gold.

By 09:40 GMT, spot gold was down by 0.2%, trading at $1,352.06 per ounce, after experiencing a nearly one percent decrease on Monday - its most significant decline in nearly two weeks. U.S. gold also experienced a slight dip, falling 0.1% to $1,355.30 per ounce.

Carlo Alberto de Casa, chief analyst at ActivTrades, commented: "With tensions in the U.K. easing for now as the country prepares to welcome a new prime minister, we are witnessing both the British pound regaining some ground and demand for safe-haven assets dwindling."

De Casa added that gold's next support level lies around $1,340 per ounce. Gold has seen a significant increase of approximately $100 per ounce since the U.K. voted to exit the European Union, as investors flocked to secure safe-havens amidst escalating concerns.

The upcoming U.S. payrolls data on Friday has bolstered expectations for an interest rate hike in the United States. Kansas City Federal Reserve President Esther George stated on Monday that current U.S. interest rates are too low and hinted at her readiness to reinitiate her push for higher rates within the Fed's yield curve, thereby pressuring gold prices while supporting the dollar, in which the metal is priced.

INTL FCStone noted: "Not long ago, gold prices would have faltered in response to rising stock prices; however, the current sentiment suggests that monetary easing policies are likely to persist for some time, providing support not only for gold but also for equities."

According to a recent Reuters poll of euro money market traders, the European Central Bank is unlikely to make any further adjustments to its monetary policy during its upcoming meeting next week. Meanwhile, silver prices climbed 0.6% to $20.41 per ounce, while platinum fell for the first time in two weeks, experiencing a 0.1% decline to $1,099 per ounce. Palladium saw an increase of 0.6%, trading at $623.92 per ounce.

text:
The fleeting connections between things have always fascinated me, and I've found myself drawn to breaking free from the divide that exists between imagination and reality. This was the theme of a lecture given by Paul luard many years ago, and it resonates with me even today. Although he was referring to the surrealists, like his friend Max Ernst, his words could just as easily describe the work of Forestier - I sense in his art a blend of freedom and unity that I find captivating.

luard may have also recognized a broader purpose in the surrealist movement; one that sought to challenge an order based on inequality and strove for the total emancipation of humanity. While the confinement of disabled and distressed individuals in institutions may no longer be fashionable, the system that allows their suffering to continue unchecked is very much alive. It manifests itself in news media that normalizes harmful rhetoric and in legal systems that justify violence against vulnerable people.

Some readers might balk at the suggestion that our current reality in Europe and America could be considered fascist. I once did, preferring instead to think of fascism as a relic of the past, vanquished 80 years ago. However, others have long argued that fascism is not just something that exists on the fringes or among white supremacists, but rather a pervasive and insidious force that operates within our own societies.

Simone Weil's essay from 1940 on Homer's Iliad highlighted the indifference of the strong towards the weak, noting how force can dehumanize and degrade individuals until they are no more than corpses. In the aftermath of World War II, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno argued that fascism had found a new life in the combination of bureaucracy and capitalism that dominated postwar society - a system that suppresses moral feelings and treats human beings as mere cogs in a machine.

In conclusion, I believe it is essential to recognize that fascism exists not only on the fringes but also within our own societies, hiding in plain sight. We must be vigilant in challenging systems of reality that allow for the mistreatment and dehumanization of vulnerable individuals, lest we find ourselves complicit in their suffering.

One important dimension of Sellars’s view was a semantic theory that differed just enough from the then-dominant theories to enable Sellars’s followers to begin to make sense of Hegel’s logic, a major step towards resuscitating Hegelian philosophy. Brandom’s contribution to the recent ‘non-metaphysical’ interpretation of Hegel is that semantic theory is key to the revival of Hegelian thought, so let’s examine it closely. The analytic tradition relied on what seemed to be a straightforward conception of semantics. Whereas syntax concerns intralinguistic relations (wellformedness, rules for composition of legal strings of the language), semantics – the discipline concerned with specifying the content of linguistic expressions – concerns the relation between language and the world. But Sellars denies that semantics requires a basic word-world relation at all. Rather, the content of a linguistic expression – and also the content of a concept or a thought – is a matter of the proper inferential relations it bears to other expressions or thoughts. So semantics is primarily a matter of word-word relations, instead of word-world. In explicating this ‘inferentialist’ theory of meaning, Sellars extends the notion of inference to cover not just the formally valid inferences explored in formal logic – inferences that depend only on the syntactic structure of the sentences involved, and, thus, inferences in which the non-logical words are irrelevant to the validity of the inference – but also material inferences. Material inferences are inferences whose validity is not guaranteed by the syntax of the sentences alone. For example, the inference from ‘The ball is red’ to ‘The ball is coloured’ is not formally valid, but it is a perfectly good inference. In Sellars’s view, semantics is not all about intralinguistic connections, for our responses to the world around us, called language-entry transitions (or observation reports), and the way we act on our stated intentions and desires, called language-exit transitions, are also important elements in the constitution of meaning. How one interacts with the world (and with other people) is important for interpreting one’s beliefs and desires. The result is that the content of a word or sentence, and equally the content of a concept or thought, is determined by its position within a complex linguistic economy that includes observation reports, inferences, various speech acts, etc, all responsive to norms of proper usage. This is a holistic and non-foundationalistic semantics, for there are no expressions whose content is determined independently of other expressions in the system. Sellars’s younger colleague Brandom picked up this thread of Sellars’s thought and brought to the fore the fact that our concepts have histories, stories about how they came to have the connections that make them the concepts they are. Brandom then used this view of semantics to interpret Hegel’s conception of dialectic. Brandom includes not only material connections between concepts, but also material exclusions: what a concept excludes or is

text:
A friend once left her baby with me, and he started crying nonstop. In an attempt to soothe him, I found myself in one of life's most primal roles - that of a surrogate nurse. Without much thought, I offered my breast to the little guy, who eagerly latched on. It was a surprising moment, as I had never considered sharing what I thought of as my own daughter's milk with an unrelated baby. But it felt surprisingly natural, in the way that some things just do, even when they seem outside of our cultural norms. Since then, I've often thought of this little guy as a sort of son.

This experience got me thinking about the ancient Roman tale of Caritas Romana, where a mother nurses another woman's child. This story suggests that kinship systems may have been defined not just by bloodlines but also by milk lines. It reminds me of older goddess-centered cults and religions, where breastfeeding was seen as a sacred act.

The story of Romulus and Remus, the twin founders of Rome, who were nursed by a she-wolf, is another famous example. There are also tales that imbue the recipient with divine powers, showing ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. One such tale is even credited with giving the Milky Way its name - Tintoretto's The Origin of the Milky Way (c1575) shows the infant Heracles suckling from the goddess Hera while she sleeps. When the goddess woke up, Heracles was so strong that he accidentally sprayed her milk across the heavens and formed the Milky Way.

The tradition of adult breastfeeding as a cure is evidence of these ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. Images of fantastical plenitude and eroticism abound, with depictions of nursing nymphs and multibreasted mermaids. These images seem to defy reality, giving us a glimpse of a world beyond sexual reproduction and familial constraints.

The pagan breasts of powerful goddesses were eventually assimilated into Christian beliefs and imagery. The scene of filial piety described in the original version of Roman Charity became linked to the Christian virtue of charity, which was often represented as a breastfeeding woman. The Madonna nursing the infant Jesus, the ultimate image of maternal-divine care, became an iconic symbol of this tradition.

In conclusion, the act of breastfeeding has been seen as a sacred, magical, and divine act throughout history. From ancient Rome to Christian art, breastfeeding has been used to represent not just biological motherhood but also spiritual kinship and healing power. It's a powerful reminder of our shared humanity and the deep bonds that connect us all.

text: required of Athenian citizens are essential to understanding the trial. Socrates’ own commitments to his city influenced the trial’s course, and those commitments were core parts of Athenian political culture, shaping the relationship between public speech and responsibility. Indeed, the actions of Socrates, Meletus and the jury must be understood in the context of the Athenians’ emphasis on the role of the responsible citizen in the democratic state, on their ideal of civic responsibility. Thus it is a story, in many ways, of civic engagement, in some respects far removed from the politics of recognition that characterise contemporary US debates. In his retelling of the trial, the late Roman-era writer Maximus of Tyre claimed that Socrates refused to respond to Meletus’ charges, maintaining a dignified silence throughout. Indeed, dissidents who seek to deny the validity of a given political system have periodically adopted a principled refusal to participate in a court proceeding or legal system. But, Socrates did acknowledge the authority of the court. He actively participated in his own defence – though it was, in many ways, an idiosyncratic one. Having thus accepted the Laws’ fruits, on what grounds could Socrates now turn around and deny their legitimacy? The point is that Socrates did not dispute the legitimacy of the trial, he did not take a stand outside a corrupt political system; rather, he participated as a citizen. As a citizen, his civic obligation required him to answer charges that he had acted in ways that harmed that community. Plato’s version of the defence speech makes clear that Socrates sought to vindicate himself, to show that he had not harmed the community. Indeed, Socrates represented himself as a civic benefactor, who ought to be rewarded. His record of contributions to the civic life of Athens, he believed, merited the honour of free meals at the state hearth. Plato’s many dialogues establish the commitments of Socrates the citizen, and paint a vivid portrait of Athenian political culture. In the dialogue Crito, Socrates explains that despite his conviction, it was not ethically permissible for him to escape from the prison. In Crito, Plato gives readers a ‘dialogue within a dialogue’, in which the personified Laws of Athens speak to another, counterfactual Socrates. This counterfactual Socrates intends to avoid punishment by escaping from prison and fleeing to some distant land. The Laws of Athens in Plato’s dialogue are not only the formal, written rules of the Athenian state, but also its civic norms. They ask the counterfactually disobedient Socrates what he found lacking in them, and when he had discovered that lack. The Laws point out to Socrates that it was through them (the laws concerning marriage) that he came into the world. The Laws point out that, through the norms concerning parents’ responsibility for their children, they nurtured him. Finally, through them he received his education. Socrates acknowledges all of this.

text:
Guattari's issue wasn't with the leaders of parties per se, but rather with the artificial relationships they forged with mass movements. It was never about a specific person - be it someone suffering from schizophrenia or Stalinist - but about how groups fracture into separate entities, disconnected not just from one another, but also from their own lives.

In the spring of 1968, a wave of revolutionary zeal swept through France, far removed from La Borde at first. Students and workers took to the streets in protest against consumer capitalism's destructive forces. This uprising was spontaneous, unsupported by either the established Leftist institutions or the French Communist Party. Guattari was an active participant and organizer, but his mentor Lacan dismissed it as mere hysteria. For Guattari, this was a moment of betrayal: if the Communist Party couldn't recognize a revolution when it was happening right in front of them, and if Lacan refused to grasp the power of collective desire as it coursed through the streets, then something needed to be done. It was around this time that Guattari encountered Deleuze's work for the first time. Finally, he found an intellectual ally for his therapeutic and political goals.

At that point, Deleuze's scholarly output encompassed studies of prominent Western philosophers like David Hume, Baruch Spinoza, Immanuel Kant, and Friedrich Nietzsche. He had also penned two more 'original' works - Difference and Repetition (1968) and The Logic of Sense (1969). Deleuze contended that the history of Western thought, at least since Plato, has been under the sway of several misconceptions about the nature of thought itself.

Firstly, contrary to most philosophers' assumptions, thought isn't representational - it doesn't function by creating mental images of the world, which can be judged as true or false based on their accuracy. Instead, argued Deleuze, thought is creative and inextricably linked to what it contemplates.

Secondly, Deleuze claimed that because Western philosophy has judged thought based on its ability to represent the world, it has elevated sameness and accuracy above all else. Plato and Descartes serve as good examples. In his philosophy of forms, Plato stated that any particular entity gains its qualities by reflecting its idealized, abstracted form. This form - identical only in relation to itself - is considered the foundation of knowledge. A man is a man only insofar as he embodies his own ideal form; and to know what a man is, is to know the essence of 'man'. Similarly, Descartes' "I think, therefore I am" championed identity and the individual. A person's ability to understand herself allows for all subsequent understanding, according to this argument. In both cases, the basis of understanding arises from something unique, individualized, and unchanging. Essentially, Deleuze argued that Western thought has relied too heavily on ideas that are not grounded in reality or change.

text: René Descartes’s dog, Monsieur Grat (‘Mister Scratch’), used to accompany the 17th-century French philosopher on his ruminative walks, and was the object of his fond attention. Yet, for the most part, Descartes did not think very highly of the inner life of nonhuman animals. ‘[T]he reason why animals do not speak as we do is not that they lack the organs but that they have no thoughts,’ Descartes wrote in a letter in 1646. Followers of Descartes have argued that consciousness is a uniquely human attribute, perhaps facilitated by language, that allows us to communicate and coordinate our memories, sensations and plans over time. On this view, versions of which persist in some quarters today, nonhuman animals are little more than clever automata with a toolkit of preprogrammed behaviours that respond to specific triggers. Insects such as bees and ants are often held up as the epitome of the robotically mechanistic approach to animal nature. Scientists have long known that these creatures must possess a large behavioural repertoire in order to construct their elaborate homes, defend against intruders, and provision their young with food. Yet many still find it plausible to look at bees and ants as little more than ‘reflex machines’, lacking an internal representation of the world, or an ability to foresee even the immediate future. In the absence of external stimuli or internal triggers such as hunger, it’s believed that the insect’s mind is dark and its brain is switched off. Insects are close to ‘philosophical zombies’: hypothetical beings that rely entirely on routines and reflexes, without any awareness. But perhaps the problem is not that insects lack an inner life, but that they don’t have a way to communicate it in terms we can understand. It is hard for us to prise open a window into their minds. So maybe we misdiagnose animal brains as having machine-like properties simply because we understand how machines work – whereas, to date, we have only a fragmentary and imperfect insight into how even the simplest brains process, store and retrieve information. However, there are now many signs that consciousness-like phenomena might exist not just among humans or even great apes – but that insects might have them, too. Not all of these lines of evidence are from experiments specifically designed to explore consciousness; in fact, some have lain buried in the literature for decades, even centuries, without anyone recognising their hidden significance. Based on such evidence, several biologists (notably Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney), and philosophers (Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra) now suggest that consciousness-like phenomena might not have evolved late in our history, as we previously thought. text:
I often find myself feeling down or on edge, and it's hard not to acknowledge those feelings. After all, we have direct access to our own minds, don't we? This is a view that has been widely accepted in philosophy for much of the 20th century, known as the 'privileged access' perspective on self-knowledge. Ren Descartes even used introspection to arrive at his famous conclusion: "I think, therefore I am." He recognized the ease with which we can perceive our own minds, stating, "There is nothing more easily or clearly perceived by me than my own mind."

However, there are those who argue that self-knowledge isn't as immediate as it seems. We might infer our feelings based on certain signs  like a racing heart and heavier breathing potentially indicating anxiety about an upcoming presentation. But what if we're wrong? Perhaps I'm just excited instead. This kind of psychological reframing is something sports coaches often use to help athletes stay calm under pressure.

The philosopher who most famously proposed this inferential view is Gilbert Ryle, who suggested in The Concept of Mind (1949) that we gain self-knowledge by applying the same tools we use to understand others to ourselves: "The things I can discover about myself are the same as those I can discover about others, and the methods of discovery are much the same." Ryle's idea is encapsulated in a popular New Yorker cartoon where a husband says to his wife, "How should I know what I'm thinking? I'm not a mind reader."

Many philosophers have dismissed the strong inferential view as too extreme, writing it off before it could fully develop. However, if we look at history, the idea that we have some sort of special, direct access to our minds is actually the exception, rather than the rule. For ancient Greeks, self-knowledge was a journey, something to be pursued and worked towards, as captured by the saying "Know thyself," inscribed on the Temple of Delphi. This implies that most of us don't know ourselves very well.

This view persisted into medieval religious traditions. The Italian priest and philosopher Saint Thomas Aquinas suggested that self-knowledge is a process, not an all-encompassing truth. The ancient Greeks and medieval philosophers remind us that understanding ourselves requires effort and introspection  it's not something we automatically possess.

A Change is Gonna Come was recorded in February 1964 in a session produced by Hugo Peretti and Luigi Creatore, and was quietly included on Cooke’s album Ain’t That Good News (1964). As Guralnick chronicled in Dream Boogie, after the session, Cooke played the song for Bobby Womack, who was Cooke’s protégé. Womack said the song sounded like death. On 22 December 1964, A Change Is Gonna Come was finally released as a single. Eleven days earlier, Cooke had been shot and killed at a Los Angeles motel, in what was later ruled a justifiable homicide. The song wasn’t a tremendous commercial hit; it topped out at number 31 on a Billboard chart in 1965. But it became an anthem of the Civil Rights movement and remains an enduring symbol of that era of protest. It was Cooke’s last, and perhaps most lasting, song. When a piece of art skews too heavily towards rhetoric or sloganeering, the effort often lands cold It is an unlikely protest song: it is not rough, angry, or street-level. The dramatic backing orchestration invokes glitzy nightclubs and movie palaces, not barricades and lunch-counter sit-ins. The stakes of good taste seem higher than ever, separating those whose virtuous choices reinforce health and life from those whose bad appetites lead them towards disease and death. But promising health and wellbeing as the outcomes of individual good taste has implications that we might pause to consider. The food that gets counted as ‘good’ (‘real food’) is less evidently technological, more ‘natural’, less ‘processed’ – and often more expensive. As the historian Rachel Laudan has noted, eating less processed food means processing more of your own food – a redistribution of labour towards the private household, and often women. Unlike Finck’s democratising vision of flavour and health, good food is often associated with the resources, the access and the time to procure, prepare and enjoy it. Good taste – and good health – remains elite, a mark of distinction, and chiefly a privilege of the fortunate or the wealthy. More importantly, food habits are portrayed primarily as matters of individual choice (reflections of good or bad taste) and personal responsibility, rather than the results of complex social and technological systems. Flavour is necessary, but it is not health.

text:
John Berger's story isn't just about power or fame; it's about everyday choices, the decisions we make - or don't make - in our lives. After the success of "Ways of Seeing," Berger found himself at a crossroads, both personally and historically speaking. In his late 40s, he was internationally renowned, but he chose to turn down most offers for museum positions, academic posts, and the like. Instead, he opted for a simpler life - one of haymaking and storytelling in a small Alpine village.

But don't be fooled by the romantic image of Berger as a rural storyteller. Despite living in a chalet without central heating or running water, he was still a respected writer with a circle of famous friends. Yet, one could argue that his choice was an escape from the cutthroat, status-oriented world of intellectuals during the neoliberal era.

Berger's decision to move to the countryside wasn't a whim; it was about finding roots and a sense of belonging. As he once put it, "The question is continually learning to be embedded in life." This need for roots emerged during a time when his iconic work "Ways of Seeing" was making waves in British and American classrooms.

Of course, Berger remained politically committed. His political views evolved and expanded, encompassing a broader understanding of history and experience. Yet, he continued to grapple with the same fundamental question: finding a way to be a part of life. The photograph taken of him at the 1973 Frankfurt Book Fair captures this struggle perfectly. Taken by his lifelong friend Jean Mohr, it shows a middle-aged writer, worn down and detached, lying on the floor as others bustle past in a blur. One wonders what Berger was thinking about then, what he was longing for. It was at that fair where he met Beverly Bancroft, a young American assistant at Penguin Books. Within a year, they were married and soon had a son.

In the end, Berger's story is one of finding purpose and meaning in life beyond the trappings of fame and power. It reminds us that we can know ourselves only by what we shatter, but where do we go when the demolition is complete? For Berger, the answer lay in simplicity and a deep connection to the land and community around him.

text:
The delicate dance of maintaining coherence in quantum systems can be compared to keeping a house of cards standing - it requires minimal energy yet is incredibly taxing to maintain. A quantum computer's intricate quantum states are like the fragile cards, easily disrupted by even the slightest disturbance. This mantle falls on an array of support systems and engineering solutions that consume vast amounts of power. We're still in the dark about what the actual energy-to-computation ratio for quantum computing will look like in practice.

Beyond the power consumption concerns, there are other challenges. A quantum computer with 'n' qubits has the potential to perform 2n calculations in a single cycle, but setting up those computations can be an arduous task of managing data flow. Simulating our entire universe, which consists of roughly 10^89 particles and photons, might require only 296 qubits according to some calculations. However, how on earth do we program in the initial conditions for all 10^89 entities? And if we manage that feat, how do we discern the correct solutions from the quantum simulation's complex output?

Simulating a human brain may be slightly easier but still presents challenges. To set up the computation, we would need to quantify and initiate at least 10^14 neural connections - a number found in a typical human brain. We'd also want this quantum brain to have high throughput and a high-fidelity interface with the physical world. But these are unknowns, potentially insurmountable obstacles. I admit that I may be oversimplifying the techniques and technological tricks that could be employed, but my personal vision of the future is not overly optimistic.

The exponential growth in machine intelligence proposed by some is questionable. The mathematics behind exponentially improving AI might be sound, but the practical barriers to achieving such growth could prove insurmountable. To give a glimpse of where this leaves us, let's imagine a scenario that mixes futurism with Fermi's Paradox. Suppose an advanced cosmic intelligence managed to convert itself into machine form or was overtaken by its super-smart but not exponentially better machine creations. What happens next?

Being confined by efficiency limits, these machines might find themselves looking back at their past for new ways forward. One thing they would know is that biology works remarkably well. Some researchers estimate that the modern human brain has reached its computational limits, but it may only require a slightly more intelligent machine to re-engineer such a complex organ. In other words, there could be the possibility of creating machines as sophisticated as humans by leveraging the power and efficiency of biology.

text: Rawls’s evil, bad and unjust men. ‘What moves the evil man is the love of injustice,’ Rawls wrote in A Theory of Justice (1971), ‘he delights in the impotence and humiliation of those subject to him and relishes being recognised by them as the wilful author of their degradation.’ The sun was setting on the slaverholders’ way of life and, in their own minds, they play the leading roles as victims and heroes in turn The contemporary traffickers and slaveholders I spoke with are not motivated by a love of injustice. They are instead driven by cultural inertia, a desire for profit or, more frequently, a need for basic sustenance. Instead of evil villains, we find husbands, fathers, mothers and neighbours working with the cultural materials available to them, surviving as best they can. The terms used here – slavery and slaveholder – never crossed the lips, nor perhaps even the minds, of the men I spoke with. As we broke bread, drank tea, explored plantations and silk-production houses, swam in deep wells, climbed coconut trees and talked late into countless evenings, the term slavery never came up. What came up, time after time, was respect, honour and dignity. The sun was setting on their way of life and, in their own minds, they play the leading roles as victims and heroes in turn. In so many ways, we all find ourselves at this juncture: in the interregnum between generations, eras and epochs. Tremendous changes have provided new opportunities for marginalised people, while the formerly powerful find themselves suddenly in possession of a form of power – caste and land – that lacks much exchange value in the new world. For the recently powerful, this transition can be devastating and demoralising. What psychologists call loss aversion is real; humans don’t want to lose what we have, no matter what. This is true whether the loss is occurring in Birmingham, Alabama – or Bihar, India. The real question is how we respond to loss – by lashing out, by learning, by opening up, or by closing down. Surely Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn was right when he wrote in the The Gulag Archipelago (1973): ‘If only it were all so simple! If only there were evil people somewhere insidiously committing evil deeds, and it were necessary only to separate them from the rest of us and destroy them. But the line dividing good and evil cuts through the heart of every human being. And who is willing to destroy a piece of his own heart?’

text:
In the later half of the 20th century, Wilfrid Sellars's perspective on semantics played an essential role in making sense of Hegel's logic. This was a crucial step towards reviving Hegelian philosophy, as his followers began to understand the intricacies of Hegel's logic. Robert Brandom has been instrumental in interpreting Hegel from a 'non-metaphysical' perspective more recently. To fully grasp this interpretation, let's delve into Sellars's semantic theory, which he believed is vital to the resurrection of Hegelian thought.

At that time, the analytic tradition predominantly relied on a simple understanding of semantics. Syntax dealt with intra-linguistic relations like well-formedness and rules for constructing language strings. On the other hand, semantics - the study of the meaning of linguistic expressions - focused on their relation to the world. However, Sellars argued that semantics does not require a basic word-world relationship at all. Instead, he proposed that the content of a linguistic expression or a thought is determined by its inferential relationships with other expressions or thoughts. Therefore, in his view, semantics primarily concerns word-word relationships instead of word-world ones.

To further explain this 'inferentialist' theory of meaning, Sellars broadened the concept of inference to encompass both formally valid inferences and material inferences. While formal logic deals with inferences that depend only on the syntactic structure of sentences, ignoring non-logical words, material inferences are based on context and experience. For example, the inference from 'The ball is red' to 'The ball is colored' may not be formally valid, but it makes perfect sense in a given context.

According to Sellars, semantics involves more than just intralinguistic connections. Our responses to the world around us (language-entry transitions or observation reports) and our actions based on intentions and desires (language-exit transitions) are also integral to the constitution of meaning. The way one interacts with the world (and others) is crucial in understanding their beliefs and desires. As a result, the content of a word or sentence, as well as the content of a concept or thought, is determined by its position within a complex linguistic economy that includes observation reports, inferences, various speech acts, etc., all guided by norms of proper usage.

This holistic and non-foundationalist semantics, with no expressions having independent content, was a significant contribution from Sellars. His younger colleague, Brandom, emphasized another aspect of Sellars's thought: our concepts have histories. Building upon this view of semantics, Brandom interpreted Hegel's conception of dialectic, incorporating not only material connections between concepts but also material exclusions - what a concept includes or excludes based on its historical development.

text: on the pages of 1984 Winston’s rebellion is private and introverted – trying to steal moments away from the telescreens to write in his secret diary, like a teenager putting on her headphones and blasting her music behind a locked bedroom door. Until, that is, Winston begins an affair with Julia. It is only through romance that his rebellion comes to vivid, dangerous life. How completely adolescent! Only when a boyfriend or girlfriend shows up can real independence begin. 1984 is effective as a teen novel not because of its political message, but because it dramatises the internal psychic struggle of growing up. What makes 1984 immortal is that it ends with a real insight into the futility of teenage rebellion. Every teen becomes an adult, joins the machine, and comes to love Big Brother. Teens hate this fact – many of them hate the ending – but it stays with them, because they know it’s true. In 1984’s sibling, Animal Farm (1945), the animals with whom we identify see their ultimate betrayal: the pigs standing like their former human masters in their former masters’ house. But in 1984 it is Winston himself who gives in at the end. He does love Big Brother, because Big Brother, also known as adult society, has manipulated language, power and thought to perpetuate its system. By being born into it, we have already lost. Depressing, perhaps cynical – but teens see their very lives scribbled on the pages of 1984. It’s more fun to read a dystopian novel with a happy ending. But the novel that you pass on to your children is the one that first taught you the truth. When a child asks for the same book three hundred times, she is telling her parents what she needs to learn, what she needs to come to terms with. Adults do the same thing. Books are psychologists, using imagination therapy to elicit secrets that their readers did not know they kept. A child does not realise what he reveals when he names this doll ‘mummy’, that one ‘daddy’, and then has them shout at one another. Nor do we tend to realise what we are revealing about ourselves when we push a book into the hands of three friends. Maybe the bestseller list, stripped of the fly-by-night entries and dopamine drips, is a snapshot of the national psyche. It might be telling us what we need to learn, what we are coming to terms with. More certainly, I know one thing: I hope my daughter doesn’t love The Carrot Seed as much as I did.

text: Is it right to target and eliminate anyone who embodies a universal concept, but is that a justification for taking their life? If it is, we must accept that (1) it reduces the individual to a one-dimensional abstract identity, thereby (2) favoring the nonexistence of universals over the tangible existence of individuals as unique beings. This action effectively subordinates the individual person to some other universal as a dominant force or value. For the Buddhist mission to eradicate suffering, any value that could be associated with eliminating an individual-as-universal cannot be ethically equated with an individual's capacity to partake in their liberating project  the very purpose of Buddhism. This concept may seem abstract when considering conventional ethical systems, such as those governing legal warfare. However, a Buddhist perspective on conflict aims to delve deeper and challenge these established norms. Beyond the obvious act of taking a life, the Buddhist argues that the underlying metaphysical and cognitive conditions for this situation are fundamentally at odds with the true nature of human existence. These practices are conventional, rooted in collective mental processes that misconstrue the essence of individuals as both separate entities and social beings. If so, the Buddhist suggests that our perception of reality is skewed, and we have the capacity to change it. After all, our conventional understanding of reality is malleable  that's why we call it, and why it is, merely conventional. It could be reconfigured to align more closely with the true nature of being  a complex philosophical and ethical journey I can't explore here  rather than straying from this path, as it so often seems and feels to be. In light of this, it's worth questioning why so many who witness or participate in such conflict instinctively feel something fundamentally wrong about it. Metaphysical truth, for the Buddhist, is felt in a deep sense of wellbeing, harmony with the world, and connection with other sentient beings, rather than discord. It's no surprise that satya, a Sanskrit word central to Buddhist philosophy, signifies both 'being' and 'truth', so that true understanding manifests as living in accordance with this truth. To live otherwise is to miss the mark  in other words, to transgress. If so, it's not only the lethal agent who strays, but the entire system of human delusion that allows, and even encourages, such actions in the first place. I write these words eight months after a sovereign state deployed military forces into a neighboring sovereign state, during which time at least 6,370 civilians have been killed, and over 9,770 injured. Can it truly be believed that, in the 21st century, we still think it appropriate to take the lives of our fellow beings in this way? And is there not a growing number among us who question this notion?

text:
In the Bible, we find instances where God appears to deceive not just demons and sinners, but even the faithful. Consider the story of Abraham, the patriarch of the Jewish people, who was ordered by God to sacrifice his son Isaac. As Abraham held a knife over his bound and trembling son, God intervened at the last moment, sparing Isaac's life.

Two centuries later, John Calvin found himself grappling with this same issue while reflecting on the passage in I Kings where God is said to 'will that the false king Ahab be deceived.' God commanded the Devil to lie to Ahab through all his prophets. For Calvin, this was a troubling passage, as it seemed to suggest that God not only allows evil, but actively commands it.

Since Augustine, theologians had attempted to absolve God of any blame for evil by distinguishing between what God wills and what He permits. But Calvin argued that scripture goes further than this distinction implies. God does more than just allow the Devil to lie to Ahab; He commands it.

Calvin struggled to understand how a God who hates all liars, and commands us never to lie, could seem to lie Himself. He concluded that while God may never lie, the reasons for this are beyond our comprehension. Much like the Devil, God has a way of making bad deals sound good as He tempted the Devil to crucify the innocent Jesus.

Even those who argued that God by definition could not deceive were forced to acknowledge that God does sometimes deceive. This theological backpedaling often takes place off the philosophical stage, in commentaries on the Bible, sermons, and letters, particularly when discussing the life of Christ.

For example, why did God need to send His only Son to redeem humanity? And why did Jesus conceal His divinity, appearing as a man instead of God? Gregory of Nyssa, Augustine's slightly older peer, suggested that we read Jesus' life as something like a 33-year ruse.

The Devil had deceived Adam and Eve into disobeying God's command not to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. By making a bad deal seem good, he promised them they would not die if they ate the fruit, and that they would be like God themselves. Deceived, they ate, and in eating, they became Satan's slaves, subject to death and disease, pain and suffering.

To undo this tangle of despair, Gregory argued, God decided it was best to deal with the Devil much as the Devil had dealt with Adam and Eve - by making a bad deal seem good. In other words, Jesus came to Earth not as God, but as a man, to deceive the Devil into thinking He was just another human, vulnerable to temptation and death. Through this ruse, Jesus was able to overcome the power of sin and death, offering humanity a way out of despair and into salvation.

text:
The robotic explorers touch down on Mars, their descent cloaked by the warm hues of dust storms. They slice through the thin, frosty air in aeroshells and parachutes, landing gently within cushioned airbags and the whoosh of braking rockets. Armed with cameras, spectrometers, antennas, and manipulator arms, they are on a tireless quest for any trace of Martian moisture - like thirsty travelers lost in the desert. Their mission is guided by NASA's 'follow the water' strategy, as water has an uncanny affinity for life: wherever it exists on Earth, life isn't far behind. There are compelling reasons to believe that water serves as the foundation of biology not only here on our planet but across the cosmos. Hydrogen and oxygen, respectively the first and third most abundant elements in the universe, are all you need to create it. In its liquid state, water is unmatched in its ability to support life's necessities. It facilitates the transmission of biochemical energy, nutrients, and waste, shapes protein structures and interactions, shields against cosmic radiation, and retains heat remarkably well. Interestingly, unlike most substances in the universe, water expands when it freezes, forming an insulating layer of ice that prevents oceans, lakes, and other liquid reservoirs from solidifying during prolonged cold spells.

For researchers studying Mars, following the water has been a fruitful endeavor. Every mission sent to Mars in search of water has found it, and we now understand that our neighboring planet was once a warmer, wetter, more hospitable world. However, billions of years ago, this changed as Mars cooled, lost most of its atmosphere and water, and fell into a quiet slumber. Yet present-day Mars still harbors a dormant aquifer, locked beneath the surface as ice, which may awaken periodically, gushing to the surface in temporary salty flows.

The next phase of NASA's Mars exploration program is to 'seek signs of life.' Unofficially, it seems that this journey began with the successful landing of the car-sized Curiosity rover in the summer of 2012. While Curiosity can detect organic carbon in samples of Martian rock and soil, it appears to be primarily following the water. So far, it has traversed what looks like an ancient riverbed and found evidence suggesting that some of Mars's ancient water was drinkable. However, whether there is or ever was anything on Mars worthy of a drink remains an unexpectedly unanswered question for Curiosity, despite its ample time to explore. Thanks to its nuclear power source, it should be able to continue its quest for quite some time yet.

text:
In this exploration, I delve into two key aspects of practical reason as employed by the Stoics in their pursuit of a life free from suffering. The indirect function, on one hand, involves thoughtful planning and foresight that enables the Stoic to choose the path through life with less pain. On the other side, there's the direct function, where instead of attempting to remove or circumvent life's challenges, the Stoic alters their perspective towards these hurdles, thus modifying their emotional response. One is about practicing differently, while the other revolves around altering one's thinking. The unique contribution of Stoicism in ethics stems from the nature of mental change it encourages, asserts Schopenhauer.

Firstly, the Stoic recognizes that painful feelings of lack don't stem directly from not having, but rather from desiring to have and not acquiring it. This insight suggests that to eradicate these pains entirely, we need to eliminate our desires. Additionally, the larger our ambitions regarding what we want and the higher our hopes of achieving them, the more intense the disappointment when we fall short. If we can't help but desire certain things, then we should at least make sure our desires are realistic and attainable. Schopenhauer warns against expecting an overabundance of happiness in the future, as we're likely to be setting ourselves up for disappointment. "Every intense pleasure is a delusion," he remarks, drawing on his pessimistic outlook. In the same vein, he likens our pursuit of happiness to the application of a blister remedy  it only offers temporary relief.

The Stoic strives for ataraxia, a state of inner calmness and serenity amidst life's tumultuous events. Schopenhauer believes that acknowledging the inevitability of suffering can help attain this goal. Pain and suffering hurt more when we perceive them as accidental or avoidable. Although it might be true that any specific suffering could have been avoided, suffering as a whole is unavoidable and universal. If we can internalize this truth, Schopenhauer suggests, we may worry less about experiencing suffering or at least approach it with the same acceptance we have for other unavoidable aspects of life, such as aging (for most of us) and death.

The last piece of advice is to avoid believing that the world owes us happiness, rather than suffering. If we believe the world is supposed to make us happy, we're setting ourselves up for disappointment, especially since achieving our perceived sources of happiness often leads to new unfulfilled desires taking the place of old ones. Moreover, we'll harbor resentment towards the obstacles that prevent us from attaining the happiness we feel entitled to. As Schopenhauer observes, some individuals externalize this resentment by blaming others for their perceived shortcomings.

In Mind and Cosmos, Nagel’s suggested teleology does not involve a creator; it is merely a law-like tendency in the universe that somehow loads the dice in favour of the appearance of consciousness. In this conception, Nagel writes, ‘things happen because they are on a path that leads to certain outcomes’. (It is important that other laws of nature be non-deterministic, as quantum physics arguably implies they are, in order for the teleological law to have some purchase over events.) As Nagel puts it, it might be that the universe exhibits ‘a bias toward the marvellous’. In the not-so-distant past, when Barker dabbled in erotica, Anais Nin observed that it tickled the poet's funny bone more than pleading for meals from friends. Here I was, sitting across from a friend in a caf, staring down at my sandwich  a luxury that would drain this week's budget dry. The check arrived, and my friend dropped a generous tip into the jar, chuckling about his newfound prosperity. I scraped together the last coins in my purse, feeling a bitter pang of resentment. It caught me off guard. Despite knowing he deserved every penny, I reminded myself that I had chosen this path  the one leading to full-time writing. If only my erotica would sell faster! (I'd managed to move just 10 copies so far, earning a paltry 15.) The future always loomed with an anxious hum, but lately, it had grown shrill and piercing, warning of impending changes in my life. Money was a recurring theme in the romantic erotica I read for research  perhaps a reflection of a decade marked by austerity. Many stories depicted heroines grappling with financial desperation, reducing love to a transactional act: love in an era of capitalism.

In Georgia Le Carre's The Highest Bidder (2019), Freya, a struggling college student, finds herself so cash-strapped that she joins an elite London club to auction off her virginity. There, she signs a non-disclosure agreement and struts onto the stage, bare as nature intended, while unseen male billionaires bid for her. Fortune smiled on Freya, with her fetching herself a cool 1 million, sparing her from ending up with an aged man whose skin sagged like a deflated balloon. Instead, she found herself in the arms of a young man who looked like a modern-day Adonis  someone she had known since childhood. While the Cinderella narrative offers a glimmer of hope, fairy godmothers remain conspicuously absent, and alchemy is achieved through the heroines' degradation. It seems that this device helps us empathize with these characters, allowing us to comprehend their desperation, which in turn, permits us to derive pleasure from their romantic entanglements.

In Jade West's Buy Me, Sir (2007), Melissa is a young woman of 21, burdened by the responsibility of raising her baby brother while working as a cleaner for a law firm. Desperate to capture the heart of Alexander Henley, the criminal lawyer who owns the firm, she learns that he has an affinity for Debbie Harry and creates a sexy video of herself, masturbating, as an offering for his 'dealer'  the man who procures women for him. text:
Melancholy isn't just a human affliction; Burton suggests that even creatures like horses, fish, peacocks, and palm trees can experience it. He found such tales captivating, as they align with his fascination for the unusual and peculiar. The Anatomy of Melancholy, more than just a medical textbook, offered him an expansive canvas. Its title page promises to delve into melancholy 'in all its kinds, causes, symptoms, prognostics, and cures  philosophically, medically, historically.' His research isn't limited to medical texts but also includes poetry, classics, religious treatises, chronicles, and even works by Geoffrey Chaucer and William Shakespeare. Burton's project was ambitious, and it spilled over the confines of two covers. The Anatomy, first published in 1621, underwent four more editions before his death and a sixth, posthumous edition in 1651. It grew from its initial 350,000 words to over 500,000 words. Burton was unable to let the topic rest. With each new edition, he added more case studies of melancholics, further causes, symptoms, or treatments, and as a skilled writer, new ways to express this ancient condition. His favorite literary technique was congeries  piling up words.

In the first edition, melancholy was described as 'a most delightful humor, to walk alone and meditate.' With each subsequent edition, he added to his description until, by the sixth, it had become 'a most delightful humor, to be alone, dwell alone, walk alone, meditate, lie in bed whole days, dreaming awake as if one were asleep.' However, this pleasant state doesn't last long. As melancholy is indulged, it takes over, replacing pleasurable sensations with fear and sorrow: 'Thus, by degrees, and through the shoehorn of idleness and voluntary solitude, melancholy, this feral fiend, is drawn on.' The melancholic becomes 'a cankered soul marinated in cares and discontents, tedium vitae, impatience,' which later editions expanded to include 'agony, inconstancy, irresolution.'

There's something telling in these ever-growing word heaps. They mirror the dangerously proliferating nature of melancholy, evident not only in the expanding subject matter but also in the author's perspective. Burton, under the pseudonym Democritus Junior, intertwines his personal experience with the text, claiming that 'I write about melancholy to keep melancholy at bay.' Since idleness is the root cause, there's no better remedy than keeping busy. Yet it seems too late for that. When he first embarked on this task, he aimed to:

...create an antidote out of what was the primary cause of my illness.

text:
In the 17th century, Wanley's "Wonders of the Little World" introduced us to kings with oversized foreheads and dwarf pirates. This blend of reality and fantasy seeped into our histories, prompting writers like Daniel Defoe to question the line between the real and unreal in "The Secrets of the Invisible World Disclosed" (1729). People have always been curious, and their wondering has left its mark on history and philosophy.

Including these sprawling, chaotic works in our historical narratives might seem messy or a confession that we've fallen prey to curiosity  something Augustine and Hobbes warned against. Yet life is often messy, and striving for tidiness can come at too high a cost, as contemporary historians and philosophers remind us.

Immanuel Kant tried to address this issue in his "Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim" (1784). He carefully outlined the blurred boundaries between ourselves and others driven by a powerful sense of mind. Similarly, Kant discussed the quiet wonder we feel when we encounter natural or man-made phenomena that defy our expectations in "Critique of the Power of Judgment" (1790).

G W F Hegel built on this sense of wonder by historicizing it as the realization of an immanent freedom, spirit, or mind. However, some readers like Robin George Collingwood and Benedetto Croce saw that the dialectic does not eliminate earlier thought; instead, it transforms it.

Leopold von Ranke, often seen as either a hero or villain in history, spoke of intuition as well as the critical analysis of archival materials. The boundaries of philosophical histories were never tightly defined, despite the stories we tell about them. While Hegel saw history as the unfolding of freedom, others like Sarah Josepha Hale envisioned a world ruled by women, and William Howitt warned that the future would resist rationality in favor of a return to spirituality and intuition.

In another largely unacknowledged realm of historiography, Kyoto philosophical historians such as Kitar Nishida, Keiji Nishitani, Hajime Tanabe, Rabindranath Tagore, and Ranajit Guha revisited Hegel and Aristotle through the lens of Buddhist thought. They found wonder in the ordinary and humble, rather than the rare or novel.

Ignoring the more unsettling and unusual aspects of our histories and philosophies can give us a masculinist and arrogant view of the past. By acknowledging these quirks and embracing our curiosity, we can gain a richer understanding of ourselves and our world.

text: Many academic fields can be said to ‘study morality’. Of these, the philosophical sub-discipline of normative ethics studies morality in what is arguably the least alienated way. Rather than focusing on how people and societies think and talk about morality, normative ethicists try to figure out which things are, simply, morally good or bad, and why. The philosophical sub-field of meta-ethics adopts, naturally, a ‘meta-’ perspective on the kinds of enquiry that normative ethicists engage in. It asks whether there are objectively correct answers to these questions about good or bad, or whether ethics is, rather, a realm of illusion or mere opinion. Most of my work in the past decade has been in meta-ethics. I believe that there are truths about what’s morally right and wrong. I believe that some of these truths are objective or, as they say in the literature, ‘stance-independent’. That is to say, it’s not my or our disapproval that makes torture morally wrong; torture is wrong because, to put it simply, it hurts people a lot. I believe that these objective moral truths are knowable, and that some people are better than others are at coming to know them. You can even call them ‘moral experts’ if you wish. Of course, not everyone agrees with all of that. Some are simply confused; they conflate ‘objective’ with ‘culturally universal’ or ‘innate’ or ‘subsumable under a few exceptionless principles’ or some such. But many people’s misgivings about moral objectivity are more clear headed and deeper. In particular, I find that some demur because they think that, for there to be moral truths, let alone objective, knowable ones, morality would have to have a kind of ‘foundation’ that, in their view, is nowhere to be found. Others, anxious to help, try to show that there’s a firm foundation or ultimate ground for morality after all. It’s my view that both sides of this conflict are off on the wrong foot. Morality is objective, but it neither requires nor admits of a foundation. It just kind of floats there, along with the evaluative realm more generally, unsupported by anything else. Parts of it can be explained by other parts, but the entirety of the web or network of good and evil is brute. Maybe you think that’s weird and even worthy of outright dismissal. I once thought the same thing. The purpose of this essay, which is based on my book Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System (2022), is to encourage you to start seeing this aspect of the world as I now see it. The first question we should ask is: what exactly is a ‘foundation’? We can get clearer on what a foundation is by querying whether a moral theory like utilitarianism might count as one. With Sri Lanka needing 362 runs for victory, they finished their innings on 78/1 after the match was abandoned due to rain at 5:12 pm local time (1612 GMT). Dimuth Karunaratne and Kusal Mendis were unbeaten on 37 and 17 respectively.

The draw marked Sri Lanka's sixth consecutive draw in Tests at Lord's, having lost two previous matches. The major contributions to England's first-innings total of 416 came from Alastair Cook (167 not out) and Joe Root (85). In response, Sri Lanka could only muster 288, despite fifties from Karunaratne and Silva. England declared their second innings on 233/7, with Alex Hales falling just short of a maiden Test century, scoring 94 - his third fifty of the series, after previously being dismissed in the 80s as an opener.

The upcoming schedule sees Sri Lanka move on to two one-day internationals scheduled for June 21st. The draw leaves England leading the inaugural Super Series against Sri Lanka by a score of 10 points to 2, with each white-ball match offering two points for a win.

On this point, history now sides with Coleridge against the materialists, and philosophers sympathetic with the intent of materialism now generally identify not with ‘materialism’ but with ‘physicalism’, or the view that the fundamental components of the Universe are whatever physics will eventually conclude they are. Current thinking in quantum physics construes these elements as fundamental forces, which Coleridge himself argued. An understanding of Coleridge’s thinking, then, provides insight into the beginnings of the analytic-Continental divide and a bridge between materialist and dynamic (powers-based) views in the sciences. It also illuminates Coleridge’s poetry as the expression of a unified view of the world not as mere matter clumping together in happy coincidence, but as the evolution of the power of ideas in a world of dynamically forged syntheses that resound back to the powers from which these creative forces arose. ‘In Xanadu did Kubla Khan / A stately pleasure-dome decree …’ So begins Coleridge’s poem on the power of words and imagination in physical and poetic creation.
