text:
Title: Bringing Native Peoples Back into the Picture: A Call for an Inclusive U.S. History

In today's world, it's essential to question our understanding of history, particularly when it comes to 'The Invasion of America'. Comparing this narrative with a map of 'Territorial Acquisitions', produced by the US Geological Survey in 2014, raises some unsettling questions. The large, single-toned areas on the map signify exchanges between imperial powers, ignoring the native peoples who had long claimed these lands as their own.

It's crucial to reconsider our historical narrative, focusing more on the dispossession of indigenous populations. Such a revised history erases the artificial barriers that earlier generations drew to diminish the significance of native presence, avoids overemphasizing the rise of the nation-state, and better reflects the multicultural makeup of modern America, which is rapidly becoming majority non-white.

The themes of this inclusive history resonate with contemporary concerns, such as state-sanctioned social engineering, large-scale population displacement, environmental degradation, and global capitalism. However, the most compelling reason for this reevaluation is its faithfulness to the past. As a teacher in Georgia, I am mandated by law to ensure my students understand U.S. history as part of their public university education, borne out of the belief that an informed citizenry is vital to democracy. Good citizens need good history. A history that neglects the conquest of the continent fails to provide a complete picture, leading to misinformation and flawed debates about the present.

In planning for our future, let's retrace our steps and put the dispossession of native peoples back on our map.

text:
Title: Navigating the Divide: The Complexity of Believing in God and the Existence of Evil

In today's world, there are numerous debates that stir strong feelings, and the question of whether or not to believe in God is one of them. On both sides, you'll find individuals who feel just as passionate about their beliefs - some who believe wholeheartedly in a divine entity, while others who argue against its existence. If you find yourself firmly on either side, you likely know your moral duty, as William James Clifford would have it.

I grapple with the idea of God's existence, especially in light of the suffering and evil that exists within our world. For me, the image of Anne Frank's tragic end at Bergen-Belsen serves as a painful reminder of the death of God as I understand Him. However, I am not so confident that my perspective is absolute.

Much like many thoughtful Christians, I view the Bible as a collection of stories that illustrate how a nomadic people developed their understanding of their Creator. The Old Testament, in particular, can be seen as a journey - from infancy to adulthood, where the loving God of the gospels emerges. Certainly, there are challenges in distinguishing between literal and metaphorical interpretations, but I believe these difficulties can be addressed.

On the other hand, there are aspects that make the existence of God seem implausible to me. For one, the idea itself is unclear - the Christian God seems like an uneasy blend of Greek and Jewish conceptions of the Divine. Furthermore, I find it challenging to reconcile the existence of evil with a benevolent God. To me, God ceased to exist in Anne Frank's final moments at Bergen-Belsen.

Lastly, I believe that faith often serves as self-deception. However, I acknowledge that others may find comfort and purpose in their beliefs. Questions like "Why is there something rather than nothing?" or "How can a computer made of meat think?" are intriguing, and when believers use their faith to make sense of these mysteries, I am hesitant to judge. If someone claims that the only way they can comprehend great suffering is through religion, I do not dismiss them as unintelligent or insincere.

A process theologian, following Alfred North Whitehead, might propose that God has emptied Himself of His power - 'kenosis' - to suffer alongside us. It's possible that this idea is incorrect, but I cannot condemn someone who finds solace in it. In all honesty, my own beliefs continue to evolve as I grapple with these questions.

For me, embracing belief in God would feel immoral. But I understand why others choose faith - it offers a sense of purpose and understanding that I cannot deny. Perhaps one day, I too may find a way to reconcile the existence of evil with the idea of a loving God. Until then, I will continue my journey, seeking wisdom, understanding, and compassion in a world that can often seem senseless and cold.

text:
Simone Bespaloff's life was marked by a turbulent mix of anger and peace. During her time in New York, she managed to keep her connection with the Parisian intellectual world through letters exchanged with Fessard and Marcel. Eventually, she landed a job at Voice of America's French broadcast before taking up a position as a French literature teacher at Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts. Mount Holyoke became a significant hub for French culture during the war. At gatherings organized by Wahl, Bespaloff crossed paths with Jacques Maritain, Andr Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lvi-Strauss. Hannah Arendt, who described her as "the small, dark lady who wore white gloves," also visited Mount Holyoke in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt's interpretation of Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, mirrored Bespaloff's existentialist despair. In the oppressive shadow of war, Arendt portrayed humanity as helplessly ensnared in history's web. Kafka's "nightmarish world" had become a grim reality.

In her final published work on Camus, Bespaloff expressed how her generation was forced to live in a climate of violent death. After the war, despite having been celebrated by them earlier, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a 1946 letter to Boris de Schloezer, she wrote that "the hollow subjectivity that Sartre opposes to what I call 'magical interiority' is less the foundation of a new humanism than a precursor to a new conformity." She argued that instead of liberating the individual, Sartre's existentialism eroded the magical interiority through which humans could authentically connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the scrutiny of the Other. This dehumanized 'subjectivity strangely mirrors American "individualism," which unleashes itself in action to mask the void within.' The US felt both dull and hostile to Bespaloff.

Bespaloff's move to Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. During a break in the academic term, in April 1949, for reasons that remain unclear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her own complex dance of life ended tragically. She had once written about the fleeting happiness that can be found in an instant. In her final note, referring to Camus's thoughts, she wrote: "One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy is forever out of his reach."

text: impossible. The results in the economic realm are all too obvious. While other countries have succeeded to a degree in bucking the trend toward financial oligopoly, the US has given it free reign. The system continues tottering forward because no one is able to come up with a viable alternative. The US is thus a strange combination of might and weakness; a country able to project military firepower to the far corners of the globe yet incapable of addressing its own basic structural weaknesses. It insists that its 18th century governing system is flawless and loudly thumps its chest at anyone who dares say otherwise. Yet anyone who has bothered to look into the matter knows that it just isn’t so. Although the US claims to have written the book on democracy, the fact is that ‘we the people’ have never had an opportunity to vote on the complicated checks and balances created in their name. To be sure, Americans elected delegates to special state ratification conventions that eventually gave the document their assent. But as the historian Forrest McDonald pointed out half a century ago, women, blacks, native Americans, and non-property owners were all barred from voting while three-fourths of those eligible did not even bother to take part. This hardly qualifies as a ringing democratic endorsement. Rather than a sign of strength, endless hosannas to American exceptionalism amount to so much whistling past the graveyard. Romney might tell the world that he ‘reject[s] the view that America must decline’ and proclaim his faith in American exceptionalism instead. But what can he do exactly to make the process stop? When the US columnist Ramesh Ponnuru says that decadent Europeans ‘are just waiting for someone to turn out the last light in the last gallery of the Louvre’ whereas ‘Americans are not prepared to go gentle into that good night’, he misses the point. What makes Dylan Thomas’ lines so poignant is that, no matter how much one rages against the dying of the light, it will flicker out regardless. If the phrase ‘American exceptionalism’ seems to be popping up more and more frequently, it is because many in the US are in denial about something that is by now glaringly apparent. Nothing could be sillier than the notion of strolling into the 21st century with a pre-modern plan of government. text:
Reflecting on the plight of these tormented souls, I can't help but ponder how they perceive time. Their lives are molded by ancient customs and ingrained habits, oblivious to the fact that there exists a present where things could be different. Dante observes that these figures are condemned to live in perpetual repetition, as their pasts grip them relentlessly, leaving no room for novelty or freshness.

The stark contrast is evident when our journey takes us to the frozen floor of hell. Everything is still, encased in ice, a testament to the paralysis that grips these souls. But in purgatory, every encounter presents an opportunity for growth. As Dante and Virgil ascend Mount Purgatory, the hold of the past on these souls begins to weaken. Here, the inhabitants realize something that eludes those trapped in a hellish existence: vices do not have to define us. A sense of relative freedom is born when they understand that they are not bound by their mistakes and flaws, their hate and fear.

This understanding is akin to sight - the remarkable ability of the conscious mind to grasp, intuit, and develop when given the chance. It brings release because it makes us more receptive to the possibilities that life offers, possibilities we had previously shunned. Dante notes that the souls they meet on the mountain are evolving, and they share their stories of growth and transformation. They are learning to trust and cultivate what could be called imagination, and with that comes another essential capacity - co-creation. This is the art of living well, a dance between creating and being created.

Artists, particularly poets, whom Dante encounters during his climb, underscore this point. They tell him that they often wrote without truly understanding what they were expressing, yet they were drawn to it. Now, they are learning to bridge that gap. The culmination of this process comes for Dante when he meets Beatrice on the summit of Purgatory - a moment I had expected to be filled with love and jubilation. Instead, she rebukes him harshly in two agonizing cantos.

Beatrice berates him for treating her like a deity, desiring only her image, and failing to see that her beauty could serve as an awakening for him. Once more, the theme of missed opportunities and unfulfilled potential reverberates through Dante's journey. This time, it is a stark reminder that even in realms beyond death, we must strive to truly see and understand those around us.

On November 16, 2016, Tom Marsico, a renowned investor and philanthropist, added a bit more to his already substantial wealth. An auction at Christie's in New York sold Claude Monet's "Grainstack" for an astounding $81.4 million, shattering the record for a Monet painting at auction. That price, which was over six times what Marsico had paid for it in 2002, is a testament to the exceptional returns that Monet offers as an investment. Consistent stellar performances by Monet in London and New York auction houses are becoming routine. However, the sky-high prices fetched for his paintings today bear little resemblance to Monet's financial struggles during his lifetime. The tales of critical resistance to Impressionism and the ensuing financial woes of the movement's artists are well-known folklore. Indeed, Monet did face some early hardships. A juror for the annual Paris Salon in 1868 dismissed his works, effectively impeding sales opportunities. That same year, he was denied credit for paint and, according to legend, survived one winter on nothing but potatoes. Yet, for most of his career, Monet was far from a starving artist. His diet of potatoes soon gave way to French delicacies like foie gras, truffles, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor raved about the culinary delights enjoyed at Monet's home. After turning 50, Monet became a perpetual millionaire: quite the outcome for such a gourmand. His wealth afforded him a fleet of cars, tailored tweed suits from a Parisian tailor, and a team of gardeners to maintain his famous gardens. In this regard, he shares more similarities with contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their powerful dealers, affluent collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than he does with artistic outsiders such as Vincent van Gogh, who lived in poverty. Monet's surname is almost a pun for 'money,' a fact that headline writers and others appreciate. In essence, he was the Roaring Twenties' precursor to Koons, as his 1922 sale of one canvas to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist four years before his death. Monet has long been the preferred artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, Steve Wynn, and numerous Russian and Asian billionaires. Monet's canvases serve as symbols of both aesthetic appreciation and financial power, dazzling consumer goods that also function as lucrative investments. Monet's paintings served a similar purpose during his lifetime -

This decision sent us trekking into national forests every spring with our tents and recording equipment, surrounded by living orbs of light that are ‘bettering the stars’, in the words of the poet Richard Newman. If the female is interested, she waits two seconds before making a half-second flash of her own Though animal communication is often considered the domain of ecologists and zoologists, physicists and mathematicians have much to contribute when it comes to understanding the energetic cost of communicating, the amount of information being transmitted from one organism to another, and how difficult or easy it is for the recipient to detect the sender’s signal. Anyone who’s ever used an online dating platform is familiar with the dilemmas around how much energy to invest in creating a profile, how many text messages to send and how many emojis to use. It turns out these considerations around cost, signal-to-noise ratio and compressibility are universal criteria that almost all communication systems must meet. But the firefly system, with a traceable mode of communication and the ability to track the signals from all individuals in the swarm, offers unparalleled advantages over other systems that display what scientists call ‘mate-search communication’. Fireflies’ dazzling light displays are, in fact, courtship rituals: flying males announce their presence as suitable mates to the females on the ground. Their light signal is composed of a species-specific on/off light pattern repeated periodically. A good example is Photinus carolinus, a synchronous firefly species documented in the southeast region of the US. A male would fly about a metre off the ground. Every 15 seconds or so, he makes several consecutive flashes, one second apart. The female P carolinus stays closer to the ground in low vegetation. If the female is interested, she waits two seconds before making a half-second flash of her own at the third second. Flash production is a voluntary action, resulting from the well-timed release of the neurotransmitter octopamine that then triggers a chemical reaction in the insect’s lantern. The current, state-of-the-art hypothesis is that fireflies control their flashing by regulating how much oxygen goes to their light-producing organs, though whether that’s accurate is still unclear. What’s certain is that, unlike a light bulb, a firefly’s light is ‘cold light’, with only minimal energy lost as heat. It is the ‘eccentrics’, he suggests, who keep the world running through their generous supply of bold perspectives, fresh insights and new ideas. ‘Precisely because the tyranny of opinion is such as to make eccentricity a reproach, it is desirable, in order to break through that tyranny, that people should be eccentric,’ he writes. The more eccentrics there are, the better the moral and intellectual state of the world: ‘Eccentricity has always abounded when and where strength of character has abounded; and the amount of eccentricity in a society has generally been proportional to the amount of genius, mental vigour, and moral courage which it contained.’ It is this redeeming ‘eccentricity’ that contrarians possess in abundance. The novelty and sharpness of their thinking come in large part from their determination to stay outside the circle that any group, explicitly or tacitly, draws in the sand to define itself. Left out as they are, contrarians are not only in a good position to observe how herding, marginalisation and exclusion work, but they no longer have anything to lose by articulating and broadcasting their heretical views. They are what ‘public intellectuals’ should ideally be – uncompromising ‘critics of society’ – and what, in practice, very few of them are. It’s the vigour of their dissent, the force of their language, and the seriousness of their commitment – their ‘quality of opposition’, in Gide’s words – that turn them into such formidable figures. That, incidentally, is also what distinguishes genuine contrarians from mere provocateurs, for whom challenging the establishment is not a matter of intellectual duty and inner conviction, but above all a form of attention-seeking and a histrionic compulsion to entertain. The peculiar cut of the contrarians’ minds, their innate distrust of anything authoritative or established, their iconoclasm and radical separation from the society into which they were born, all conspires to give them access to a higher truth than their society can afford to hear. Contrarians don’t care for fads and fashions, authorities and hierarchies, and have little patience for the rituals of the establishment. Since they have cut off their ties with their tribe, nothing prevents them from seeing things as they are. Their dissent doesn’t only free them, it gives them new eyes. Outstandingly learned as he may have been already, Spinoza’s philosophical formation was complete only when he was formally expelled from his community. text:
Stepping onto the chessboard feels like walking into a long-awaited gathering. All my old friends are here - the royal pair, their confidants, the steadfast ranks of noble infantry. I adjust them carefully, making sure they're perfectly placed in the heart of their starting squares, an anxious shuffle and gentle touch. These pieces mean something to me. They've been companions for years, my responsibility, and I'm grateful to my opponent for allowing me to tend to them meticulously under the threat of defeat. Chess has been a part of my life since I was five; it's offered friendship, sanctuary, and growth. I've worn the title of grandmaster for twenty years - a lifelong honor bestowed upon those who consistently demonstrate mastery in international competitions. It's not always easy to climb to the top, but chess has been my constant, even when life felt uncertain.

I haven't trained or played with serious intent for over a decade now. While I've found fulfillment in academic and public policy work, I often miss the thrill of competition and the clarity it brings. I yearn for the strength and purpose that came with making quick decisions under pressure, the luck of close calls, and the chase towards victory. But most of all, I pine for that focused state of mind - concentration.

I can still concentrate, but not as reliably or intensely as when chess was my life. From a distance, it seems like an acceptable excuse to immerse oneself in deep thought for extended periods. In Umberto Eco's The Island from the Day Before (1994), he pens a love letter that includes the line: "Only in your prison does [my heart] enjoy the most sublime of freedoms" - that could easily be said about chess and concentration.

I believe that concentration is essential to a fulfilling life, the building block for a sustainable civilization, and that chess can teach us more about what real focus means. Every skilled pursuit requires attention, but few demand the intensity that chess does. First, Wittgenstein cannot argue for a particular view of the nature of meaning, a consequence of which is that the very argument for that view is meaningless. We do in fact understand Wittgenstein’s philosophical argument. It is not a ladder that, once climbed, needs to be kicked away, as Wittgenstein advised in the Tractatus. Second, this kind of philosophy is impoverished. If, as Wittgenstein holds, philosophy’s task is to take the propositions of science and ordinary life and ‘exhibit them in a logical system with primitive terms and definitions’, philosophy really is of not much use at all. In a note from 1929 he said: The standardisation of the colours of beer is not philosophy, but in a sense it is an improvement in notation, and a clarification of thought. Both objections hold for the Vienna Circle too, although one thing Ramsey did share with them was an incredulity toward Wittgenstein’s idea that there is something inexpressible yet important – that one could peer through the fence, glimpse the unsayable, and stand in awe of it. Ramsey’s approach, and his rebellion against Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle, were starting to become fully formed. He summed it up perfectly in another note: We cannot really picture the world as disconnected selves; the selves we know are in the world. What we can’t do we can’t do and it’s no good trying. Philosophy comes from not understanding the logic of our language; but the logic of our language is not what Wittgenstein thought. The pictures we make to ourselves are not pictures of facts. text:
Living in harmony through collective actions: Bridging the gap between traditional Darwinian beliefs and proponents of Gaia songs isn't always straightforward, but it's worth the effort. Just as a song requires singers, life on Earth seems to require these individual steps working together.

Songs, themselves, don't reproduce, but they are re-created and do evolve. The current nitrogen cycle may not be identical to the one on Archaean Earth, but it can be seen as a continuation of that cycle - a legacy passed down through time, much like a family surname in my earlier analogy. If we think of songs as enduring entities (rather than replicators/reproducers), and the various species that occasionally come together to perform them as their interpreters, Hull's replicator/interactor framework provides a powerful explanation.

However, there is a potential problem here - it seems strange to suggest that patterns of interaction, which are not physical entities, could cause the evolution of species, which are. It's uncertain whether the Jones family name directly impacts the reproductive behavior of individual Joneses, although stories passed down through generations about family lineage might have some influence. But this conundrum isn't unique to this perspective - it's a challenge that many theories face.

In fact, Dawkins himself pondered over the idea that genes are immortal information while their physical manifestations and vehicles they produce are temporary. He referred to these material genes as 'lumbering robots', recruited to serve their selfish ends. The evolutionary biologist David Haig wrote: "Material genes were physical objects, but informational genes were the abstract sequences of which material genes were temporary vehicles." He further explained that continuity resides in the recursive representation of immortal patterns by ephemeral avatars. So, this issue isn't a new one - it's been debated for some time.

Adopting a replicator/interactor framework seems to lessen this debate, as 'perpetuation' can mean persistent presence rather than continuous presence. Replication is taken to include reproduction, recurrence, and re-creation. Processes such as the nitrogen cycle can be considered entities that are perpetuated (the replicators, if you will).

Beyond scientific benefits, embracing Gaia as part of a Darwinian framework could encourage us to view nature holistically - considering its evolutionary trajectory and how we might influence it. After all, we're already doing that, whether we realize it or not. It would be comforting to bridge the divide between traditional Darwinism and the concept of Gaia, even though there's still much work needed to strengthen this theory. I can only hope that Darwin, if he had lived another 138 years, would appreciate our efforts to reconcile Gaia within a selectionist framework, without pushing his grand theory too far beyond its limits.

text:
Graham Bateson's journey of self-discovery began with a letter to his father in 1925. A year prior, his parents had convinced him to call off his engagement to a Swiss woman. She wasn't English, she wasn't a scientist - therefore, she wasn't the right fit for him. To make amends, he was sent instead to the Galpagos Islands to replicate the fieldwork that Charles Darwin had carried out a century before. But Bateson found the locals more captivating than the wildlife. He felt compelled to break free from the constraints of ordinary science and delve into anthropology.

A decade later, on the opening page of Naven, he acknowledged that while an artist could capture the emotional essence or spirit of a society in just a few pages, anthropologists limited themselves to the technical aspects of their organization. Bateson aimed to bridge this gap between structure and emotion. As a scientist, he sought to encroach on the territory of art, or perhaps, practice science as an artist might.

In doing so, Bateson was challenging the deeply ingrained beliefs of Western civilization, which held that the scientific observer is entirely separate from their subject of study. "There are times," he mused, "when I find myself believing there's something that is separate from something else."

Naven gets its name from a series of bizarre rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which left Bateson baffled for years. In 1927, he ventured to the South Pacific, hoping to explore the relationship between individuality and culture, intending to debunk the British anthropological model that viewed human societies as biological organisms with behaviors entirely explainable through their collective needs.

But the rigid determinism of this model didn't sit well with Bateson. It failed to account for conflict among individuals, and overlooked the realms of aesthetics. The opposing behavior patterns within the tribe fueled escalating competition between men and women. When he arrived among these tribes, however, he was at a loss as to what to observe or how to approach his research. Other anthropologists had developed intricate questionnaires and aggressive methods of interrogation, but Bateson abhorred interference in others' lives. He believed that questionnaires implied a presumption of already knowing the vital questions.

In 1929, he settled along the Sepik River, alone with the Iatmul - a people who had recently given up cannibalism and built their culture around 'a constant emphasis on spectacle.' Bateson soon realized that the belligerent atmosphere in the tribe was affecting his own personality. He found himself growing rougher in his methods.

text:
Isaiah Berlin, a renowned thinker and writer, held an almost unparalleled status as an intellectual and moralist in the Western world during the latter half of the 20th century. He passed away on November 5, 1997. Soon after his demise, Henry Hardy, his editor and friend, described him as "one of the most remarkable and delightful individuals I've ever known, not just wise, but also insightful in judgment, and genuinely good."

Berlin's seminal essay on liberty - much like many of his essays which started as lectures - begins by distinguishing two concepts of political freedom. The first, referred to as 'negative liberty', is defined by the degree to which I am free from the intrusion of others or authorities. To be negative free, according to Berlin, doesn't mean being completely liberated from physical or psychological constraints; it merely indicates the extent of freedom from human interference or coercion, such as the freedom to refrain from participating in politics. In stark contrast is 'positive liberty', which is the freedom to act, rather than the freedom from something: for instance, the freedom to run for parliament.

At first glance, one might think that we're presented with an oversimplified distinction between two sides of the same coin. Berlin doesn't disagree, acknowledging that these concepts seem to be logically close. But what sets his account apart is his realistic and historical treatment of each concept, demonstrating not only a substantial and practical difference between negative and positive liberty but also showing how failing to recognize its significance can lead to far more damage than mere intellectual confusion.

The essence of Berlin's argument lies in flipping the traditional understanding: he makes theory respond to reality instead of the other way around. This bold move allows history and real-world experiences to take center stage in making sense of things. And what history and our current experience reveal is that negative and positive liberty cater to genuine and legitimate human needs and ideals.

Negative freedom has been the cornerstone of political liberalism, emphasizing the importance of individuals being left alone to their own devices, so long as their actions do not excessively harm others. In a similar vein, positive liberty has been at the heart of emancipatory theories of politics, from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism.

Berlin underscores that one of the major lessons of modern history and contemporary reality is how dangerously vulnerable the concept of positive liberty can be.

Which tack to take might depend partly on whether one thinks life sentences are ever a just and appropriate punishment. Proponents of levelling up can’t simply assume the answer is yes, when that is the very question at issue. Here is one reason to think levelling down is appropriate. While the US criminal justice system treats Black and brown people worse than it treats whites, it seems plausible that the treatment of white people adheres more closely to our society’s views of justice, since white people are thought to represent the ‘normal’ or the default. People of colour, then, are subject to a surplus penalty rather than white people being let off easy. If that is so, reducing the penalties Black people pay rather than increasing those of whites would bring us closer to justice. You cannot reasonably believe that someone should spend their whole life in prison unless you believe they will always present a significant threat to public safety, or that they deserve such a sentence. I have already argued that the overwhelming majority of the more than 200,000 people currently serving life sentences in the US do not present a lifelong threat to public safety. Whether they ought to spend their whole lives in prison depends, then, solely on whether they deserve it. They can deserve it only if they are fully responsible for their criminal acts. In the law, responsibility is best understood by what it excludes. One who is not responsible (liable) for their criminal act has a partial or complete excuse. A complete excuse, making one wholly nonculpable, has required, since at least the 19th century, that one not know what one is doing, not know it is wrong, or not be able to control one’s actions (be under the influence of an irresistible impulse). Under these definitions, most people serving life sentences are probably responsible for their acts. Growing up in environments of deprivation increases the likelihood that a person will go on to commit crimes But a broader understanding of responsibility is essential. For one thing, the insanity defence is a complete excuse that renders a person legally innocent (which doesn’t mean they walk free; they are likely to be institutionalised in a psychiatric facility). But there are many more whose responsibility is diminished, even though not entirely absent. The Death Penalty Information Center in its 2021 report found that: [A]ll but one of the 11 people executed in 2021 had one or more significant impairments, including: evidence of mental illness; brain injury, developmental brain damage, or an IQ in the intellectually disabled range; or chronic serious childhood trauma, neglect, and/or abuse. My mother had left the Dominican Republic a few years earlier, secured the only job she could get, earning the minimum wage in a garment factory, and petitioned for my brother and I to join her. In 1985, we entered New York City’s overcrowded public school system, where the free lunches supplied a good portion of our sustenance. Like many immigrants, we were poor, exposed, and disoriented by our uprooting. It was not an auspicious beginning for the career I would have as student, academic administrator and faculty member at an Ivy League university. text:
Moral dilemmas, as we commonly understand them, are often depicted as an internal battle between our higher moral self and our baser instincts. This image of good versus evil can be found in popular stories, like the angel and devil on each shoulder, or the 'two wolves' parable, or the concepts of Ego and Id, true self and false self. It echoes religious beliefs that place us between angels and animals, torn between our conscience and temptation, duty and desire. This perspective is also present in philosophical thought, from Plato to Immanuel Kant, where life's moral battles are often portrayed as a fight between moral duty and earthly desires.

In this traditional view, the self is fragmented, and we strive for unity by suppressing our darker impulses, refusing to give in to immoral thoughts or actions. Moral philosophy, therefore, often assumes that there's always a right choice when faced with moral dilemmas. Any difficulties in making the right decision are due to internal resistance, not an inability to do all the good we aspire to.

However, this view overlooks a crucial reality: in many cases, the problem isn't how to suppress our darker impulses, but how to navigate when we have too many good or neutral demands on our limited time, energy, or resources. In other words, the challenge isn't whether to be moral at all, but how to allocate our moral efforts effectively under conditions of scarcity and conflict. Dealing with this type of moral predicament requires a different way of thinking about moral agency and leading a good life.

There are at least three broad categories of values that frequently lead to incompatible yet legitimate moral claims, each revealed through a different practical stance we take towards the world as we try to decide what to do and who to be. On this view, we're all fragmented, but this fragmentation isn't about an internal struggle between good and evil selves. Instead, moral conflict should be understood in terms of competing dimensions of the good  not all of which can be met in any given moment.

These three fundamental normative domains or classes of value can be thought of in terms of the traditional literary distinction between first-, second- and third-person perspectives. A story told from the first-person perspective lets us empathize with the protagonist's struggles; we say 'I' along with them. In the second-person perspective, the focus is on others; the 'you' takes center stage.

text:
Living life through individual steps is akin to having a song  there are always singers. Bridging the gap between traditional Darwinian thought and Gaia enthusiasts can be likened to harmonizing two distinct melodies. Just as songs don't reproduce themselves, but rather are replicated and evolve, so too does the current nitrogen cycle differ from that of the Archaean Earth, yet it continues its evolutionary symphony. If we imagine songs as persisters instead of replicators/reproducers, and the various species that periodically unite to perform them as their interactors, Hull's replicator/interactor framework provides a compelling explanation.

However, this perspective raises questions about whether patterns of interaction, which are not tangible entities themselves, can drive the evolution of species. It's unclear if the Jones family name exerts influence on the reproductive behavior of its bearers, though family legends could possibly boost confidence and encourage reproduction. Yet, this conundrum is not exclusive to this theory. In fact, even Dawkins acknowledged that genes, as information, are eternal while their physical manifestations are temporary  mere vessels serving their self-centered purposes. As evolutionary biologist David Haig puts it:

"Material genes were physical objects, but informational genes were the abstract sequences of which material genes were temporal carriers ... Continuity lies in the recursive representation of immortal pattern by transient avatars ..."

Thus, the debate over the immaterial versus the material is not a novel one. Adopting a replicator/interactor framework seems to alleviate this issue, as 'perpetuation' encompasses differential persistence  persistence need not mean constant presence. Replication includes reproduction, recurrence, and re-production, while entities like the nitrogen cycle can be seen as continuous.

Embracing this perspective would benefit science by encouraging us to view nature holistically, with an evolutionary journey that we shape or steer. After all, humanity has been unconsciously engaging in this practice for ages. Alleviating the divide between traditional Darwinian thought and Gaia proponents seems like a step towards harmony, though much work remains to be done to strengthen and validate the theory. We'll never truly know if Darwin would have endorsed this Gaia-centric view within a selectionist framework, but I hope he'd appreciate our efforts to make Gaia compatible with his groundbreaking ideas, without pushing his grand theory beyond its limits.

In this current cycle, Turchin's prediction of chaos in 2020 was significantly influenced by. Back in 2010, Cliodynamics, a new discipline's flagship journal, debuted, with its inaugural article (by American sociologist Randall Collins) diving into modeling victory and defeat in battle, considering material resources and organizational morale. Following the argument Comte made centuries ago regarding scientific complexity progression, Turchin firmly disagreed that complexity rendered human societies unfit for quantitative analysis. Instead, he argued that it was precisely this complexity that necessitated mathematics.

Weather forecasts were once seen as unreliable due to the intricacy of dealing with the necessary data. However, advancements in technology (such as satellites and computers) now make it possible to mathematically describe and model interactions between the system's various components  and thus know when to carry an umbrella. With equal conviction, Turchin asserted that the cliodynamic approach was not deterministic. Instead, it would provide governments and political leaders with insights into the likely outcomes of competing policy choices.

Crucially, cliodynamics also benefited from the surge in interest in digital humanities. Existing archives were being digitized, uploaded, and made searchable  each day bringing more data that encouraged quantification and enabled mathematical analysis  including the Old Bailey's online database, which Wolf had found himself entangled in. In 2014, the subtitle of their flagship journal was changed from The Journal of Theoretical and Mathematical History to The Journal of Quantitative History and Cultural Evolution. As Turchin explained in his editorial, this shift aimed to position cliodynamics within a broader evolutionary analysis; echoing Russian-American geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky, he claimed that 'nothing in human history makes sense except in the light of cultural evolution.'

Given Turchin's ecological background, an evolutionary approach to history is hardly surprising. However, given the historical consequences of making politics biological, it raises potential concerns. Even more striking perhaps is the explicit evolutionary turn mirrored a broader shift towards the natural sciences among some members of the humanities. The experiences of World War II and imperialism made Western scholars deeply cautious about using biology and evolution to explain culture and society  the links to xenophobic and sexist politics seemed far too close for comfort. Attempts in the 1970s to establish biosocial rules that could apply to both human and non-human societies led to bitter intellectual schisms that divided disciplines like anthropology down the middle. By the 1990s, the focus may have shifted from race to IQ score, with the emergence of Richard Herrnstein and Charles Murray's The Bell Curve.

text: the classroom, I learned to put away my body. Outside of the academy, however, specifically through fanfiction, I was learning to read with it. I discovered fanfiction in 2001, when I was 16, unhappy at school, and frequently mistaken for a boy in women’s bathrooms. I’d joined a number of web mailing listservs, desperate to make friends, and I remember vividly the first time one of the fanfiction writers I followed posted a story she’d written about a character from The Lord of the Rings. I was staggered. It was like nothing I’d ever read before. Suddenly, I had a word for a genre that I had known intimately for years. I’d always fantasised about stepping into books and having adventures with my favourite characters. Now I wrote them down and shared them. Strangers left positive comments on each chapter. I discovered that I could write. Later, I learned that there was a name for this kind of story: ‘Mary Sue’. The name comes from the short satire ‘A Trekkie’s Tale’ (1973), written by the mathematician Paula Smith and first published in a Star Trek fanzine to parody the flood of (in her opinion) terrible fanfiction stories in which all-American teenagers with names such as Mary Sue stride into the world of the Starship: Enterprise and win Captain Kirk’s fickle heart. Smith’s parody is embarrassingly similar to my first story, and thousands like it online, written by young women experimenting with fanfiction. But as I read more widely, I discovered a rich vein of sophisticated and brilliantly written fanfiction, offering insights into characters I knew and loved. While bingeing on Harry Potter fanfiction, I was taking courses at high school, then university, in classical and medieval literature. I learned Latin, and read Virgil, Cicero and St Augustine in the original language. I wrote cold, sober, critical essays, but stayed up late into the night with fanfiction, my guilty pleasure, seemingly as far removed from the study of literature as it was possible to be. I moved to Canada to go to graduate school. By then I knew that fantasising about meeting Geoffrey Chaucer, or imagining what Queen Eleanor of Aquitaine might do if she met Sir Lancelot, was not the way serious readers did it. But I struggled with cognitive dissonance, since it was obvious to me that fanfiction had made me good at reading. Fanfiction makes its source texts richer for its loving readers. It amplifies allusions and hidden currents, pulls out notes of characterisation and subtleties of plot, and spends time with them. After reading fanfiction, I return to texts I love with a new eye – sometimes a more critical one. For example, I read hundreds of stories embroidering the relationship between the Harry Potter characters Remus Lupin and Sirius Black, which – fanfiction writers suggested – was the real reason Sirius’s family had thrown him out.

text:
Title: Herodotus' Comic Lament on Ancient Geography: The Circumambient Ocean

The iconography of the ancient Ocean was more than just vases and tile floors; it also included maps, where the ocean appeared in its physical form. Although the original drawings have long vanished, we have the historian Herodotus to thank for a detailed account of how these maps were crafted. Interestingly, his account elicited a rare moment of humor from this usually stoic scholar. "I chuckle," he wrote, disparagingly, "at the many who draw maps of the world in such an illogical manner; they make the Earth rounder than one could draw with a compass, and place Ocean all around the edges; and they make Europe and Asia equal in size."

The map design Herodotus describes can be visualized as follows: Why was this amusing to him? After months of research and analysis for what would eventually become my first published scholarly article, I too pondered over his laughter. The map holds good Homeric authority, as Ocean was depicted on the edge of Achilles' shield in Book 18 of the Iliad - a shield that, in essence, embodies an early image of our world. Was Herodotus being so dismissive of such a venerable mythical tradition, so deeply rooted in Greek literature?

One possible explanation for his amusement, it seems to me, lies in the satirical phrase "rounder than one could draw with a compass." There's an air of perfection in this geometry that Herodotus finds incongruous. Real-world geography, he suggests, does not form such flawless circles. While Achilles' shield may have been circular, Ocean's placement around the edge holds no cartographic significance.

A second source of humor for Herodotus stems from his concern with the veracity of information. No one, he asserted in a separate discussion, has ever observed the waters of Ocean lapping at the shores of northern Europe or eastern Asia. Thus, these regions might very well be connected to other landmasses, making the Atlantic and Pacificwhile known by the former name, the latter was yet unnamed disconnected bodies of water. In another passage, when discussing the river Nile, Herodotus goes so far as to call Ocean a downright fabrication: "I doubt that any River Ocean truly exists, and I believe that Homer or some other early poet invented the name and introduced it into his poetry."

This bold assertion could well be the first ever attempt in Western literature to question established geographical beliefs.

text: It is a familiar story. A superpower goes to war and faces a stronger-than-expected insurgency in distant lands, yet has insufficient forces to counter it because of political and military constraints. The superpower decides to hire contractors, some of whom are armed, to support its war effort. The armed contractors prove to be both a blessing and a curse, providing vital security services to the campaign, yet at times killing innocent civilians, causing strategic setbacks, and damaging the superpower’s legitimacy. Without these contractors, the superpower could not wage the war. With them, it is more difficult to win. The armed contractors in question are not in Iraq or Afghanistan but in northern Italy, and the year is not 2007 but 1377. The superpower is not the United States but the papacy under Pope Gregory XI, fighting the antipapal league led by the duchy of Milan. The tragic killing of civilians by armed contractors did not occur in Baghdad but in Cesena, 630 years earlier. The military companies employed were not DynCorp International, Triple Canopy or Blackwater, but the Company of the Star, the Company of the Hat and the White Company. Known as free companies, these for-profit warriors were organised as corporations, with a well-articulated hierarchy of subcommanders and administrative machinery that oversaw the fair distribution of loot according to employees’ contracts. CEO-like captains led these medieval military corporations. The parallels between medieval and contemporary private military companies (PMCs) are strong. Today, the US and many others hire contractors to fulfil security-related contracts in the world’s most dangerous places. In the late Middle Ages, such men were called condottieri – literally, ‘contractors’ – who agreed to perform security services described in written contracts, or condotte. Both modern and medieval contractors were organised as companies, their services available to the highest or most powerful bidder for profit. Both filled their ranks with professional men of arms drawn from different countries and loyal primarily to the paycheck. Both have functioned as private armies, usually offering land-based combat skills rather than naval (or aerial) capabilities and deploying force in a military manner rather than as law enforcement or police. KUWAIT CITY  The Euro 2016 tournament has ignited football fervor across the Arab world, transforming cafes and eateries into bustling centers of excitement, eclipsing the nightly television soap operas that traditionally dominate Ramadan viewing. In the holy month of Ramadan, television audiences surge as families gather to watch their favorite shows after dusk, with rival soaps from both state and private channels typically garnering the highest ratings. However, this year's Ramadan coincides with the Euros, adding another enticing dimension to viewers' entertainment options.

In Cairo, cafes have adorned their entrances with flags of participating countries to lure football fans. Matches are scheduled during daylight hours when devout Muslims are fasting, as well as evening games that clash with the iftar meal, which breaks the daily fast at sunset. In Tunisia, where cafes close during the daytime in Ramadan, families gather in their homes to watch the games after breaking their fast. Evening matches begin shortly after Muslims break their fast, a time when most Tunisians traditionally spend with their families watching television.

Television viewership in the region spikes during Ramadan, with ratings remaining high well into the night and advertisers dedicating substantial portions of their budget to the small screens' prime season. Since most games are broadcast on foreign satellite channels, local Tunisian networks have scheduled daily analysis programs on the matches.

In Kuwait, whose national team was banned by FIFA in October from participating in World Cup qualifying matches due to alleged government interference in sports, citizens find solace in following the Euros. Germany, the reigning world champions, is the most popular team among fans in Kuwait and many other Arab countries, surpassing even the defending European champions.

In Dubai, one restaurant has constructed a large tent on the beach with giant screens to showcase the games.

There’s neither reason in history nor some kind of Hegelian teleology or theodicy. History itself is without meaning or direction. Only historical analysis, theoretically informed, might begin to discern the pattern of its ‘absurdity’ and ‘abysmalness’. The analytical grid that Koselleck suggested as the baseline of his theory of history – before and after, below and above, inner and outer – also conditions historical writing itself. It’s crucial for the viewpoint of historians, whether they are a contemporary or born later, and are thus an eyewitness or a retrospective narrator of events, respectively; whether they are situated higher or lower in social or political terms, for example, whether they are on the side of the winners or losers of the conflict they are describing; and, finally, whether they belong to the political, religious, social or economic community whose history they are writing about, identifying themselves more or less critically with it, or whether they are looking on from outside. For Koselleck, the decisive distinction between his theory of history and hermeneutics (or Michel Foucault’s ‘discourse analysis’) is his insistence that not all experiences can be captured by language. As he explained in a lecture delivered in honour of his teacher Hans-Georg Gadamer in 1985: When the flexible distinction between inside and outside is intensified into an impassioned dichotomy between friend and enemy, when the relation between above and below leads to slavery and irrevocable humiliation or to exploitation and class struggle, or when the tension between the sexes leads to degradation – this is when events emerge: chains of events, cascades of events that withhold themselves from language, to which every word, every sentence, every speech can only react. It’s precisely these cascades of events that we’re struggling to put into words that interested Koselleck as a historian and theorist. Some of his most compelling essays explore the limits of language. One example is ‘Terror and Dream’, written originally as the afterword to the German edition of the German-Jewish writer and émigrée Charlotte Beradt’s The Third Reich of Dreams (1966; English translation 1968), a remarkable collection of excerpts from about 300 dream protocols that Beradt had collected from her Berlin neighbours and smuggled out of Nazi Germany before war and genocide. In this essay, subtitled ‘Methodological Remarks on the Experience of Time during the Third Reich’, Koselleck argues that these dream protocols capture the past reality of the Third Reich more than any other historical source. What brings about a human? Aristotle knew that animals with two sexes create new animals from the ‘union of male and female’. So, like his predecessors, he acknowledges that males and females each contribute something to conception. But what? Aristotle assumes that male ejaculate, semen, contains seed. Closely studying semen, he argues it must be a ‘residue’ of a ‘growth nutriment’. On Aristotelian biology, nutriments contribute to a body’s natural, healthy growth. If a body does not use up nutriments, the leftover becomes a residue. Aristotle argues his semen = residue theory explains various ‘facts’. For example, healthy males produce more semen, because sick ones use up more nutriment. Children do not produce semen because they are still growing, using up their nutriment without leftovers. Because nutriment helps bodies gain weight, people and goats who are ‘getting rather too fat’ do not have nutriment leftover – explaining why they produce ‘less semen and are less desirous of sexual intercourse’. For Aristotle, blood is the most important nutriment, travelling around the body bringing life, growth. He reasons that semen must be a residue of blood. But blood is runny and red whereas (he notes) semen is ‘thick and white’. Why doesn’t semen look more like blood? Aristotle’s answer is that males ‘concoct’ blood residue, thickening and purifying it, turning it into semen. Female bodies do not concoct semen, which is why they regularly lose leftover blood – a nifty explanation for menstruation. Why do only males concoct semen? Because concoction requires heat. And, as on the Table of Opposites, Aristotle connects males-hot, females-cold. One reason to think females provide the material is that, after conception, the new creature grows inside its mother. As Aristotle notes, further material ‘must constantly be added’ to an embryo ‘that it may increase in size’. New creatures grow regardless of what their fathers are doing, so this material must come from their mothers. And if you think females provide the material cause of a new creature, then you had better deny they also provide the efficient cause. If women possessed the material and efficient causes of generation, they could become pregnant without sex – something you didn’t see happening in classical Greece. Let me clarify something - yes, I'm a professor at Yale University, but I'm no genius. When I first told my four adult children about teaching a new course on genius, they found it downright hilarious. 'You? You're no genius! You're more of a grinder.' And they weren't wrong. So how is it that now, some dozen years later, I'm still teaching a successful course on genius at Yale, and have even written an Amazon Book of the Year selection titled "The Hidden Habits of Genius" (2020)? The answer: I guess I just had the audacity to be ignorant, as Nikola Tesla would put it.

I started my career with dreams of being a concert pianist, during the Cold War era. The United States was then trying to outdo the Soviet Union in every way possible. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a 23-year-old pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition - akin to the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Brooklyn's Bobby Fischer defeated Boris Spassky in chess. With my interest in music and my towering height, I too would become the next Cliburn, or so my mother believed. Although we weren't financially well-off, my parents managed to provide me with a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, D.C. Before I knew it, I was off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music, where every opportunity seemed to present itself. And I had a strong work ethic: by the age of 21, I had logged an estimated 15,000 hours of focused practice. (Mozart supposedly needed only 6,000 to reach the level of master-composer and performer.) Yet within two years, I could see that I would never make a living as a concert pianist. The one thing I lacked was musical talent - no special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no absolute pitch - all things crucial to a professional performer. 'If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach' - that's the mantra of conservatories such as the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day in the same studio teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists? My instinct was to find a bigger stage in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn to become a college professor and researcher of music history - a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I landed a job at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the "three Bs": Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms. Yet the composer who truly captivated me was an M: Mozart. My interest in him deepened with the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a time, the entire world seemed consumed by this funny, passionate man.

Consider the way in which his thought is treated in a German-language history of philosophy published at the beginning of the 19th century, Wilhelm Gottlieb Tennemann’s 11-volume History of Philosophy. In the work’s final volume on modern philosophy, covering the period from Locke to David Hume, Wolff’s name is mentioned fewer than 10 times, and only in passing. Tennemann’s history of philosophy is extremely important because it went on to influence other English and French histories of philosophy of the period, so Wolff’s meagre portrayal there likely shaped how he was perceived abroad. The damage done by this to Wolff’s fate in particular was exacerbated by what the philosophical giants on the horizon had to say about him. Struggling to make ends meet, I found myself sitting across from a friend at a caf, nibbling on a sandwich that was more expensive than my weekly budget. The grill marks on the bread looked like leering teeth - a luxury I couldn't afford. As I handed over the remaining coins in my purse for the bill, I felt a pang of resentment. I reminded myself that he deserved his money, and I was choosing to live this lifestyle as a full-time writer. If only my erotica would sell! Only 10 copies had been sold so far, earning me a paltry 15.

I'm no stranger to the constant hum of uncertainty about the future. But lately, it has turned into an aggravating shrillness, warning me that I need to reevaluate my life soon. Money is a recurring theme in the romantic erotica I read for research. Many stories depict heroines who are financially desperate, using sex as a transaction - love in an age of capitalism.

In Georgia Le Carres The Highest Bidder (2019), a college student named Freya is so broke that she joins an exclusive London club to auction off her virginity. After signing a non-disclosure agreement, she struts out on stage naked while unseen male billionaires bid for her. Luckily, she's the subject of an intense bidding war, selling for 1 million. She ends up with a young man who resembles a 'Greek God,' whom she knew from her childhood. The rags-to-riches element gives these stories a Cinderella feel, but there are no fairy godmothers around, and magic is achieved through the heroines' degradation. It's almost as if their poverty makes it easier for us to empathize with why they would go through such humiliation, giving us permission to find pleasure in their trysts.

In Jade Wests Buy Me, Sir (2007), Melissa is 21 and struggling to raise her baby brother while working as a cleaner for a law firm. She's determined to seduce Alexander Henley, the criminal lawyer who owns it. She learns that he has a thing for Debbie Harry, so she dyes her hair blonde and creates a sexy video of herself masturbating for his 'dealer,' the man who solicits women for him. The romantic heroes in these books flaunt their wealth with yachts and helicopters.

It's an interesting reflection on how a decade of austerity has affected our perception of love and sex in society, especially when money is involved. These stories depict desperate heroines who use their bodies to escape poverty, often finding temporary relief through humiliating transactions or exploitative relationships. It's a stark reminder that capitalism can make us all feel like we're selling ourselves short at times.

Rhetoric, after all, is the art of adapting oneself to the moment; it requires adjustments and reactions, a constant recalibrating of our interactions with others. It was precisely this need to adapt, to change, to act and react, to speak and deceive, that seemed so foreign to the philosophical conception of God as eternal, perfect and unchanging. As it turned out, miraculous interference proved hard to distinguish from deception At the turn of the 14th century, another source of tension between scripture and philosophy made the threat of divine deception more pronounced. Aristotle’s writings, most of which had been lost to Europeans for centuries, had started to become available during the 12th century. Intellectuals, keen for the newest thing, incorporated Aristotle’s works into the curriculum of the various medieval universities and Aristotle’s ideas honed the cutting edge of 13th century intellectual life. But Aristotelian philosophy often cut across scripture, not with it. According to Aristotle, God – the ‘Unmoved Mover’ – was part of the universe, the best part, but still part of it, giving it form and energy according to the necessary and unchangeable dictates of reason itself. Theologians could hardly sit by idly as students and teachers absorbed this deflated image of God. If scripture taught anything about God, it taught that God transcended a universe that he had created ex nihilo, out of nothing. God is omnipotent, they asserted, he can do anything. Demonstrating God’s omnipotence required theologians to describe all the ways that God could miraculously interfere with the ordinary course of nature. As it turned out, miraculous interference proved hard to distinguish from deception. Early in the 14th century, William of Ockham, known to posterity for his razor, asked his readers to carry out a simple thought experiment. Imagine you are looking at a star. Title: Sendai, Japan: G7 Advanced Economies Set to Discuss Growth Boosting Strategies Ahead of Official Meeting

SENDAI, JAPAN - A disagreement on fiscal policy and currencies is expected to set the stage for a frank exchange of views among G7 advanced economies regarding ways to stimulate growth at an informal symposium preceding the official G7 meeting scheduled for Friday. The symposium's participants concurred that rather than relying on short-term measures, each country should focus on policies tailored to their unique economic circumstances. As a result, the G7 finance leaders, while expressing concerns about potential risks to the global economic outlook, may struggle to agree on specific actions to invigorate stagnant worldwide growth.

Bank of Japan Governor Haruhiko Kuroda told reporters on Thursday that he expected a candid discussion on achieving price stability and economic growth through the use of monetary, fiscal, and structural policies tailored to each country's individual needs.

The Risks of Brexit Highlight Agenda

At Friday's G7 session on the global economy, the risk of a British exit from the European Union in a June referendum (Brexit) will occupy a prominent position. The finance leaders are set to engage in discussions about this issue, as well as other topics such as the need to enhance cybersecurity in light of growing financial transactions on a global scale. According to sources familiar with the group's deliberations, there is an increased awareness among G7 leaders that immediate action is required in this area.

In February, a cyber theft targeting a Bangladesh central bank account served as a reminder of the need for improved cybersecurity measures within the global financial network (SWIFT), which banks use to transfer billions of dollars daily. With such incidents becoming more prevalent, SWIFT has cautioned its users about the potential threat posed by cyber attacks.

In addition to discussing cyber security, the G7 finance leaders will also consider the need for enhanced cooperation in addressing other global economic challenges during their meeting on Saturday.

In 2010, Cliodynamics, the flagship journal for this new discipline, appeared, with its very first article (by the American sociologist Randall Collins) focusing on modelling victory and defeat in battle in relation to material resources and organisational morale. In a move that paralleled Comte’s earlier argument regarding the successive stages of scientific complexity (from physics, through chemistry and biology, to sociology), Turchin passionately rejected the idea that complexity made human societies unsuitable for quantitative analysis, arguing that it was precisely that complexity which made mathematics essential. Weather predictions were once considered unreliable because of the sheer complexity of managing the necessary data. But improvements in technology (satellites, computers) mean that it’s now possible to describe mathematically, and therefore to model, interactions between the system’s various parts – and therefore to know when it’s wise to carry an umbrella. With equal force, Turchin insisted that the cliodynamic approach was not deterministic. It would not predict the future, but instead lay out for governments and political leaders the likely consequences of competing policy choices. Crucially, and again on the back of the abundantly available and cheap computer power, cliodynamics benefited from the surge in interest in the digital humanities. Existing archives were being digitised, uploaded and made searchable: every day, it seemed, more data were being presented in a format that encouraged quantification and enabled mathematical analysis – including the Old Bailey’s online database, of which Wolf had fallen foul. At the same time, cliodynamicists were repositioning themselves. Four years after its initial launch, the subtitle of their flagship journal was renamed, from The Journal of Theoretical and Mathematical History to The Journal of Quantitative History and Cultural Evolution. As Turchin’s editorial stated, this move was intended to position cliodynamics within a broader evolutionary analysis; paraphrasing the Russian-American geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky, he claimed that ‘nothing in human history makes sense except in the light of cultural evolution’. Given Turchin’s ecological background, this evolutionary approach to history is unsurprising. But given the historical outcomes of making politics biological, it is potentially worrying. More startling perhaps is that this explicit evolutionary turn was mirrored in a broader shift towards the natural sciences on the part of (some members of) the humanities. As noted, the experiences of the Second World War, and of imperialism more generally, made Western scholars deeply wary of using biology and evolution to explain culture and society: the links to xenophobic and sexist politics seemed far too close for comfort. Attempts in the 1970s to establish biosocial rules that could apply to human and nonhuman society likewise resulted in vicious intellectual schisms that split disciplines such as anthropology down the middle. Neither The Birth of Tragedy nor The Open Society is celebrated today for its author’s fidelity to historical accuracy. Nonetheless, both remain iconic because Nietzsche and Popper were each on to something resonant about the relationship between myth and philosophy, and the curious symbolic role of Plato in our inherited understandings of that relationship. To some extent, both their critiques were right, in the fractured way everyone is a little right in the parable about the blind men and the elephant. Plato continues to be celebrated as a figure who set philosophy on a rational foundation by isolating a distinctly rigorous, critical mode of reasoning from the clutches of myth and received wisdom. And yet, his dialogues are also run through with myths of his own invention: the Myth of Metals, the Symposium’s account of the origin of love, and the stories about the afterlife which close the Phaedo, Gorgias and Republic. Squaring both these aspects of Plato’s thought is difficult because we remain wedded to two distinct, but related, ideas in our intellectual heritage. The first is the idea that philosophy is fundamentally opposed to myth. And the second is that it was Plato who first pointed this out. Concerns about myth are often bound up with concerns about misinformation. But these anxieties go beyond a worry about factual inaccuracies that require correction. Rather, when scholars and commentators designate something as a myth, they are usually grappling with a more existential threat: a symbolically powerful narrative that seems immune to correction altogether, but that, nonetheless, has the power to captivate our imaginations. The language of myth, for instance, has recently been used to talk about frontier imagery in US immigration policy – ‘the myth of the border wall’ – as well as nostalgic appeals to a somehow more authentic version of a nation’s people – from the ‘myth of a “real” America’, to the ‘myths of Englishness’ that made rounds during the Brexit campaign. One reason myths make us uneasy is that we expect them not to have a place at the heart of the assumptions and beliefs that found modern culture. That is, there is an expectation that we, as a society, ought to have outgrown myths. This expectation stems from an evolutionary account of culture that goes back at least to ancient Greece, when Presocratic thinkers adopted rhetoric that distinguished their methods of investigation from the oral tradition associated with ancient myths about the Olympian deities, by focusing on the unverifiable nature of such stories. But it was in 18th-century Europe that myth came to be seen as a cultural relic that was fundamentally at odds with modern life. In such a situation, the social and legal disadvantage of the labourer is so great that it makes no difference whether the legal system enforces a classical property relationship or not. Davis and the legal scholar Robert Cover both observed that there’s a direct relationship between homicide and slavery. We see this when we peruse the literature describing the transportation of slaves in the transatlantic, trans-Saharan, Silk Road and Indian Ocean slave trades. In some cases, the death rates of transported slaves exceeded 30 per cent. Indeed, the presence of high rates of homicide and unexplained death among certain populations in ancient and early modern societies was a clear marker of slavery. We see high rates of homicide and unexplained death among the low-status migrant-labourer populations in the Persian Gulf as well. Perhaps the best documented evidence of this are the assessments of conditions affecting workers from Nepal. The International Labour Organization has also issued a report showing an alarmingly high rate of death among otherwise healthy Nepali migrant workers, particularly in Saudi Arabia, Oman, Bahrain and Kuwait. BORDEAUX  Adam Szalai and Zoltan Stieber scored second-half goals, propelling Hungary to a triumphant return to a major tournament for the first time since the 1986 World Cup. Gabor Kiraly, at 40 years old, made history as the oldest player ever to feature in the European Championship, surpassing Lothar Matthaus of Germany in this record.

Austria's captain, Christian Fuchs, and winger Martin Harnik were the only two holdovers from their debut campaign eight years ago, starting for Marcel Koller. A strike from outside the area by David Alaba crashed against the post early on, with Austria threatening throughout. Alaba, operating in central midfield as usual for Austria, soon issued a warning to Hungary with a first-time shot, while Kiraly demonstrated his agility by saving a low strike from Zlatko Junuzovic.

Marko Arnautovic then sent a dangerous low ball across the face of goal as Austria continued their onslaught, but Harnik lost his footing as he tried to slide home at the far post. The best chance of the opening half fell to Hungary captain Balazs Dzsudzsak, who missed wide after being picked out by Laszlo Kleinheisler on the right.

Just beyond the hour, Kleinheisler slipped in Szalai, played onside by Fuchs, with the powerful striker poking the ball underneath Robert Almer for his first goal at any level since December 2014. Austria seemed to have equalized when Martin Hinteregger rifled in from the edge of the area, but referee Clement Turpin disallowed the goal due to a foul on Tamas Kadar, with Dragovic receiving a second booking and dismissal.

Stieber's late goal, three minutes from time, triggered delirious celebrations as he chipped Austria keeper Almer with a sublime finish, securing Hungary their first European Championship win since 1964. It was a stunning upset for the Hungarian team.

text:
SINGAPORE - Brent crude oil prices experienced a significant rebound on Wednesday as investors held onto hope that a tentative agreement between Saudi Arabia and Russia to freeze oil production at January levels could lead to a broader deal among producers, potentially resulting in supply cuts.

By 0455 GMT, Brent crude had gained 34 cents to reach $32.52 per barrel, recovering from its $1.21 decline the previous day and surpassing its early Tuesday high of $35.55 per barrel. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude followed suit, rising 19 cents to $29.23 per barrel after ending the previous session with a 40-cent drop.

The agreement between Russia and Saudi Arabia on Tuesday stipulated that oil production would be limited to January levels, contingent upon other exporters joining in. However, they fell short of agreeing to make immediate cuts to tackle the global oversupply.

Iraq, Qatar, and Venezuela expressed their intent to freeze output at January levels, provided a deal could be reached, while OPEC member Iran may receive special terms for freezing its production levels, sources said. The market's initial surge on Tuesday was swiftly tempered by the realization that no immediate supply cuts were imminent.

Ben Le Brun, a market analyst at Sydneys OptionsXpress, described the situation as "a buy the rumour, sell the fact event." He explained that while the market is adjusting to the idea that this isn't entirely negative news, it's not as favorable as initially anticipated. Investors are primarily hopeful for production cuts.

According to a note from Singapore-based Phillip Futures, freezing output at January levels would have minimal impact on the overall supply glut. Saudi Arabia's oil production has remained relatively stable at over 10 million barrels per day (bpd) throughout 2015, essentially maintaining the status quo rather than making significant changes.

Investors are keeping a close eye on upcoming US oil inventory data for further insights into oil prices. According to a Reuters poll of analysts, US crude stocks increased by 3.9 million barrels to 505.9 million barrels in the week ending February 12. The weekly inventory reports from industry group the American Petroleum Institute (API) and the US Department of Energy's Energy Information Administration (EIA) will be released on Wednesday and Thursday, respectively, one day later than usual due to a public holiday on Monday.

LOAHREPakistan cricket team coach Waqar Younis has tendered his resignation, effective immediately, a little over three months before the expiry of his contract. The announcement comes on the heels of former T20 captain Shahid Afridi's resignation one day prior.

Their departures follow Pakistan's dismal performances in recent tournamentsthe Asia Cup in Bangladesh and the World T20 in India, where they managed to win just three out of eight games across both events, failing to advance past the first round. The last series Pakistan won was in October 2015, when they clinched a 2-Test series against England in the UAE. Since then, they have lost the ODI and T20 series to England, followed by the T20 and ODI series against New Zealand, winning only two out of twelve limited-overs matches during their tenure as coach.

Waqar's resignation letter was submitted to PCB chairman Shahryar Khan and the board earlier today. It marks his second stint as head coach, having previously held the position in 2010-11 before leaving due to personal reasons. His most recent contract ran from 2014 until 2016.

Lawrence’s philosophy of parenthood was based on just-about-benign neglect. He thought we’d become too self-conscious, and that we inflicted self-consciousness on our children by loving them too much.
