Courtesy WikimediaThere was more to Ocean’s iconography than vases and tile floors; there were also the maps, on which Ocean appeared in his physical, non-personified form. Thanks to the historian Herodotus, we know much about how these early maps were drawn, though the drawings themselves have not survived. Surprisingly, their arrangement drew from the normally sober historian a rare moment of comic levity. ‘I laugh,’ Herodotus wrote dismissively, ‘when I see the many who have drawn maps of the Earth in a nonsensical way; they make the Earth rounder than one would draw it with a compass, and put Ocean all around the outside; and they make Europe and Asia equal in size.’ The scheme he describes could be illustrated like this: Why did Herodotus find this so funny? Indeed, I puzzled over his laughter for many months as I hacked away at what became my first published scholarly article, some three decades ago. The map has good Homeric authority behind it, since Homer had similarly placed Ocean around the rim of Achilles’s shield, described at length in Book 18 of the Iliad – a shield that, in some respects, represents a pre-cartographic image of the world. Did Herodotus really have the temerity to scorn such a time-worn mythic tradition, enshrined in the Greek world’s most revered literary text? One clue to Herodotus’s mirth, it seems to me, lies in the satiric phrase ‘rounder than one would draw it with a compass’. There’s too much perfection in this geometry. Actual features of the Earth, Herodotus seems to imply, do not form perfect circles. Achilles’s shield might have formed a circle, but the fact that Ocean stood at that shield’s perimeter has no cartographic significance. Similarly, the exact symmetry of ‘Asia’ and ‘Europe’ – taking ‘Asia’ (as many Greeks did) to mean the southern half of the world, including Africa, and ‘Europe’ the northern half – seems to Herodotus all too simple. A second problem Herodotus has with Ocean stems from the historian’s concern with the reliability of his information. No one, he asserts in a separate discussion, has ever seen the waters of Ocean lapping at northern Europe, or eastern Asia. So these two regions, for all he knows, might well be connected to some further landmasses that make the Atlantic and the Pacific – Herodotus knew of the first name, but not the second – into two disconnected bodies of water. Indeed in another passage, a discussion of the river Nile, Herodotus goes so far as to call Ocean an out-and-out lie: ‘I don’t know that any river Ocean exists, and I think that Homer or some other of the early poets invented the name and introduced it into his poetry’. text:
SINGAPORE - Brent crude oil futures experienced a rebound on Wednesday amid optimism that a deal between Saudi Arabia and Russia to freeze oil production at January levels could pave the way for a broader agreement among producers, potentially leading to production cuts and price support.

By 0455 GMT, Brent had risen 34 cents to $32.52 per barrel, having dropped $1.21 in the previous session. Earlier on Tuesday, it had climbed to $35.55 a barrel. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil was up 19 cents at $29.23 a barrel, following a 40-cent decline in the previous session.

The agreement between Russia and Saudi Arabia on Tuesday stipulated that they would maintain production levels from January, but only if other oil exporters joined in. Iran could be granted special terms for freezing its production levels, according to sources. Iraq, Qatar, and Venezuela have also expressed their readiness to freeze output at January levels, contingent upon a deal being reached.

The initial surge in oil prices on Tuesday due to the deal's announcement was quickly tempered by the realization that there would be no immediate supply cuts aimed at addressing global oversupply. Ben Le Brun, market analyst at Sydney's OptionsXpress, described it as a "buy the rumor, sell the fact" event.

Le Brun further stated, "The market is gradually coming to terms with the idea that this isn't bad news, but not quite the good news that it was expecting. Investors are hoping for actual production cuts." The agreement to maintain output at January levels is expected to have minimal impact on the overall supply glut, as per a note from Singapore's Phillip Futures on Wednesday.

Saudi Arabia has kept its oil production steady slightly above 10 million barrels per day (bpd) throughout 2015, implying that "all that was achieved was an agreement to continue what they were doing," according to Phillip Futures. Investors are now looking towards the release of US oil inventory data later today for further direction on oil prices. According to a Reuters poll of analysts, US crude stocks increased by 3.9 million barrels to 505.9 million barrels in the week ending February 12.

Weekly inventory reports from the American Petroleum Institute (API) and the US Department of Energy's Energy Information Administration (EIA) are due for release on Wednesday and Thursday, respectively. These reports were delayed by a day due to a public holiday on Monday.

On November 16th, 2016, investment guru and philanthropist Tom Marsico, one of the wealthiest men in the U.S., saw a fatter wallet: Christie's in New York auctioned off Claude Monet's "Grainstack" for a whopping $81.4 million. This record-breaking price for a Monet painting at auction was more than six times what Marsico paid for it back in 2002, demonstrating that investing in a Monet might just be one of the most profitable moves you can make. Auction houses in London and New York have seen stellar performances by Monet time and again. Yet, the sky-high prices paid for his paintings today are hardly a reminder of Monet's struggles during his lifetime. The tales of critical rejection faced by Impressionist painters, including Monet, and their financial hardships are well-known. However, it's important to note that while Monet did face some early setbacks, he wasn't exactly starving throughout most of his career.

In 1868, for instance, a juror dismissed his works at the annual Paris Salon, potentially hindering sales. That same year, he was even denied credit for paints and reportedly subsisted on potatoes during one harsh winter. However, for much of his life, Monet wasn't living hand to mouth. His diet soon moved beyond potatoes to indulgent treats like foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Prigord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor gushed about the exceptional cuisine at Monet's home. After turning 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was never short of cash: a fortunate turn of events for such a food enthusiast. His wealth kept him rolling in cars, tailored suits from Paris, and a team of gardeners to tend his famous gardens. In this sense, he shares more similarities with contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, their high-profile dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than with artistic outliers such as the penniless Vincent van Gogh. Monet's surname fits him well, as headline writers often appreciate its near homonymy with 'money'. He was indeed the Jazz Age's precursor to Koons, because in 1922, four years before his death, the sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist.

Monet has long been the go-to artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, hotelier Steve Wynn, and various Russian and Asian billionaires. His paintings are not just a testament to aesthetic taste but also serve as lucrative financial assets. During his lifetime too, Monet's works functioned similarly  they were investment goods masquerading as art.

text:
Human interactions can be quite intricate, not just social but also political. From playgrounds to the halls of power, people are constantly forming, adjusting, breaking, and complicating relationships. Consistent signals, like speech patterns or clothing styles, provide a sense of orientation in the chaotic social landscape. However, race might not always hold the weight its significance as a societal issue suggests, according to Cosmides, Tooby, and Kurzban's experiments from 2001. People do tend to categorize each other based on race automatically. Yet, it's possible to diminish race as a significant category in a matter of minutes by using neutral colors as markers. When people are told that individuals wearing grey or yellow shirts belong to two groups, they rely more heavily on the shirts than the ethnic appearance of the wearers to determine their group affiliation. Other researchers have reported that accent can overshadow race, with young children preferring friends who share the same accent, regardless of race.

This phenomenon might resonate with people living in Britain, where the Union flag obscured visible ethnic differences among Team GB athletes during last summer's Olympics, and young Londoners, regardless of their heritage, share a unique accent characteristic of their remarkably diverse city. Although Britons may be hazy about what it means to be British, the majority no longer view it as racially defined. Simultaneously, there is a widespread distrust towards Muslims and an inclination to perceive immigrants as unwelcome. The British in-group has undergone a transformation, redefining both itself and its 'others.' Britain has become a fairer nation, more inclusive than ever before  a development unthinkable just a generation or two ago. However, this reinvention of alliances inevitably opens the door for potential new biases and injustices. Perhaps we must accept that our relationship with 'them' is an ongoing process, always evolving and never truly complete.

text:
A chilling scene from Ringu (1998) - Sadako Yamamura, clad in her white burial shroud, emerging menacingly from a well, her dark hair plastered against her face. Once more, the seeds of unease were sown by 20th-century technology, this time it was a videotape harboring an unseen terror. Echoes of this can be traced back to the telephone in earlier times. Ringu is a prime example of J-horror - a genre that thrives on fearsome, vengeful spirits drawing heavily from Japan's kabuki tradition. However, are the Japanese yrei (ghosts) now destined to disappear completely into the abyss of consumerism, irony, and eventual disregard? If so, then maybe Yanagita was right to have his concerns about the people of Tokyo and Osaka at the dawn of the 20th century. Despite Japan's impressive technological advancements and adoption of global ideologies, there was a risk of losing sight of the most profound question that these spirits could provoke: not 'Are they real?', but rather 'How real are we?'

This question lingers at the summit of Osorezan, but it dissipates as you descend, surrounded by paved roads, streetlights, and Japan's omnipresent 24-hour convenience stores. A world constructed by human hands, on a human scale, with human purposes in mind reinforces our illusion of mastery - strengthening the 'me' that Yanagita feared could dominate us. It's no wonder remote mountaintops are so often associated with revelations and purity - everyday life rarely exposes its artificial nature, its 'virtual' essence.

Yet, perhaps Yanagita underestimated our hunger for a balance between mastery and mystery. We seem to crave both. J-horror thrives on the curiosity, doubts, and fears about the world that reside within us - about the darker aspects of human existence, about the pain we inflict on each other, creating grudges that can never truly be left behind. Even about a hidden world more fundamental than the one we navigate daily, where there might be more shadow than light.

In Japan, some speculate whether the traumas of 2011 may have triggered a subtle cultural shift - a recalibration of sorts. This change may not become apparent until looking back retrospectively. They observe a broader context in which people are seeking a more harmonious coexistence between human control and spiritual mystery, a balance that perhaps we've lost sight of amidst our relentless pursuit of progress.

text:
The incapability of understanding basic human decency, such as not treating the leader of a constitutional republic like a common dog, seems beyond the comprehension of some individuals. This was tragically demonstrated in the case of Charles J. Guiteau, who, though he possessed an acceptable level of intelligence, was clearly mentally unstable. Despite his mental instability, Guiteau was convicted and executed. Throughout history, the legal system has been slow to respond to medical expertise, even as our understanding of mental illness has grown through the fields of psychiatry and psychology. As early as the 16th century, Johann Weyer, a Dutch physician, challenged the Saxon Code for its inhumane treatment of individuals with mental illness, arguing that it failed to reflect the complexities of these conditions. His arguments were dismissed by the judiciary: "Weyer is not a lawyer, but a physician - thus his perspective on the connection between mental disorders and legal transgressions holds no weight."

Fast-forward nearly 400 years, and in 1950, the divide between legal definitions of insanity and psychiatric realities persisted. United States Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter remarked that "the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity" continued to be a point of contention. In the following almost four decades, as Toole writes, the definition of exculpatory mental disease in criminal law remained largely unchanged, unaffected by advances in medical theory and practice.

The current legal definition of insanity has no basis in psychiatry; it is a purely legal construct, independent of the scientific understanding of brain function and behavior. In his 1943 work, Mind, Medicine and Man, psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote that "the great majority and perhaps all murderers know what they are doing, the nature and quality of their acts, and the consequences thereof, and they are therefore legally sane." This lack of a basis in psychiatry has led to the perception that mental health experts are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective. In reality, mental health professionals often disagree on whether a defendant fits into the legal definition of insanity, even when there is consensus on their diagnosis and level of impairment.

As forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School stated in 1998: "Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defense, not about mental illness." In his work 'On Responsibility' (1996), federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard wrote that "with contemporary psychiatric and psychological data, the legitimate experts, who must testify about mental conditions, are unable to make accurate and scientific determinations within the rigid legal definitions the law imposes." Trials where juries base their decisions on contradictory expert testimony result in what can only be described as a 'battle of experts,' effectively guaranteeing arbitrary outcomes.

We tend to judge the moral culpability of an individual by the severity of our emotional response to their actions, rather than considering the complexity of mental health conditions that may have influenced those actions. This oversimplification does a disservice not only to the legal system but also to our understanding of mental illness and its impact on human behavior.

Members of the United States Women's National Soccer Team, led by Alex Morgan, took to social media to express their grief, with Morgan stating, "Hurting for Dallas right now." She went on to lament that it often feels like the country is constantly waking up to more tragedies than not. "I want to wake up from this nightmare," she added.

NBA superstar LeBron James shared a powerful message with his 31.5 million social media followers, stating, "We are all hurting tonight." He emphasized that violence is not the answer to the chaos unfolding in America. "#StoptheViolence" was the poignant hashtag he used to end his tweet.

text:
Exhibiting artworks requires specific guidelines and considerations to preserve their integrity, aesthetics, and interactive elements. Let's discuss three main categories of rules that shape how artists' visions are presented in museums.

First, the display rules dictate what exactly should be exhibited - whether it's a unique piece created by the artist, a type of object designated by them, or a new arrangement specifically designed for the exhibition. For instance, Flix Gonzlez-Torres' 'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) is displayed by providing a pile of appropriate hard candies on the gallery floor, allowing visitors to take some. On the other hand, El Anatsui's Drifting Continents (2009), a large wall-hung sculpture made of liquor bottle tops and copper wire, requires the museum to acquire the fabricated panels and give installers flexibility in how they fold, drape, and orient them for optimal presentation.

Second, conservation rules focus on preserving essential elements of a work to maintain its material integrity. These rules are crucial in determining how the components will appear to future viewers and identifying which parts contribute to the artwork's ongoing existence. Takashi Murakami's paintings, whose paint is designed to flake away over time, embrace change within the work as an integral part of its development rather than treating it as damage.

Lastly, participation rules outline audience engagement with the artworks. For example, Jill Sigman's Hut Project (2009-) invites visitors not only to appreciate the visual aesthetics but also to engage in various activities such as contributing small notes or drawings. Similarly, Paul Ramrez Jonas's The Commons (2011) features a riderless horse sculpture with a cork surface pierced with thumbtacks, encouraging viewers to contribute items or messages, transforming the artwork into a collective communication platform.

Ultimately, these rules guide both the museum staff and visitors in experiencing and appreciating artworks as intended by their creators, fostering a deeper understanding and connection with the works on display.

'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) by Flix Gonzlez-Torres. Courtesy the Art Institute of Chicago/Wikipedia
The Hut Project (2009-) by Jill Sigman
The Commons (2011) by Paul Ramrez Jonas. Materials: cork, pushpins, notes

text: a three-stage practice for bringing about political change through schizoanalysis. First, find those processes of desire that deviate from capitalism; then follow each to their most extreme conclusions, to allow them to escape from the restrictions of capital; and finally, align these different processes to create a ‘molecular revolution’. What, exactly, does that mean? Deleuze and Guattari argued that it was impossible to know in advance what such a revolution would look like. Instead of fomenting one according to a pre-ordained plan, they proposed a politics of experimentation. The bodily ordeals of the French dramatist Antonin Artaud’s ‘theatre of cruelty’, and the drug-induced adventures of the American writer William S Burroughs, were two of Deleuze and Guattari’s favourite examples of how one might explore alternative organisations of desire. The duo took a broad view of who might be involved in this enterprise. Rejecting the traditional Marxist idea that the working classes were the seedbed of change, the authors wanted a broader umbrella under which to unite all marginalised groups. They claimed that those oppressed by patriarchy (women), racism (people of colour) and heteronormativity (what we’d now call the LGBT community) were all suffering thanks to the same machinery of despotic and imperial capitalism. It’s only by bringing together these ‘minoritarians’ that an anti-capitalist revolution could succeed. Because the philosophical image of the individual is based on the apparently autonomous figure of the white male subject, it is through a process of ‘becoming-woman’, and of ‘becoming-minoritarian’, that the spectre of individuality can finally be banished. Anti-Oedipus ruffled a lot of feathers. Lacan was furious, and forbade any discussion of the text in his seminars. Many political Leftists, while sympathetic to Deleuze and Guattari’s aims, admonished their recklessness and heterodoxy. Despite (or perhaps because of) these criticisms, the book was a runaway success, selling out in a matter of days and attracting a two-page review in Le Monde. It wasn’t just the content, but also their form and working style that defied convention. Deleuze and Guattari’s books speak in a multitude of voices, not easily reduced to either of the two authors. ‘Since each of us was several, there was already quite a crowd,’ they say in A Thousand Plateaus (1980), the sequel to Anti-Oedipus. This sentiment was later echoed by Guattari: ‘We are very different … I’m more attracted to adventurous things; let’s call it a conceptual commando who likes to visit foreign lands. Gilles, on the other hand, is a philosophical heavyweight, he has a whole bibliographic administration.’ ‘We didn’t collaborate like two different people. We were more like two streams coming together to make a third’ By working in this way, they tried to combat the tendency towards a sterile, deadening individuality, in a practical as well as a theoretical sense. A Thousand Plateaus contains 15 chapters that range over a dizzying variety of topics, from geology to linguistics,

DUBLIN  Foreign Minister Charlie Flanagan told Reuters on Wednesday that Ireland would need to consider measures to support firms exporting to Britain if the UK votes to leave the European Union. Ireland's economy is considered particularly vulnerable among EU member states if Britain decides to exit, and ministers like Flanagan have campaigned in Britain ahead of Thursday's referendum to encourage Irish voters residing there to vote "Remain."

Ireland trades approximately 1.2 billion euros ($1.35 billion) of goods and services with the United Kingdom each week, with Irish farmers and food producers being particularly vulnerable as major UK suppliers. Flanagan said concerns about the potential impact on trade have been raised in meetings with trade bodies during the campaign.

When asked if there are any policy steps that could aid exporters, Flanagan replied, "It's too early for me to speculate as to the nature of any actions that we may take, but we are fully aware of the challenge that lies ahead and will respond accordingly."

Should Britain vote to leave, Ireland would prioritize national interests across a range of issues, with an emphasis on economic and trade matters. Flanagan anticipated that negotiations for withdrawal could take significantly longer than the two-year timeline proposed by Brexit campaigners. He also highlighted the possibility of reintroducing a hard border between the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland, which he believes would pose a difficult challenge due to its potential impact on both trade and the peace process.

Flanagan cautioned that any new border restrictions could jeopardize an 18-year old peace agreement between Irish nationalists seeking a united Ireland and their Protestant rivals who wish to maintain Northern Ireland's British status. In such a scenario, Flanagan expressed concern over the negotiation on border controls being done at EU level rather than under Ireland's control.

In his office in Dublin's government buildings, Flanagan stated, "At best, I would predict a period of uncertainty that would affect the progress made in recent years (in Northern Ireland). And in terms of the peace process, any reintroduction of border controls, either for trade or security purposes, could pose us with a difficult challenge."

KARACHI  In a surprising turn of events, private-sector lending contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the current fiscal year, contradicting the State Bank's efforts to boost credit flow via three cuts in policy rates. The latest data, released on Tuesday, revealed this stark contrast.

Analysts argue that banks have been favoring government papers over private-sector investments, as the low borrowing costs and heavy investment in long bills and PIBs have not translated into increased investment and spending by businesses and consumers. Consequently, the flow of loans to various companies dropped from Rs281.9 billion during July 1, 2014 to April 3, 2015 to only Rs178.9 billion during the same period this year, according to central bank figures.

The reduced supply of lendable funds for the private sector is attributed to banks' heavy investment in government treasury bills, despite falling borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have increasingly opted to place their funds into long-term bills and PIBs, attracted by high returns. The cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points since November 2014, following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector.

However, the government disagrees with this analysis, stating that private-sector credit continues to grow healthily (at 10.2 percent in FY15) primarily driven by businesses, particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict an expansion in bank lending in the coming quarters, with a moderate uptick expected in consumer loans.

In an effort to address these challenges and implement financial sector reforms, the State Bank has collaborated with World Bank experts to develop a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS). The central bank aims to launch the NFIS by the end of this year, taking into account complex legal, taxation, border trade requirements, and impaired access to finance.

text:
Title: Ireland Takes Encouragement from Stalemate Against Classy Sweden

Ireland can draw some optimism from their 1-1 draw against a strong Swedish side, who failed to register a single shot on target despite the efforts of talisman Zlatan Ibrahimovic. The Swede was kept relatively quiet by Ireland's defense in a performance that hinted at growing potential for O'Neill's team.

"We have two incredibly challenging matches ahead against two highly skilled teams," said O'Neill post-match. "However, if I can take anything from today's game, it was the impressive play of our players. They demonstrated their determination and are developing well within the international arena."

The atmosphere at the Stade de France was electric, with both teams backed by passionate fan bases. The vibrant yellow and green supporters brought a festive spirit to the stadium in stark contrast to the tournament's recent violent incidents. Ireland showed an impressive desire to win, making two attacking substitutions after Sweden equalized, while Sweden seemed sluggish and lacking bite until Ireland took a well-deserved lead three minutes into the second half.

Ireland's goal came from a brilliant half-chance by Wes Hoolahan, which caught out Ibrahimovic, the 36-year-old captain. Ibrahimovic, who left Paris St Germain at the end of last season after helping them win two straight domestic trebles, looked far from royal on Monday. In the first half, he struggled to find his footing on a pitch where he had previously won two French Cup and two League Cup trophies. Although he provided the cross that led to Ireland's own goal in the 71st minute, Ibrahimovic missed a chance to score an almost certain winner in the closing stages when Martin Olsson's cross narrowly eluded him.

Despite the late miss, Ireland can take heart from their performance. Manager O'Neill praised his team's resilience and determination, while Sweden manager Janne Andersson expressed disappointment with his side's first-half display. "Ireland were the better team for the first 50 minutes," he admitted. "Credit to Ireland. Our attack was poor in the first half. They managed to shut us down effectively."

Reflecting on his own performance, Ibrahimovic acknowledged that he could have done better, saying, "I tried, but there's always room for improvement."

text:
The residential schools system, once hailed as philanthropic, was a grim reality that marked a shift from a policy of annihilation to "saving" through assimilation. Sir John A. Macdonald and Duncan Campbell Scott, key figures in this era, held beliefs that the white man knew what was best for Indigenous children - superior even to their own families and communities. The ultimate goal was clear: assimilate them completely until there was no Native person left in Canada.

During a parliamentary committee in 1920, Duncan Campbell Scott, Canada's Deputy Superintendent of Indian Affairs, made it explicit: "Our aim is to continue until there isn't a single Indian in Canada who hasn't been absorbed into the body politic."

Today, some may question how such practices of separation and indoctrination could have been considered progressive or benevolent just a century ago. Yet, these weren't mere accidents or historical anomalies; they were deliberate and presented as enlightened and humanitarian. The residential schools weren't the only means by which these supposedly progressive and compassionate methods of separation and re-education were implemented. Forced adoptions and 'care' systems, such as the 'Stolen Generations' in Australia and the 'Sixties Scoop' in Canada, demonstrate that these progressive practices have persisted and evolved.

The fact that Indigenous children today are disproportionately represented in care systems worldwide suggests that these practices continue to be enacted.

These ideas behind the Indigenous residential schools didn't emerge out of thin air. They were part of a longer history of Eurocentrism, whether informed by Christianity, Social Darwinism, or today's neoliberalism. In this mindset, peoples of color were viewed as inferior or 'Other.'

One of the roots of this perspective can be traced back to the papal bull Inter caetera (1493), issued by Pope Alexander VI. This document allowed colonizing Europeans "full and free power, authority, and jurisdiction of every kind" over non-Europeans, effectively permitting their dispossession, enslavement, and mass murder. The papal bull is part of the so-called 'doctrine of discovery,' a set of legal and religious precepts that certain European nations believed granted them free reign to colonize the world.

For many Indigenous people in Canada, Pope Francis' 2022 apology for the abuses at the country's residential schools was a long-awaited step towards healing and reconciliation. But the scars left by this dark chapter in history remain deeply ingrained, serving as a stark reminder of the ongoing impact of Eurocentric beliefs and practices on Indigenous communities worldwide.

text: Daniel Kahneman observed that people often take an approach to reasoning that can be described as ‘anchoring and adjustment’. Presented with some piece of information – the asking price for a condo, early results from exit polls, a sample answer to a trivia question – people tend to use that information as a starting point in formulating their own estimates, hypotheses and predictions. If I ask you to guess the lifespan of the average tortoise, after first asking you whether most tortoises live past the age of nine, you’re likely to name a lower number than your friend who was asked whether they often live past the age of 70. The same cognitive logic is what makes red herrings so effective. They exert a gravitational pull on our speculation. Information can also act as an anchor when we know it’s false or even irrelevant. If you recognise some false trail as a red herring, it can still affect what Tversky and Kahneman described as the ‘availability’ of other ideas. It will constrain your imagination and make some lines of reasoning much easier to see than others. Characters make particularly useful vessels for misleading information, because they add deniability. A character can lie or be mistaken without making the story as a whole inconsistent or dishonest. This handy fact works nicely with another predictable feature of human cognition: our relatively bad memory for source information. Especially if we’re distracted (say, by some compelling, emotionally engaging story content), we can encounter claims in dubious settings, or even see them being debunked, but later remember only that we’ve heard them somewhere before. Then if we encounter the claims again, that sense of familiarity can make us more likely to take them seriously. The psychologist Daniel Schacter, a major figure in the cognitive and neurological study of memory, calls this failing and others like it the ‘sins of memory’, and stories can use them to plant information in places where we are likely to remember the misleading bits while losing track of the caveats. Fortunately, despite our tendency to lose track of this information, we never fully lose track of the distinction, so if a story shows us that a source we trusted was unreliable, we get it. The source is blown, but the story itself can still be on the up-and-up. If stories play their cards right, they can even introduce entirely new information at the dénouement but finesse the introduction so that it doesn’t seem to be new. A very common and surprisingly dependable example is the case of the underspecified clue: Sherlock Holmes observes the knees of a suspect’s trousers, but ‘reminds’ Watson of the fact that they were dirty and worn only as the solution to the mystery is revealed. text: their ability’ became the tacit watchword, a neat variant of the Marxist injunction ‘to each according to their need’. The first aptitude-testers promoted the idea that each person had an innate, more-or-less fixed intellectual capacity. In the context of the early 20th century’s growing eugenics movement, the tests were often deployed to justify widespread racial discrimination. Terman claimed that what he called borderline deficient scores on the Stanford-Binet were ‘very, very common among Spanish-Indian and Mexican families of the Southwest and also among Negroes’. ‘Children of this group should be segregated into separate classes,’ he wrote in 1916. ‘They cannot master abstractions but they can often be made into efficient workers … From a eugenic point of view they constitute a grave problem because of their unusually prolific breeding.’ In Terman’s mind, then, low IQ scores were simply and unarguably the result of objective deficiency. We now understand just how wrong that notion was. Today, many psychologists understand IQ and aptitude tests to be ‘culture-bound’ to one degree or another – that is, they evaluate abilities prized in the dominant Western culture, such as sorting items into categories, and can privilege those raised in that milieu. Such inequities have persisted despite attempts to make the tests fairer to those from non-dominant cultures. As the US marinated in social Darwinism after the First World War, the government began devising its own sinister solution to the ‘grave problem’ of which Terman had warned. The US Supreme Court case Buck v Bell in 1927 ruled for compulsory sterilisation of the ‘feeble-minded’ in the name of public welfare. For more than four decades thereafter, US states sterilised thousands of people with low IQ scores; a disproportionate number of victims were nonwhite. In later years, though aptitude tests’ eugenic roots would fade from view, the ranking of test-takers according to perceived social value would continue unabated. Though I didn’t fully grasp it until well into adulthood, I was a direct beneficiary of the test-driven hierarchy that Terman worked so hard to establish. As smarts go, I was never in Beatrice Carter’s league, but my 1980s aptitude test scores were good enough to earn me the label ‘gifted’. With this distinction came the message, from a hundred different directions, that my essence was somehow superior. I was fawned over, head-patted, even loved, in a way that many of my peers were not. I was given certificates to commemorate scores I’d gotten on tests for which I’d never studied. Starting in fifth grade, I reaped the most tangible benefit of my test-taking knack – I was invited to attend academic summer programmes run by the Johns Hopkins Center for Talented Youth in Baltimore. Nor did I think about those connections as a twentysomething vegan, up on my ethical high horse, wanting nothing to do with the confinement, let alone the deaths, of fellow creatures. I assumed I could remain aloof from all of that. Only later did I begin to see more clearly. Those connections are, in the literal sense of the word, vital. They keep us alive. The teacher and the student, the artist and the office worker, the doctor and the attorney, are all utterly dependent on the farmer. Whatever romantic notions we might have about ourselves and our ethically or environmentally motivated food choices, the boundaries between vegans, vegetarians and veal eaters are somewhat ambiguous. We are all part of the same food systems. We can — and should — advocate changes in those systems, promoting both animal welfare and ecological health. Our efforts, however, will be most effective if people of all dietary persuasions can collaborate, remembering that we, like the foods we eat, inhabit an integrated whole, not isolated kingdoms. My wife and I, for instance, don’t buy beef or veal, yet we applaud the local farmers who produce those meats in humane, ecologically sound ways. And we recognise that the yogurt we eat is linked to the lives and deaths of cows, just as our garden is. It is easy to forget, of course. I know I do. In the bustle of everyday life, the interconnections slip my mind. I eat a bowl of salad and see nothing but greens. Then the phone rings. It’s a neighbour calling. Woodchucks have begun to obliterate her garden, in spite of the electric fence. I’m one of the few hunters she knows. Would I be willing to lend a hand? Ah, yes, I think. Hidden costs. Taking a deep breath, I fetch my .22 rifle.

She got a job with the Voice of America’s French broadcast before moving to Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts, where she taught French literature. Mount Holyoke became an important outpost for French culture in the US during the war. At gatherings of exiled scholars organised by Wahl, Bespaloff met Jacques Maritain, André Masson, Marc Chagall and Claude Lévi-Strauss. This ‘small, dark lady who wore white gloves’, as her translator Mary McCarthy described her, also made an impression on Hannah Arendt who visited in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt’s reading of Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, echoed Bespaloff’s existentialist despair. Under the dark shadow of war, Arendt describes humanity as inescapably trapped in history’s meshes. Kafka’s ‘nightmare of a world’ had become reality. In an essay on Camus, her last published work, Bespaloff describes how history forced her generation ‘to live in a climate of violent death’. After the war, despite previously having been fêted by them, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new generation of French existentialists, especially Sartre. In a 1946 letter to the musicologist Boris de Schloezer, Bespaloff wrote that ‘the hollowness of subjectivity that Sartre opposes to what I call “magical interiority” is much less the foundation of a new humanism than the harbinger of a new conformity.’ She argued that, instead of liberating the individual, Sartre’s existentialism destroyed the magical interiority through which humans can authentically connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre degraded the subject into an object under the gaze of the Other. This objectified ‘subjectivity curiously aligns with American “individualism”, which unleashes itself in action to mask the absence of the individual.’ Like Helen’s Troy, the US felt both dull and hostile to Bespaloff. Bespaloff’s journey to Mount Holyoke was her final exile. During term break, in April 1949, for reasons not entirely clear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her own complex fugue ended with a tragic cadence. She had written earlier of the happiness that can be found in an instant. In her final note, alluding to Camus’s claim, she wrote: ‘One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy is forever out of his reach.’

She is being tossed about by the winds of hell as she speaks, mirroring the storms of passion she felt in life, but her account evokes our sympathy: who has not been there, or wanted to be so joined? text:
The elusive nature of connections between various elements, and a longing to break free from the divide between imagination and reality, were the driving forces behind the surrealists' philosophy. This discourse was delivered several years before luard's visit to Saint-Alban, and the artists he spoke about were indeed the surrealists - Max Ernst among them, a close friend of luard. Yet, I believe the description could just as effortlessly apply to Forestier, whose work seems to embody this fusion of freedom and unity.

I suspect that luard saw more than just artistic vision in Forestier's work; he may have glimpsed a broader ambition that he himself championed through the surrealist movement - one that 'refuses to serve an absurd order based upon inequality,' and aims for 'the total emancipation of humanity.'

While the practice of isolating distressed and disabled individuals in secure institutions may not be as prevalent today as it once was, the mindset that disregards their plight persists. It's evident in media outlets that provide a platform for statements like: 'Two-thirds of COVID-19 fatalities would have probably died within the year anyway.' It rears its head when the murders of disabled individuals by their caretakers are labeled as 'mercy killings.' And it's invoked when lawyers claim that a man, gasping for breath with a police officer's knee crushing his airway, died from 'underlying health conditions.'

Many readers might feel uncomfortable at the notion that the current system of reality, prevalent in Europe and America today, could be described as 'fascism.' As I once did, many prefer to view fascism as a relic of the past, defeated 80 years ago in Europe and lying dormant since then, except for the occasional outbursts of a few extremists. Others might understand fascism as an enduring threat, but see it as a distinct segment of the political spectrum, primarily promoted by white supremacists and ethno-nationalists we've heard so much about in recent years.

However, the concept that fascism should be viewed as something more profound and pervasive - something that exists within as well as without - has a rich history. In her 1940 essay on Homer's Iliad, Simone Weil observed the strong's indifference towards the weak and pondered on the use of force throughout human history: 'Pushed to its extreme, it transforms a person into a mere thing - a corpse while they are still alive.'

In the aftermath of the war, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno were clear that fascism had found continuity in the blend of bureaucracy and capitalism that defined the post-war world, a political system that 'relieves its people of the burden of moral feelings,' and 'is deeply in harmony with pure reason ... treating human beings as objects.'

text:
The strange pull of absorption isn't born from focus and concentration, but its opposite - a profound disconnection that leaves you detached from the world. It feels like being played, not by choice but because you've become ensnared. Studies by Schll show that slot machine addicts, trapped in their compulsion, experience a strange sense of relief, even joy, when they lose. Only when they've lost everything do they find the freedom to walk away. In her book "Addiction by Design" (2012), Schll describes hardcore players who would rather soil themselves than abandon their posts - adding a chilling new layer to the idea of being a hostage to one's own fate.

When the slot machine lights up and chimes with a win, the gambler feels a sense of near despair. They know they must stay put until they lose it all again. Losing is not seen as defeat but as the price paid for the experience - the thrill - of being distracted while being skillfully, if covertly, drained. Casinos cause real harm to people. I've seen it firsthand. Gamblers don't play with chance and possibility like computer scientists running simulations to predict outcomes. Instead, gaming infects a gambler's soul, like malware, altering their thoughts and feelings, intertwining with their life, and eventually taking control. It's not an external model of the world, but a virus inside. My father never managed to free himself from the grip of gambling: his whole life was ruled by the rhythmic drums of the tables. He lost a fortune over the years, and we lost much more - pieces of him. Casinos stole his ability to discern reality from its imitation. Instead of offering him a chance to practice his actions in the world, gambling became a substitute for it. My father was a perfect candidate for being manipulated. He was gullible and willful, but cowardly when faced with confrontation. In many ways, he resembled Schll's slot machine addicts: frustrated by a world that had grown too complex to navigate, they reduced their interaction with it to a single manageable transaction - bet or don't bet, play or don't play. I find this deeply sad, and it doesn't explain why I was drawn to professional blackjack myself. There was the thrill, sure, but there was also something else - an adventure, a sense of danger that preceded it, a lure that I couldn't resist.

text:
MEXICO CITY  FIFA revealed details of its delayed bidding process for the 2026 World Cup on Tuesday, as the revamped FIFA Council convened for its inaugural meeting since it replaced the former executive committee under reforms implemented in February. In a strategic role, the council mandated that potential host nations must comply with specific criteria concerning human rights, sustainable event management, and environmental protection.

FIFA President Gianni Infantino, elected in February, has pledged to increase the number of teams competing from 32 to 40  a proposal met with strong opposition by Europe's top clubs. FIFA stated that decisions on several key aspects, including the number of teams and tournament format, eligibility of continental confederations to bid, and the possibility of joint bids, are anticipated by October.

Infantino has expressed interest in fostering regional collaboration for the tournament, potentially dispersing it across various countries within a region. A consultation process outlining new standards for prospective hosts will run until May 2017, followed by a preparatory phase concluding with bid submissions in December 2018. Evaluations will take place from January 2019 to February 2020, and a final decision is expected three months later.

FIFA was compelled to overhaul its bidding process following the controversial awarding of the 2018 and 2022 tournaments to Russia and Qatar in December 2010. Both events are under investigation by Swiss authorities, while allegations surrounding labor abuse in Qatar's construction industry have stained preparations for the event. Qatari officials have maintained that they are working diligently to resolve the issue.

The hosts will ultimately be chosen by FIFA's 209 member associations at its annual congress. Despite this, the council will continue to compile a shortlist of three candidates, as was previously the practice with the old executive committee. Gibraltar and Kosovo's applications for membership were announced for submission to the congress on Friday, with both potentially eligible to compete in 2018 World Cup qualifying matches if accepted.

Benin was suspended immediately due to legal intervention in its football federation, while the council's decision not to suspend Greece was ratified. FIFA Council member Fernando Sarney commended Infantino's leadership on Tuesday: "Things are getting calmer at FIFA," said the Brazilian representative to reporters. "Gianni is focusing on football, traveling, engaging with people, and bringing football back into our discussions because previously we talked about everything but football."

KARACHI  In response to the growing threat of cyber attacks, the State Bank of Pakistan (SBP) has issued comprehensive guidelines aimed at bolstering the cyber security defenses of banks, Microfinance Institutions (MFBs), and Development Financial Institutions (DFIs).

Under these guidelines, financial institutions are encouraged to constantly enhance their cybersecurity measures and procedures to proactively counter potential threats. The guidelines cover four key areas: risk management responsibility and ownership, periodic evaluation and monitoring of cybersecurity controls, regular independent assessment and testing, as well as industry collaboration and contingency planning.

To ensure that financial institutions stay vigilant against the evolving nature of cyber threats, the SBP mandates the Board of Directors (BoD) to regularly assess the effectiveness of their cybersecurity systems and action plans in light of emerging risks. The senior management, meanwhile, is tasked with ensuring that a comprehensive cybersecurity management plan exists within each institution and is routinely reviewed and updated for implementation.

It is essential to acknowledge that cyber threats have become a global concern, continually evolving in terms of complexity and impact, despite advancements in cyber services delivery. In light of these new risks, the SBP requires banks, DFIs, and MFBs to take appropriate measures to comply with these guidelines by December 31, 2016.

text:
In a god, or at least that's what I pondered. I wasn't exactly convinced about the gods of Mount Olympus, but I believed that some form of divine presence was crucial for maintaining order in the ideal society I envisioned. Plato himself suggested that non-believers should be imprisoned, subjected to extreme thought control, served only by slaves, and buried anonymously upon death. I can't help but think that such draconian measures, as we saw in the 20th century with regimes like Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia, have shown us all too clearly the dangers of enforcing uniform beliefs.

It's not clear whether a universal belief in God or a lack thereof is inherently good for society, regardless of whether such a deity truly exists or not. The social sciences aren't particularly helpful in this regard. There are surveys, many sponsored by religious organizations, suggesting that religion plays a role in fostering morality and maintaining social order. For instance, one study by the Barna Group found that religious individuals are less likely to gossip than atheists. On a more serious note, in the United States, people from states with higher levels of religiosity tend to give more to charitable organizations, though often these donations go towards their own churches. Conversely, many European countries have lower levels of religiosity but greater state-supported social networks and thus higher levels of health and longevity. In other words, a state that endorses and implements programs like Obamacare might have higher wellbeing than a state that does not, even though it may be more religious and charitable. A non-believer might be just as morally good as a believer, but choose to contribute through taxes rather than voluntary donations.

One intriguing finding, based on internet downloads, is that porn consumption in the U.S. is highest in Utah, a state with a strong Mormon presence. Regardless of the side effects, it's unclear whether we'd be better off if everyone were made to believe in God or if they were made not to believe in Him. The answer isn't straightforward, and we have to allow people the freedom to make their own choices, even if it causes tension.

This brings me back to Dawkins and his provocative claim that teaching religion is a form of child abuse. While I don't entirely agree with him, I do think he raises an important point. If you've carefully weighed the evidence and come to a belief in God, then, morally speaking, you should hold onto that belief. But weighing the evidence requires taking science and other empirical pursuits more seriously than many religious believers are willing to do. In my view, it's your obligation to pass on your beliefs to your children, provided they've been purified through critical analysis. However, if someone holds religious beliefs that I consider unfounded, based on evidence that should be universally accepted, then I have a problem with it.

text:
Over the past few weeks, I've found myself oscillating between a mostly vegan and vegetarian diet. In hindsight, perhaps sticking with the vegan route would have been wiser, giving my body more time to adjust. But amidst the uncertainties of the COVID-19 pandemic, I felt a lingering fear that my immune system was compromised, making me more susceptible than usual. Despite this unease, I couldn't shake off the feeling that I might contract the virus. As it turned out, I did catch COVID-19 a few weeks later.

During this short experiment, I found myself grappling with choices that were crystal clear yet difficult to make. If one decides that a fully plant-based or near-vegan diet isn't sustainable and something must be added, three options present themselves, each with unique justifications: ethically farmed meat (especially beef), wild-caught fish, and conventionally farmed dairy products.

These options aren't exhaustive, but they are readily accessible within an urban or suburban setting in a developed country. Let's assume we're considering one of these as our occasional indulgence. Which should it be? The problem is that these choices don't lend themselves to easy comparison or measurement. This philosophical term, 'incommensurability', refers to the idea that alternatives can't be fairly compared using a single standard. Each choice brings a distinct moral perspective into play.

Let's start with ethically farmed beef (option 1). By this, I mean meat from cows who live good lives overall, and possibly even better lives than most non-human mammals. This isn't your average supermarket meat labeled 'free range', but a select few products from specialized farms that prioritize animal welfare. In many cities, you can now find these options in specialty butcheries. While it can be expensive compared to less humanely produced meat, it's worth considering where feasible.

The cows on these farms live good lives overall. However, the act of slaughter is still inherent to this choice. This brings us to option 2: wild-caught fish. These creatures are harvested sustainably from their natural habitats, minimizing the impact on their ecosystems. Lastly, we have dairy products (option 3), conventionally farmed but offering a different set of ethical considerations. Each choice presents unique moral issues that need careful thought before making a decision.

The concept I'd like to discuss is what I refer to as 'pro-extinctionist views.' While it might sound alarming, a surprising number of philosophers have expressed agreement with this perspective. To set the record straight, pro-extinctionists don't advocate for wanton destruction or disregard for human life. They understand that the prospect of our extinction is horrific and tragic, and they would never wish for billions to perish.

However, what sets pro-extinctionists apart is their belief that the state of Being Extinct may be preferable to the alternative - Being Extant or continuing to exist. This idea can stem from various perspectives, but one compelling argument suggests that if we cease to exist, we would also prevent future human suffering from occurring.

Cosmologist Carl Sagan, who espoused a further-loss view, estimated that if humanity survived on Earth for another 10 million years, over 500 trillion people could exist. If we consider space colonization, philosopher Nick Bostrom calculated the number of biological humans per century within the Virgo Supercluster to be 1 followed by 23 zeros. However, these numbers increase significantly when we factor in digital people living in virtual-reality simulations. Bostrom's calculations found that some 1058 digital people could exist throughout the universe as a whole.

The motivation behind these estimations was the further-loss view that by not existing, we would forfeit the opportunity to create value for all those future people. On the other hand, one could argue that even if these future people live fulfilling lives, the pain they experience throughout their lives could still accumulate to an enormous amount. Conversely, if we go extinct, none of that suffering will materialize.

Another aspect worth considering is the idea that some types of suffering can never be compensated for by happiness. Consider a painful surgical procedure that leaves you bedridden for a month, followed by 50 years of happiness. While you may have found the surgery and recovery process unbearable, you might still consider it 'worth it' due to the joy you experienced afterward.

However, when we look at some of the worst atrocities in the world - child abuse, genocides, ethnic cleansings, massacres - we realize that even decades of happiness may not outweigh the suffering these events entail. If we were to go extinct, none of this pain would come to pass.

These cause vast amounts of suffering before the person dies. Prospective parents impose these risks on the children they create. The magnitude of the risk obviously varies, depending on such factors as one’s geographical and temporal locations, and one’s sex. Even controlling for these variables, the lifetime risks are often difficult to quantify. For example, rape is significantly underreported, but there are conflicting data on just how underreported it is. Similarly, studies on mental diseases such as major depressive disorders often underestimate the lifetime risk, in part because some of the subjects have not yet experienced the depression that will later affect them. Even if we take the low estimates, in the cumulative risks of all the different misfortunes that can befall people, the odds are stacked deeply against any child. The risks of cancer alone are substantial: in the United Kingdom, roughly 50 per cent of people will develop the disease. If people imposed that sort of risk of that sort of harm on others in non-procreative contexts, they would be very widely condemned. The same standards should be applied to procreation. The foregoing arguments all criticise procreation on the grounds of what procreation does to the person who is brought into existence. These I call philanthropic arguments for anti-natalism; there is also a misanthropic argument. What is distinctive about this argument is that it criticises procreation on the grounds of the harm that the created person will (likely) do. It is presumptively wrong to create new beings that are likely to cause significant harm to others. Homo sapiens is the most destructive species, and vast amounts of this destruction are wreaked on other humans. Humans have killed one another since the origin of the species, but the scale (not rate) of killing has expanded (not least because there are now so many more humans to kill than there were for most of human history). The means by which many millions of humans have been killed have been dismally diverse. They include stabbing, hacking, slashing, hanging, gassing, poisoning, drowning, and bombing. Humans also visit other horrors on their fellows, including persecuting, oppressing, beating, branding, maiming, tormenting, torturing, raping, kidnapping, and enslaving. text:
Brea's collection of biota from the Late Pleistocene is truly impressive,' Harris shared with me. 'We've got over 600 species of animals and plants from the last phase of the last Ice Age, and there's more diversity preserved here than any other deposit of similar age.' And the sample size for many of those species is mind-blowing. 'If you wanted to study modern wolves,' Harris said, 'you'd be lucky if a museum had 20. Here, we have over 4,000 dire wolves.'

As the camel grazes and munches on shrubby plants, carbon and nitrogen isotopes from those plants get absorbed into its tissues and bones. Unlike large charismatic megafauna, many of the 600 species we've excavated are still around today, but not in this part of the world. For example, a particular type of snail found in the asphalt is only found in cooler, wetter habitats at elevations of about 5,000ft in California and Arizona. Thanks to La Brea's natural asphalt, we have an incredibly detailed understanding of the plants and animals from that time.

'Asphalt works wonders as a preservative,' Harris explains. In most cases, bones trapped in asphalt are not even mineralized, they are the actual bones of the animals we find. They may look like fossils, but they aren't like stone dinosaur bones. When a museum volunteer uncovers a Smilodon skull, they're actually touching the stained bone of that big cat. Early 20th-century paleontologists would boil the bones in kerosene to remove tar and largely ignored the microfossils found in the sludge from skeletal parts. Today, La Brea researchers take great care to collect every fossil they can find. Each bone is so carefully collected and documented that the entire bonebed can be reconstructed from notes. The matrix surrounding those bones is also labeled so its association with a particular grid square is recorded. This matrix is then treated with biodiesel to reduce many pounds of sediment and asphalt into siftable slurry, which yields small fossils - some of the most important clues in our collection.

Visitors might be drawn to sabercats and mammoths, but it's the small fossils that fill out the ancient environment with a much greater degree of resolution. And even when bones go into the lab, volunteers spend a considerable amount of time picking out those tiny fossils that have been preserved inside skull cavities or in the matrix clinging to bones. 'They keep you chained to your chair until you identify all the little bones,' my friend and Page Museum volunteer Herb Schiff joked as he showed me a dire wolf skull. A pain for volunteers, perhaps, but a treasure trove of information for researchers.

While I would gladly wait an hour in the Phoenix heat for a meal as delicious as this, I couldn't imagine waiting for 4 to 6 hours like some people do. Title: A Fuel Station Turned Coffee Haven - Copper Star on Melrose!

I recently stumbled upon this hidden gem, Copper Star, situated right in the heart of Melrose. I never thought a gas station turned coffee shop would be such a perfect fit! Their locally roasted coffee might not be my absolute favorite, but it's certainly leagues above average here in Phoenix. On those mornings when I'm running late, their drive-thru is a lifesaver!

For those who aren't into coffee, they've got you covered with a variety of chai, tea, smoothies, Italian sodas, and even unique drinks like black cherry limeade. Their food selection is impressive too. There's a wide array of baked goods, including some of the best bagels around, and decent lunch options such as sandwiches and a tasty hummus platter. But where they really shine is their homemade cupcakes. I've even spotted Mayor Gordon here on several occasions indulging in his sweet fix!

Despite being housed in a converted gas station/garage, the atmosphere is surprisingly cozy. The sandblasted block walls and exposed ceiling give a nod to its industrial past, but the rotating art work, comfortable couches, easy chairs, small bistro tables, and long 'work' tables offer a pleasant contrast. The staff is friendly and helpful, with owner Bill acting as a great neighborhood resource - he knows everything happening along 7th Ave!

However, I would caution against using this place for serious work requiring internet access, as the wifi can be quite intermittent at best. But for grabbing a coffee and cupcake, meeting a friend, or getting some old-fashioned reading done, Copper Star is an excellent choice!

When faced with the challenge of composing an ethical rulebook that applies universally to every situation, we quickly realize that what constitutes a moral justification can differ significantly depending on context. For instance, something that increases happiness may not always be a valid reason to act upon it. Consider the example of a sadist  their pleasure from inflicting pain might actually argue against an act of cruelty, rather than for it. Conversely, denying someone happiness might sometimes be morally right, as in situations requiring 'tough love.' The key lies in understanding the intent behind actions; someone who seems unkind may indeed be acting out of kindness, and it is our task to discern this.

Happiness itself is a complex concept that continues to intrigue psychologists and happiness researchers. Early utilitarians like Bentham held a simplistic view of happiness as pleasure, but more recent investigators propose a multifaceted perspective, with several factors contributing differently to an individual's overall happiness. This aligns well with the particularist view that evaluation is always holistic in nature.

Aristotle's concept of eudaimonia, often translated to 'happiness,' was broader and less subjective than mere pleasure or momentary satisfaction. It encompassed the general quality of one's life as a whole. For particularists, individual perception and judgment are crucial in resolving ethical dilemmas; there is no universal ethical system that can replace our own wisdom and discernment.

While principles might provide practical guidelines, they are not infallible. They offer useful rules of thumb, but exceptions always exist. At the core, ethics stems from individuals who possess character, judgment, and wisdom  qualities that modern society and even philosophical schools like Kantian and utilitarian ethics struggle to acknowledge seriously. Wisdom, as distinct from knowledge, remains an elusive concept that demands our attention and respect.

The price, which broke the record for a Monet painting at auction, was more than six times what Marsico paid for it in 2002, proving that few investments offer a more buoyant rate of return than a Monet. Star performances by Monet in the auction houses of London and New York have become routine. Yet the exorbitant prices paid for his paintings today don’t stand in any kind of chastening counterpoint to Monet’s commercial fortunes during his own lifetime. The stories of the critical resistance to Impressionism – and consequent financial hardships of the movement’s painters – are, of course, the stuff of popular legend. To be sure, Monet did suffer some early hardships. ‘We have enough of that kind of painting,’ sniffed a juror in 1868, rejecting his works for the annual Paris Salon and thereby scuppering his chances of making sales. That same year, Monet was (no doubt wisely) refused credit for paint, and he supposedly spent one entire winter eating nothing but potatoes. For much of his career, though, he was no starving artist, and the diet of potatoes soon came to be replaced by such favourites as foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Périgord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. ‘You get the best cuisine in France at his house,’ enthused one visitor treated to the delights of gastronomy chez Monet. The fact is that, after the age of 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was unrelentingly flush with cash: a happy outcome for such a bon vivant. His wealth kept him in a fleet of automobiles, tweed suits from a fine English tailor in Paris, and a sizeable staff of gardeners to tend his famous gardens. In this respect, he has more in common with Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their high-octane dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than he does with artistic outliers such as the penniless Vincent van Gogh. His surname is an apt one, as headline writers and other punsters appreciate, enjoying its near-homonymity with ‘money’. He was, in fact, the Jazz Age’s answer to Koons, because in 1922, four years before his death, the sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world’s most expensive living artist. Monet has long been the go-to artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, the hotelier Steve Wynn, and assorted Russian and Asian billionaires. text:
We've all experienced it - that moment of revelation, that spark of insight, born from the discomfort provided by the contrarian thinkers among us. In his book On Liberty (1859), John Stuart Mill even goes so far as to praise eccentricity. It is these 'eccentrics', he suggests, who keep our world vibrant with their courageous ideas and innovative perspectives.

'Eccentricity has always abounded where strength of character has abounded,' Mill writes. In essence, the more eccentrics there are, the better off we are - morally, intellectually, and spiritually. The world needs these fearless thinkers who dare to question the status quo, who refuse to conform to societal norms and expectations.

Contrarians possess a unique quality of thinking that sets them apart from the rest of us. They remain on the outskirts, observing how groups define themselves and how they wield power through exclusion and marginalization. This position allows them to see things more clearly, to challenge the herd mentality, and to articulate their unorthodox views with conviction.

In an ideal world, public intellectuals would embody this quality of opposition. However, in practice, very few do. The vigor of their dissent, the force of their language, and the seriousness of their commitment make contrarians formidable figures. They are not simply attention-seekers or provocateurs; they are genuine critics of society who refuse to compromise their beliefs.

What sets genuine contrarians apart from mere provocateurs is their intellectual duty and inner conviction. For them, challenging the establishment is not a means to seek attention or entertain, but a matter of principle. Their radical thinking, iconoclasm, and separation from society give them access to a higher truth - one that our society may be uncomfortable hearing, but ultimately needs to embrace.

Contrarians don't follow trends or conform to authorities; they have little patience for the rituals of the establishment. Since they have cut themselves off from their tribe, they can see things as they truly are. Spinoza, for example, found his philosophical enlightenment only after being expelled from his community.

In conclusion, contrarians serve a crucial role in society by challenging the status quo and pushing us to question our beliefs and values. Their radical thinking, innovative ideas, and fearless dissent remind us that there is always room for growth and improvement - even if it means stepping outside the circle drawn in the sand.

text:
The emergence of sentient beings brings about two potential paths: one may choose to disregard tales of self-improvement and continue amassing 'karmic' baggage, or one can opt to live by these narratives and strive to minimize 'karmic' burdens. Ultimately, the choice lies with you. In this journey, a common yet often unanswered question arises: what is this dharma that these stories speak of?

To answer this question isn't an easy feat. Even the Mahabharata, an epic tale as vast as an ocean, reminds us that 'the essence of dharma is concealed within a cave!' Vishnu Sukhtankar, the first editor of The Critical Edition of The Mahabharata (1944), acknowledged this when he said, "I won't attempt to give you another perfect definition of Dharma, a task which... has taxed better brains than mine." However, self-aware humility hasn't stopped scholars or laypeople from expressing their opinions.

One traditional answer is to trace the etymological origins of 'dharma'. The root in Sanskrit language is dhr-, meaning 'to hold', 'to support', or 'to maintain'. This term first appeared in the Rigveda, a vast collection of religious poetry compiled between 1400 BCE and 1000 BCE. In Book 8, we find one of its earliest meanings: Him [the God Indra], the double lofty, whose power holds together the two halves of the world - mountains and plains, waters and sun - through his strength. Here, 'dharma' refers to the act of preventing the world from falling into chaos.

However, 'dharma', like a chameleon, has evolved over time, even within a single text. Stephanie Jamison, co-translator of the monumental Oxford University Press Rigveda (2014), once wrote that while 'porridge' evokes Goldilocks for many, 'oatmeal' doesn't share the same cultural reference. Similarly, while dhrman appears 63 times in the Rigveda and was relatively stable at that stage in its history, this hasn't eased the struggle of modern translators, given that at least three millennia of meanings have seeped into the term since those verses were first penned.

In 1928, the German linguist Karl Friedrich Geldner translated the Rigveda and used no fewer than 20 different renderings - from 'law', 'order', 'duty', 'custom', 'quality' - to describe dharma in various contexts. In a recent conversation with Bibek Debroy, translator of the epic 10-volume Penguin edition of the Mahabharata (2015), he noted that "The basis of Sanskrit is the Rigveda."

text:
Quantum computing, with its potential for unimaginable power, is an intriguing field that has captivated the minds of many. However, it's not all sunshine and roses; maintaining the delicate balance of quantum states in a quantum computer is no walk in the park, consuming vast amounts of energy to keep things running smoothly. We're still unsure about what the actual energy-computation relationship for quantum computing will look like.

But it's not just energy consumption that gives us pause. A quantum computer with 'n' qubits can perform 2^n computations in one go, which sounds fantastic, but setting up these computations could turn into a data flow nightmare. For instance, simulating our Universe, with its approximately 10^89 particles and photons, might only need 296 qubits, theoretically speaking. However, entering the initial conditions for all those particles is a challenge few would relish. And if that's not daunting enough, sifting through the results of such a simulation to find the correct solutions is a task straight out of science fiction.

Simulating a human brain might be a tad easier, but even then, you'd need to account for and initialize at least 10^14 neural connections - a number roughly equivalent to those found in a human brain. And if we want our quantum brain to interact with the world around us effectively, we'll need to crack another nut: creating a high-throughput, high-fidelity sensory interface. This is another unknown that may prove too steep a challenge to overcome.

I must admit, I'm oversimplifying things. The techniques and technological tricks that could be employed are vast and varied. But my own perspective might still be too narrow. Nonetheless, I think it's important to approach the optimistic predictions of artificial intelligence with caution. While we may be able to construct machinery capable of matching or surpassing human intelligence, it's questionable whether it would lead to exponential growth in computing power as often proposed. In essence, while the math behind exponentially improving machine intelligence might be sound, the practical barriers could prove insurmountable.

To delve deeper into this, let me indulge in a bit of speculation - something futurists are quite fond of. Suppose an intelligent cosmic entity manages to convert itself into a machine form or is overtaken by its super-smart, yet not exponentially superior, machine creations. In that case, their future actions could be influenced significantly by efficiency limits. These machines might find themselves looking back at their past for new avenues of progress. One thing they would know is that biology works exceptionally well. Some estimates suggest that the modern human brain has reached its computational limit, but a slightly more intelligent machine might be able to re-engineer such a complex organ. In other words, there could be potential in merging biological systems with advanced technology for future advancements.

This is just speculation, of course. But it's an interesting thought experiment that sheds light on the challenges we might face in our quest for artificial intelligence. It also serves as a reminder that while we may dream of exponential growth, practical constraints could prove to be formidable obstacles.
