text:
Graham Bateson's journey of self-discovery began with a letter to his father in 1925, a year after his parents convinced him to call off his engagement to a Swiss woman. She wasn't English, she wasn't a scientist - and thus, she didn't fit the mold they had set for him. As compensation, he was sent to the Galpagos Islands to replicate the work of Charles Darwin from a century prior. However, it was the inhabitants that captivated Bateson, not the fauna. In the letter, he pleaded with his father not to be disappointed, expressing his desire to abandon 'ordinary, impersonal science' and instead study people, anthropology.

A decade later, in the opening pages of Naven, he lamented that while artists could capture a society's emotional essence in a few pages, anthropologists limited themselves to the technical aspects of its organization. Bateson aimed to bridge this gap, blending science with art. As a scientist, he sought to delve into the artistic realms and do science in an artistic manner. Thus, Bateson found himself at the forefront of a movement that challenged the traditional scientific approach, particularly the notion that the observer is completely separate from the subject being observed. However, this mindset was deeply ingrained in Western civilization. 'There are moments,' he mused, 'when I catch myself thinking there's something that's apart from something else.'

Naven takes its name from a series of strange rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which perplexed Bateson for years. In 1927, he ventured to the South Pacific with the intention of exploring the relationship between individuality and culture, hoping to debunk the prevailing British anthropological model formulated by Alfred Radcliffe-Brown. This model viewed human societies as biological organisms, where individual behavior could be entirely explained by the needs of the whole. Bateson found this rigid determinism stifling and unconvincing, particularly because it didn't account for conflict between individuals and ignored aesthetics.

However, once among the tribes of the region, he was at a loss as to what to observe or how to proceed. Other anthropologists used elaborate questionnaires and aggressive interrogation methods, but Bateson abhorred any interference in others' lives and believed that questionnaires implied one already knew the crucial questions. In 1929, he settled along the Sepik River, alone with the Iatmul, a people who had recently given up cannibalism and built their culture around 'a constant emphasis on the spectacular.'

It didn't take long for Bateson to realize that the belligerent atmosphere within the tribe was affecting his own personality. He admitted that he had become 'rougher in my methods.'

As of 0211 GMT, U.S. crude was up 5 cents to $40.25 per barrel, having reached a peak of $40.55 earlier in the day. The front-month contract added 1 cent to trade at $41.55. Thursday's closing price saw an increase of $1.21 to $41.54 per barrel, after earlier reaching a yearly high of $41.60 - a level revisited earlier on Friday.

In a note, BNP Paribas analysts stated that the oil market's renewed appetite has surged more than 50 percent from its December lows.

The optimism stems from the prospect of key oil-producing countries reaching an agreement to address output levels in a dialogue, although it is widely acknowledged that any decision reached will likely only amount to a freeze, not a reduction, in production. OPEC members, spearheaded by Russia, are scheduled to meet on April 17 in Doha with the aim of forging the first global supply deal in 15 years.

text: Russell could be nasty. But in fact, he was not entirely dismissive of pragmatism. He respected Peirce, who, like Russell himself, was an outstanding logician. Russell learned a lot about Peirce in 1914, while visiting Harvard to give the Lowell Lectures. Peirce was the talk of the department while Russell was there, partly because he died in the middle of Russell’s visit. Russell was asked, and agreed, to return to Harvard to teach a seminar on Peirce and to edit the first volume of his posthumously collected papers, but the First World War intervened, with Russell ending up in jail for his anti-war activities. But he remained interested. His The Analysis of Mind (1921) explored, in a positive way, the pragmatist idea that belief is a disposition to behave. Moreover, the young and brilliant Frank Ramsey, who came on the Cambridge philosophical scene in the early 1920s, was a card-carrying pragmatist. He had pragmatist leanings from the beginning of his undergraduate days, encouraged he said, by Russell. In 1923, he read the first volume of Peirce’s posthumously collected papers and his pragmatism grew in strength and scope. Importantly, Ramsey is the analytic philosopher’s philosopher, making stunning contributions to the philosophy of language, logic and truth theory. Ramsey picked up on the pragmatist idea that we should not try vainly to offer a proof of, say, induction, but rather, we should vindicate it by showing how it is essential to practice. He also gave pragmatist or agent-centred treatments of probability, causality and scientific laws. He argued, with Peirce, that belief is a habit of action, and employed that idea in figuring out how to measure partial belief and formalise choice. He was attracted to Peirce’s account of truth and died (at age 26) in the middle of writing a book that was trying to work that out. Pragmatist arguments and concerns pervade the analytic philosophy that reigned in Oxford and Cambridge In 1929, the year before he died – and the year that Wittgenstein returned to Cambridge after his self-imposed exile as a school teacher in Austria – Ramsey pressed his pragmatist arguments against Wittgenstein’s Tractarian theory that language pictures the world, and that what cannot be said in Wittgenstein’s logical language is a kind of nonsense, sometimes of mystical significance. Wittgenstein’s later philosophy, formulated in his Philosophical Investigations (1953) and other writings, is also arguably a kind of pragmatism with its emphasis on the primacy of practice and meaning as use. As Wittgenstein acknowledged, he was heavily influenced by Ramsey. In subsequent decades, these two pragmatist approaches – Ramsey’s and the later Wittgenstein’s – led very different lives in British philosophy. Wittgenstein was famously influential, especially in Oxford. Ramsey was quietly influential, especially in Cambridge. Generations of Cambridge philosophers rediscovered his insights, often piecemeal and usually without an appreciation of where Ramsey himself had got them. Nonetheless, pragmatist theses, arguments

text: In assigning consciousness to something, we can never fully bridge the knowledge gap. Yet, Wittgenstein questions: if these 'automata' are pretending to feel pain, for instance, how do you truly know they're mimicking it? For Wittgenstein, criteria don't guarantee certainty in statements but guide our usage of the concepts within them. Our ability to label their actions as pain is merely a reflection of our linguistic capabilities. If all the criteria are met, there's nothing more you're missing - what matters is whether you recognize them as someone in distress. As Cavell writes, 'acknowledgement can never be stretched by knowledge.' Similarly, your own mind, feelings, aren't inherently hidden from understanding by another. This relationship is delicate, not because of some mystical barrier, but because some people fail to acknowledge the humanity of others. Wittgenstein observes a 'proto-Levinasian moment', where a Self encounters the Other. In this encounter, our level of acknowledgment shapes our attitude towards them. This attitude is inherently ethical for two reasons: firstly, it defines the boundaries of our obligations to another. Secondly, denying another's humanity means denying the very shared foundation upon which we try to communicate or express anything at all. This, in turn, implies the lack of meaningful basis even for establishing ourselves as subjects. As Cavell puts it, 'I am bound to stand to myself in the relationships I may or may not stand to others.' It's only by acknowledging the Other that the Self can truly become human. In Wittgenstein's Investigations, his examples are educational. The most dramatic encounter is the one we examined: the case of the nonconforming student (described earlier in 185 of the Investigations). As a teacher, Wittgenstein knows that the heart of the teacher-student relationship arises when the student does something unexpected and against our instructions. This pupil doesn't conform to our expectations. They don't share our perspective. So, what do we do with them? In his Brown Book notes, which anticipate the Investigations, Wittgenstein humorously remarks: "If a child does not respond to a suggestive gesture, society typically responds by..."

text: In 1696, Damaris Cudworth Masham, an Englishwoman and a reluctant philosopher, stepped from obscurity to publish a book whose title – A Discourse Concerning the Love of God – concealed the feminist gems within. For instance, she insisted, contrary to some philosophers and theologians of her day, that mothers were not corrupting forces but foundational to the pursuit of knowledge. Then in 1705 she entered the public sphere again with another work, more radical than the first, titled Occasional Thoughts in Reference to a Vertuous or Christian Life, in which she argued that women should contribute to all intellectual subjects: ‘I see no Reason why it should not be thought that all Science lyes as open to a Lady as to a Man.’ Masham was also close to the Enlightenment philosopher John Locke. Their friendship endured for almost 20 years. It was an intellectual, personal and at times romantic exchange that began before her marriage and endured after Locke moved into her home with her husband and children. No need to write letters when you can share ideas near the fire in the evenings. This was a period of great philosophical flourishing for Masham, during which she wrote her two and only books. If it were not for Masham’s affiliation with Locke, I’m certain that these two books would have been completely lost to us. In fact, she probably wouldn’t have written them. I resisted exploring her close bond with Locke when I wrote my dissertation about her ideas years ago. At the time, it seemed the right thing to do. I wanted to treat Masham’s body of work as I would have treated any male philosopher’s: independent of his private life. I eventually realised my mistake. No person’s work exists in a vacuum, and by acknowledging Masham’s relationship to Locke – he broke her heart yet encouraged her philosophical work – I gained a deeper appreciation of her oeuvre, as well as her experience of being a woman in philosophy. When I finally sat down to read her letters to Locke, I was not in a good place. I was in a relationship with a philosopher a few years older than myself, a man who had initially been my teaching fellow, then my lover, and eventually also my colleague – and to whom I frequently looked for validation. I’d pursued a career in philosophy with the dream of living a life of enquiry, but I didn’t expect it to be such a boys’ club or that I’d be managing chronic self-doubt and self-censorship. As I read Masham’s correspondence, I realised that I, too, had a John Locke – that is, a man in philosophy on whom I depended for a sense of belonging in the field. text: thought that someone would waste her time getting a manicure. Or think of the manicured socialite who can’t see the value of dedicating one’s life to dusty Latin manuscripts. Whatever he’s into, the moralising jerk exudes a continuous aura of disdain for everything else. Furthermore, mercy is near the heart of practical, lived morality. Virtually everything that everyone does falls short of perfection: one’s turn of phrase is less than perfect, one arrives a bit late, one’s clothes are tacky, one’s gesture irritable, one’s choice somewhat selfish, one’s coffee less than frugal, one’s melody trite. Practical mercy involves letting these imperfections pass forgiven or, better yet, entirely unnoticed. In contrast, the jerk appreciates neither others’ difficulties in attaining all the perfections that he attributes to himself, nor the possibility that some portion of what he regards as flawed is in fact blameless. Hard moralising principle therefore comes naturally to him. (Sympathetic mercy is natural to the sweetheart.) And on the rare occasions when the jerk is merciful, his indulgence is usually ill-tuned: the flaws he forgives are exactly the one he recognises in himself or has ulterior reasons to let slide. The interplay between components and wholes, along with the concept of shape or gestalt, has been a significant topic in thought. Ernst Mach, an influential scientist-philosopher, once described a gestalt as something greater than just sensory perception itself. A melody, for instance, is made up of individual notes but it's the way these notes are arranged with order and rhythm that defines its essence. Building on Mach's ideas, Ehrenfels made the study of these 'gestalts' central to his research, a move that would prove groundbreaking in psychology.

In everyday language, we often say that the whole is more than just the sum of its parts. However, the challenge was to pinpoint what exactly sets the whole apart from a simple collection of its constituent pieces. Using a melody as an example, Ehrenfels demonstrated how it remains recognizable despite changes in pitch or tone. So, if every note has been altered, what is it that makes a melody distinct? It appears to be something relational that binds the parts together.

Ehrenfels' work was deeply rooted in Mach's 'Analysis of Sensation' published in 1886. In a letter to a friend, Ehrenfels acknowledged sending Mach his paper "Gestalt Qualities" and receiving a friendly response from Mach, who noted that he had previously discussed these ideas in 1865. This seems like an unusually humble acknowledgement of intellectual continuity on Ehrenfels' part, but it seemed to be brushed off by Mach with a casual wave.

Ehrenfels, interestingly, was Max Wertheimer's first teacher. Wertheimer went on to study in Berlin under Stumpf and then in Wrzburg under Oswald Klpe, where he completed his degree. Later, as a graduate student, Wertheimer developed gestalt psychology, which proved more successful than any preceding school of thought in highlighting the dynamic principles that organize our perceived world.

Ehrenfels played a crucial role in emphasizing the logical gap between perceiving individual parts and perceiving the whole, a difference that can subjectively be far more significant than the parts themselves. Years after Ehrenfels was his student, Ehrenfels sent Brentano a letter containing language that seems to come from something he wrote during his student years. The prose is direct:

"Regarding our relationship..."

Perhaps this passage is a quotation from something Ehrenfels penned during his time as a student. It's certainly frank, especially for a letter written to a former teacher.

text:
Simone Bespaloff's spirit oscillated between fury and peace during her time in New York. Despite being in exile, she managed to maintain ties with Parisian intellectual circles through letter exchanges with Fessard and Marcel. A chance opportunity led her to work for the Voice of Americas French broadcast before moving on to teach French literature at Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts. This small liberal arts college became a significant beacon for French culture during the war, hosting gatherings of exiled scholars orchestrated by Wahl. It was here that Bespaloff crossed paths with luminaries such as Jacques Maritain, Andr Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lvi-Strauss. Mary McCarthy, who translated her works, once described Bespaloff as a "small, dark lady who wore white gloves." Hannah Arendt, who visited in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka, also found Bespaloff intriguing. In her essay on Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, Arendt's interpretation mirrored Bespaloff's existentialist melancholy. Under the ominous veil of war, Arendt depicted humanity as tragically ensnared within the tangled threads of history. The reality of Kafka's "nightmarish world" had become a grim reality.

In an essay on Camus, her last published work, Bespaloff pondered how history compelled her generation to live in a climate of brutal death. Post-war, despite initially being celebrated by them, Bespaloff became a critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a 1946 letter to musicologist Boris de Schloezer, she asserted that Sartre's rejection of "magical interiority" in favor of subjectivity was not the foundation of a new humanism but a precursor to conformity. She argued that instead of liberating the individual, Sartre's existentialism obliterated this magical interiority, which enables authentic human connection. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the scrutiny of the Other. This objectified 'subjectivity strikingly resembled American "individualism," which unleashed itself in action to conceal its void.' The United States felt dull and hostile to Bespaloff, much like Helen's Troy.

Bespaloff's journey to Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. During a term break, in April 1949, for reasons still shrouded in mystery, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her complex symphony of life ended tragically. In her final note, alluding to Camus's claim, she wrote: "One might imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy remains forever elusive."

text: of penile bells and whistles, including spines, penis bones or two heads, sometimes all in the same animal. Given this variety, one question that burdened the world of genitalia researchers until recently was how many times the penis had evolved in amniotes. The only obvious fact was that its presence was a constant work-in-progress. Birds offer a great example, with only 3 per cent having penises – ducks most famously so, with their ballistically everted corkscrew versions – with the rest using a structure that doesn’t do much of anything, or no penis at all. In vertebrate species without a penis that still rely on internal transfer of gametes, the solution involves the cloaca, which is Latin for ‘sewer’. This all-purpose structure passes the fluid, including semen, that exits the organism. Animals can use the cloacal kiss, the bringing of the two ‘sewers’ together, to achieve transfer of semen. Among reptiles, cloacal kisses are not common. In fact, the only reptile that uses a cloacal kiss is the tuatara, the sole representative of an ancient lineage that traces back to the time of the dinosaurs. The absence of a penis from the tuatara, considered to sit at the base of the amniote family tree, implies that some later lineage evolved the appendage. But with the array of apparent differences in penises across amniote species, how many times did the penis evolve anew? The answers had been all over the place, but rarely was the proposed response ‘once’. The tuatara – or at least, some very old samples of tuatara embryos – resolved it all. In the early 20th century, Arthur Dendy, a zoologist working in New Zealand, sent some eggs collected from local tuatara nests to a US museum. There, the specimens were processed and then lost to time for decades. But a serendipitous recovery of these samples many years later revealed that the embryos were just the right sex at just the right developmental timepoint to see whether these animals initiate a starter penis before dispensing with it, much as human embryos begin making a tail that later disappears. After researchers digitally rebuilt the tuatara samples, they could see that, at the embryonic region where penises begin, the tuatara embryo did start to develop a penis. As living tuatara born around the time that these eggs were collected demonstrate – they can live for up to a century – the starter penis ends up being a nonstarter. With this evidence from the tuatara, researchers had the proof they needed that a penis – whether it survived the embryo stage or not – sat at the base of the amniote family tree. The programme for making a penis had evolved only once for our kind. The process for building one was present in the tuatara, with the same timing as in other amniotes, using the same starting materials as ours.

LONDON - The Trent Bridge ground in Nottinghamshire witnessed a historic day on Monday, as the English One Day Cup match between Notts and Northamptonshire produced a record-breaking performance that statisticians are still verifying may be unparalleled anywhere in the world.

The batting extravaganza began from the outset, with Nottinghamshire's Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb shattering the best English one-day partnership record by amassing a staggering 146 runs off just 97 deliveries. Lumb went on to set another personal best and a new county record for Nottinghamshire with his 184, as they posted an impressive total of 445/8 - the second-highest score in a List A match worldwide.

In response, Northamptonshire mounted a valiant effort to chase down what would have been another record for a team batting second. However, they fell short despite Rory Kleinveldt's impressive 128, with their total of 425 giving Nottinghamshire a hard-fought 20-run victory. The day saw an astonishing 870 runs scored in total, surpassing the previous record of 868 runs set by Surrey and Glamorgan in 2002.

After the match, Lumb joked: "It was a nail-biting finish. What a fantastic game of cricket! Never in doubt." He added, "I was surprised they took it that deep, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was incredible. It was an incredible game. I don't think we could have done much more. I'm proud of all the boys."

The Nottinghamshire partnership surpassed the previous English record of 318 set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. Lumb and Wessels' record-breaking stand is now the third-highest partnership in List A history, behind only two other performances - one set by Gloucestershire in 2007.

List A matches are contested over 40 to 60 overs.

text:
It's a shame that this winter, with its blankets of snow, isn't accompanied by some humility from us humans - a deeper connection to the planet we call home. Snowy owls aren't toys or characters in fantasy stories; they're messengers from places many of us will never visit.

Sure, technology has made the Earth feel smaller and more accessible. From our cozy homes, we can virtually trek the rugged slopes of Ben Nevis, mingle with elephants across the vast Serengeti, or float down the Colorado River beneath the Grand Canyon's vibrant walls. But let's face it, the virtual is no substitute for the real experience. Most of us will never see a pod of beluga whales breaking through icy Arctic waters or caribou migrating across expansive tundra. Polar bears won't be paying us a visit this winter, nor any other. Instead, we have snowy owls. From the remnants of wild places, no such messengers will come.

Once, tallgrass prairies covered the American Great Plains, providing a home for ornate wildflowers and grasses as tall as us. But now, they're mostly gone, swallowed up by plows and pavement. Bison won't be venturing from the remaining patches to the Rust Belt, nor will the regal fritillary butterfly, with its chestnut and peach wings adorned with glowing silver spots, depart its dwindling habitats to visit our suburbs and shopping malls.

No ornate butterfly fish, as bright as a snowy owl's eyes, will swim from the coral reefs to remind us of the biodiversity we're losing. Coral reefs, once teeming with life for millions around the world, are now critically endangered - victims of overfishing, pollution, and ocean acidification. No arm of coral from the Great Barrier Reef will visit the Northeast this winter.

Maybe snowy owls can represent those other earth-bound emissaries for us. Whether we watch them through binoculars or on a screen, perhaps they can remind us of the world - from the Highlands to the Serengeti, from the Arctic to the ocean. Or at least, they might help us find humility. Over a century ago, Aldo Leopold found his humility in another predator. In his early years as a forest ranger, the great conservationist and philosopher rarely passed a day without feeling a sense of connection to the natural world around him.

We need that same connection today - not just to appreciate the beauty of nature, but to understand our role in preserving it for future generations. Snowy owls may be messengers from distant lands, but they're also reminders of what we stand to lose if we don't act now to protect our planet.

The leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru demonstrated a dedication to some Pan-Asian ideals that resonated strongly at the Bandung Conference of 1955. This gathering of 29 Asian and African nations, representing more than half the world's population, was the final significant manifestation of Asian unity, eventually overshadowed by Cold War tensions and nation-building projects. Pan-Islamism has experienced a series of ebbs and flows over the past century. From Turkey and Egypt to Indonesia and Algeria, the concept of Muslim intellect and global Muslim solidarity served as a powerful tool for 20th-century nationalist leaders and movements. By the mid-1960s, most of the world's Muslims had shaken off the yoke of European colonial rule. The Turkish Parliament had abolished the Ottoman caliphate back in 1924, and by the 1950s, its influence had waned significantly. Yet, nearly half a century into the 21st century, Pan-Africanism and Pan-Asianism seem to have faded away, while Pan-Islamism and the concept of Muslim world unity persist. Why is this so?

The explanation lies in the latter stages of the Cold War. In the 1980s, a new form of Muslim internationalism emerged as part of the rise of political Islam. This wasn't a clash between the primordial civilizational traditions of Islam and the West, or a reassertion of authentic religious values. It wasn't even a continuation of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism; rather, it was a new creation born out of the Cold War. A Saudi-US alliance began promoting the idea of Muslim solidarity in the 1970s as an alternative to the secular Pan-Arabism of Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser, whose country aligned with the Soviet Union. Any visions of an 'Islamic' utopia would have faltered if not for the failures of many post-colonial nation-states and the subsequent disillusionment of many Muslims.

The idea that Pan-Islamism embodies authentic, ancient, repressed Muslim political values in revolt against global Westernization and secularization was initially a paranoid concern of Western colonial officers, but it has more recently been espoused by Islamists. Western analysts and journalists have erred in accepting Islamist claims about Islam's inherent political values at face value. The kind of Islamism associated with Egypt's Muslim Brotherhood or Ruhollah Khomeini's Iran did not exist before the 1970s. Neither the Indian Muslims meeting Wilson, nor the late Ottoman-era caliphs, sought to impose Sharia in their societies. On the contrary, the first Pan-Islamist generation was highly modernistic: they advocated for women's liberation, racial equality, and cosmopolitanism. Indian Muslims, for example, were proud that the Ottoman caliph had Greek and Armenian ministers and ambassadors. They also wanted to see the British Crown appointing Hindu and Muslim ministers and high-level officials in their governments. None would have desired or anticipated the division of Turks and Greeks in Ottoman lands, Arabs and Jews in Palestine, or Muslims and Hindus in India. Only the most basic form of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism remains today.

Biologists and philosophers lined up to give the malefactor a kicking. His ideas were ‘outdated’, complained some. Another wrote: ‘I regret the appearance of this book.’ Steven Pinker sneered at ‘the shoddy reasoning of a once-great thinker’. The Guardian called it ‘the most despised science book of 2012’. So what made everyone so angry? The thinker was Thomas Nagel, the book was Mind and Cosmos, and the idea was teleology. In ancient science (or, as it used to be called, natural philosophy), teleology held that things — in particular, living things — had a natural end, or telos, at which they aimed. The acorn, Aristotle said, sprouted and grew into a seedling because its purpose was to become a mighty oak. Sometimes, teleology seemed to imply an intention to pursue such an end, if not in the organism then in the mind of a creator. Right away, it’s important to be clear about this position: virtually all pro-extinctionists accept the default view. They would agree that a catastrophic end to humanity would be horrible and tragic, and that we should try to avoid this. No sane person would want billions to die. However, pro-extinctionists would add that the outcome of Being Extinct would nonetheless be better than Being Extant – ie, continuing to exist. Why? There are many possible answers. One is that, by no longer existing, we would prevent future human suffering from existing too, which would be less bad, or more good, than our current situation. There are several ways of thinking about this. The first concerns the possibility that the future contains huge amounts of human suffering. According to the cosmologist Carl Sagan – who defended a further-loss view in his writings – if humanity survives for another 10 million years on Earth, there could come to exist some 500 trillion people. This number is much larger if we colonise space: the philosopher Nick Bostrom in 2003 put the figure at 1023 (that’s a 1 followed by 23 zeros) biological humans per century within the Virgo Supercluster, a large agglomeration of galaxies that includes our own Milky Way, although the number rises to 1038 if we include digital people living in virtual-reality computer simulations. Subsequent number-crunching in Bostrom’s book Superintelligence (2014) found that some 1058 digital people could exist within the Universe as a whole. What motivated these calculations from Sagan and others was the further-loss view that the nonexistence of all these future people, and hence all the value they could have created, is a major opportunity cost of Being Extinct. However, one could flip this around and argue that, even if these future people have lives that are overall worthwhile, the suffering they will unavoidably experience during the course of these lives could still add up to be, in absolute terms, very large. However, if we go extinct, none of this suffering will exist. The second consideration is based on the idea, which I find very plausible, that there are some kinds of suffering that no amount of happiness could ever counterbalance. Imagine, for example, that you need to undergo a painful surgical procedure that will leave you bedridden for a month. You go through with the surgery and, after recovering, you live another 50 very happy years. The surgery and recovery process might have been dreadful – not something you’d want to relive – but you might still say that it was ‘worth it’. In other words, the decades of happiness you experienced after surgery counterbalanced the pain you had to endure. Title: The Ongoing Struggle for Worker Rights in the Persian Gulf Region

The recent changes in business regulation within Qatar have drawn mixed reactions. Critics argue that these reforms merely skim the surface of a much deeper issue. Similarly, the UAE's efforts to educate employers on issues like racism, diversity, and inclusion regarding migrant labor are viewed skeptically as potential image-enhancement measures rather than genuine steps towards change.

Similar criticisms have been levied against the UAE's reforms in its treatment of migrant workers. Some argue that these changes are merely cosmetic, intended to improve the country's global standing rather than addressing the systemic issues at hand. The anticipated abolition of Saudi Arabia's kafala labor system in 2021 offers a glimmer of hope, but it doesn't erase the grim reality Finley described as a "morally reprehensible system of human exploitation" - six modern-day slave societies thriving in the Persian Gulf.

For genuine abolition to occur, we must address and eliminate the roots of this exploitative system. These include racialization of labor and rampant worker abuse and exploitation. It's encouraging to see the launch of the Ijm on Slavery website, which aims to gather a consensus among Islamic scholars that slavery and slave trading are illegal under Islamic law.

Each of these Persian Gulf city-states is governed by Muslim governments. I encourage Muslim scholars within these communities to join this consensus, helping to eradicate this morally reprehensible system of human exploitation in our region.

text: Here is a true story. A young philosophy lecturer — let us call him Shane — is charged with the task of introducing young minds to the wonders of philosophy. His course, a standard Introduction to Philosophy, contains a section on the philosophy of religion: the usual arguments-for-and-against-the-existence-of-God stuff. One of Shane’s students complains to Shane’s Dean that his cherished religious beliefs are being attacked. ‘I have a right to my beliefs,’ the student claims. Shane’s repeated interrogations of those beliefs amounts to an attack on this right to believe. Shane’s institution is not a particularly enlightened one. The Dean concurs with the student, and instructs Shane to desist in teaching philosophy of religion. But what exactly does it mean to claim ‘a right to my beliefs’? It often comes up in a religious context, but can arise in others too. Shane could just as easily be teaching Marxist theory to a laissez-faire capitalist student, or imparting evidence for global warming to a global warming sceptic. Whatever the context, the claim of a right to one’s beliefs is a curious one. We might distinguish two different interpretations of this claim. First, there is the evidential one. You have an evidential right to your belief if you can provide appropriate evidence in support of it. I have, in this sense, no right to believe that the moon is made of green cheese because my belief is lacking in any supporting evidence. This sort of right can’t be what Shane’s student is asserting. After all, the arguments Shane was asking his students to explore were, precisely, evidence for and against the existence of God. When the student complained, he did so to preclude this gathering and examination of evidence. He regarded the very examination of this evidence as an attack on his right to believe — and so can hardly be talking about his evidential right to believe. Instead, the student’s assertion seems to be what we might call a moral right to believe. The student is asserting that he has a moral right to believe what he will, even if there is not sufficient evidence to establish that belief — indeed, even if the preponderance of the available evidence suggests the belief is false. This moral right to believe is a truly curious beast. We can have moral rights to different sorts of things — most obviously, to commodities (food, shelter), freedoms (of thought, expression, pursuit of happiness) and treatments (non-discrimination). But what exactly, does it mean to have a moral right to any of these things? While many people claim not to understand the notion of a right, the idea is really very simple. text:
Putnam and David Kaplan challenged the prevailing notion, suggesting that numerous terms don't refer by matching objects with a mental checklist, but rather directly. According to Kripke, when we introduce a proper name, it's often tied directly to an individual. For instance, during a baptism, we might say, "By 'Aristotle', we mean this man [pointing]." Our intention is that the name should then identify that person in any real or imaginary situation, even those where they don't fit the descriptions associated with the name.

Let's consider a hypothetical scenario where Aristotle takes up gardening, while his brother is the one taught by Plato and the one who teaches Alexander. That didn't happen, but it could have. Kripke points out that if 'Aristotle' merely means 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander', then 'Aristotle' would refer to the wrong person in this hypothetical scenario.

Indeed, it appears that the actual situation might turn out to be one where Aristotle has few, if any, of the characteristics we associate with him. Historians could potentially uncover evidence suggesting that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for others' achievements and managed to deceive everyone. If this is true, then the name 'Aristotle' can't merely mean 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander'. Because in such a scenario, it would be a trivial truth, not a debatable historical fact, that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke posits that many common nouns, such as 'gold', 'water', and 'tiger', also refer directly, rather than based on some mental checklist. We define 'gold' by stating, "Gold is this substance" [pointing to a sample], allowing the world itself to determine the conditions under which something qualifies as gold.

Putnam, another advocate of the 'theory of direct reference', provides the most famous example of how a substance's name functions in this way. He invites us to imagine Twin Earth, similar to our own but with doppelgngers instead of humans. The only difference is that on Twin Earth, the liquid filling the oceans, lakes, and rivers isn't H2O, but another substance - XYZ. In 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered, both worlds would call their liquid 'water'. Since it's 1750, they associate the same mental checklist with that term: clear, thirst-quenching, boils at 100C, and so on. Now suppose a glass of XYZ is brought from Twin Earth to our world and presented to Locke. Locke would believe it's water because it would fit his mental checklist. But would he be correct?

SINGAPORE  Crude oil prices dipped on Thursday as concerns over a global economic slowdown, particularly in China, and record-high U.S. crude inventories at the Cushing delivery point weighed heavily on markets. Goldman Sachs forecasted that prices would remain low and volatile until the second half of the year.

International benchmark Brent crude futures LCOc1 were trading at $30.67 per barrel at 0601 GMT, a decrease of 17 cents. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures CLc1 stood at $27.01 per barrel, dropping by 44 cents and nearing the $26.19 a barrel intra-day high recorded just shy of 65 million barrels, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) released on Wednesday. The U.S.-based brokerage Phillip Futures concurred with this assessment.

The oil market is currently grappling with an oversupply of crude, combined with a slowing Chinese economy, which has led Goldman Sachs to predict that prices will remain low until the second half of the year. In a note to clients, the bank stated, "The risks of China growth concerns and oil price downside have materialized faster than we anticipated." They added, "We expect oil prices will continue to fluctuate between $20 per barrel (operational stress level) and $40 per barrel (financial stress level) with significant volatility and no discernible trend until 2H2016."

Chart analysts are predicting that crude prices could soon dip below $25 per barrel, as weakening technical indicators continue to put pressure on the market. Matthew Sferro, a technical analyst at New York's Informa Global Markets, stated that if WTI fell further, it would likely test the support level at $25.04 per barrel, established in April 2003.

Oil prices have fallen by almost 75 percent since mid-2014, when global production outpaced demand by approximately 2 million barrels per day. Trading activity in Asia was subdued due to China's New Year holiday, which lasts all week, and Japan's public holiday.

The current nitrogen cycle is not that of the Archaean Earth, but it can be seen as its continuation, insofar as earlier cycles stimulated the evolution of species that then evolved to perform later versions. Indeed, the nitrogen cycle could be viewed as the equivalent of the surname Jones, in my earlier parable. If songs are cast as persisters (in place of replicators/reproducers) and the various species that periodically assemble to implement them are cast as their interactors, Hull’s replicator/interactor framework has considerable explanatory power. A problem here might lie in the implication that processes or patterns of interaction, which are arguably not material things, can cause the evolution of species, which are. It’s not clear that the Jones family name itself has an impact on the reproductive behaviour of Joneses, though confidence-building family legends about reproductive prowess might do. But this dilemma is not unique to ITSNTS, in any case. In fact, Dawkins himself considered that genes as information are eternal, while their material instantiations, physical genes and the vehicles they produce are ephemeral – the ‘lumbering robots’ recruited to serve their selfish ends. Of this view, the evolutionary biologist David Haig writes: [M]aterial genes were physical objects but informational genes were the abstract sequences of which material genes were temporary vehicles. Material genes were identified with gene tokens and informational genes with gene types … Continuity resides in the recursive representation of immortal pattern by ephemeral avatars …So the immateriality/materiality problem is not a new one. Moreover, it at least seems to be lessened by adopting a replicator/interactor framework, where ‘perpetuation’ can mean differential persistence – persistence doesn’t require continuous presence – replication is taken to include reproduction, recurrence and re-production – and processes such as the nitrogen cycle can be considered entities that are perpetuated (the replicators, as it were). Beyond the benefit to science, ‘Darwinising Gaia’ would also have some political benefits. It might encourage us to look at nature as a coherent whole, with an evolutionary trajectory that we can foster or deflect as we choose. After all, we are already doing that, whether we realise it or not. Certainly, it would be a relief to heal the rift between traditional Darwinian thinking and believers in the possibility of Gaia, though there’s still much work to do to cement and validate the theory. And we’ll never really know what Darwin might have accepted as ‘Darwinian’, had he lived another 138 years. I’m just hoping that he’d applaud these efforts to render Gaia acceptable within a selectionist framework, and that he wouldn’t think we’d stretched his grand theory past the breaking point.

text:
Simone Bespaloff, a woman known for her tempestuous spirit, found moments of tranquility even amidst the chaos of war-torn Europe. In New York, she maintained connections with Parisian intellectual circles through letters with Fessard and Marcel. Before long, she secured a position with Voice of America's French broadcast, eventually moving to Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts where she taught French literature. During these dark times, Mount Holyoke emerged as an essential bastion for French culture in the United States. Scholars such as Jacques Maritain, Andr Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lvi-Strauss were among those Bespaloff encountered at gatherings organized by Wahl.

Mary McCarthy, who would later translate her works, described Bespaloff as a small, elegant woman who wore white gloves. Hannah Arendt also took notice of her when she visited in August 1944 to give a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt's interpretation of Kafka, which later appeared in Partisan Review, resonated deeply with Bespaloff's existentialist perspective. In the gloom of war, Arendt painted humanity as helplessly ensnared in history's web, and Kafka's "nightmare world" had become a stark reality.

In her final published work, an essay on Camus, Bespaloff expressed how her generation was forced to live under the shadow of violent death. After the war, despite once being celebrated by them, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a letter to Boris de Schloezer in 1946, she wrote that Sartre's subjectivity was not the cornerstone of a fresh humanism but rather a precursor for conformity. She argued that instead of liberating individuals, Sartre's existentialism eroded the magical inner sanctum through which humans could genuinely connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the gaze of the Other, a phenomenon she saw mirrored in American individualism.

The United States felt both drab and unwelcoming to Bespaloff, much like ancient Troy to Helen. Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. In April 1949, for reasons that remain unclear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her complex journey ended in a tragic denouement. In her final note, echoing Camus's words, she wrote: "One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy forever eludes him."

So how did it come to pass that now, some dozen years later, I continue to teach a successful course on genius at Yale, and have written an Amazon Book of the Year selection, The Hidden Habits of Genius (2020). Because I had shown an interest in music, and was also tall with enormous hands, I, too, would become the next Cliburn, at least so my mother declared. Soon, I was packed off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music, where, once again, every opportunity was placed before me. I had everything going for me except one: I lacked musical talent. But who wants to spend each day in the same studio teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists? My intuition was to find a larger arena in a university. Or is it merely the Machiavellian machinations of our naturally selected genes, in a conspiracy with the pro-life cultures, traditions and ideologies that grew out of an unquestioning obedience to these survival urges? The answer depends on what happens after we die. Most religions hold that our consciousness and identity persist after death, in one otherworldly realm or another, but it’s rarely the same one. Indeed, there are a wide variety of possible destinations for those who believe in an afterlife, most of them assigned according to our behaviour during life. Some of these places are better than life as we know it, but some are worse. If there is an afterlife, whether you want to live a long life or a short one would depend to some extent on whether your afterlife would be an upgrade or a demotion. Those who believe that they are going to hell, or even somewhere just slightly less agreeable than Earth, would be wise to eat right, exercise and look both ways before crossing the street. To those bound for a better place, on the other hand, an early demise has something to recommend it. There are a few living authors who claim to have visited heaven, but judging from their cheerful media appearances, that first hit of eternal bliss hasn’t left them overly desperate for another. Heaven is no heroin, apparently. The story of Colton Burpo, the four-year-old boy whose trip to the great beyond inspired his father’s book Heaven is for Real (2010), reassures us that our souls resemble ourselves in our 20s and 30s, thereby undercutting another reason to long for a premature end. In any case, the longest conceivable human life is so minute compared with the unfathomable enormity of forever that, if there is an eternal afterlife, any mortal span will vanish into statistical insignificance by comparison. The same goes for the hellbound: an extra few decades of mortal life isn’t much of a buffer against an eternity of lava baths and acid showers. If there is any risk of being reincarnated as a dissatisfied pig, you’d be wise to cling to human existence for as long as you can Length of life can still matter, but only if our actions in life determine our eternal destination. If getting to heaven is a matter of having certain beliefs or performing certain deeds, it would be devastating to expire before you managed to meet those requirements. Title: Not My Kind of Thai Haven

Perhaps I'm spoiled by the amazing Asian food scene in the Bay Area, but this place just didn't hit the mark for me. We both ordered red curry-based dishes, which limited my tasting experience a bit... My dish was Duck with Red Curry, and honestly, it left me unimpressed. The duck itself was tasty, but the curry was extremely bland - to the point where I wouldn't have identified it as curry if not told so.

The duck came with plain steamed vegetables on the side, which, as the meal progressed, seemed to dilute my already unremarkable curry sauce even further. The vegetables themselves were also underseasoned and could have benefited from a good stir-frying or some sort of accompanying sauce.

Our server was friendly enough, but there were small oversights that detracted from the overall experience. With only one other table in the restaurant, it felt like she forgot about us at times. When we asked her about the dishes, I got the impression she was just going through the motions - almost as if she wanted to hurry up and go home. Granted, it was around closing time, so maybe that played a role.

The food wasn't bad by any means; it simply fell short of being exceptional. It managed to provide nourishment, but I don't see myself returning anytime soon. If I were to rate my meal based on the Yelp definition of 2 stars ("Meh. I've experienced better"), I would have to agree - that description fits my experience here perfectly.

This incredibly detailed understanding of the plants and animals of the time is made possible by La Brea’s natural asphalt. ‘Asphalt acts as a wonderful preservative,’ Harris says. In most cases, bones trapped in asphalt are not even mineralised, and are in fact the original bone of the actual animals. They still count as fossils, but they are not like stone dinosaur bones. When a museum volunteer uncovers a Smilodon skull, they are touching the actual, stained bone of the big cat. Early 20th century paleontologists boiled the bones in kerosene to clean off the tar and generally paid little attention to the microfossils that littered the sludge that came off the skeletal parts. Today, researchers at La Brea take great care to get every fossil they can. Not only are the bones so carefully collected and documented that the entire bonebed can be reconstruct from notes, but the matrix surrounding those bones is also labelled so that its association with a particular grid square is recorded. That matrix is then treated with biodiesel to reduce many pounds of sediment and asphalt into siftable slurry that yields small fossils. These are some of the most important clues in the collection. Sabercats and mammoths attract the most visitor attention but the small fossils fill out the ancient environment with a much greater degree of resolution. Cesare Beccaria witnessed the scene with horror. It was hard to believe that such cruelty could be regarded as a rational response. At the time of the event, Beccaria was only in his mid-20s, but already had strong political and philosophical views. Born in 1738, the first son of a prominent Milanese aristocrat, he was educated in a stifling Jesuit school in Parma. After studying law in Pavia, he returned to Milan where his eyes were opened by Montesquieu’s Lettres persanes (1721), or Persian Letters, an epistolary novel giving an outsider’s critical angle on Parisian customs. text:
Solitary confinement - an experience so profound and devastating, it's hard not to question the sanity of those who endure it. The walls become more than mere concrete, they transform into a black hole, an abyss with no edges or escape. As a philosopher, I would describe this abyss as a disconnection from reality, a void that consumes any sense of 'elsewhere' or 'otherwise.' It's an emptiness that strips us of our basic understanding of where we are in the world.

A prisoner who beats his own body against these walls is fighting against this abyss. He's making a statement, declaring, "These walls might trap me, but I refuse to let them defeat me!" It's an expression of hope, a desperate search for that 'there' that we so desperately hold onto, even if it means tearing ourselves apart in the process.

Resistance might be self-destructive, but it's a testament to the emotional and existential damage prolonged solitary confinement can inflict on a person. Other prisoners find ways to cope with this overwhelming sense of isolation. Robert King, who spent 29 years in solitary confinement in Louisiana, would pace up and down his cell for hours, counting every inch. He needed to assert control over his space, to feel like he wasn't just another animal trapped within these cold walls.

Just as the body battering itself against the walls resists and reinforces the limits of extreme isolation, so too does pacing. But unlike the destructive act of self-harm, pacing develops a more sustainable coping mechanism, a way of clinging to control even when change is impossible. The prisoner who paces isn't accepting his confinement, but rather circling it like a wounded animal, trapped yet fighting for survival.

Pacing is not submission, but the nervous repetition of limits, a habit formed around the impossibility of adjusting to an intolerable situation. It's a way of coping, but even this strategy can become its own pathology. If our 'here' is connected to their 'there,' it cannot help but affect us, shaping our ability to see, hear, and make sense of our lives. In this sense, pacing is not a sign of acceptance, but rather a desperate attempt to hold onto something, anything, in the face of unrelenting despair.

Reflecting on the past five years, I find myself pondering the Shroud of Turin, a revered artifact for many devout Catholics, despite its questionable age. It's been infused with such profound faith over the last seven centuries that it has transcended its dubious origins and become significant. Some may argue that it began in falsehood, but through the collective belief of the faithful, it has taken on a deeper meaning. This year alone, over a million people have made reservations to view it.

While the imperfectly ancient brushwork on a fragment of papyrus from the 20th century might suggest forgery, the so-called 'Wife of Jesus' fragment hasn't been as big a draw for Catholic pilgrims since its debut in 2012. This Coptic papyrus, where Jesus refers to 'my wife', stands in stark contrast to the Turin shroud. Instead of affirming the synoptic gospels, it challenges Rome's most cherished beliefs about Christsuggesting that Jesus valued his female disciples and didn't prioritize males over them, as well as questioning the celibacy ideal for Christian clerics.

The 'Wife of Jesus' fragment's composition and syntax are suspicious, with awkward use of indirect objects in Coptic brushwork where a reed pen would typically be used. While some may dismiss it as a fraud, its discovery has sparked renewed interest in the role of women among Christ's disciplesthe Marys, Junia (an apostle), and Phoebe, whom Paul described as a deacon in the New Testament.

Joel Baden of Yale and Candida Moss of the University of Notre Dame wrote in The Atlantic last year that: "For the most part, texts and narratives supporting female discipleship come from outside the traditional canonno surprise given that the canonical New Testament was compiled long after Jesus' death by a male-led Church."

If the 'Wife of Jesus' fragment is indeed a forgery, it serves a purpose. It aims to reclaim religious experience from those who would define it as a male-dominated realm and presents an image of Christ who grants women equal status among his closest followers in worship.

The American West has long been fertile ground for those seeking truth or profitthis is why archaeology plays such a significant role in the Mormon Church, which depends heavily on questionable history. For instance, there are tales of golden plates in upstate New York to which, around 1823, an angel named Moroni guided Joseph Smith, who later founded the Latter Day Saint movement.

Maybe you share them with other people; maybe you don’t. But this creativity has meaning (even a little bit) to you, and it still matters. It might be a small daydream, a flight of fancy, or a genuine ‘aha’ moment that could have larger implications. While creativity is commonly defined as both novel and task appropriate, mini-c adjusts that slightly to add ‘to you’. Mini-c should be new to you and should meet your needs, even if that is simply to distract yourself for a moment. A person may have dozens or more moments of mini-c a day. Some folks make up lyrics to popular melodies to describe their current actions (as in, ‘I’m leaving… in my Kia. Don’t think I’ll try to use the heat’ to the tune of ‘Leaving on a Jet Plane’). Others think of spontaneous puns that they note to an empty room, or figure out how to complete a recipe when they’re out of butter, or doodle elaborate cartoon renditions of their co-workers while on a Zoom call, or hum jazzy variations on a melody, or devise a makeshift way of keeping a wobbly chair from collapsing. text:
Understanding why we take preventative measures now leads us to consider if octopuses have a concept of time. The evidence suggests they do, but how might this temporal understanding be structured in an octopus? Given our surroundings shape human metaphors for time, it's intriguing to ponder what time metaphors an octopus might use. With equal proficiency interacting with objects behind or in front of its body, past events could be just as manipulable as future ones  notions like 'let's put the past behind us' would be meaningless for an octopus. Furthermore, its ability to change visual perspective by swimming upward means all events in time  past, present, and future  could be surveyed from a single vantage point, allowing it to identify patterns over extended periods.

Adopting octopus-inspired metaphors for time might profoundly alter our relationship with the past. It would help us see how our past is causally linked to the present, potentially shifting our focus away from an obsession with future progress. This new perspective could enable us to excavate historical ideas and use them as sources of innovation, transforming fields like medicine and politics. Additionally, it might broaden our understanding of what constitutes a meaningful event, all thanks to time metaphors that are free from constraints.

One way to explore this is by examining the narratives we construct about our lives. According to narrative psychologists, a coherent sense of self relies on a unified life story that connects experiences in time. Traumatic events can disrupt this narrative, leading to conditions like dissociative identity disorder where a person adopts an entirely different personality following an experience too difficult to integrate. Treatment for dissociative identity disorder involves moving flexibly through the past and future  much like an octopus navigates space  to first rebuild the individual's disrupted sense of self, and then shift focus toward a desired future self.

Similar concepts have been applied at a societal level following periods of mass trauma, such as war. For instance, after World War II, Carl Jung argued that the German people needed to confront their collective guilt to move forward towards a more liberal future. Today, writers like Ta-Nehisi Coates expose historic injustices against African Americans, suggesting that acknowledging and repairing past wrongs are essential for addressing racial inequality now and moving forward.

In the Aymara language, spoken in Peru, tomorrow is called 'the day at my back.' Embracing a more octopus-like approach to time might help us confront our past, learn from it, and navigate our future with greater understanding and resilience.

text:
Penises come in all shapes and sizes, from spiky to bone-like, sometimes even two in the same animal. This diverse array of genitalia has left researchers puzzled for years - specifically, they've been wondering how many times the penis has evolved in amniotes (a group that includes mammals, reptiles, and birds).

One thing we know for sure is that the development of a penis is an ongoing process. For instance, only 3% of bird species possess penises, with ducks being one of the most notable examples due to their corkscrew-like, ballistically everted versions. The rest of the bird population relies on a structure that doesn't serve much purpose or lacks a penis entirely.

In vertebrates without a penis, the transfer of gametes (reproductive cells) happens internally through a structure called the cloaca - Latin for 'sewer'. This all-purpose organ passes fluid, including semen, out of the body. Animals can engage in what's known as the cloacal kiss, where the two 'sewers' are brought together to facilitate the transfer of semen. Among reptiles, this behavior is relatively rare; only one species - the tuatara, a reptile that traces its lineage back to the dinosaur era - regularly practices it.

The absence of a penis in the tuatara, which sits at the base of the amniote family tree, suggests that a later lineage evolved this appendage. However, given the variety of penises across amniote species, one question that has lingered is: how many times did the penis evolve anew?

Answers have been as varied as the penises themselves, but rarely does 'once' come up. However, recent discoveries involving tuatara embryos have provided a clearer answer. In the early 20th century, a zoologist named Arthur Dendy, working in New Zealand, sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. These specimens were processed and then lost for decades. But a lucky recovery of these samples many years later revealed something remarkable: the embryos were at the right stage of development to determine whether these animals initiate a starter penis that eventually disappears, much like human embryos begin developing a tail that later vanishes.

After researchers digitally reconstructed the tuatara samples, they could see that the embryonic region where penises start to develop showed signs of penis growth in the tuatara embryo. This was significant because living tuatara can live for up to a century, and those born around the time these eggs were collected demonstrated that the starter penis never fully developed.

With this evidence from the tuatara, researchers now have proof that a penis - whether it survives the embryonic stage or not - was present at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the program for making a penis evolved only once in our lineage. The same timing and starting materials used to build penises are found in tuataras as in other amniotes.

text: The line between attributing consciousness to something and bridging the gap of understanding is always blurred. Yet, Wittgenstein poses a question that lingers in our minds - if these 'automata' are pretending to feel pain, for instance, how can we be certain that it's indeed pain they're feigning? For Wittgenstein, these criteria don't establish the truth of statements but rather define the use of the concepts within those statements. Our ability to recognize whatever they might be 'feigning' as pain is simply our capacity to speak the language we do. But if all the criteria are satisfied, you're not missing any pieces of the puzzle - what's at stake here is whether you acknowledge them as a someone, in pain. As Cavell writes, "The gap of acknowledgement can never be filled with knowledge." In a similar vein, our own minds, our emotions, aren't logically hidden from being understood by others. This connection is fragile, not in the sense of a metaphysical barrier but in the sense that some people fail to acknowledge the humanity of others. Wittgenstein points out a crucial aspect of the teacher-student relationship - when the student does something incorrect or unexpected. On this reading, Wittgenstein witnesses what Rupert Read calls a 'proto-Levinasian moment', where a Self encounters the Other. In this encounter, the degree of acknowledgment we adopt shapes our attitude towards them. This attitude is inherently ethical because it defines the limits of our obligations to others and because denying another's humanity involves the simultaneous denial of the mutuality upon which our attempts to communicate or express anything at all depend. This denial implies the absence of meaningful grounds even for constituting ourselves as subjects. As Cavell puts it, "I am fated to stand to myself in the relations in which I may or may not stand to others." It is only by acknowledging the Other that the Self can truly be a human being. In Wittgenstein's Investigations, the examples of such encounters are pedagogical. There is no more poignant encounter than the one we often face - the case of the deviant student (as described in 185 of the Investigations). As teachers, we know that the crux, the risk, of the teacher-student relationship arises when the student does something wrong and goes against our expectations. This pupil doesn't comply with our instructions. They don't see things as we do. So what do we do about them? In Wittgenstein's lecture notes that precede the Investigations, known as the Brown Book, he humorously notes society's typical response: "If a child does not respond to the suggestive gesture, the answer is to hit him."

text:
In the heat of the moment, he weighs his actions, considering his own nature and that of his audience, his aims and the hurdles in his path. The sophist, on the other hand, resorts to corrupt and awkward tactics to attain equally questionable ends. No one questioned the integrity of Christ's methods, disguises, or deceptive words, even if it was simpler to assert their righteousness than to defend it. Whether successful or not, portraying God as the consummate rhetorician, the ultimate orator, only served to exacerbate the divide between these two conceptions of God. After all, rhetoric is about adapting, adjusting, and reacting - a constant dance of interactions with others. It was this need for adaptability, change, action, and reaction, speech and deception, that seemed so alien to the philosophical conception of God as eternal, perfect, and unchanging. As it happened, distinguishing miraculous intervention from deception became increasingly difficult.

At the dawn of the 14th century, another source of tension between scripture and philosophy surfaced, making the specter of divine deception more palpable. Aristotle's writings, largely lost to Europeans for centuries, were beginning to resurface during the 12th century. Intellectuals, ever eager for novelty, integrated Aristotle's works into the curriculum of various medieval universities, propelling his ideas to the forefront of 13th-century intellectual life. However, these philosophical ideas often clashed with scripture rather than complementing it. According to Aristotle, God - the 'Unmoved Mover' - was part of the universe, the pinnacle, but still a part of it, providing it with form and energy according to the necessary and immutable dictates of reason itself. Theologians could hardly remain idle as students and teachers absorbed this diminished image of God. If scripture taught us anything about God, it taught that he transcended a universe he had created ex nihilo, out of nothing. He is omnipotent, they asserted, capable of anything. Demonstrating God's omnipotence required theologians to describe all the ways that God could intervene miraculously in the ordinary course of nature. As it turned out, distinguishing miraculous intervention from deception was becoming increasingly challenging.

Early in the 14th century, William of Ockham posed a straightforward thought experiment to his readers. Imagine you are gazing at a star. text:
Isaiah Berlin was a renowned thinker and writer, widely respected as an intellectual and moralist in the latter half of the 20th century. He passed away on November 5, 1997. Shortly after his death, Henry Hardy, his editor and friend, described him as "not only the most remarkable and delightful, but also, in so many ways, the wisest, the surest in judgment, and the best." Berlin's influential essay on liberty, much like many of his other essays that began as lectures, distinguishes between two concepts of political freedom. The first is termed 'negative liberty', which refers to the extent to which I am free from the intrusion or coercion of others. Being negatively free, Berlin explains, does not equate to being entirely unconstrained. It simply denotes the degree of freedom from human interference in areas such as personal choices and political engagement. On the other hand, 'positive liberty' is the ability to act or do something, often stemming from an individual's desire to be self-governing. At first glance, one might consider these ideas as two sides of the same coin. However, Berlin's account offers a realistic and historical perspective, demonstrating that there is a substantial difference  not just a logical one  between negative and positive liberty. This distinction can have far-reaching consequences if not recognized, leading to more harm than mere conceptual confusion.

What sets Berlin apart is his approach. He challenges the conventional understanding by making theory respond to reality rather than the other way around. This bold shift enables history and real-world experiences to take center stage in interpreting these concepts. From a historical and contemporary perspective, negative and positive liberty address legitimate human needs and ideals. Negative freedom has been the cornerstone of political liberalism, emphasizing the importance of individual autonomy, so long as actions do not harm others unfairly. Conversely, positive liberty lies at the heart of emancipatory theories in politics, ranging from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism. In essence, Berlin demonstrates that modern history and our current reality underscore the fact that the concept of positive liberty can be incredibly vulnerable, as evidenced by its catastrophic misuse.

The uncoupling of in-group love from out-group hate has implications for whether contact between people of different cultures brings them together or pushes them apart, as the controversial political scientist Robert Putnam noted in a 2007 lecture entitled ‘E Pluribus Unum’. If attitudes within groups do not determine attitudes between them, it is logically possible that diversity reduces solidarity both within them and between them. Putnam claimed that, across America, diversity had undermined trust and social cohesion as a whole: ‘Americans distrust not merely people who do not look like them, but even people who do.’ In diverse settings, he found that people ‘hunker down’ and withdraw from each other. His argument sent researchers into the field across North America and Europe. Their findings mostly supported Putnam’s case. Nevertheless, some studies found that diversity did in fact promote trust and cohesion in cases where people from different backgrounds had a meaningful degree of contact with each other. Perhaps that shouldn’t be surprising: after all, trust is an expectation about how others will behave. If you share their culture, norms and values, you might be confident in your ability to predict their behaviour even without having anything to do with them. If you don’t, you just have to get to know them personally. The kernel of the us-them problem is the question of why the tiniest and most apparently inconsequential differences are enough to trigger the group-forming reflex. One explanation is that small differences can be indicators of larger differences in culture, kinship and affiliation. Accents and quirks of speech, easily checked and hard to fake, can be giveaways, as the Bible tells us: And the Gileadites took the passages of Jordan before the Ephraimites: and it was so, that when those Ephraimites which were escaped said, Let me go over; that the men of Gilead said unto him, Art thou an Ephraimite? Then they took him, and slew him at the passages of Jordan: and there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand. That chillingly precise casualty figure seems to suggest this is one passage of the Old Testament that can be taken literally. The shibboleth was a test of who was on whose side; and that, according to the evolutionary psychologists Leda Cosmides, John Tooby and Ray Kurzban, is what group markers are really all about. Now, we often say in casual speech that the whole is not the sum of its parts, but the task was to say exactly what the difference was between the whole and the collection of its parts. Ehrenfels took a musical melody as the perfect example of a whole that’s so much more than its parts (though the same thing could be said for a word or a sentence). A melody is easily recognised as the same even if it is raised or lowered by a musical interval. What is it then that’s the melody, if all of the component notes have changed? text:
The combination of human rights activism, forensic anthropology, and emerging DNA technology, embodied in organizations like the Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team (EAAF) and the Grandmothers of Plaza de Mayo, gave birth to a powerful, groundbreaking movement. This movement leverages scientific advancements to provide new avenues for victims' families and societies transitioning from conflict or authoritarian rule to cope with their violent pasts.

While the promise of forensics-based human rights isn't just about identifying remains, it extends to a deeper, more nuanced understanding of justice at individual, familial, and societal levels. For many grieving families, this means the opportunity for funerals, public mourning, and rewriting historical records  all actions that can significantly alter collective memory. This new form of justice holds special significance in cases where legal justice is unattainable due to amnesty laws, time's passage, or indifferent governments who benefit from silence.

This forensics-based human rights movement allows voices outside the state  perceived as credible thanks to the legitimacy of science  to narrate violent historical accounts. It has spread globally, from Latin America to Africa, Europe, and recently, the United States. Navigating how societies confront their history is always complex. Collective memory does not represent objective history but a tangled web of present-day hierarchies, identities, and emotional and moral ties to the past. It's like layers of sediment shaped by countless variables including emotions, power relations, culture, storytelling, and time's passage.

Those in positions of privilege, such as politicians, often have more opportunities to advance 'official' narratives that serve them. In societies scarred by political violence, war, or genocide, some governments deliberately silence violent pasts for their benefit. Scholars like Claire Whitlinger and Eviatar Zerubavel argue that this silencing undermines social solidarity by threatening open communication, a cornerstone of democratic political cultures. However, state-imposed silences are not invincible and can be challenged by countering techniques.

Those who challenge the memory of state violence, such as human rights workers, must be viewed as legitimate to be heard and taken seriously. As Pierre Bourdieu pointed out in 1982, an actor's success often depends on their 'authority.' Thus, social actors presenting counter-memories of state terror and violence must be seen as reasonable and not motivated by revenge or corrupt intentions, especially during critical moments like a transition to democracy or the breaking of state-imposed silences.

So, how can a movement find a voice that is both legitimate and devoid of political bias? Social actors should focus on presenting their message in a way that resonates with reasonableness and integrity rather than vengeance or self-interest. In high-intensity situations, this is particularly important to avoid being dismissed as partisan.

text:
The rugged, veined rocks of Bohusln in western Sweden hold one of Europe's highest concentrations of Bronze Age art. During a recent October visit, I stumbled upon these intriguing carvings hidden off the side of a road, which once marked the shoreline of the North Sea - a distant memory from 2,500 to 4,000 years ago when these figures were first etched. The petroglyphs have since been painted pillar-box red for tourists, giving them an appearance reminiscent of a giant child's doodle. While they lacked refinement, they were brimming with energy. Hunters and warriors pursued stags amidst longships, blending seamlessly into a procession of bulls and towering giants precariously balanced on slender legs. Land, sea, human, and animal seemed to merge and intertwine. The Bronze Age rock art in Bohusln, Sweden, is believed to depict the performance of a ritual. I found myself trying to piece together a story, imagining it as an ancient cartoon strip unfolding before my eyes. We may never know why these inscriptions were made or what they intended to convey. The blunt-limbed figures offer no insight into where such a tale might begin or end. Yet, perhaps the tangled quality of these petroglyphs is their true message - they speak of a culture deeply rooted in the sea and nature.

Similar sites on the Cornish and Iberian peninsulas suggest that cultural influences traversed what the Beowulf poet later called 'the whale roads' - the open waters connecting Bronze Age Scandinavia to the rest of Europe for trade and conflict. Today, we find ourselves in a similarly interconnected age, where our reliance on fossil fuels, nitrogen fertilisers, cobalt in smartphones, antibiotics, and plastic binds us to distant times and places. The world of the Anthropocene is woven from this intricate web of relationships, each one leaving an imprint on the future. For two centuries, humans have walked upon the same earth as the Bohusln petroglyphs, yet they remain vibrant and powerful, echoes of a living presence. In their silence, they pose questions that resonate today: How do we communicate across the currents of time and matter with the deep future? Are our intentional signs and symbols the ones that leave the most lasting impact, or are our unintentional traces the ones that endure?

I reside in Edinburgh, perched on the edge of the North Sea. I've come to see these waters as a kind of Anthropocene laboratory, offering a glimpse into the long-term consequences of how we live today. Its past can help us understand our future. As Pangea was breaking apart 200 million years ago,...
