text:
In the world of penises - yes, you read that right - there's quite a spectrum of diversity, from spines to bones, even two heads, sometimes all found within the same creature. This variety has left scientists puzzled about how many times the penis has evolved in amniotes, our group of animals with eggs that are protected by shells. The only certain fact is that it's an ongoing work-in-progress, a constant evolution if you will.

For instance, birds offer a striking example, with only 3% sporting a penis  ducks being the most famous case, their penises being quite the spectacle, shooting out like corkscrews - while the rest have a structure that's more or less useless, or none at all. In species without a penis, they rely on an all-purpose organ called the cloaca, which is Latin for 'sewer', to transfer gametes internally. Animals can use what's known as a cloacal kiss - bringing these 'sewers' together - to achieve this transfer of semen. Among reptiles, cloacal kisses are rare, with the tuatara being the only exception.

The absence of a penis in the tuatara, an ancient creature that traces its lineage back to the time of dinosaurs, suggests that a later lineage evolved the appendage. However, with such apparent differences in penises across amniote species, how many times did the penis evolve anew? The answers had been varied and rarely did they propose 'once'.

But then came the tuatara embryos. In the early 20th century, a zoologist working in New Zealand sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. Decades later, these samples were rediscovered and it was found that they were at the right stage of development to observe whether these animals initiated a starter penis before discarding it, much like human embryos begin making a tail that eventually disappears. After digitally reconstructing the tuatara embryos, researchers discovered that, in the region where penises begin to develop, the tuatara embryo did indeed start to grow a penis. However, living tuatara born around the same time as these eggs were collected (they can live for up to a century) demonstrated that this starter penis never fully developed.

With this evidence from the tuatara, researchers finally had the proof they needed that a penis - whether it survives the embryo stage or not - sat at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the programme for making a penis had evolved only once for our kind. The process for building one was present in the tuatara, with the same timing as in other amniotes, using the same starting materials as ours.

Back around a decade ago, an intriguing moment unfolded during a dinner party at my home that significantly reshaped my intellectual journey in Classics. My good friend Richard Thomas, fresh from giving a talk at Baylor University, casually mentioned one of his students had found an 'Isaiah acrostic' hidden within Vergil's Georgics - a first-century BCE poem often perceived as merely about farming but delving into life and the universe. This offhand comment introduced me to two fascinating aspects of ancient Greek and Latin poetry that I hadn't fully considered before, despite decades spent immersed in Classics: acrostics and Judaism.

The tension between biblical and classical traditions has long been palpable. Tertullian famously asked, "What does Athens have to do with Jerusalem?" Similarly, St Jerome felt compelled to break ties with his beloved classical authors after a haunting dream where he was accused of being more Ciceronian than Christian. One reason Vergil holds such significance within the Western tradition is that his Fourth Eclogue - depicting the arrival of a divine boy heralding a new Golden Age - offered a soothing interpretation for many: it was viewed as a pagan prophecy of Christ's birth, thus enabling Christianity to absorb rather than dismiss the Classics. However, post-Enlightenment readers often rejected this Christian interpretation; they had no interest in viewing Athens through Jerusalem's lens.

Regardless of one's stance on the supernatural, there is ample evidence suggesting personal and intellectual exchanges between Jews and non-Jewish Greeks and Romans before and after Christ's birth. Jews made up roughly 10-20% of the Roman Empire's population, and they appear frequently in classical texts. The Septuagint - a Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures created around the 3rd century BCE - was widely accessible to educated non-Jewish individuals throughout the Mediterranean world. Classics scholars and intellectual historians should devote more attention to Jewish texts' impact on classical authors, as I argued in my 2018 paper 'Was Vergil Reading the Bible?' I proposed that the answer is likely 'Yes,' and that some scholars are starting to acknowledge Jewish themes as a crucial part of his work.

However, scholars' neglect of acrostics might stem from an assumption they are too simplistic for sophisticated authors. After all, even a child can create an acrostic. The most basic type involves spelling a word vertically by the first letters of successive lines: Catches mice, Adorable whiskers, Tail's up  look out! It is undeniable that such vertical words exist in the columns of long poems; the challenge lies in determining whether they hold any significance.

text:
Life's complexity can sometimes feel like a puzzle, especially when it comes to understanding how genes work and how they contribute to our traits and behaviors. While genes are indeed the building blocks of life, they don't always function as straightforward deterministic causes of specific traits. An organism can often manage just fine with modifications or even loss of a gene because other genes compensate for its absence, or because any one change has only a minor impact. For example, many human diseases are associated with certain gene variants that are the norm in other species. It's not uncommon for us humans to carry a significant number of defunct or disrupted genes, even if we ourselves are healthy. This can include genes whose mutations have been implicated in disease contexts.

All this might seem puzzling: genes are molecules and hence fundamental causal agents of life, yet their effects are highly probabilistic and hard to pin down or predict. However, the issues are actually quite straightforward once you understand that life works as a collection of uncertainties. This understanding can help us make sense of what is inherent in our nature and why.

With this in mind, let's turn to the topic of behavior and free will. What we know about genetics and evolution challenges molecular reductionism, which is the attempt to use genetic variants to predict not only physical traits but also complex phenomena such as intelligence or creativity. These traits seem to emerge out of nowhere, yet they clearly exist and have a material basis.

However, this raises a conundrum: if science tells us that the world is entirely material and follows universal laws of causation, then even the idea that we are responsible for our thoughts and actions comes under question. Personality, intelligence, criminality, political preference, and even moral decisions must, in principle, be predictable from our inherent, inherited genome. Yet, our thoughts and actions seem to be even farther beyond the reach of gene-based prediction than physical traits such as diabetes or height, which we've seen to be extremely complex in their causation.

Is this just a temporary limit in scientific knowledge, or something more profound? It's an ongoing debate that continues to challenge our understanding of life and our place in the universe.

In my classroom, I learned to suppress my physical self. However, it was outside the academy where I truly found myself - through the realm of fanfiction. I stumbled upon this fascinating world in 2001, at the tender age of 16, a time when I felt lost and misunderstood, often mistaken for a boy in women's restrooms. Desperate to connect with others, I joined various web mailing listservs and found solace in the stories these writers shared - one such story was about a character from The Lord of the Rings that left me spellbound. It was unlike anything I had ever read before. For years, I had dreamt of stepping into books and embarking on adventures with my favorite characters. Now, I was penning down these dreams and sharing them with the world. Strangers would leave heartwarming comments on each chapter, validating my newfound ability to express myself creatively.

Later, I came across a term for this kind of story: 'Mary Sue'. The name originated from a short satire called 'A Trekkie's Tale', written by mathematician Paula Smith in 1973 and published in a Star Trek fanzine. The satire was a critique of the influx of fanfiction stories she deemed poor, featuring all-American teenagers who, like my own early stories, would strut into the world of the Starship: Enterprise and capture Captain Kirk's heart. As I delved deeper into fanfiction, I discovered a treasure trove of beautifully written pieces that offered insights into characters I held dear.

As I devoured Harry Potter fanfiction during my high school and university years, I found myself simultaneously studying classical and medieval literature. I learned Latin and read works by Virgil, Cicero, and St Augustine in their original language. I wrote analytical essays that were as cold and sober as the academic world demanded, but when night fell, I would lose myself in fanfiction, my secret indulgence.

Eventually, I moved to Canada for graduate school. By then, I knew that fantasizing about meeting Geoffrey Chaucer or imagining Queen Eleanor of Aquitaine's encounters with Sir Lancelot was not the way serious readers behaved. But I grappled with cognitive dissonance, as it became increasingly clear that fanfiction had honed my reading skills.

Fanfiction enhances the richness of its source texts for its dedicated readers. It deepens allusions and unearths hidden undercurrents, delves into characterization and subtle nuances of plot, and spends time exploring these elements in detail. After immersing myself in fanfiction, I return to my beloved texts with a fresh perspective - often a more discerning one. For instance, reading hundreds of stories that reimagined the relationship between Remus Lupin and Sirius Black in Harry Potter made me realize that their bond might have been the reason for Sirius's family's estrangement from him.

Reintegration is a complex issue when it comes to former rebels in Uganda, particularly those associated with the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA). They were often seen as both perpetrators of terrible violence and victims themselves, many having been abducted as children. For civilians, reintegrating these individuals meant transforming 'animal-like' former rebels into 'human beings.' The process was described as 'taming,' where they were taught to abandon their old ways in the wilderness (Lum) and learn hygiene, social norms, and other skills. This process was referred to as 'repairing their minds.'

The brutalized, traumatized individuals who were once part of the LRA had crossed the line of human behavior and needed to be re-educated, disciplined or counseled to regain humanity. However, these former rebels didn't always see themselves in the same light as civilians did. Yes, they killed with machetes and beat people to death with logs, but they didn't always have access to guns or drones like state armies. Yes, they were kidnapped and forced into fighting against their will, but some found the spiritual and political aspects of the rebel cause appealing. They often regretted being returned home to towns and villages, where they encountered alcoholism, unemployment, and infidelity. In the Lum, they saw themselves as gorillas engaged in 'gorilla warfare.' Identifying with animals wasn't a fall from grace but rather a sense of elevation to a higher status of power and ferocity.

Many found the process of reintegration confusing. They viewed their time in the army as a period of living a meaningful and active life surrounded by close friendships and family. They considered each other as brothers and sisters, united in a spirit of togetherness and mutual support, as if they were blood relatives. Some even claimed that their relationships in the Lum were stronger and more supportive than those they had with civilians after leaving the army. Why were they treated like violent, dirty beasts who needed to learn humanity? Some former rebels retorted, "We too are human beings." They didn't want to be seen as brutal killers or traumatized victims. Yet, their claims to humanity seemed awkward. If they identified as animals, did that not put them in the realm of the divine and protected by holy spirits? Their claims to humanity aimed to avoid being treated as outcasts. But humanity could not recognize the truth of their experiences, beliefs, and ways of being, which often transcended human realms, entering into divine and animal domains.

The struggles of these former rebels were multifaceted, reflecting the complexities of identity, violence, and reintegration in post-conflict societies.

In the heart of Athenian democracy lies the tale of Socrates' trial, a story deeply rooted in the citizen's role and responsibility within their state. The actions taken by Socrates, Meletus, and the jury must be viewed through the lens of the Athenians' emphasis on civic duty, their belief in the responsible citizen in a democratic society. It is more about active participation than contemporary debates over recognition. Maximus of Tyre, writing in the late Roman era, described Socrates as maintaining a dignified silence in response to Meletus' charges. Yet, dissidents who question political systems have often chosen silent protest or refusal to participate in court proceedings. However, Socrates accepted the authority of the court. Despite this acceptance, he defended himself in an unconventional manner, participating as a citizen should  to answer for any actions that may harm the community.

Socrates' defense was not about challenging the trial's legitimacy; instead, it was about proving his innocence and demonstrating his contributions to Athenian society. He saw himself as a civic benefactor deserving of honor, such as free meals at the state hearth. Socrates' commitments as a citizen are clearly depicted in Plato's dialogues, providing a rich portrait of Athenian political culture.

In Plato's dialogue, "Crito," we encounter a unique scenario  a 'dialogue within a dialogue.' Here, the personified Laws of Athens speak to an alternate Socrates who wishes to evade punishment by fleeing the city. The Laws represent not just the written rules but also the civic norms of Athens. They ask the disobedient Socrates about his grievances with them and when he discovered these flaws.

The Laws remind Socrates that they brought him into this world (through laws concerning marriage) and nurtured him (through norms regarding parental responsibility). Socrates acknowledges their role in his life, a testament to the profound impact of Athenian civic culture on its citizens.

In essence, Socrates' trial is not merely a historical event but a reflection of the citizen's duty towards their state and society. It serves as a timeless reminder that even in the face of adversity, one must uphold their civic obligations.

Every year, this unlikely congregation gathers to lay wreaths at the foot of the 19th-century statue that glowers over the piazza from beneath its friar’s cowl; flowers, candles, poems and tributes pile up against the plinth whose inscription reads: ‘To Bruno, from the generation he foresaw, here, where the pyre burned.’ In the four centuries since he was executed for heresy by the Roman Inquisition, this diminutive iconoclast has been appropriated as a symbol by all manner of causes, reflecting the complexities and contradictions inherent in his ideas, his writings and his character. Giordano Bruno was born in Nola, at the foot of Mount Vesuvius, in 1548, a soldier’s son who joined the prestigious Dominican convent of San Domenico Maggiore in nearby Naples at the age of 17. He excelled in philosophy and theology, but his precocious intellect proved as much a liability as an asset; he was constantly in trouble with his superiors for questioning orthodoxies and seeking out forbidden reading material. In 1576, he was forced to flee the convent under the cover of darkness to avoid an interrogation by the local Inquisitor after being discovered reading Erasmus in the privy (he had thrown the book down the hole to hide the evidence, but someone was determined enough to fish it out). So began an itinerant life that saw Bruno progress north through Italy to France, transforming himself along the way from defrocked friar (he was excommunicated in absentia for his unauthorised flight), to university teacher, to personal tutor to King Henry III of France, all in little more than five years. This remarkable rise illustrates Bruno’s audacity and charisma but, although he began to move in influential circles, gaining a reputation for his prodigious memory and the artificial memory system he devised, his position always remained precarious; he made enemies with as much alacrity as he attracted admirers, and was thrown into prison in more than one city for giving offence in his public lectures. At the sprawling outdoor venue of Tunisia's World Social Forum, an international gathering of civil society organizations, an unusual mix of sounds filled the air. The Tunisian torture choir, as I'll call them for convenience, were there alongside a group of rap artists and street dancers, masked in Guy Fawkes attire. Despite the contrast, it all somehow seemed to fit harmoniously within the vibrant atmosphere of this historic event - the forum's first time being held in an Arab country, no less, and two years after Tunisia sparked a wave of revolutions across the region.

The choir consisted of men chained together, dressed in prison uniforms, singing about the thousands of political prisoners from Tunisia's previous regime who endured horrific abuse behind bars. Now free, they find themselves lost and without support as they struggle to reintegrate into society. The rappers, mostly Tunisian students, were voicing their opinions on economic issues, unity, employment, and the revolution's demands: work, freedom, dignity - the same demands that led to the downfall of dictator Zine El Abidine Ben Ali in January 2011. Though the oppressive regime may be gone, those three demands are still unfulfilled.

As I approached the choir, I could see two members enacting scenes of torture, one using a stick to simulate beating and contorting the other's body into stress positions. Close by, a group of Palestinians were chanting and dancing traditional Debke dances, aware that Palestine was on many minds at this meeting of global justice movements. Amidst them, an argument over Syria had broken out among a cluster of forum delegates - one of many heated debates about the conflict that took place during the forum's three days. Some were reportedly aggressive, a stark reminder of the deep divisions surrounding the devastating war.

As participants from around the world converged on Tunis at the end of March, they were met with a renewed sense of optimism in a country where tourism has taken a hit due to media-fueled fear over political turmoil. The capital's streets, once eerily quiet following travel advisories, are buzzing again with life. The politically charged protest culture now dominating the streets is not only for the benefit of visitors but has been growing steadily since the revolution. It's a testament to the newfound freedom of expression in Tunisia and a reassertion of the power and importance of protest as a political tool.

Artists across various mediums are reclaiming public spaces once controlled by the government, reinvigorating the streets with their voices. It's not just about speaking out; it's about asserting that change starts here, in the heart of the people.

The incapacity of our legal system to grasp the gravity of its actions, as if the leader of a great constitutional republic were merely a stray dog to be shot down, is a stark reminder of Charles J. Guiteau's conviction and execution in 1882. Although he clearly showed signs of mental instability, his intellect was not questioned.

Over centuries, the courts have been stubbornly resistant to medical expertise, even as our understanding of mental illness has expanded through fields such as psychiatry and psychology. As early as the 16th century, Johann Weyer challenged the Saxon Code's treatment of insanity, stating that it failed to reflect the true nature of mental illness. The response from the bench was dismissive: "Weyer may be a physician, but he is not a lawyer; therefore, his perspective on the link between mental disease and legal transgressions holds no weight."

Fast forward to 1950, and the chasm between legal definitions of insanity and psychiatric realities was as wide as ever. Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter lamented the disconnect between "the pathological processes that give rise to the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity." In the subsequent four decades, this divide has persisted, with the legal definition of exculpatory mental disease remaining largely unchanged and untouched by advancements in medical theory.

The current legal definition of insanity bears no resemblance to any recognized psychiatric condition; it is a purely legal construct, owing nothing to the realms of psychiatry and neuroscience. As psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote in 1943: "With rare exceptions, murderers are well aware of what they're doing, and the consequences of their actions, regardless of any psychiatrist's opinion."

This lack of a psychiatric correlate fuels the perception that mental health experts are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective. In reality, mental health professionals often disagree on whether a defendant meets the legal definition of insanity, even when they concur on the defendant's diagnosis and level of psychiatric impairment. As forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School put it in 1998: "Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defense, not about mental illness."

In his work 'On Responsibility' (1996), federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard noted: "Contemporary psychiatric and psychological data prevent legitimate experts from making accurate and scientific determinations based on the rigid legal definitions the law imposes ... Trials that hinge on a jury's decision about a defendant's guilt based on conflicting expert testimony often devolve into 'battles of experts,' which almost guarantee arbitrary outcomes."

We tend to judge the wrongdoing of an individual by the intensity of our outrage at their actions. When called upon to testify, mental health professionals are faced with this same challenge: to reconcile the complexity of human behavior with the rigid constraints of the law.

My friend Richard Thomas, who had just given a talk at Baylor University, mentioned that a student of his had discovered an ‘Isaiah acrostic’ in Vergil’s Georgics, a 1st-century BCE poem ostensibly about farming but really about life and the universe. This remark simultaneously opened the door to two phenomena in ancient Greek and Latin poetry that I had not really thought about, despite a lifelong career in Classics: acrostics and Judaism. The relationship between the biblical and the classical traditions has always been fraught. As Tertullian testily asked in his screed against pagan writers: ‘What has Athens to do with Jerusalem?’ Similarly, St Jerome felt compelled to abandon the classical authors he loved after a nightmare vision in which the judge accused him of being a Ciceronian, not a Christian. One of the many reasons Vergil is central to the Western tradition is that his Fourth Eclogue, which portrays the birth of a miraculous boy ‘sent down from heaven’ to inaugurate a new Golden Age, helped calm these fears: it was seen by readers from late antiquity until the 18th century as a pagan prophecy of the birth of Christ, thereby allowing Christianity to assimilate the Classics rather than merely rejecting them. Post-Enlightenment readers, however, tended to react against the Christian interpretation – they had no desire to view Athens through the prism of Jerusalem. Whatever one may think about the supernatural dimension, there is abundant evidence for personal and intellectual contact between Jews and non-Jewish Greeks and Romans before and after the birth of Christ. Jews composed something like 10-20 per cent of the population of the Roman Empire; there are many overt references to Jews and Judaism in classical texts; and the Septuagint – the Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures undertaken in the 3rd century BCE – would have been accessible to educated non-Jewish people throughout the Mediterranean world. Classicists and intellectual historians should be paying far more attention than they presently do to the impact of Jewish texts and culture on classical authors. In my paper ‘Was Vergil Reading the Bible?’ (2018), I argued that the answer to that question is probably ‘Yes’, and that at least some scholars are beginning to realise that Jewish themes are an important component of his meaning. Scholars’ lack of attention to acrostics, on the other hand, may stem more from an intuitive sense that they are beneath the dignity of sophisticated authors. Indeed, acrostics are an art form simple enough for a child to create. text:
In the Bible, we find instances where God seems to deceive, not just demons and sinners, but even the righteous. Take Abraham, the patriarch of the Jewish people, for example. God asked him to sacrifice his son Isaac, only to miraculously stop him at the last moment as Abraham held a knife above his bound and trembling boy. This event occurred centuries before John Calvin pondered over a similar idea in I Kings, where God is said to 'wish that the false king Ahab be deceived', using the devil to fulfill this wish by sending a lying spirit among all the prophets.

Calvin found this passage particularly powerful, as it challenged the long-held notion that God cannot will evil. For centuries, theologians like Augustine had tried to absolve God from any wrongdoing by distinguishing between what God wants and what He allows. However, Calvin argued that scripture clearly shows that God does more than just allow evil; He actively deceives. He likened it to the infinite and inscrutable nature of God  a being who hates all liars, commands truth, yet seems to lie nonetheless. While Calvin admitted that God might never actually lie, he acknowledged that understanding this paradox is beyond our human comprehension.

Even those who believed that deception was incompatible with God's divine perfection found themselves admitting that God does indeed deceive at times. These theological discussions often take place off the main stage, not in treatises but in commentaries on the Bible, sermons, and letters  especially when they delve into the life of Christ. After all, why did God need to send His only Son to redeem humanity? And why did Jesus conceal His divinity upon arriving on Earth, choosing instead to appear as a man named Jesus?

Gregory of Nyssa, Augustine's contemporary, suggested that we interpret Jesus' life as a 33-year-long ruse. The devil had initially deceived Adam and Eve by making them believe that eating the fruit from the Tree of Knowledge would not lead to death but grant them divine wisdom. Deceived, they ate, and in doing so, became slaves to the devil, subject to suffering and death. To counteract this web of despair, Gregory argued that God chose to deceive the devil just as the devil had deceived Adam and Eve.

Panama City, Panama  After nearly a decade of expansion work, Panama is set to officially open its expanded canal this weekend to accommodate larger cargo ships, aiming to boost transit revenues and global trade. The grand inauguration ceremony will take place on Sunday along the banks of the canal. President Juan Carlos Varela will unveil the newly constructed locks and third shipping lane added to the existing 102-year-old canal.

Foreign dignitaries, including the presidents of Taiwan, Chile, as well as other Central American nations, are expected to attend the ceremony. A Chinese class cargo ship will be the first vessel to test the new infrastructure, making its way through from the Atlantic and exiting into the Pacific a few hours later. The newly accessible Neopanamax vessels, which measure approximately 50 feet wider and 1,000 feet longer than the previous Panamax ships, can now navigate the expanded canal.

Annual cargo volumes are projected to double over the next decade, with Panama hoping to triple the $1 billion in shipping fees it currently receives annually. Given recent linkages between Panama and the "Panama Papers" scandal involving offshore businesses owned by the world's wealthy and influential figures, the expanded canal is seen as an opportunity to rehabilitate the country's tarnished image, with the head of the Panama Canal Authority, Jorge Quijano, stating to AFP this week, "This will show the 'real face of Panama.'"

In addition, world trade stands to benefit from the canal's role as an inter-oceanic highway for goods between the United States and Asia. With more cargo being transported on larger ships, lower transportation costs are expected. Furthermore, Panama has its sights set on tapping into the lucrative market of transporting liquefied natural gas (LNG) between the United States and Asia, particularly to Japan. The older canal was unable to accommodate LNG vessels due to their size; however, with the expansion, these ships can now traverse the waterway.

"The inauguration of the expanded Panama Canal represents new possibilities for international trade," stated President Varela. Currently, around five percent of global maritime commercial traffic utilizes the canal, offering a valuable shortcut between North America and Asia.

text: ‘If there’s one theme in all my work it’s about authenticity and self-expression,’ said the philosopher Bernard Williams in an interview with The Guardian in 2002. Authenticity was already an influential cultural ideal during Williams’s lifetime (he was born in 1929 and died in 2003) but it has become only more so since. What is more familiar and compelling than the injunction to be true to oneself, to keep it real? Williams explored the force and appeal of this ideal, and his work still helps us makes sense of it. But, as he was also keenly aware, being true to yourself can be dangerous. In his essay ‘Moral Luck’ (1976), Williams discusses Paul Gauguin’s decision to leave Paris in order to move to Tahiti where he hoped he could become a great painter. Gauguin left behind – basically abandoned – his wife and children. This was on the face of it a very selfish thing to do, and you might think that Gauguin’s action was morally indefensible. Williams, however, thinks that Gauguin’s eventual success as a painter constitutes a form of moral luck, in that his artistic achievement justifies what he did. It provides a justification that not everyone will accept, but one that can make sense to Gauguin himself, and perhaps to others. ‘Look,’ we can imagine Gauguin saying to himself, ‘I was right … I knew I had it in me.’ Williams imagines Gauguin to be conflicted. He also freely admits, however, that his ‘Gauguin’ is not necessarily true in all details to the historical French artist. Williams introduces Gauguin as a useful prop in a thought experiment designed to explore the role that authenticity, achievement and luck play in justification. Williams also just assumes, for the purposes of argument, that Gauguin did in fact succeed, which is to say that Gauguin did create valuable art, and that this art was a great expression of his gifts as a painter. The story of moral harm in pursuit of art, with the overall endeavour somehow justified by the art, is a familiar one both in fact and fiction. The fiction tends to be written by men, and their protagonists tend to be men. Life imitates art and vice versa, and in reality those who justify their selfishness (and worse) in the name of art tend to be men too. Williams, however, does not discuss in any detail the harm caused by Gauguin: nor does he discuss the way in which many of Gauguin’s Polynesian victims were used as subjects (and objects) in his most famous paintings. This is something I will return to in what follows. text:
Discussing the fascinating world of penile anomalies among amniotes, let's consider the curious cases of spines, bones, or even double heads found in various species - some even sharing these traits simultaneously. This variety has left researchers puzzled about the number of times the penis may have evolved in this group. A standout example is the avian kingdom, where a mere 3% possess functional penises; ducks being the most renowned for their explosive, corkscrew-like counterparts. The remaining birds make do with a structure that serves little purpose or lack one entirely. In vertebrates devoid of a penis but still capable of internal fertilization, the solution lies in the cloaca - Latin for 'sewer'. This multi-purpose structure handles the exit of bodily fluids, including semen. Animals can perform what is affectionately known as the 'cloacal kiss' to facilitate semen transfer by bringing their respective cloacas together. Among reptiles, this practice is rare, with only one species - the tuatara - employing it. Given the tuatara's ancient lineage dating back to the dinosaur era, the absence of a penis suggests that subsequent lineages developed the appendage. However, the diversity in penile structures among amniotes raises the question: How many times did the penis evolve anew? The responses have been inconsistent, with few proposing a single origin.

The enigma was finally unraveled by studying the tuatara - or rather, ancient samples of tuatara embryos. In the early 20th century, a zoologist named Arthur Dendy, working in New Zealand, sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. Decades passed before these specimens were rediscovered, preserved in time. Their revival provided fascinating insights: the embryos were at the right developmental stage for researchers to observe whether the animals initiate a rudimentary penis before discarding it, similar to how human embryos develop and later lose their tails. Upon recreating these tuatara embryos digitally, scientists observed that, where penises begin to form, the tuatara embryo indeed started to develop one. However, living tuataras born around the time these eggs were collected demonstrate that this initial penis never fully materializes - it's a false start in their development.

This evidence from the tuatara provides conclusive proof that a penis, whether it survives into adulthood or not, is rooted at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the genetic blueprint for creating a penis evolved only once among our lineage. The process for building one appears to be consistent across different amniotes, using the same raw materials as us.

But the most revealingly naive prediction was this, scheduled to come true a mere 18 years from now: ‘The ability to control the genetics of humans, animals, and agricultural plants will greatly benefit human welfare; widespread consensus about ethical, legal, and moral issues will be built in the process.’ This is truly a marvellous apogee of technocratic utopianism. Global agreement on ‘ethical, legal, and moral’ issues has been out of reach for all of recorded human civilisation, at least through the traditional means of reasoning and persuasion. Just because we might become as gods with regard to the molecular building blocks of life doesn’t mean we won’t continue to bicker and squabble. After all, that’s just what communities of gods spend most of their time doing in polytheistic mythologies. Or perhaps we are meant to read this future consensus as one propagated by morally improving chemistry. But such obligatory improvement of all citizens will be a kind of neural slavery in the service of a totalitarian liberalism. And so we come full circle: in a future of ubiquitous mandatory enhancement, the truly radical act might be, after all, to downgrade yourself.

text: to the pain and even to the walls themselves. What would drive someone to do this? Recall Shane Bauer’s description of solitary confinement as an ‘abyss’. An abyss is a chasm without edges. It’s an emptiness that has become palpable and insistent, like a black hole that sucks everything into itself. As a phenomenologist, I would say that the abyss is an experience of space unhinged from the world and from the sense of an ‘elsewhere’ or ‘otherwise’. It is an experience of space without horizon, without the basic coordinates of ‘here’ and ‘there’ by which we orient ourselves in everyday life. The prisoner who bashes his own body against the walls of a rec yard is both refusing and confirming the abyss of solitary confinement. The self-battering body makes a statement of sorts: these walls might confine me absolutely, but I absolutely refuse to be confined! There is a world out there – a ‘there’ to which my ‘here’ is correlated – and I will find it, even if I have to hurl myself against it, or destroy myself in the process. This kind of resistance might be self-defeating, but it remains an eloquent expression of the depth of emotional and ontological harm that prolonged solitary confinement can inflict on a person. Some prisoners find other ways of coping with the exhaustion of spatial horizons. Robert King, who was convicted of robbery in 1969 and spent 29 years in solitary confinement in Louisiana, writes: Some days I would pace up and down and from left to right for hours, counting to myself. I learned to know every inch of the cell. Maybe I looked crazy walking back and forth like some trapped animal, but I had no choice – I needed to feel in control of my space. Like the act of bashing one’s body into walls, the habit of pacing both resists and reinscribes the limits of extreme isolation. But pacing develops a more sustainable coping mechanism, a way of feeling in control of your space even if you don’t have the power to change places. The prisoner who paces both refuses to sit still within his allotted space, and refuses to destroy his bodily integrity by bashing against the walls. He affirms himself as a living, moving being, even though the world has been diminished to the point where he is no longer able to live and move freely. if our ‘here’ is intertwined with their ‘there’, it cannot help but affect our own capacities to see, hear, and make sense of our lives Pacing does not express an acceptance of limits, but rather a nervous retracing of them, a habit formed around the impossibility of habituating oneself to what is an intolerable situation. In this sense, it is a way of coping, but even this coping mechanism is still a pathology that can become its own

Herodotus didn’t follow his own argument through to its logical conclusion – that the voyage to the underworld in Book 11 of the Odyssey, and perhaps all of Odysseus’ wanderings, must also be fictions. But a later geographer named Eratosthenes pressed Herodotus’s argument home in exactly this way. The entire tale of Odysseus’ wanderings, Eratosthenes claimed – including such memorable episodes as the Cyclops, the six-headed monster Scylla, and the Lotus-eaters with their intoxicating fruit that takes away all desire for effort or action – was nothing more than an exokeanismos, an ‘Oceaning-out’ of the narrative line. Readers might derive pleasure from it, but they couldn’t learn about the geography of the Earth from it. For Eratosthenes, Ocean therefore became associated not only with fiction, but with the kind of wholesale inventions we today group under the rubrics ‘science fiction’ and ‘fantasy’. It formed an alternative universe, complete with its own exotic peoples and monstrous life forms – the kind of realm we moderns, almost invariably, locate in outer space. text:
Doubting Thomas has been unfairly characterized as a symbol of weak-willed faithlessness. Instead, his steadfast loyalty to Jesus, despite initial disbelief in stories of His resurrection, should be commended. True skepticism lies not in blind disbelief, but in the balanced suspension of judgment until there is enough evidence to support a belief.

The early Church's insistence on a legalistic, faith-based version of Christianity set the stage for the Enlightenment and modern science to challenge the Church's claims. The Church required weekly professions of faith, which inadvertently provided ammunition for skeptics who demanded evidence to support their beliefs.

In contrast to Thomas, contemporary skepticism is more about going along with the status quo out of a sense of obligation, rather than expecting it to deliver on its promises. We all have our own versions of Thomas' stories  the translucent wraith seen at a stately home or the disembodied footsteps heard upstairs when everyone is asleep. These tales are no more believable than a long-range weather forecast, yet they occasionally crossed the line between highly unlikely and just barely credible. However, as the culture of spectacle has become more prevalent, it seems that the laws of physics have no greater claim to truth than poorly produced YouTube videos or other sensational claims.

The distinction between the visible and invisible, material and spiritual, phenomenal and noumenal has blurred over time. They are now mutually permeable, with each affecting the other in ways we can't fully understand or explain. We find ourselves peering into the heart of things, trying to make sense of the mysteries that surround us.

text:
The role of harmful mutations and stringent selection by natural processes has long been debated. It's not uncommon for an organism to function just fine even with modifications or loss of certain genes, as other genetic factors step in to compensate, or because a single modification exerts only minor effects. For instance, many known gene variants strongly linked to severe human diseases are the norm in other species. Even whole-genome sequencing studies have revealed that we all carry a considerable number of nonfunctional or significantly disrupted genes, including those implicated in various disease contexts, despite our own good health.

This information may seem puzzling: genes, being molecular entities, are fundamental agents of life, yet their effects are probabilistic and difficult to predict. Don't be alarmed; although genetics and evolutionary research can get technical, the concepts are essentially straightforward. A comprehension of how life and evolution function as a system of uncertainties can lead us to a deeper understanding of our inherent nature and why.

Let's delve into the intriguing subject of behavior, specifically free will. The insights from genetics and evolution undercut the explanatory power of molecular reductionismthe idea that genetic variants can be used to predict not only physical traits but also higher-level phenomena like calculus skills or poetic creativity. For scientists endeavoring to decipher life's complexity, this might be a challenging phase, much like winter. However, it's essential to remember that complex organization arises from the intricate web of interactions among various causal factors.

Even though individual factors cannot be solely blamed for specific developments, complex phenomena such as people, skills, personalities, languages, and even sports teams manifest tangibly, and they have a material rather than mystical basis. In fact, emergent complexity poses the same challenge in diverse contexts like biology, ecology, anthropology, sport, and free will.

However, here lies the predicament we mentioned earlier: if science asserts that the world is purely material and governed by universal causal laws, then the notion of personal responsibility for our thoughts and actions becomes vulnerable. Traits like personality, intelligence, criminality, political preferenceeven moral decisionsin principle should be predictable from our inherent genetic makeup. Yet, our thoughts and actions appear to be even more elusive than physical traits like diabetes or height, which we've seen to be highly complex in their causation. Is this merely a temporary limit in our scientific knowledge, or...?

text:
The portrayal of East Europeans during the early Cold War era as helpless victims under foreign oppression was a simplistic perspective that failed to capture the diverse experiences within Communist Eastern Europe. This narrative, while widely accepted at the time, implied that East Europeans were not responsible for their governments' actions and that none truly supported Communism, but were instead forced into compliance.

However, historians of Eastern Europe in recent decades have challenged this notion, revealing a more nuanced picture. Despite the Iron Curtain being perceived as an impenetrable barrier, East Europeans had access to ideas and information from the West. Western radio stations like the BBC, Voice of America (VOA), and Radio Free Europe (RFE) played a significant role in this regard. RFE was specifically created in 1950 to broadcast to Eastern Europe, presenting itself as a non-governmental organization funded by donations from ordinary US citizens  the 'truth dollars' aimed at enlightening captive East Europeans. In reality, RFE received funding from the CIA.

RFE's mission was to provide a counterpoint to Eastern Europe's Communist-led news media, which they deemed as 'fake news': propaganda with no basis in fact. They aimed to challenge this propaganda by exposing its lies and transmitting objectively true news stories to Eastern Europe. However, their anti-Communist ideology heavily influenced their sense of what was newsworthy, leading to biased broadcasts that often presented as the truth.

One of RFE's programs, 'The Other Side of the Coin,' was dedicated to exposing lies in Communist news. This show, which had separate Czech and Polish versions, would repeat stories from the local news and then provide the "real" facts. In a May 1951 episode for the Czech version, the narrator stated that the Czechoslovak press heaped up so many lies that the general rule of thumb was to always assume the opposite of what the Communist-dominated media asserted. Some believed racial discrimination didn't exist in the US because they had read about it in Polish newspapers.

To gauge the impact of their broadcasts on Eastern Europe, western radio stations like RFE relied on refugees who had illegally crossed the Iron Curtain. Data gathered from these refugees suggested that audiences at home considered western radio broadcasts as more truthful and objective than their local news media.

text: To display Félix González-Torres’s ‘Untitled’ (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991), the museum must acquire a supply of the appropriate wrapped hard candies and display approximately 175 lbs’ worth in a pile on the gallery floor, available for audience members to take. To display Drifting Continents (2009) by El Anatsui, a massive wall-hung work made of liquor bottle tops connected with copper wire, the museum must come into possession of the panels painstakingly fabricated in Anatsui’s studio, but the installers have the latitude to make decisions about how to fold, drape, and orient them to best suit the space and their own taste. To display Jana Sterbak’s Vanitas: Flesh Dress for an Albino Anorectic (1987), the museum must fabricate a new dress out of salted flank steak and allow it to desiccate over the course of the exhibition. Rules for display govern both what to display – specific objects created by the artist, a type of object the artist has designated, or a physical array newly constituted for the occasion – and how to display it: whether in a very specific physical configuration, or in one of a range of ways defined by the artist’s aesthetic sensibility or by some other set of criteria. Untitled (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) by Félix González-Torres. Courtesy the Art Institute of Chicago/WikipediaThe second group are rules for conservation. These have to do with which elements of a work must be preserved for the work to maintain its material integrity. Rules for conservation are important in shaping how the material components of a work will appear to future viewers, but also in identifying which elements are essential to the work’s ongoing existence. When Murakami created abstract paintings whose paint is designed to flake away over time, he embraced within the works a process of change that is normally treated as damage to be prevented and repaired. Whereas paintings have traditionally been intended to have a relatively stable surface and be conserved with the goal of maintaining it, Murakami redeploys painting as an essentially time-based medium. Finally, we have rules for participation. These specify what, if anything, we as audience members may or must do to experience the work. The works in Jill Sigman’s Hut Project (2009-) are designed not just to be appreciated for their visual aesthetic, but to set up social situations: we are invited to participate in various ways, from contributing a small note or drawing, to coming in for tea. Paul Ramírez Jonas’s The Commons (2011), a monumental sculpture of a riderless horse atop a pedestal, offers a cork surface pierced with thumbtacks as an invitation for viewers to contribute whatever items or messages they like, making the work into a site of collective communication that papers over the object the artist supplied. The Hut Project (2009-) by Jill SigmanThe Commons (2011) by Paul Ramírez Jonas. Materials: cork, pushpins, notes

text: The robots come to Mars out of a sky tinged peach from whirlwind-lofted dust. They cut through the thin, cold air in supersonic aeroshells and parachutes, making planetfall in bouncing airbag cocoons and bursts of braking retrorockets, bristling with cameras and spectrometers, antennas and manipulator arms. Some stay where they land; others rove for years on end. Sniffing the air, sifting through soil, wheeling across a cratered landscape, the robots doggedly seek any wisp of Martian moisture, like lost desert travellers dying of thirst. They are looking for life, following a strategy that NASA officials describe as ‘following the water’. This strategy pays heed to water’s eerie harmony with life: wherever one is found on Earth, the other isn’t far behind. There are good reasons to believe that water is the ideal cornerstone of biology, not only on Earth, but elsewhere in the Universe. All you need to make it is hydrogen and oxygen, respectively the first and third most abundant elements in the cosmos. In liquid form, water is unparalleled in its attunement to life’s needs. It facilitates the transmission of biochemical energy, nutrients, and waste. It shapes the structures and interactions of proteins and other macromolecules. It shields against hostile cosmic radiation, and it has a remarkably robust capacity for retaining warmth. Most strangely, unlike almost every other substance in the Universe, when water freezes, it expands instead of contracting, forming a protective, insulating shell of ice at its surface. This helps oceans, lakes, and other liquid reservoirs avoid freezing solid when exposed to prolonged cold. For researchers who study Mars, following the water has paid off handsomely. Every mission sent to Mars seeking water has found it and, as a result, we now know that our neighbouring world used to be a warmer, wetter, more habitable place. Billions of years ago, that all changed, as the planet cooled and lost most of its air and water, and settled into a quiet senescence. But present-day Mars still harbours a slumbering aquasphere, locked in the ground as ice, which may stir every so often, erupting to the surface in evanescent briny flows. Officially, the next stage of NASA’s Mars exploration programme is to ‘seek signs of life’, a phase that supposedly began in the summer of 2012 with the successful landing of the car-sized Curiosity rover. It’s true that Curiosity can sniff out organic carbon in samples of Martian rock and soil, but it mostly seems to be following the water, too. So far, it has rolled through what looks to be an ancient dried-up riverbed, and found evidence suggesting that some of Mars’s ancient water was fresh enough to drink. Whether there is or ever has been anything on Mars to actually drink it is, surprisingly, not a question that Curiosity is well-equipped to answer, though it should have lots of time to try. Analysts anticipate that OPEC will prioritize maintaining market share over production cuts to boost prices. By 0300 GMT, US benchmark West Texas Intermediate for January delivery was trading at $41.94, up 29 cents from the previous day's close, while Brent crude for January stood at 44.79 a barrel, rising 18 cents.

Bernard Aw, market strategist at IG Markets in Singapore, commented, "Crude oil remains soft ahead of the OPEC meeting, as many investors expect the organization to remain firm on its production target." Iran is expected to announce plans to expand output after crippling western sanctions are lifted under a landmark deal reached with major world powers in July aimed at curbing its nuclear program. The lifting of these sanctions will allow Iran to increase oil exports, further contributing to the global oversupply of crude oil.

Iran's deputy foreign minister Abbas Araghchi stated last week that his country expects the deal to take effect early next month, once Tehran has fulfilled its commitments. Oil prices have been on a downward trend since mid-2014, when they reached above $100 per barrel, with the decline picking up pace after OPEC's November 2014 meeting indicated that output would remain high.

Furthermore, data released Tuesday showed that a key measure of China's manufacturing activity fell to its weakest level in more than three years in November, underscoring weaknesses in the world's largest energy consumer. text:
This novel, "Infinite Jest," feels incompatible with human life as we know it. It's a sense of radical and pervasive evil, not just a characteristic but an essence of conscious existence. It's like a poison that seeps into the very core of one's being at its most fundamental levels. It's a nausea that reaches down to the cells and the soul... It's hard to put into words, really, except as a sort of catch-22 where any decision or action associated with human agency  sitting or standing, doing or resting, speaking or keeping silent, living or dying  feels not just unpleasant but truly terrible. No description I've come across has managed to capture quite so vividly and straightforwardly the agony I experienced during those moments. The book's most famous lines revolve around suicidality, and the inescapable logic it brings with it. It compares it to the choice faced by someone trapped inside a burning building, deciding whether to jump: Don't be fooled by those who leap from burning windows. Their fear of falling is just as great as ours, standing safely on the ground or peering out from the same window; that's the constant fear... It's not a desire for the fall; it's terror of the flames. And yet, no one standing outside, yelling and urging you to hang on, can truly understand the terror beyond falling. The suicidal person, in essence, is not misguided but rather facing choices that are unimaginable to those who do not also feel the flames closing in.

I can't quite explain what reading all this meant to me. This book seemed like a mirror at that moment, reflecting my own feelings back to me. More concretely, I found a powerful validation of my unique sense of this 'mental illness.' It wasn't seen as some mistake or irrational reaction on my part, but rather as something happening within me  a looming shadow, as the book often refers to it  and all my dread and despair were just logical responses. But once I finished reading "Infinite Jest," my understanding of myself  and thus my will to live  began to fade rapidly. It was only a few days later that I tried to take my own life. At that point, the book had stopped keeping the flames at bay for me. I believe it had been keeping me alive up until then.

That's why, when he came to my door... (the rest of the essay continues)

The best description we can make of ourselves is as storytellers of a sort.’ However, the Harrisons’ emphasis on dialogue and storytelling closely paralleled one of the characteristic traits of the fringe survivalist movement. This community’s literature abounds with stories about how society collapsed, and provided a necessary prelude to detailed narratives about how prepared individuals kept themselves and their loved ones alive. As the sociologist Richard G Mitchell noted after his participant-observation studies of survivalists and preppers, the heart of their endeavours is ‘constructing “what if” scenarios in which survival preparations will at once become necessary and sufficient’. Creative and artful in their own grim way, these ‘survival scenarios’ are, Mitchell wrote in Dancing at Armageddon (2001), ‘akin to contemporary legends told in future tense’. In a sense, their projections of the present into the future are morality tales about who will survive (and how) a coming catastrophe caused by nuclear war, economic collapse, communism or natural disaster. Sketch for Survival Piece V: Portable Orchard (1972), at California State University, Fullerton. Courtesy the Whitney Museum of American Art, New YorkThe Harrisons deliberately aligned their art-making with a similar focus on preparedness. By 1974, they had made seven installations in the Survival Pieces series. Works such as Portable Fish Farm and Portable Orchard (1972) were created, the Harrisons said, to offer people ‘a self-sufficient survival resource’. Their blend of research, art-making and storytelling was predicated on fears about the future, aimed ‘to teach lessons about survival in an ecologically blighted world’. But they also contained seeds of hope for averting undesirable futures. After the Survival Pieces series, the Harrisons grounded their artworks even more deeply in narrative and storytelling. The best example of this is their monumental installation The Lagoon Cycle. Conceived and created over a decade (1974-84), this seven-part epic – it is 360 ft long and 8 ft tall – is organised as a dialogue between two characters, ‘the Lagoon Maker’ and ‘the Witness’. The former, a devotee of technological solutions infused with hubris, proposes increasingly elaborate ways to carry out sustainable food production – what if the Salton Sea in southern California could be reclaimed, its pollution sent to the Pacific Ocean, and used as a large-scale fish farm? The Witness, in turn, reacts to these schemes, encouraging their partner to stop thinking like a technocrat and consider the real-world ecological effects of their schemes. The cycle’s final part, titled The Seventh Lagoon – The Ring of Fire the Ring of Water (1984), ends with a map of the world, its coastlines now underwater. The Witness asks of their partner: and in this new beginningthis continuously rebeginningwill you feed me when my lands canno longer produce?and will I house youwhen your lands are covered withwater?and togetherwe will withdrawas the waters riseFrom The Lagoon Cycle, The Seventh Lagoon

Rabada was undoubtedly the standout bowler of the match, claiming three wickets for 31 runs, highlighted by a destructive opening burst that saw him take the wickets of both openers within the first eight overs.

After Parnell dismissed Andre Fletcher in his first over, Rabada induced an edge from Johnson Charles, which was caught comfortably by Chris Morris at second slip. Bravo and Pollard responded with determination and aggressive strokeplay to counter the rampant South Africans, but the big innings came to an end when Bravo was caught at long-on off Morne Morkel. ISLAMABAD: England and Wales Cricket Board (EWCB) director Andrew Strauss announced on Wednesday that a points system will be implemented across all formats for the upcoming series against Sri Lanka and Pakistan this summer. Known as the Super Series, the competition begins with the first Test between England and Sri Lanka at Headingley on Thursday.

As is customary, individual series trophies will still be up for grabs in the Tests, ODIs, and one-off T20Is against both touring sides. However, a new system will also be introduced whereby each side can earn four points for a Test victory, two points for a draw, and two points for a win in a white-ball match. An overall win may not be secured until the final match of each series, which will be the T20Is this summer and an unspecified prize of 25,000 will be put up by the home board.

Strauss conceived the idea himself and explained the reasoning behind it at a press conference at Headingley on Wednesday. "Today I am introducing the Super Series," Strauss said. "The winner of this competition will be declared the overall tour winner for both the Sri Lanka and Pakistan series across all formats."

According to Strauss, the aim is to ensure that cricket continues to evolve and develop in a meaningful way. "We believe that the Super Series will provide the necessary context and relevance for every game of cricket played," he said. "By connecting the formats, we can ensure that every match counts for more than just itself."

Two separate white-ball teams and a red-ball team will be united by something greater than their immediate interests, without replacing any of the individual series awards this summer. "There will still be a Test series victory plus the one-day international series award, but there are some worrying signs, so we need to act now before it's too late," Strauss added.

text:
Athletics, as we know it today, is often seen as a great equalizer in society, a meritocratic arena where anyone, regardless of their social class or wealth, can strive to become a professional athlete. Modern developed societies have made sports accessible through widespread youth programs and mandatory physical education classes that cater to individuals across the socioeconomic spectrum. However, this is starkly different from ancient Greece, where only a select few could participate in sports.

In ancient Greek cities, only male citizens were allowed to train and compete in sports. Even among this group, participation was limited by informal constraints. Most Greeks worked on small farms and lived close to subsistence level, with little time for long training sessions or extended absences to train at the gymnasiums located in city centers. This excluded a significant portion of the population: women, foreigners, non-Greeks, and poor men. Thus, athletics was closely associated with a specific political and socioeconomic group  relatively wealthy Greek males.

The Greeks themselves had little insight into the origins of athletic nudity, which was another striking aspect of their sports culture. Athletes in ancient Greece were always expected to train and compete naked. The word 'gymnasium' comes from the ancient Greek word 'gymnos', meaning 'naked'. While we no longer associate this term with its original meaning, it originally referred to a 'naked place'. The reasons for nude athletics remain elusive. Although many texts from classical Greece survive, they provide little insight into the meaning of nudity in society or art.

The few sources that touch on naked sport suggest that the Greeks themselves were unsure about its origins. Some attributed it to the Spartans and Cretans, implying that it set them apart as a civilized people. However, there is no clear explanation for how, when, or why this cultural practice emerged. Archaeological and iconographic evidence offers more insight. The earliest coherent collection of images of nude males in the Aegean takes the form of diminutive bronze figurines found in ritual deposits from just a few sites.

Reading through a collection of profound and multifaceted books regarding my country's dark past, I couldn't help but let out a childlike cheer when the Nazis in Tarantino's Inglourious Basterds were engulfed in flames. Despite his intellectual prowess, the movie struck a chord deep within him, stirring emotions he'd harbored for years. In an interview with Die Zeit, Tarantino was asked about German reactions to Inglourious Basterds. His response was thought-provoking: "Besides the Jews, I believe the last three generations of Germans are the ones who have dreamt of killing Adolf Hitler." American history and German imagination - Tarantino nailed it both.

Tarantino isn't the first American director to follow a film about Nazis with one about American slavery, as Spielberg did when he made Amistad after Schindler's List. Both films serve as reminders that we shouldn't use Nazism to end discussions on evil but to start them, and that our own crimes demand the same level of scrutiny. In Django Unchained, Tarantino took it a step further than Spielberg in Amistad, by making the film's only decent white character German. The role could have been any European, but Tarantino reinforced his character's identity with frequent references to Germany. Furthermore, he made us confront our prejudices by having Christoph Waltz, the actor who played one of the most memorable SS officers in cinema history, be the only white person in Django to find American slavery utterly abhorrent.

The German influence on Tarantino's films is apparent in Django, reflecting the German concept of Vergangenheitsaufarbeitung. During the filming of Inglourious Basterds, he spent six months living in Berlin, long enough to grasp how Germans continue to confront their troubled past head-on. An interview with Gates for The Root illuminated just how deeply this influence seeped into his work: "I think America is one of the only countries that has not been forced, at times by the rest of the world, to look its own past sins squarely in the eye. And it's only by doing so that you can possibly move past them."

He doesn't shy away from drawing direct comparisons. If there were a Nuremberg trial, he says in the same interview, then D W Griffith, the director of The Birth of a Nation - the film credited with rekindling the Ku Klux Klan - would be charged with war crimes. And The Clansman, the book upon which that film was based, stands alongside Mein Kampf for Tarantino in terms of its heinous imagery: "It is evil. And I don't use that word lightly."

Some critics have questioned whether a white director should make a film about slavery, but... well, let's just say it's a conversation worth having, shall we?

They were sick of being told that pragmatism was the philosophy of American democracy, that Dewey was the great American intellectual figure of their century, and the like. Gaining a clearer perspective on labor inequalities, specifically the unpaid work often performed by women, can provide an unique epistemic vantage point that's usually unavailable to many men. This idea is central to feminist epistemology  the understanding that our personal experiences significantly shape how we perceive and understand the world, as well as how our perspectives are received. Miranda Fricker's work has highlighted how stereotypes, social hierarchies, and traditional gender roles impact the acquisition, dissemination, and credibility of knowledge, coining the term 'epistemic injustice' for forms of injustice often overlooked before.

Fricker also drew attention to two specific types of epistemic injustice: 'testimonial injustice,' where one's testimony is not considered credible because they belong to a group subject to prejudice (such as women, LGBTQ+ individuals, or people of color); and 'hermeneutical injustice,' where lack of interpretive resources make it difficult to understand one's own experiences due to dominant assumptions and meanings. For instance, the concept of sexual harassment has helped women make sense of uncomfortable experiences that were previously dismissed as mere flirting by others.

Similarly, Kate Manne in 'Down Girl' (2019) shed light on a systemic bias in public life and politics, misogyny  defined not as individual hostility towards women but as a social mechanism for controlling and punishing women who challenge patriarchal norms. This helps explain many experiences that women encounter daily. Both philosophers have drawn our attention to sources of harm that were previously unseen or overlooked. They also introduced concepts that enable us to see things differently, because there are aspects of reality we might not perceive without the appropriate conceptual tools.

These works build upon the feminist tradition, such as Simone de Beauvoir's 'The Second Sex' (1949) and philosophers like Nussbaum, who have argued that freedom and rights mean little if our circumstances prevent us from acting upon them. The idea that legal barriers may fall but actual equality might still not be achieved has been a recurring theme in feminist thought since at least the 17th century  for example, in the work of writers like Mary Astell, Mary Wollstonecraft, and Harriet Taylor.

We must also remember that non-white thinkers, such as Sojourner Truth, Anna Julia Cooper, Audre Lorde, and W E B Du Bois, who belong to groups more marginalized than most white women, have long been arguing that their knowledge is not recognized or given sufficient credit. In essence, they've challenged the myth of a 'view from nowhere.' These thinkers remind us that epistemic injustice is not a new issue  it has been a concern for centuries. However, understanding and addressing it can help bring about much-needed change in our society.

Memory isn't always black and white, it's more like a blurred snapshot from a distance. We might clearly remember the event itself and who was involved, but the peripheral details can sometimes be hazy. This is because our mind is often juggling multiple tasks under high-stress situations, which can affect how we perceive and recall events.

The identity of the person responsible for such an incident becomes engraved in our memory due to our survival instincts. We need to remember them to avoid them in the future. The environments where we share our story and the people we discuss it with can influence the way we recount the event. In certain situations, we might even intentionally mislead others for self-protection reasons - for instance, if we fear that sharing the truth could put us or someone else in danger.

In my personal experience, as an adult, it took me some time to pinpoint the exact timeline of the incidents I experienced. I had to go through old photos, recall connected events at school, and gather the courage to ask questions from my mother.

The need for consistency also pushes victims to create a straightforward narrative that's easy to remember. Susan Brison, a professor of philosophy at Dartmouth College in New Hampshire, writes about her harrowing experience of being raped and left for dead while out on a walk in France. When she finally testified in court, she felt a sense of relief because she no longer had to keep the story straight. Any minor discrepancy could have been used against her. She lived in constant fear of forgetting or misremembering details, especially during the trial when the perpetrator was present. After her testimony, Brison could finally think freely about the attack - what she remembered, how it affected her, and how she made sense of it all. For months before that, her brain had been stuck on repeat, struggling to move forward.

Brison argues that this pressure to maintain a perfectly consistent account can hinder the healing process. Of course, when someone's future and freedom are at stake, there needs to be a way to verify credibility and ensure a fair trial. However, in cases like the Kavanaugh hearing, accusers aren't just expected to testify with a consistent narrative and maintain it during cross-examination. They're also held accountable for statements made on text or email, social media, or to colleagues, therapists, friends, and family. Any differences across these domains will likely be used to question their credibility, stall the investigation, and (hopefully for the defense) exonerate the accused.

In the chilling spring of 1764, the citizens of Milan bore witness to an appalling act - the execution of Bartolomeo Luisetti. Accused of sodomy, he was subjected to asphyxiation and then burned at the stake, all in full public view. Across Europe, those in power believed that justice must not only be served, but also seen to be served; that punishment must be harsh, instilling fear in the masses. Cesare Beccaria, a young man of just 26 years, was present, his heart heavy with disgust and disbelief. It seemed unthinkable that such barbarity could pass as reason.

Born in 1738 to a prominent Milanese aristocrat, Beccaria's early life was marked by the strict teachings of the Jesuits at a school in Parma. His studies in law at Pavia only furthered his disillusionment with society. But it was through Montesquieus Lettres persanes, an epistolary novel offering a critical perspective on Parisian customs, that Beccaria's eyes were truly opened. He embraced the ideas of the Enlightenment without hesitation, finding inspiration in French and British intellectuals such as Claude Adrien Helvtius, Denis Diderot, David Hume, and John Locke.

In 1759, Beccaria joined a group of young Milanese aristocrats who rebelled against the oppressive local elite, including their own families. They saw the interests of the few as consistently outweighing those of the many, and the laws as tools for strengthening the privileged class. Their collaborative efforts led to the creation of a reform-focused magazine called Il Caff. In 1762, Rousseau's The Social Contract was published, providing Beccaria with an ideological foundation for his beliefs. Two years later, in 1764, Beccaria penned his treatise On Crimes and Punishments, which would be published 25 years before the French Revolution.

Beccaria's manifesto against cruel punishment quickly spread throughout Europe, sparking reforms of oppressive institutions across the continent. At that time, Europe was a deeply hierarchical society, where a select few held unaccountable power over the masses. Enlightenment thinkers saw inequality as the root of social injustice, and Beccaria sought to radically transform his society through reforms of its institutions and laws. This ambition was shared by all Enlightenment thinkers, though the methods for achieving this transformation varied. France chose revolution, while Milan pursued a more gradual approach, with Beccaria and his peers playing significant roles in shaping the city-state's administration.

European radical thinkers recognized the Church as one of the strongest enforcers of inequality and subjugation. The ideas of Niccol Machiavelli continued to inspire Italian political minds, emphasizing the role of Christianity in perpetuating such issues.

In many academic disciplines, morality plays a central role. Among these, normative ethics stands out as one of the least detached approaches to studying morality. Unlike other fields that examine how people and societies perceive and discuss morality, normative ethicists strive to determine what is genuinely good or bad, and why, without the influence of societal biases or individual perspectives.

Meta-ethics, a related philosophical subfield, takes a step back to analyze the nature of these inquiries made by normative ethicists. It ponders whether there are objectively correct answers regarding good and bad, or if morality is instead an illusion, mere opinion, or subjective based on personal beliefs. Over the past decade, much of my work has been dedicated to meta-ethics. I firmly believe that there are truths about what is right and wrong in terms of objective moral values. In other words, torture isn't wrong because we disapprove; rather, it hurts people immensely, making it morally unjustifiable.

I also believe that these objective moral truths can be discovered and that some individuals are more adept than others at understanding them. If you prefer, you could refer to them as 'moral experts.' However, not everyone shares my convictions. Some people may simply be confused, equating 'objective' with 'universal,' 'innate,' or 'following a few absolute principles.' But many others harbor deeper misgivings about moral objectivity. Specifically, they argue that morality must have a solid foundation to exista foundation they believe is nowhere to be found. Others aim to prove that there is indeed a firm foundation for morality.

In my opinion, both sides of this debate are on the wrong track. Morality is objective but does not necessitate or accommodate a foundation. Instead, it exists independently, much like the evaluative realm as a whole. While parts of morality can be explained by other components, the entire network of good and evil is intrinsically complex and cannot be reduced to simple principles.

You might find this perspective strange or even worthy of dismissal. I once felt the same way. For example, consider a possible situation where Aristotle took up gardening instead of his brother who was taught by Plato and taught Alexander  something that didn't happen historically but could have. In such a scenario, 'Aristotle' would refer to the wrong person if it merely meant 'the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander'. Kripke highlights that if 'Aristotle' is defined as 'the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander', then it would be incorrect to claim Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander  a potentially contested historical fact rather than a trivial truth.

Kripke proposed that common nouns like 'gold', 'water', and 'tiger' also refer directly instead of via some mental checklist. We define 'gold' as "that substance" (pointing to a sample) and let the world determine what qualifies as gold rather than any description associated with it. The example Putnam used to illustrate how the name of a substance functions in this way is not his own, but belongs to another influential figure, Putnam himself.

Imagine two Earths: one identical to ours (Earth), and another (Twin Earth) where humans' doppelgngers live. The only difference between them is that the liquid filling their oceans, lakes, and rivers on Twin Earth isn't H2O but another substance  XYZ. In 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered, both Earths' inhabitants called this liquid 'water'. They associated the same mental checklist with the term: clear, thirst-quenching, boils at 100C, and so on. Now suppose a glass of XYZ is brought from Twin Earth to Earth and presented to Locke. Given it matches his mental checklist for 'water', Locke would believe it's water. However, would he be correct?

This fits in well with the particularists’ view that evaluation is always a holistic matter.
