text:
The violence perpetrated by the LRA in the context of so-called love marriages is indeed disturbing to our contemporary notions of love and marriage. However, it doesn't mean that their relationship was inherently violent or meaningless, nor was she a passive victim of a sexual crime. Labeling their relationship as a 'crime against humanity' oversimplifies the situation and ignores their unique experiences, simply because it challenged the relatively recent and not universally accepted idea that marriages should be between two consenting individuals who are already in love.

Forced marriages were just one of the many ways in which the LRA violated the principles of human community membership, according to some Acholi civilians. These civilians questioned the humanity of the LRA because they associated them with the 'bush' (lum), a space considered cruel and inhospitable for human life by ordinary people. While people might venture into the lum for firewood or hunting, they didn't view it as an ideal living environment. The LRA was seen as dangerous wild animals - lions and elephants - wreaking havoc and violence upon civilians. However, the rebels experienced the lum differently; to them, it represented life, purity, and development. Their base camps in the bush were not unlike their civilian counterparts' homes in terms of daily activities such as cooking, bathing, etc. They found solace, affection, and training in the bush, even considering it sacred at times.

Rebels also built holy spaces (yards) where they derived supernatural powers through spiritual practices to heal and perform miracles. Compared to Acholi social life, which they saw as rife with drunkenness, adultery, and laziness, the lum was a space of discipline and hard work. They abstained from alcohol in the bush, enforced strict rules against adultery, and worked tirelessly, traveling long distances through dense vegetation.

The LRA practiced what they called 'gorilla warfare', which differed significantly from traditional guerrilla tactics. While many civilians claimed that the rebels had lost their humanity and become mere animals, this was not entirely accurate. They had transformed themselves into something more - cunning, fierce, and respected gorillas. Identifying as animals wasn't a fall from grace but an elevation to a higher status.

Many rebels eventually returned to civilian life, either through capture or desertion. Civilians and rehabilitation organizations who processed returning rebels were wary of them, showing a mix of fear and pity. The returning rebels underwent close scrutiny as a result.

text:
Under a silver-washed sky, a balmy summer's eve about a year since the catastrophic tsunami, Fukuji found himself peering through a veil of mist, trying to discern two figures walking along the beach. A woman and a man, his heart skipped a beat as he recognized his own wife amidst the fog. He called out her name, and she turned, offering him a weary smile. It was then that Fukuji could see who the stranger was  a man he knew all too well, someone Fukuji's wife had loved before their marriage. Both perished in the tsunami.

"I am married now," his wife said over her shoulder, her voice cracking with sorrow. "To this man." The words hung heavy in the air like a shroud, and Fukuji felt his breath catch in his throat. "But don't you love our children?" he cried out in desperation. His wife paused at that, and the floodgates of tears broke open.

Fukuji stood there, lost for words, staring at the ground beneath his feet as if searching for answers. When he looked up again, the couple had vanished like ghosts into the misty night.

This tale, as poignant and tragic as it may be, is said to be true. Or at least that was the intention of the man who penned it, Kunio Yanagita. A pioneer in Japanese folklore, Yanagita traveled from the quaint villages of Tno in Japan's northeastern Thoku region, collecting stories like this and publishing them as Legends of Tno in 1910. His mission was to reawaken a sense of wonder and connection with nature's enigma  the unknown  that he felt city dwellers had begun to lose amidst the clamor and smog of urban life.

Fast forward to today, and eerily similar stories are being shared across Japan. On March 11th, 2011, a devastating earthquake shook the Thoku coastline, giving birth to an unforgiving wall of water that surged inland, swallowing everything in its path  homes, offices, bridges, vehicles, and lives. Television footage captured from helicopters revealed familiar landmarks replaced by desolate muddy landscapes, as the fabric of daily life was torn apart and carried out to sea or scattered across a disfigured, unrecognizable terrain.

People searched desperately for loved ones, first through phone calls and later by sifting through the debris left behind as the waters receded. Some continued their search for weeks, months, even years, as the death toll mounted towards 20,000. It wasn't long before survivors began reporting sightings of spectral figures  people dressed in warm clothing during the summer months, hailing taxis that vanished into thin air or toys belonging to lost children moving on their own accord.

One woman even opened her door to find a drenched stranger standing there, asking for a change of clothes. When she returned with something to offer him, an entire crowd of soaking wet people had gathered at her doorway. Here was a new kind of reality, one that defied explanation and left many questioning the boundaries between life and death.

text: Melancholy is not even restricted to human beings: Burton claims that one variety, love melancholy, can be found in horses, fish, peacocks and even palm trees, which have yearned so much to be together that their trunks have grown bent towards each other. Such stories were irresistible material for Burton, who had an eye for the exotic and the bizarre, and whose work goes far beyond the genre of a medical textbook. The Anatomy’s title page advertises that it will explore melancholy ‘with all the kinds, causes, symptoms, prognostics and several cures of it … philosophically, medicinally, historically opened and cut up’, and its source material includes not only medical tracts but poetry, the classics, religious treatises, chronicles and even the works of Geoffrey Chaucer and William Shakespeare. Burton’s ambitious project was not easily contained within two covers. After its first appearance in 1621, the Anatomy reached four further editions by the time of his death and a sixth, posthumous edition in 1651. By that time, it had swollen from its initial (and already substantial) length of 350,000 words to more than 500,000 words. Burton could not let his subject-matter lie. With each new edition, he added extra case histories of melancholics; further causes, symptoms or treatments; and, as a skilled literary writer, new ways of expressing an old condition. His favourite rhetorical technique is congeries, the piling-up of words. In the first edition, he described melancholy in its early stages as ‘a most delightsome humour, to walk alone, and meditate’. In successive editions, he added to his description, until by the sixth it had become ‘a most delightsome humour, to be alone, dwell alone, walk alone, meditate, lie in bed whole days, dreaming awake as it were’. But, he noted, this happy condition does not last for long. The more melancholy is indulged, the more it takes hold, until fear and sorrow supplant the pleasurable sensations: ‘so by little and little, by that shoeing horn of idleness, and voluntary solitariness, melancholy this feral fiend is drawn on’. The melancholic becomes ‘a cankered soul macerated with cares and discontents, tedium vitae, impatience’, to which Burton added, in later editions, ‘agony, inconstancy, irresolution’. There is something symptomatic in these ever-growing word-heaps. They reflect the dangerously proliferating nature of melancholy, visible not only in the expanding subject-matter but in the perspective of the author. ‘I would … make an antidote out of that which was the prime cause of my disease’ Burton – as Democritus Junior, though with repeated references to his own biography – embeds his own experience within his text, claiming that: ‘I write of melancholy, by being busy to avoid melancholy.’ Since idleness is the great cause of the condition, there is no better preventative measure than busyness. text: synthesis that draws inspiration from African ethnophilosophy while addressing the concerns of non-African philosophies. Ethnophilosophy is a term made popular by the African philosopher Paulin Hountondji, who used it in a pejorative sense to describe the philosophical enterprise that seeks to define traditional African worldviews as philosophically valuable and – for some African scholars – a unique African contribution to humanity’s philosophical heritage. Hountondji is right to have rejected the idea that ethnophilosophy is substantive African philosophy. But he should have seen the great value of ethnophilosophy as a wellspring of African ideas. Western philosophers such as the productive Thaddeus Metz at the University of Johannesburg, who ventured to become specialists in African philosophy, have benefitted greatly from ethnophilosophical resources. Ethnophilosophy, consisting of African traditional worldviews and the writings of professional philosophers on traditional African phenomena, supplied me with the building blocks of consolation philosophy. From the monistic worldview of the Idoma people of Central Nigeria I borrowed the idea of mood as the essence of things. From Senghor, I borrowed the idea of the melancholy being, and proceeded to weave an elaborate philosophy of human existence. From African philosophers such as Placide Tempels, Asouzu, Ramose and Chimakonam, I borrowed the idea of an interdependent universe of missing links, the cosmic picture of the African extended-family system vouched for by Julius Nyerere and Senghor. Consolation philosophy is African philosophy. It’s nevertheless universally applicable as I believe its submissions are sufficiently general to interest the European, the American, the Indian and the Chinese. My thoughts have continued to evolve, steadily enriched by my interaction with my fellow African philosophers, the Western analytic and continental philosophers, and my budding interest in Oriental philosophies. My own struggle of reason as an African philosopher is negotiating a philosophical compromise from my position between the devil and the deep blue sea.

Failing a world in which we ‘know what to do’, French philosophy since Derrida’s generation has continued to work both within phenomenology and without, and has continued to tackle the big questions of traditional philosophy. As we have seen, a great deal of this work serves to disrupt conventional ascription of knowledge and meaning to a neutral white consciousness or to any sort of transcendental realm, outside of the system. But attempts have also been made to move in the other direction, beyond phenomenology – or to reject it outright – by thinkers who are concerned that this becomes too much of a human-centred approach to philosophy. The world having meaning only in terms of humans, they argue, runs the risk of divorcing us completely from the ‘real world’ – the things themselves. text:
Ren Descartes' dog, Monsieur Grat (or 'Mister Scratch'), was a frequent companion on the philosopher's contemplative walks during the 17th century. Despite this close bond, Descartes often undervalued the inner lives of nonhuman animals. In a letter from 1646, he stated that animals do not speak as we do because they lack thoughts, not vocal organs. This perspective, which still lingers in some circles today, posits that consciousness is a uniquely human trait facilitated by language and communication. According to this view, animals are seen as nothing more than sophisticated machines with pre-programmed behaviors triggered by specific stimuli or conditions. Insects such as bees and ants have been used to illustrate this mechanistic approach to animal nature. While we know that these creatures possess a vast behavioral repertoire necessary for building their complex nests, defending against threats, and caring for their young, many continue to view them as reflex machines with no consciousness or awareness of the world around them. In the absence of external stimuli or internal triggers such as hunger, it's assumed that their minds are empty and their brains switched off.

However, there is growing evidence suggesting that consciousness-like phenomena may not only exist in humans but also in insects. Not all of this evidence comes from experiments specifically designed to explore consciousness; in fact, some has been overlooked for decades or even centuries. Recently, several biologists (Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney) and philosophers (Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra) have argued that these consciousness-like phenomena might be much older than previously thought. They propose that consciousness may have evolved during the Cambrian era, around 500 million years ago, suggesting an ancient evolutionary origin.

text:
In the small town of Steubenville, Ohio, two local high school football players were involved in a harrowing incident that left many across the nation bewildered. Over the course of an evening, one of the players took advantage of their victim, sending explicit texts and photos to friends, as well as onlookers capturing the event on video. This heinous act was witnessed by numerous people who, instead of reporting it or helping the victim, chose to stay silent and continue partying.

The events that unfolded in Steubenville are a stark reminder of the power of social norms and their impact on our actions, especially when it comes to sexual assault and bullying. Fraternities, sports teams like the local football team, and even the military share many similarities with more collectivist societies: they value conformity and punish those who defy the group's social norms.

Alan Berkowitz, a California psychologist specializing in rape prevention, explained that bystanders often hesitate to intervene due to fear of violating social norms. 'In moments like these, all sorts of barriers come into play,' he says. 'You might be afraid of embarrassing someone else, or being retaliated against, or thinking no one else cares.' In a powerful peer group, such as a group of young men, the pressure to fit in is immense.

Social norms themselves aren't inherently good or bad; however, in some collectivist cultures, they can encourage members to look the other way when faced with sexual assault. Steubenville isn't New York City, where the Kitty Genovese narrative suggested that city size and anonymity caused widespread apathy. The tragedy of Steubenville demonstrates just how flawed this assumption is  even if every single witness or partygoer had acted, it wouldn't have saved the victim if they were all bound by strong social norms that discouraged intervention.

The rise of social media has only intensified the bystander effect, creating more witnesses and offering them a cloak of invisibility. On the night of the assault, someone posted a photo on Instagram of the victim, unconscious and being carried by her attackers. Friends of both the perpetrators and victims exchanged messages, sharing details about the evening's events, while one witness even took a video of the unconscious girl being sexually assaulted (claiming to have deleted it the following day). A particularly disturbing YouTube video also surfaced, featuring someone describing the sexual assault and mocking the victim as 'dead.'

In the era of social media, the bystander effect persists, but we must strive to overcome it. Each person has the power to make a difference  even if it's just reporting what they've seen or helping the victim in any way possible. By standing up for others and breaking free from harmful social norms, we can create a culture that supports survivors instead of perpetuating their pain.

Judges may provide instructions about 'explainable' discrepancies to juries, but these are unlikely to challenge deeply ingrained cultural narratives.

text: One’s faculties don’t always shut down, but they are engaged in high-stakes multitasking. This can explain the fuzzy contours of some of our peripheral perceptions, even as we maintain with crystal clarity the memory of the physical act and who did it. The identity of the perpetrator is reinforced after the fact by survival instincts, because we need to remember whom to avoid. In the aftermath, the contexts in which conversations take place, and the types of interlocutors we talk to, can affect both the precision of our narrative and how we reconstruct the story for ourselves. In some settings, we might have good reason to intentionally misdirect the listener – if we fear that the person might try to seek revenge against our perpetrator, for example. Here, inconsistency can be a form of self-protection. In my case, as an adult, it took some time before I could establish the time period in which my assaults occurred. I had to look at old pictures, recall related events at school in order to figure out what grade I was in, and gather up the courage to direct some questions to my mother. Secondly, the demand for consistency encourages victims to create a narrative that can be memorised and rendered in simple form. Susan Brison, a professor of philosophy at Dartmouth College in New Hampshire, writes in her book Aftermath (2002) about being violently raped and left for dead while out on a morning walk in France. When she finally gave her testimony in court, her immediate feeling was one of relief that she would no longer ‘have to keep the story straight … I could let go of the details I’d kept alive in my mind.’ Any small deviation could have been used to discredit her. She had lived with great anxiety over her ability to memorise and repeat what happened, especially in court, in the same room with the man who had tried to kill her. With the testimony over, Brison was finally able to think freely about the attack: what she remembered, how she understood the event, how it affected her. Her brain could then continue to operate, after months of being stuck on repeat-mode. That is why the demand to maintain a perfectly consistent account, she argues, can ‘impede the process of recovery’. Of course, when an accused person’s future and freedom are at stake, there needs to be some method of ascertaining credibility and ensuring a fair trial. But today, as in the Kavanaugh case, accusers must not simply testify with a consistent narrative and maintain consistency in cross-examination. They are also held accountable for other statements they might have made on text or email, on social media, or to co-workers, therapists, friends and family. text: identifies two functions of practical reason that the Stoics used in their quest for a painless existence. There is the indirect function, on the one hand, where careful planning and forethought allow the Stoic to pick out and follow the least painful path through life. On the other, there is the direct function, where instead of removing or avoiding obstacles in life’s path, the Stoic reconsiders these obstacles in a way that changes his feelings towards them. One is a change in practice, while the other a change in thinking. Stoicism’s distinctive contribution to ethics lies in the nature of the change in thinking it recommends, according to Schopenhauer. First, the Stoic observes that painful feelings of privation ‘do not follow immediately and necessarily from not-having, but rather from wanting-to-have and yet not having’. It then becomes obvious that to avoid these painful feelings altogether, we must eliminate the wanting-to-have part. Furthermore, the bigger our ambitions about what we want to have and the higher our hopes of achieving them, the sharper the pain when we fail. If we cannot help wanting to have some things, then we should at least keep those wants within realistic and achievable proportions. Perhaps lapsing back into his own pessimism, Schopenhauer adds that we should become suspicious of ourselves if we begin to expect a great amount of happiness waiting for us in the future; we are almost certainly being unrealistic. ‘Every lively pleasure,’ he says, ‘is a delusion.’ ‘The external motive for sadness plays the same role that a blister remedy does on the body’ Thus the Stoic aims for ataraxia, a state of inner calmness and serenity however turbulent the world outside might be. Schopenhauer believes his observations about the inevitability of suffering can help to achieve this aim if taken on as convictions. Pain and suffering sting all the more if we think they are accidental and could have been avoided. While it might be true of any particular suffering that it could have been avoided, suffering in general is unavoidable and universal. If we manage to take this on board, Schopenhauer thinks, we might worry less about encountering suffering, or at least worry about it in the way that we worry about other things we can’t avoid, such as old age (for most of us) and death. The last thing we should do is believe the opposite: that we are destined to find happiness in life rather than encounter suffering. If we believe the world owes us happiness, we are bound to be sorely disappointed, not least because, when we do achieve whatever we think will make us happy, we will have new unfulfilled desires that will supersede the old ones. We are also bound to feel resentment towards the obstacles that stand between us and the happiness we feel entitled to. Some people, Schopenhauer observes, concentrate and externalise this

text: his prose fiction, Beckett increasingly felt able to break down what might be expected from a novel and from a story. By the time he came to write his major postwar novels – the ‘trilogy’ of Molloy, Malone Dies and The Unnamable (all first published in French, between 1951 and 1953, and later adapted by Beckett into English) – the accommodation of the mess was complete. Molloy is narrated by a man who is unsure whether he’s writing a diary or dictating to an audience. He often finds himself at a remove from his body, with a disintegrating sense of self and world. And he sees himself as a kind of ‘contemplative’ who has, in true Schopenhauerian fashion, abandoned his will to live: My life, my life, now I speak of it as of something over, now as a joke which still goes on, and it is neither, for at the same time it is over and it goes on, and is there any tense for that?Molloy’s narration eventually collapses midway through the novel. He starts to doubt whether he is really telling us his own story or is rather making it up, compelled by some other presence, some voice that pushes the whole novel onwards. Perhaps, Molloy wonders, he is ‘merely complying with the convention that demands you either lie or hold your peace’. As the trilogy progresses, each subsequent narrator confesses that he’s the author who’d made up the previous narrator. The fictions keep collapsing, keep revealing themselves as fiction, but we don’t seem to get any closer to the authorial presence underneath all the stories. It seems then that Beckett’s experiments with quietism in the 1930s eventually led to an aesthetic of incoherence, mess and powerlessness that shaped his most celebrated novels. ‘I’m working with impotence, ignorance,’ Beckett told one interviewer. ‘I don’t think impotence has been exploited in the past.’ But what of the preoccupation that we started with? That wish to be free from anguish and pain? That hope for some kind of salvation? ‘It was always there I huddled, in the innermost place of human frailty and lowliness’ In his later years, Beckett occasionally seemed keen to distance himself from soteriological systems. In 1986, just three years before his death, he told an interviewer that, unlike Schopenhauer, unlike Leopardi and unlike certain ‘oriental thinkers’, he didn’t propose a ‘way out’ or a ‘hope of an answer’ or a ‘solution’. The only solution, Beckett said, grimly, was death. While this might seem to be the final word on the matter, it’s worth recalling that, for the quietists such as Molinos, the truest beatitude – and perhaps even the truest salvation – was found in the renunciation of hope for salvation. The theologian William Inge called this ‘the mystic paradox’ in a book on Christian mysticism that Beckett read and took notes on in the 1930s.

In the heat of the moment, one must consider their own character and that of their audience, their aims and the hurdles they face. The sophist, on the other hand, employs dishonest and inappropriate methods to achieve equally questionable outcomes. It was never in doubt that Christ's actions, disguises, and seemingly deceptive words were righteous, wise, and just, although it was often simpler to assert this virtue than to justify it. Whether successful or not, portraying God as the ultimate rhetorician, the ultimate orator, intensified the divide between these two ways of perceiving God. After all, rhetoric is about adapting to the situation; it demands adjustments and reactions, a constant readjustment of our interactions with others. It was this need to adapt, to change, to act and react, to speak and deceive that seemed so alien to the philosophical conception of God as eternal, perfect, and unchanging.

As it transpired, distinguishing miraculous interference from deceit proved challenging. At the turn of the 14th century, another source of tension between scripture and philosophy made the threat of divine deception more palpable. Aristotle's writings, which had been lost to Europeans for centuries, were beginning to resurface during the 12th century. Intellectuals, always eager for the new, integrated Aristotle's works into the curriculum of the various medieval universities. Aristotle's philosophy often contradicted scripture rather than complementing it. According to Aristotle, God  the 'Unmoved Mover'  was part of the universe, the best part, but still part of it, shaping and energizing it according to the necessary and immutable dictates of reason itself. Theologians could not stand idly by as students and teachers absorbed this diminished image of God. Scripture taught that God transcended a universe he had created out of nothing. God is omnipotent, they asserted, he can do anything. To demonstrate God's omnipotence, theologians described all the ways God could miraculously interfere with the ordinary course of nature. It turned out that distinguishing miraculous interference from deception was problematic.

Early in the 14th century, William of Ockham posed a thought experiment to his readers. Imagine you are gazing at a star. Now, imagine that God, who can do anything, destroys the star while maintaining your vision of it. What you now see is a non-existent star. From theological thought experiments to the daily life of the Church, the possibility of divine deception seemed to arise everywhere. Robert Holkot asked his readers to ponder the celebration of the Eucharist. At the moment of consecration, God miraculously transforms a piece of bread that never ceases to look like anything but a piece of bread into the very body of Christ. The question of divine deception was becoming increasingly relevant in both scholarly discourse and daily Church life.

These means of control reflect the true lesson behind the microbiome: not that nature is inherently harmonious and cooperative, but that all the best relationships take work. text:
Putnam and David Kaplan challenged the prevailing notion by suggesting that many terms refer not through objects matching a mental criteria list but directly. According to Kripke, when we introduce a proper name like "Aristotle," it's usually connected straight to an individual. For instance, during a baptism ceremony, we might say: "By 'Aristotle,' we mean him [pointing]." Our intention is that the name should then refer to that person in any actual or hypothetical situation, even when they may not fit the descriptions associated with the name.

Let's imagine a possible scenario where Aristotle took up gardening while his brother was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. That didn't happen, but it could have. Kripke notes that if "Aristotle" simply meant 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander,' then the name would point to the wrong individual in that hypothetical situation. It seems plausible that the actual scenario might one day be discovered to be one where Aristotle had few or none of the attributes we attribute to him. Historians could potentially unearth evidence suggesting that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for others' achievements and managed to deceive people. In such a case, it's clear that the name 'Aristotle' can't only mean 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander.' Otherwise, it would be a straightforward truth rather than a debatable historical fact that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke posits that many common nouns, such as 'gold,' 'water,' and 'tiger,' also refer directly, not through any sort of mental checklist. We define 'gold' by stating: "Gold is this substance [pointing to a sample]" and letting the world determine what qualifies as gold rather than any associated description.

Perhaps the most famous example demonstrating how a substance name functions in this manner comes not from Kripke, but Putnam, another leading advocate of the 'theory of direct reference.' Putnam encourages us to envision a parallel Earth  much like ours  where doppelgngers of humans reside. The only difference between our worlds is that on Twin Earth, the liquid filling their oceans, lakes, and rivers isn't H2O, but another substance  XYZ. Suppose it's 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered. On both Earths, inhabitants call their liquid 'water,' associating the same mental checklist with that term: considering water as clear, thirst-quenching, boiling at 100C, and so on. Now imagine a glass of XYZ from Twin Earth being transported to our Earth and presented to Locke. Locke would believe it was water because it would match his mental checklist. But would it be?

In this revised essay, I've attempted to present the ideas in a more conversational style while maintaining clarity and flow. I hope you find it thought-provoking as you explore the fascinating realm of direct reference theories!

text: chemistry separates the organic from the nonorganic. The vital force was supposed to explain why bacteria shouldn’t spontaneously appear in dirt. In 1859, Louis Pasteur, after whom the process of pasteurisation is named, showed that bacteria cannot spontaneously generate. His conclusion was that all life must arise from other life. But today we know that it is not lack of a vital force that prevents spontaneous generation of a bacterium. Instead, something else is missing: information. By this we mean the structure of the bacterial cell, and the contents of its genome. This information has been acquired over billions of years of evolution and can’t be regenerated instantaneously from systems with no information, no matter how much dirt you have. In a way, this information is the real vital force: it is what separates living from nonliving. However, rather than being some mystical force possessed only by special bits of matter, it emerges over time and space via the process of selection. To understand the origin of life, we must learn what bits of matter can acquire such information, and how they do so. To answer this, we must go beyond the ideas of Claude Shannon, who spearheaded the theory of information in 1948 in part by ignoring meaning. For example, in studying radio signals or other communications, Shannon was not concerned with the content of the message transmitted. Instead, he was interested in whether the bits transmitted reduced uncertainty in a receiver about the state of the sender. To accomplish this, he counted only possibilities. If tomorrow might see rain or shine, both equally likely, and you see a 100 per cent accurate weather report saying it will rain, the number of possibilities is reduced two-fold. You gain one bit of information. The same holds true if you flip a coin and learn which side faces up, or if you observe a black hole to see whether it rotates to the left or to the right. In all these examples, you gain precisely one bit of information by your observation. Information is simply the reduction in the number of expected possibilities, in essence reducing uncertainty. This is true no matter what the message says – the meaning of a signal is irrelevant to Shannon’s theory. If we found dead Martian cats, we will have found life on Mars To develop a theory of life, we need to put the meaning that Shannon removed back into information, and, by extension, also into physics. When billiard balls hit each other, they become correlated – you can predict something about one ball from looking at the other. This is information in the Shannon sense: it is just about reducing the number of possible states that the first ball could be in once you know something about the second. These animals don’t cause as much trouble as marauding gangs of adolescent condors, but they can wind up with their own mysterious behavioural issues. Puppet-reared whooping cranes, for example, have trouble incubating their eggs in the wild; they will often abandon their nests mere days before the eggs are due to hatch. John French, Patuxent’s research manager and head of the crane programme, has a couple of different theories about the crane’s parenting problems. In the wild, nesting cranes are often swarmed by black flies; perhaps the puppet-reared cranes, raised in relative comfort, can’t tolerate the parasites and are driven off their nests. Or perhaps their own puppet parents failed to pass along a crucial piece of information about the breeding process. What the missing piece might be, French told me, ‘I have no idea.’ Meanwhile, the cranes’ failure to raise the next generation on their own guarantees, somewhat ironically, the need for more puppet-rearing. This flawed but seductive logic is taken to its extreme by the burgeoning de-extinction movement It’s tempting to see puppet-reared California condors and whooping cranes as victims of a horrific real-life version of Plato’s cave: after living in a shadow world since birth, they are suddenly dragged out into the blinding sunlight and forced to cope with an incomprehensibly rich and complex reality. Ill-equipped to live in anything but a carefully managed simulacrum of nature, they crack under the pressure. When viewed from this angle, it is hard to imagine why keepers thought puppet-rearing would produce psychologically healthy animals. But like all intensive, hands-on conservation programmes, puppet-rearing was never really about the animals. While the stated goal of any captive breeding programme is to create self-sustaining populations of wild animals that can survive and thrive without human intervention, the true reason for their existence is guilt of a very human variety. We drove the condor, the whooping crane, the panda, and countless other species to the brink of extinction; therefore, we have a responsibility to save them. Usually, our strategy amounts to fixating on the cutest or most symbolic of these threatened species and breeding as many of them as we can. Those offspring either spend their lives in zoos or are released into the very same world that killed so many of their ancestors. Every year, approximately 1,000 Nepali individuals who depart Nepal seeking work abroad end their lives in labor-destination countries, with a staggering 97% of these fatalities occurring in the Persian Gulf. This alarmingly disproportionate statistic raises concerns, as only around half of all Nepali labor migrants travel to this region annually. The majority of these workers are young and otherwise healthy, making their premature deaths even more tragic.

The Supreme Court of Nepal has recently issued a mandamus, requiring the government to perform autopsies on the bodies of deceased Nepali workers who are repatriated to their homeland. Unfortunately, the process of returning these workers' remains can take months due to bureaucratic red tape in Gulf nations. In many instances, families are left waiting for closure, not knowing the cause of their loved one's death.

Recent news reports have detailed numerous strange and suspicious deaths, including homicides, involving low-status workers in the Persian Gulf. For example, the Philippine government has prohibited its citizens, predominantly women, from traveling to Kuwait following the discovery of a Filipina domestic worker's body in her employer's abandoned freezer. The Philippines ambassador to Kuwait reported over 6,000 complaints regarding the mistreatment of Filipino workers in 2017 alone.

Embassies of India, Sri Lanka, and the Philippines have established shelters in Kuwait for their citizens, primarily women, who have fled abusive domestic work situations out of fear for their safety. In another instance, an Ethiopian maid was forced to climb onto a seventh-story window ledge in an apparent attempt to escape her employer. Instead of assisting her, the employer filmed the incident and the maid fell, sustaining injuries including a broken arm.

Incidences of employer abuse and neglect are distressingly common, and many homicides and other mysterious deaths go unreported or are covered up by authorities. African workers appear to be particularly targeted. The lack of transparency surrounding these deaths mirrors the historical misreporting of police killings of young Black men in the United States.

On a more positive note, the government of Qatar has recently announced plans to increase migrant workers' wages by 33% and relax certain kafala regulations. Employers have been granted six months to comply with these changes or face penalties from the government, including potential suspension of their operations. Whether these measures will effectively address the systemic issues plaguing this vulnerable population remains to be seen.

text: as a thinker and writer. His distinction as an intellectual and moralist enjoyed virtually unrivalled status in the West in the second half of the 20th century. He died on 5 November 1997. Shortly after Berlin’s death, his editor and friend, Henry Hardy, described him as, of all the people he had known, ‘not only the most remarkable and delightful, but also, in so many ways, the wisest, the surest in judgment, and the best’. Berlin’s seminal essay on liberty – which, like so many of his essays, began as a lecture – starts by differentiating between two notions of political freedom. The first of these is described as ‘negative liberty’, since it refers to the extent to which I am free from the interference of other individuals or authorities. Being negatively free, according to Berlin, does not mean that I am free from physical or psychological constraints. It simply refers to the degree of freedom from human interference or coercion such as the freedom to be left alone from engaging in politics. Contrasted with it is ‘positive liberty’, so-called because it is the freedom to do something rather than the freedom from something: eg, the freedom to stand for election to parliament. In many ways, this is a far richer, if more nebulous, notion that ‘derives from the wish on the part of the individual to be his own master’. So far, one could be forgiven for thinking that we are being presented with a rather obvious distinction between two sides of the same coin. Berlin doesn’t dissent, acknowledging that both concepts appear at ‘no great logical distance from each other’. But what distinguishes his account is that he treats each of these concepts realistically and historically, showing not only that there is a substantively significant – not merely a logically valid – distinction to be made between negative and positive liberty, but also that the failure to recognise its significance can cause far more harm than mere conceptual confusion. The key point here is that Berlin switches the default understanding of things by making theory more answerable to reality rather than the other way around. This bold move means that history and realism can take centre-stage in making sense of things. And what history and our current experience reveal is that negative and positive liberty respond to real and legitimate human needs and ideals. Negative freedom has been the beating heart of political liberalism, with its insistence that individuals be left alone to their own devices, so long as their actions do not unduly harm others. Similarly, positive liberty has lain at the heart of emancipatory theories of politics from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism. In this vein, Berlin shows that one of the major insights of modern history and contemporary reality is just how catastrophically the concept of positive liberty is vulnerable

text:
Jacques Derrida, born in Algeria and raised in a world dominated by French culture, often referred to it as "the over there." Despite his education being centered around France, his conversations with Mustapha Chrif, a philosophy professor at the University of Algiers, frequently revolved around reconciling religion and secularism. When diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, which would eventually take his life, Derrida acknowledged he had no definitive answer but emphasized the importance of continuing to think about it. He stated, "If we knew what to do, if knowledge could guide our actions completely, then there'd be no true responsibility." This sentiment has echoed through French intellectual circles ever since.

Post-Derrida, French philosophy has continued to navigate both within and beyond phenomenology, tackling traditional philosophical questions while challenging the conventional ascription of knowledge and meaning to a neutral white consciousness or transcendental realm. However, some thinkers have expressed concern that this human-centric approach could alienate us from reality  the physical world  at a time when we're facing environmental crises.

Bruno Latour, known for his writings on the constructed nature of scientific theories and the influence of funding and politics on research, has shifted his stance in recent years. In his article 'Why Has Critique Run Out of Steam?', he advocates for a realistic perspective, arguing that calling something constructed doesn't mean it should be deconstructed but rather treated with care and caution due to its fragility.

Quentin Meillassoux's 'After Finitude' (2008) has been described by Alain Badiou as opening a new path in the history of philosophy. Whether this is indeed a new path remains debatable, but Meillassoux's work is part of what John Mullarkey terms "Post-Continental Philosophy." These thinkers actively engage with scientific disciplines like mathematics, biology, and neuroscience, seeking to bridge the gap between philosophy and the real world.

text:
Understanding the human mind is a complex and multifaceted task, often likened to Maturana's concept of 'observer dependence.' This means that our perception and representation of the world, information processing, and response decisions are primarily influenced by our own perspective, not the system we're observing. The brain's inherent activity follows its own logic, a mystery that neuroscientists, like Buzski, argue should be the primary focus  understanding this logic from within, or 'inside-out.'

Fifteen years ago, I coined the term 'anthropogenic approach' for cognition rooted in human experiences and reflections. This is similar to what Buzski refers to as 'outside-in.' Cognitivism, despite suggesting that cognition can manifest in various physical forms, remains anthropogenic due to its derivation from our numerical computing abilities. The counterpoint I named the 'biogenic approach,' which prioritizes biology as the source of cognition and embraces an 'inside-out' perspective. If our goal is to truly understand human cognition, this biogenic/inside-out path seems the most promising, helping us move beyond a stagnant journey down a dead-end road.

Over the past 70 years, we've invested significantly  financially, intellectually, and effort-wise  into understanding cognition. Yet, our progress has been disappointingly slow. Think of how much society has evolved since the 1950s; countless beliefs have crumbled, and knowledge has exploded across various fields. We can now scientifically justify seeing ourselves in a daffodil, an earthworm, even a bacterium.

The Human Genome Project was initially expected to provide a genetic 'blueprint.' Instead, when the first draft was published 21 years ago, we realized how much remained unknown. For instance, there's too much so-called 'junk DNA' for it to be truly worthless; humans share surprisingly many genes with plants; and our genome contains genes transferred by single-celled organisms. As more whole genomes were compared, we discovered that among these shared genes were those involved in animal central nervous systems, including ours. Moreover, a critical system for human survival  the autonomous immune defense mechanisms at the cellular level  may have been inherited from bacteria or its related kingdom, archaea, potentially millions of years ago.

All this brings us to cognition. It turns out that our memory and learning processes rely on interactions with neurons stimulated by cytokines, elements of the immune system. A real surprise indeed! In the 1950s (and later), the brain was thought to be 'immune privileged,' off-limits to the immune system. Yet, we're still struggling to fully understand this interaction.

KARACHI - Lending to the private sector contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the current fiscal year 2014/15, despite the efforts of the central bank to stimulate credit flow through three reductions in policy rates, data revealed on Tuesday. Analysts argue that banks' heavy investment in government securities and low borrowing costs have failed to spur investment and spending by businesses and consumers.

The central bank stated that loan disbursements to various companies dropped from Rs281.9 billion between July 1, 2014, and April 3, 2015, to Rs178.9 billion during the same period of the previous fiscal year. Analysts contend that banks' massive investment in government treasury bills has limited the availability of lending funds for the private sector, despite a decrease in borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have been actively investing in long-term bills and purchasing PIBs due to high returns. Since November 2014, the cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector.

However, the government disputes the analysts' view that banks are disinterested in extending private sector loans and prefer to park their funds into government papers. The government, in its latest letter of intent to the IMF, stated that private sector credit has continued its healthy expansion (10.2 percent so far in FY15) driven mainly by businesses, and particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict that bank lending will begin expanding in the coming quarters. Within different financing segments, a moderate increase is expected in consumer loans.

The State Bank, with the help of World Bank experts, has developed a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS) to implement financial sector reforms aimed at meeting the financing needs of all sectors. The central bank is likely to launch the NFIS by the end of this year, after addressing complex legal, taxation, and border trade requirements, as well as impaired access to finance.

This impact can be attributed to his direct and indirect influence on key philosophers like Schelling, Hegel, Marx, Kierkegaard, Lukcs, Heidegger, Levinas, as well as neo-Kantians Rickert and Lask who shaped the understanding of Kantian philosophy in early 20th century Germany. However, outside of the confines of Hegel scholarship and the German Idealism traditionwhich includes Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel among othersFichte's philosophical ideas remain largely unknown today. His theories on self-consciousness and social recognition have been eclipsed by Hegel's influence, often reduced to a mere stepping stone leading to Hegel's 'improved' understanding of intersubjective self-consciousness. Regrettably, Fichte's insights on freedom and intersubjectivity have received scant attention outside the narrow confines of his philosophy scholarship, despite being developed prior to Hegel's, and shaping the perspectives of Schelling and Hegel themselves. A reevaluation is overdue.

Genes have long been at the heart of mainstream biological thought, with the discovery and gradual unraveling of the double helix only serving to solidify their central role. The fusion of genetics with molecular biology has indeed spawned a powerful new science. By untangling the molecular interactions within cells, we've been able to create drugs that mimic or interfere with specific processes. The discovery of enzymes responsible for DNA synthesis and editing paved the way for genetic engineering, which is often discussed in terms of its commercial applications, but whose most common use has always been in pure research. In this way, advancements in knowledge give rise to tools that further our understanding, creating a virtuous circle.

However, we seem to have reached a crossroads. As we delve deeper into the molecular processes, the gene-centric perspective becomes increasingly obscure. In the early days of genetics, scientists studying various animals would identify genes and associate them with body-scale features. Yet, they had no way of knowing whether the genes they identified in one species were the same as those known in another. With the advent of technologies for reading DNA sequences, researchers could 'read' their own genes and notice when genes in two different species were remarkably similar. This often yielded surprises. For instance, the gene 'Wingless', initially thought to be a blueprint for making wings (a name given by fruitfly geneticists based on what happens when the gene is damaged), turned out to be essentially the same as Int-1, a gene identified by cancer specialists working with mice and associated with mouse breast cancer. However, Wnt, the protein that these genes code for, isn't really about making wings or causing breast cancer; it's about transmitting signals between cells  the meaning of the signal depends on the cell and organism receiving it. This is just one of many examples. The notion of 'the gene for feature x' is giving way to a much more complex narrative. Consider something like: 'the gene for protein a, which interacts with proteins b, c, and d to enable a cell to carry out process p, which in turn allows the cell to coordinate with other cells to produce body feature x'. The length of this phrase and the weakness of the blueprint metaphor underscore a conceptual chasm opening up between the molecular-scale function of genes and the intriguing large-scale features of bodies.

Genes are important, of course  they're essential because something has to build all these proteins. But the double helix seems less and less fitting as an emblem for the complex control systems that govern life, especially at larger scales (cells, tissues, organisms, populations, ecosystems, etc.). text:
It's an exciting time to witness the mesmerizing displays of these enchanting creatures. What used to require costly and sophisticated equipment is now accessible with devices as simple as a GoPro or a smartphone. Inspired by this, I embarked on a cross-country journey with my lab to capture the synchronous flashes of fireflies. The goal was to forge a stronger bond between the mathematical theories I've grown up with and the behavior of these insects in the wild.

Our journeys took us deep into national forests every spring, setting up camp beneath canopies illuminated by these luminous orbs that, as poet Richard Newman puts it, 'outshine the stars'. Female fireflies wait a full two seconds before responding with their own half-second flash if they're interested. Although animal communication is often associated with ecologists and zoologists, physicists and mathematicians can offer valuable insights into the energetic cost of communicating, the information transmitted, and the recipient's ability to detect the sender's signal.

For instance, anyone who's dabbled in online dating knows the struggle of deciding how much effort to put into a profile, how many messages to send, or which emojis to use. It turns out that these concerns about cost, signal-to-noise ratio, and compressibility are fundamental criteria for almost any communication system. The firefly system, with its traceable mode of communication and the ability to track signals from all individuals in the swarm, offers unique advantages for studying 'mate-search communication'.

Fireflies' captivating light displays are actually courtship rituals. Males fly overhead, advertising their suitability to grounded females through a species-specific on/off light pattern. A good example is Photinus carolinus, a synchronous firefly found in the southeastern United States. Every 15 seconds or so, a male flies about a meter off the ground and emits several quick flashes, one second apart. If a female is interested, she remains close to the ground in low vegetation. Two seconds pass before she responds with her own half-second flash at the third second. The act of flashing is voluntary, triggered by the release of the neurotransmitter octopamine which initiates a chemical reaction in the insect's light-producing organ.

The prevailing theory suggests that fireflies control their flashing by regulating the oxygen supply to their light-producing organs, although this is still under debate. What's clear, however, is that unlike a traditional light bulb, a firefly produces 'cold light', with minimal energy lost as heat. This is crucial for the firefly's survival, as overheating could prove fatal to these delicate creatures. The female fireflies then use their flashes to signal their interest or disinterest.

text:
In the intricate dance of life, it's fascinating when we delve down to the minutiae of cells and genes. If behaviors were solely predetermined, individual organisms would never be able to discover innovative solutions for uncharted challenges, such as the ingenious tool-making abilities exhibited by New Caledonian crows to acquire food resources. This underscores a vital aspect of agency: the capacity to make decisions in response to unprecedented and unexpected circumstances. When a hare is chased by a wolf, it's impossible to predict its zigzagging movements or whether its strategies will be enough to evade the predator who adapts accordingly. Both hare and wolf are exerting their agency. One might question the level of cognitive environment macrophages experience, but the boundaries can blur. Perplexity may arise from the widespread belief that complex agential behavior necessitates an equally complex mind. In the generally tranquil realm of plant biology, a debate is currently raging over whether plants possess sentience and consciousness. Certain activities plants perform, like growing in specific directions based on past experiences, can appear intentional and even 'mindful,' particularly since they involve electrical signals resembling those produced by neurons. Yet if we deconstruct agency into its essential components, we can comprehend how it might emerge even without a mind that 'thinks,' at least in the traditional sense. Agency arises from two fundamental elements: first, the ability to generate different responses to identical (or equivalent) stimuli, and second, the capacity to choose between them in a goal-oriented manner. Neither of these competencies is exclusive to humans or brains in general.

The initial element, the ability to vary a response to a given stimulus, can be easily achieved. It demands only behavioral randomness, such as flipping a coin. Such unpredictability serves an evolutionary purpose, for if an organism always reacted to a stimulus in the same way, it would become an easy target for predators. This phenomenon is observed in nature: a particular species of aquatic snake triggers a predictable escape response in fish that leads them straight into the snake's jaws. An organism that responds differently in seemingly identical situations stands a better chance of outsmarting predators. Cockroaches, for instance, scurry away when they sense air movements but at an apparently random angle relative to the airflow  perhaps to confuse would-be predators. Fruit flies exhibit some random variation in their turning movements even without any stimulus; presumably this is advantageous (for foraging food, say) since it broadens your options without relying on a specific signal to do so. Such unpredictability is encapsulated in the Harvard Law of Animal Behavior: "Under controlled experimental conditions, an animal will behave randomly."

LONDON: History was made at Nottinghamshire's Trent Bridge ground on Monday, as the English One Day Cup match between Notts and Northamptonshire set not one but two new records in a spectacular display of batting. With statisticians scrambling to confirm if such a high-scoring day has ever occurred elsewhere, the big hitting started from the get-go, as Nottinghamshire's Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb shattered the best opening stand record with their 146 runs off just 97 deliveries.

Lumb continued his impressive form to register a personal best of 184 runs - also a new county record for Nottinghamshire - as they piled on an astonishing 445/8, the second-highest total ever seen in a List A match worldwide. The run-fest didn't stop there, as Northamptonshire chased down what would have been another record total for a team batting second, but ultimately fell short despite Rory Kleinveldt's valiant effort of 128 runs. Their total of 425 gave Nottinghamshire a thrilling 20-run victory in a game that saw an unprecedented 870 runs scored - surpassing the previous record set by Surrey and Glamorgan in 2002 by three runs.

Speaking after the match, Lumb quipped, "It was a bit twitchy at times, but what a fantastic game of cricket. Never in doubt." He added, "I was surprised they took it that deep, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was incredible. It was an unbelievable game  I don't think we could have done much more. I'm proud of all the boys."

The Nottinghamshire partnership surpassed the previous English record of 318 runs, set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. The English record-breaking stand is now the third highest in List A history, following on from the previous records set by Gloucestershire in 2007. List A matches are contested over 40 to 60 overs.

text:
Love marriages, as we understand them today, are sacred and founded on mutual consent and affection. However, the relationship between a child abducted by the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and their captor was not just something that clashes with modern sensibilities. It was complex, layered with power dynamics, and far from a simple narrative of victimhood or crime.

To label it as a 'crime against humanity' would be a quick judgment, neglecting the unique experiences of those involved. The LRA's actions were indeed brutal, but condemning their relationships as inhuman is a narrow perspective that fails to consider the nuances of their existence. For the Acholi people, the bush (lum) was not merely a wild space, but one associated with cruelty, madness, and chaos - a stark contrast to 'home' (gang).

To civilians, the LRA were like dangerous animals: lions and elephants that roamed the wilderness, causing havoc and violence. Yet, for the rebels, the bush was their home, a place of life, purity, and development. They lived and worked there, leading 'normal' lives, cooking, bathing, and engaging in everyday activities. The bush held significance beyond its physical aspects; it was a place of affectionate relationships, community, and valuable training.

Rebels even built sacred spaces within the bush, drawing power from holy spirits to heal and perform miracles. In contrast, they saw social life among the Acholi as rife with drinking, adultery, and laziness. In the bush, they abstained from alcohol, enforced strict rules against adultery, and worked hard. This lifestyle, they called 'gorilla warfare', a unique style of battle reminiscent of their primate comrades.

The LRA rebels were often seen as having lost their humanity, reduced to mere animals. But in truth, they had transcended it, becoming tricky, fierce, and respected gorillas. Identifying as animals was not a fall from grace, but an ascension to a higher status, embodying traits that humans often fear or admire in these creatures.

Many rebels eventually returned to civilian life, either through capture or desertion. The civilians and rehabilitation organizations that welcomed them were filled with fear and pity, subjecting them to close scrutiny. Yet, these returning rebels were not the monstrous beings they had once been portrayed as; they were complex individuals who had lived and survived in extraordinary circumstances.

Jorge Quijano, head of the Panama Canal Authority, told AFP that the expanded canal would showcase "the real face of Panama." World trade will also benefit from this new inter-oceanic highway connecting North America and Asia, with larger cargo volumes on bigger ships leading to lower transport costs.

In addition, Panama is eager to tap into the lucrative market of transporting liquefied natural gas between the United States and Asia, particularly to Japan. The old canal was too small to accommodate these massive gas-carrying vessels; however, with the expansion, they can now navigate through the canal.

I couldn't figure out how they made the masala sauce so creamy without using any cream, but it tasted incredible, and I can't bring myself to question their secret recipe!

Next time we visit, we plan on indulging in a few appetizers once again and trying the Bombay Plate to share, which comes with five entree choices and rice. That way, we can sample a variety of dishes without overdoing it. All in all, we spent around $35 before tip for our naan, two appetizers, and two single-choice entrees - not too shabby for a Saturday night out. Or is its national geography the result of a series of decisions and compromises – some of which, if reversed, could have led to a different outcome? The latter view leaves more space for analysis, more chance to examine how power worked during expansion; it’s also the realm of counterfactuals. ‘Native American societies have robust resistance to Old World diseases at the time of contact with Europeans in the 15th century’ One of the fundamental premises of the new pro-counterfactualists is this: just as there are good and bad ways to write standard histories, so too there are good and bad ways to put together a counterfactual. The historian Gavriel Rosenfeld at Fairfield University in Connecticut is working on an edited collection of Jewish alternative histories, and maintains a blog called the Counterfactual History Review, where he aggregates and analyses examples of counterfactualism in public discourse, many of which relate to the Nazi period: Amazon’s recent adaptation of Philip K Dick’s novel The Man in the High Castle (1962); the US presidential candidate Ben Carson’s argument that the Holocaust could have been prevented if Jewish people were better armed; and, yes, the ‘Killing Baby Hitler’ kerfuffle. Rosenfeld argues that a counterfactual’s point of departure from the actual timeline has to be plausible; in other words, it’s much more productive, analytically speaking, to speculate about a situation that was likely to come about, than one that is completely improbable. He also cites a ‘minimal rewrite rule’ that asks the speculator to think about only one major point of divergence, and not to assume two or more big changes in an alternative timeline. The historian Timothy Burke at Swarthmore College in Pennsylvania teaches a seminar on the topic, and wrote on his blog about a class project in which he gave groups of students counterfactual scenarios (‘Mary Wollstonecraft does not die after the birth of her daughter but in fact lives into old age’; ‘Native American societies have robust resistance to Old World diseases at the time of contact with Europeans in the 15th century’) and asked them to game out the scenario in stages. The experience shows students how to use both direct and contextual evidence from our own timeline to support counterfactual assertions. A good counterfactual scenario must be generated with attention to what’s actually known – about the setting, the time, or the people involved. The closer the counterfactual can hew to actual historical possibility, the more plausible it can be judged to be. Growing up, we all had our favorite spots like The Peach Pit on 90210, right? Well, Jimmy and Joe's is the pizza version of that place. It has a definite high school vibe - I felt out of place when I walked in with my 30-something crew. And it brought back memories when they charged me 60 cents for ketchup to dip my wings. Can you remember that feeling when someone says, "I owe you three dollars, and I'll pay you back tomorrow"? Yeah, that vibe is strong at Jimmy and Joe's!

Parking is another issue - there's limited space, and the signs almost make it seem like they don't want you to eat there. I was hesitant to even grab napkins for fear of hidden charges or limitations.

I've dined at Jimmy and Joe's several times, and here's my take: visit the place but don't order pizza to go. They excel in the one-slice-and-drink combo. But if you let their pizza cool down a bit, it loses its flavor quickly. I picked up a pizza once, and it was almost like two different pizzas - the cheese was incredibly rubbery, while the crust was as chewy as steak!

Jimmy and Joe's is perfect for a quick lunch or a business meeting, but I wouldn't recommend ordering multiple pies for a Super Bowl party or if you have guests over. It's just not worth it when they can't seem to get their pizza game back on track. Keep it casual, keep it simple - that's the best way to enjoy Jimmy and Joe's. Hopefully, they'll figure out their inconsistencies soon!

text: The red-veined rocks of Bohuslän in western Sweden have one of the highest concentrations of Bronze Age art in Europe. I was lucky enough to see the carvings during a recent October visit. The site was set back from a road, which marked where the shoreline of the North Sea would have been when the figures were first inscribed, between 2,500 and 4,000 years ago. The petroglyphs had since been painted pillar-box red so that they’d stand out for tourists, and the bright colours and wobbly lines looked like an enormous child’s drawing. But what the scene lacked in elegance it made up for in energy. Bowmen stalked antlered stags among a fleet of longships, flowing into a procession of bulls and thick-bodied giants teetering precariously atop spindly legs. Land and sea, human and animal, swam in and out of view. Bronze Age rock art in Bohuslän, Sweden, assumed to depict the performance of a ritual. Photo courtesy Wikipedia. I found myself trying to create a narrative, some sort of lost epic, as if I were deciphering a Bronze Age cartoon. We don’t know why these inscriptions were made, or what they were meant to say. The blunt-limbed figures give no clue about where such a story might begin, or where it might end. But perhaps that tangled quality is the petroglyphs’ real message. The longships speak of a culture closely aligned with the sea; the many animals point to a strong affinity with nature. Similar sites on the Cornish and Iberian peninsulas indicate that cultural influences travelled along what the Beowulf poet would later call the ‘whale roads’, the open waters that connected Bronze Age Scandinavia to the rest of Europe for the purposes of war and trade. We live in a similarly entangled age, in which our consumption of fossil fuels, nitrogen fertilisers, the cobalt in our smartphones, antibiotics, and plastic all bind us to faraway times and places. The world of the Anthropocene is made from this dense web of relationships, each of which leaves its imprint on the world of the future. Two hundred generations of humans have lived and died since the Bohuslän petroglyphs were chipped out of the granite, but they remain urgent and vivid, the evidence of a living presence. In their silence, they pose certain questions. How and why do we navigate the currents of time and of matter to communicate with the deep future? Is it our intentional signs and symbols that leave the most lasting marks, or our unintentional traces? I live and work in Edinburgh, on the lip of the North Sea. I’ve come to see these waters as a kind of Anthropocene laboratory, somewhere that lets us peer at the long-term impacts of the way we’re living now. Its past can tell us something of our future. text:
Ren Descartes' canine companion, Monsieur Grat (Mister Scratch), was a frequent companion on the philosopher's contemplative walks and enjoyed his affectionate attention. However, for the most part, Descartes didn't hold animals in high regard when it came to their inner lives. In a 1646 letter, he argued that animals don't speak because they lack thoughts, not the capacity to do so. This perspective, which sees consciousness as a uniquely human trait tied to language and communication, persists in some circles today. On this view, nonhuman animals are considered little more than intricate machines with pre-programmed behaviors triggered by specific stimuli. Insects like bees and ants are often cited as examples of this mechanistic approach to animal nature. While we know that these creatures have complex behavioral repertoires necessary for constructing their elaborate homes, defending against threats, and feeding their young, many still view them as mere reflex machines, devoid of an internal understanding of the world or the ability to foresee even immediate future events. When not prompted by external stimuli or internal needs like hunger, it's believed that insect minds are blank slates, their brains switched off. Insects are often likened to philosophical zombies  hypothetical beings relying solely on routines and reflexes with no awareness.

Yet, perhaps the issue isn't that insects lack an inner life but that we struggle to comprehend it. Our inability to fathom their minds might be due to our limited understanding of even the simplest brains' processes, storage, and retrieval mechanisms. However, recent findings suggest that consciousness-like phenomena may not be exclusive to humans or great apes; they might also exist among insects. Not all evidence comes from experiments explicitly exploring consciousness; some has been overlooked for decades, even centuries, without anyone recognizing its significance.

Based on such evidence, biologists like Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney, as well as philosophers like Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra, propose that consciousness-like phenomena may not have emerged as late in our history as previously believed. Instead, they could be evolutionarily ancient, originating during the Cambrian era around 500 million years ago. At its roots, we believe that consciousness is an adaptation that helped animals adapt to their environment and survive. However, much remains unknown about this fundamental aspect of animal nature, and future research may continue to challenge our current understanding.

These curious identity questions don’t yet have obvious answers, and it’s unclear what kind of ancestral relation fission creates. A lot would likely depend on the particulars of the fission involving ageing, growth and, in particular, symmetry. If there’s perfect symmetry during fission, then perhaps Parfit is correct and certain organisms pop out of existence – meaning fission is not only reproduction but also a very strange kind of death. However, if the fission isn’t symmetrical, then some organisms might be more like Lewis’s streets. If this is the case, perhaps colonies of bacteria are composed of many individuals that share a single identity. It’s clear that these kinds of fission meet Griesemer’s criteria for reproduction. But the problem is that fission doesn’t create clear links from parent to offspring: a clear, vertical lineage of descent. This creates difficulties for our notions of reproduction. To return to the questions that opened this essay, how do we define the relationships between organisms created through fission? Does this process produce clones, siblings or just two instances of the original? To see why, let’s return to the Devil’s Ivy. Say someone cuts a shoot from a mature Devil’s Ivy plant and replants the shoot in another pot, where it develops into a mature plant. According to Griesemer’s criterion, this counts as an instance of reproduction. But has the plant really reproduced in the sense of 18th-century-style reproduction? Is the original plant really a parent and the shoot its offspring? It’s far from obvious. First, remember that the shoot and the original plant are genetically identical clones. All that changes is physical connectedness, but cutting and replanting a shoot doesn’t resemble something like human birth. Other creatures are also able to persist through physical disconnectedness, like the ‘immortal’ jellyfish Turritopsis dohrnii which, depending on its environmental conditions, moves between being a physically connected mature medusa to an immature stage of disconnected polyps. When food is scarce or it’s otherwise threatened, this jellyfish can break apart into a colony of polyps, and then recombine again when conditions improve (slime moulds do something quite similar). The bodily transformations of Turritopsis dohrnii make the creatures of science fiction look tame. And then there are cases in the other direction, where two physically disconnected organisms become physically continuous. For example, separate trees (sometimes even different species of trees) can grow together in a process known as inosculation. This happens when the trunks, branches or roots of separate trees come in contact and then, over time, end up grafting to one another. When two physically separate trees grow into one another, do they become a new, singular organism? Philosophers of biology and theoretical biologists are grappling with such questions, dragging the issue of what counts as a biological individual into

text: seeds of their own refutations. So ingrained is this habit of disbelief that it comes to seem as though there is nothing that isn’t part of the scam. True scepticism lies in the considered suspension of belief, the opposite of that state of mind in which, as Samuel Taylor Coleridge suggested, we attend to tales of the otherwise incredible. The patron saint of this true scepticism is Thomas Didymus, or Doubting Thomas, who has erroneously come to be associated with weak-kneed faithlessness, but whose pathos consists precisely in his steadfast loyalty to his late teacher. It is this, after all, that prevents him from accepting what sound like fantastical stories of Jesus’s reappearance. When the resurrected Messiah informs him that it is blessed to believe without having seen, the moral applies to nobody present, as the other disciples have already declined to believe Mary Magdalene. The account is, of course, addressed to succeeding generations, but the salient point is that Thomas doesn’t stand condemned for his fidelity. Even so, the early councils of the Church would not be content with faith as mere obedience to divine precept and the exercise of goodwill to others. Instead, they insisted on a legalistic, faith-based version, in which a weekly statement of what one actually, literally believes is required of its participants. Thus did the Church hand the Enlightenment, and its current crop of science apostles, the free gift of an evidentiary case against itself. The visible and the invisible, the material and the spiritual, the phenomenal and the noumenal are no longer the distinct realms they once were In contrast to Thomas, contemporary scepticism takes the form of playing along with the racket because there seems to be no alternative, while privately knowing that it can’t deliver what it promises. In this cast of mind, somebody’s hyperventilating tale of a translucent wraith seen drifting about the stately home, or the disembodied footsteps that clatter up and down the stairs when everybody is tucked up in bed, is neither more nor less believable than the long-range weather forecast. The dignity of spooky stories was that, unlike obvious tissues of lies, they occasionally managed to cross the divide between the highly unlikely and the just barely credible. If they could never be proved, neither could they ever be disproved — except by pointing to the laws of physics, an alienating language spoken by experts who couldn’t conceal their contempt for ordinary gullibility. Now that so much of the culture of the spectacle evokes the same response, the laws of physics have no greater claim to finality than do poorly produced video-hoaxes on YouTube. The visible and the invisible, the material and the spiritual, the phenomenal and the noumenal are no longer the distinct realms they once were. They have become mutually permeable to their mutual diminishment. We seem to see to the heart of things,
