text:
Translating classics shouldering the weight of their originals - how does that translate on paper? Over the past century, numerous attempts have been made by various translators to tackle this task. What these examples demonstrate more than anything is that success in translation is subjective, and my personal preference leans towards Jeromite's flexibility over Augustinian adherence. Despite centuries of scholarly debate, there seems to be no straightforward answer.

One of the most debated translators of modern times is Ezra Pound. He was both lauded for his poetic masterpieces and criticized for his audacious liberties. Pound's anthology of classical Chinese poetry, Cathay (1915), is a prime example of translation without any particular knowledge of the source language (Pound relied heavily on Ernest Fenollosa's literal translations). Remarkably, this approach produced results that were more convincing than those created by fluent readers of Chinese. Some scholars even argue that Pound's intuitive phrasing was more accurate. This wasn't an isolated incident, as Pound often took similar creative liberties with texts he could read in the original, like Arthur Rimbaud's 'Au Cabaret-Vert' (1870). Here's a stanza from that poem translated by Wallace Fowlie:

For a week my boots had been torn By the pebbles on the roads. I was getting into Charleroi.
 At the Green Cabaret, I asked for bread And butter, and for ham that would be half chilled 

Pound, on the other hand, aimed to capture the carefree spirit of a young traveler. His rendition is casual, succinct:

Wearing out my shoes, 8th day On the bad roads, I got into Charleroi. Bread, butter, at the Green Cabaret And the half-cold ham 

Similarly, Samuel Beckett, while translating a simulated 'delirium' from 1930 by Paul luard, employed obvious anachronism to bring out the intensity of the original:

Thou my great one whom I adore Beautiful as the whole earth and in the most beautiful stars of the earth that I adore thou my great woman adored by all the powers of the stars 

Compare this with Richard Howard's 1965 translation, which, while respectful of luard's modernity, sounds less passionate, oddly out of tune, even old-fashioned compared to Beckett's version.

Lastly, Georges Perec's novel La disparition (1969), with no instances of the letter 'e', was once considered untranslatable in both French and English, the most common grapheme in both languages. However, Gilbert Adair managed to create a lipogrammatic translation, A Void, in 1995, which showcases many clever adaptations.

Title: Unraveling the Secrets Hidden in Whale Baleen

Whales, these magnificent creatures of the sea, have always been a source of fascination for us humans. And there's no shortage of old baleen around from the whaling era. Baleen, much like plastic in our contemporary world, was once extensively used  from corset stays to brooms to buggy whips. Museums seem to have a surplus of it gathering dust in their collections. Recently, I've found myself drowning in offers for collaborations, and I've even heard whispers of half a dozen labs delving into baleen analysis. 'It seems everyone with a hormone lab is jumping on this,' I remarked.

Baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's journey and a reliable seasonal chronometer. The stress hormone, cortisol, has attracted significant attention across wildlife biology due to its potential to uncover harmful influences. Although bowheads are thriving, we must keep in mind that their habitat is undergoing rapid changes  sea ice melting, increasing ship traffic, and the noise pollution from oil and gas exploration in the Arctic. The ultimate goal of our baleen research is to develop methods for assessing stress levels related to environmental change and human activity.

Other informative compounds are also emerging in baleen. Research dating back to the 1980s has shown that fluctuations in the ratio of stable carbon and nitrogen isotopes along the length of bowhead baleen reflect the unique prey the whales consume in their summer versus winter habitats. Essentially, baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. We are examining this pattern in bowhead baleen dating back to 1952 to determine whether the whales' diet and migrations are changing, potentially due to shrinking sea ice. Furthermore, we are studying isotope and hormone data from the same baleen samples to confirm estimates of bowheads' gestation period and calving frequency. We believe this information will help us pinpoint not just when but roughly where individual bowheads conceived.

On rare occasions, even the bodies of these whales serve as historical artifacts. Native Alaskan hunters have discovered remarkably old whaling gear in harvested animals. In the 1990s, a stone tool that had gone out of use in the 1880s was found. 'When I saw that, I just remember... my head spinning,' said Craig George, a bowhead expert and wildlife biologist with North Slope Borough who collaborates on several baleen studies. 'I immediately knew that this was absolutely remarkable.' Over the years, hunters have discovered other artifacts, but dating stone tools isn't always reliable. However, in 2007, a Yankee-era harpoon point with an 1879 patent was found lodged in another whale, suggesting its age to be between 115 and 130 years. George and his team conducted chemical analyses of bowhead eye lenses that ultimately indicated they can live for up to 200 years or more. text: a violence that is particularly upsetting to modern sensibilities about love marriages. But this didn’t mean that their relationship was meaningless or violent in itself, nor that she was a passive victim of a sex crime. To condemn their relationship as a ‘crime against humanity’ is also a way of not thinking carefully about their experience, simply because it transgressed the expectations, new in history and far from universal, that marriages should take place between two consenting individuals who are already in love. Forced marriages were just the beginning of the ways in which the LRA violated the precepts of membership in the human community, according to some. Acholi civilians in particular contested the LRA’s humanity based on where the army had lived and worked for decades – the ‘bush’ (lum). Today, for Acholi civilians, the lum, in contrast to ‘home’ (gang), is the cruel space of animals, spirits and madness, but not of human beings. While ordinary civilians might visit the lum to collect firewood or hunt wild game, they didn’t consider it a space fit for human life. Civilians imagined the LRA fighting famine, gathering wild roots, and literally dirtying themselves in the wilderness. When the LRA came out of the lum to carry out attacks, civilians saw them as they did lions and elephants – dangerous wild animals wreaking havoc and violence. The rebels experienced the lum as a place of life, purity and development. At their base camps in the lum, they led ‘normal’ lives not dissimilar to their civilian counterparts at home – cooking and bathing, among other everyday activities. Many rebels loved the bush – it was a place of affectionate relationships, community and invaluable training. To them, it could be sacred. Rebels built holy spaces (‘yards’) from which they derived supernatural powers through holy spirits to heal and perform miracles. They saw social life across Acholi villages and towns as rife with drinking, adultery and laziness. In the lum, they abstained from alcohol, had strict rules against adultery, and worked hard, battling fiercely and travelling great distances in short periods of time through thick vegetation. They practised what they called ‘gorilla warfare’, fighting in organised military units like their primate comrades. Transformed from ‘guerrilla warfare’, the actions of a small band of insurgents, ‘gorilla warfare’ was an extraordinary style of battle unique to the feared animals that the rebels had taken up. KARACHI: Despite three rate cuts by the central bank this fiscal year, private sector lending contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the 2014/15 fiscal year, according to data released on Tuesday. Analysts attribute this contraction to banks' heavy investment in government securities and low borrowing costs, which have not encouraged businesses or consumers to invest and spend.

Compared to the same period last fiscal year, loans disbursed to various firms decreased from Rs281.9 billion to Rs178.9 billion between July 1, 2014, and April 3, 2015. The central bank reported this figure.

Analysts argue that banks' heavy investment in the government treasury has reduced the supply of lendable funds for the private sector despite falling borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have been actively investing in long bills and purchasing Pakistan Investment Bonds (PIBs) due to their high returns. Following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector, the cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points since November 2014.

However, the government disputes analysts' claims that banks are uninterested in providing loans to the private sector and prefer to park their funds in government papers. In its latest letter of intent to the IMF, the government stated that private sector credit has continued its healthy expansion (10.2 percent so far in FY15), driven primarily by businesses, particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict that bank lending will begin expanding in the coming quarters. Within different financing segments, a moderate pickup is expected in consumer loans.

To address these issues, the State Bank, with the assistance of World Bank experts, has developed a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS) to implement financial sector reforms aimed at meeting the financing needs of various sectors. The central bank plans to launch the NFIS by the end of this year after overcoming complex legal, taxation, and border trade requirements, as well as impaired access to finance.

text:
Let's discuss a more equitable approach in the criminal justice system, rather than trying to "level down" Black people by harshly punishing them. The choice of direction may hinge on one's stance towards life sentences - whether they can ever be fair and justified. Proponents of "uplifting" can't presume that they are, since this is the very question at hand.

One argument for "leveling down" stems from the observation that the US justice system unfairly treats Black and brown individuals more harshly than whites. It seems reasonable to assume that the treatment of whites aligns more with our society's concept of justice, as whites are often seen as the norm or default. People of color, on the other hand, appear to bear an extra penalty rather than whites being leniently treated. To bring us closer to fairness, it might make sense to reduce penalties for Black people instead of increasing them for whites.

It's hard to justify keeping someone in prison for their entire life unless you believe they will always pose a significant threat to public safety or truly deserve such punishment. I've already argued that the majority of the over 200,000 people currently serving life sentences in the US do not present an ongoing threat to the public. Whether they should spend their whole lives behind bars comes down solely to whether they deserve it. Desert can only be established if they are fully responsible for their criminal actions.

Responsibility is best understood as what it excludes - a lack of knowledge, awareness that something is wrong, or inability to control one's actions due to an irresistible impulse. Using these definitions, most people serving life sentences likely bear responsibility for their acts. However, a broader understanding of responsibility is crucial. For instance, the insanity defense can absolve someone legally, although they might still be institutionalized in a psychiatric facility. But there are many more whose responsibility is reduced, even if not entirely absent.

The Death Penalty Information Center's 2021 report revealed that all but one of the 11 people executed last year had significant impairments, such as mental illness, brain injuries or damage, low IQs indicative of intellectual disabilities, or a history of chronic trauma, neglect, and abuse. It's highly likely that those sentenced to life also have high rates of such impairments.

In essence, we must reconsider our approach to justice to ensure fairness for all individuals, especially those who have faced adversity and may have their responsibilities diminished due to factors beyond their control.

text: ‘I was a hooker — an art hooker — for most of my career,’ says Roxx, a tattooist in San Francisco. ‘I’d listen to what people wanted and I would do their things.’ When she suggested her own ideas, many declined — only to return later to confess their regret. Today, no one tells Roxx what to do. Her clients come for a consultation in which she explores who they are and what they want their tattoo to say about them. They tell her where they want the piece and which of her other tattoos appeals to them. She takes notes and shoots photos. Unlike most tattooists, she uses no reference materials or stencils. ‘I just need to feel their energy and ask: “What would suit you? Do you want it to be warrior-like? Badass? Pretty and feminine?”’ A design takes shape, she draws it on freehand, and the work begins. ‘It’s nothing spiritual or philosophical,’ she says. When I ask her what she would create for me based on a half-hour of conversation, she exclaims: ‘I was just doing it!’ And her prescription is perfect, though I haven’t come for a tattoo. We are in 2Spirit Tattoo, her shop on Pearl Street, where she has made her name as one of the most sophisticated and original tattooists in the world, working in a style — called ‘blackwork’ — that few women practice. Her studio is a sprawling open space with natural light, white walls, wood floors, brushed aluminium ceiling lamps, and black leather tattoo tables arranged at each of her four employees’ workstations. One wall near the glass-front entrance is covered in framed photos of her clients showing off their art: precise, geometric, all-black designs that follow the musculature of the body or fan out in lacy arcs between shoulder blades; boldly etched dharma wheels rolling across chests; honeycombed netting nicked from the Filipino Kalinga tradition and tailored like clothing on chests and arms; and in one photo, a simple trio of liquid lines pouring down a woman’s back and flowing around her waist. Roxx’s workstation occupies a back corner of the shop that can be partitioned off with sliding doors. She sits on the edge of her chair, elbows on her knees, talking with increasing animation as the late morning coffee kicks in. Soothing electronica fills the space, softening the buzz of machines wielded by two artists, Michael Bennett and Matt Matik, who chat amiably with their prone clients. Two years ago when I interviewed her for my book Bodies of Subversion: A Secret History of Women and Tattoo, Roxx called her shop ‘San Francisco hippie’, but that merely describes the comfort level; there is nothing hippie about the refined aesthetic of this studio, or her elegant tattoos. In our work, we transform ourselves into narratives and visual stories. We see ourselves as storytellers of a sort, albeit in a unique way. This resonates strongly with the ethos of the survivalist fringe community. Their literature is filled with tales about societal collapse, serving as a prelude to detailed narratives on how individuals prepared themselves and their loved ones for survival.

As sociologist Richard G Mitchell observed during his participant-observation studies of survivalists and preppers, the core of their endeavors lies in constructing "what if" scenarios where survival preparations will become both necessary and sufficient. These 'survival scenarios' are reminiscent of contemporary legends told in future tense, akin to morality tales about who will survive (and how) a future catastrophe caused by nuclear war, economic collapse, communism, or natural disasters.

The Harrisons deliberately linked their art-making with this focus on preparedness. By 1974, they had completed seven installations as part of the Survival Pieces series. Works like Portable Fish Farm and Portable Orchard (1972) were created to provide people with a self-sufficient survival resource, offering lessons about surviving in an ecologically damaged world. Yet, these works held a glimmer of hope for preventing undesirable futures.

Following the Survival Pieces series, the Harrisons embedded their artworks even more deeply in narrative and storytelling. The pinnacle of this is their monumental installation, The Lagoon Cycle. Conceived and created over a decade (1974-84), it spans 360 ft long and 8 ft tall, organized as a dialogue between two characters  the Lagoon Maker and the Witness.

The Lagoon Maker, embodying technological optimism tinged with hubris, proposes elaborate ways to sustain food production  such as reclaiming the Salton Sea in southern California, sending its pollution to the Pacific Ocean, and transforming it into a large-scale fish farm. The Witness, in response, challenges these ideas, urging their partner to think beyond technocratic solutions and consider the real-world ecological consequences of their proposals.

The cycle concludes with The Seventh Lagoon  The Ring of Fire the Ring of Water (1984), which features a map of the world with its coastlines submerged underwater. The Witness asks their partner: will you feed me when my lands can no longer produce? will I house you when your lands are covered by water? and together, as the waters rise, will we withdraw to safer grounds?

From The Lagoon Cycle, The Seventh Lagoon

text:
The philosophical movement known as absolute idealism, championed by thinkers like Bertrand Russell, G E Moore, and Samuel Alexander, also defended the tangible nature of time. As realism gained traction, philosophers began to challenge McTaggart's argument against the existence of time. Early critics included C D Broad, R B Braithewaite, Susan Stebbing, John Alexander Gunn, Hilda Oakeley, J N Findlay, and Victoria, Lady Welby. As the 20th century progressed, these critiques persisted. McTaggart's paper was only 17 pages long, yet it has since sparked countless debates among philosophers, with tens of thousands of pages written about it. In the 21st century alone, it has been cited over 1,600 times - an impressive feat for a piece published in a journal decades ago. It might seem that these criticisms would have silenced McTaggart's argument, but instead, they breathed new life into it. As the saying goes, "All publicity is good publicity." The criticism kept the argument alive in philosophical discourse, ensuring its survival over the years.

If every moment of time truly exists, then 1066 and 2055 are equally real. This is a concept that may seem counterintuitive, but it has become a central theme in discussions about the nature of time. In response to these debates, philosophers have adopted McTaggart's framework, aligning their perspectives on realism with either his A-series or B-series. Over time, this process gave birth to two distinct families of time theories.

B-theories argue that time simply represents the ordering of events based on before and after. Past, present, and future are not inherent qualities of the world; they are mere human constructs. To say that a moment is "now" is akin to saying a location is "here": "here" and "now" depend on one's perspective. In contrast, A-theories assert that past, present, and future are genuine aspects of the world. The moment I wrote these words is truly in the past, and you are reading them now. These labels have developed a life of their own, independent of McTaggart.

In the 1950s, Donald Williams championed B-theory, vehemently denouncing the concept of a "moving present" as repulsive and confounding. In the 1960s, Richard Gale offered a passionate defense of A-theory. I believe that the debate between A- versus B-theories was at least partially fueled by advancements in physics. After Hermann Minkowski and Albert Einstein, a view known as "manifold theory" gained popularity among physicists: they combined time and space into a single, unified entity called spacetime. On this theory, every moment of time is as real as any part of space. This lends itself to B-theory, which posits that "now" and "here" are subjective based on one's point of view.

However, some philosophers disliked manifold theory for this very reason: if every moment of time exists, then 1066 is just as real as 2055. Some felt that this depiction of time is too static, failing to capture the essence of time's flow, its "jerk and whoosh," as Williams put it. Philosophers who support A-theory often reject manifold theory, denying that every moment of time is real. For example, in 1923, Broad argued that the past and present are real, while the future is not. Today, the philosophy of time remains a vibrant and ongoing debate.

Whales and their history have always fascinated us, especially with remnants from the whaling era still around like old pieces of baleen. Baleen was much like plastic in its day, used to make everything from corsets to brooms to buggy whips. Museums seem to have an abundance of it tucked away in dusty corners. Recently, I've been flooded with collaborative offers and heard whispers of at least half a dozen labs exploring baleen analysis. As one scientist put it, 'everybody with a hormone lab is jumping on this.'

Baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. The stress hormone cortisol has attracted a great deal of attention within wildlife biology due to its potential to reveal harmful influences. Although bowhead populations are thriving, they face rapid changes in their habitats as sea ice melts, shipping traffic increases, and oil and gas exploration activities pick up speed in the Arctic. The ultimate aim of our baleen research is to develop methods that allow us to assess stress levels related to environmental change and human activity.

Interestingly, other informative compounds are being discovered in baleen as well. Research dating back to the 1980s has shown that variations in the ratio of stable carbon and nitrogen isotopes along a bowhead's baleen reflect the distinct prey they consume in their summer versus winter habitats. Essentially, baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. We are examining this pattern in bowhead baleen dating back to 1952 to see if the whales' diet and migrations are changing, possibly due to shrinking sea ice.

In collaboration with others, Hunt and I are studying isotope and hormone data from the same baleen samples to confirm bowheads' gestation period and calving frequency. We believe we can determine not just when but roughly where individual bowheads got pregnant. In some instances, bowhead bodies themselves have become historical artifacts. Native Alaskan hunters have discovered remarkably old whaling gear in slaughtered animals. For example, a stone tool from the 1880s was found in the 1990s, leaving me speechless when I first heard about it. Over the years, hunters have found a few other items, but dating stone tools can be unreliable. Then in 2007, a Yankee-era harpoon point with an 1879 patent was discovered lodged in another whale's shoulder, placing the whale's age at approximately 115 to 130 years. Our chemical analyses of bowhead eye lenses eventually indicated they can live to 200 or older. Studies of keratin-based tissues in other marine animals suggest that baleen may have even greater potential for future research. For instance, a 2012 paper demonstrated this possibility.

text:
On November 16th, 2016, investment guru and philanthropist Tom Marsico, one of the wealthiest men in the U.S., saw his wealth grow a bit more as Christie's in New York auctioned off Claude Monet's Grainstack for $81.4 million. This record-breaking price for a Monet painting was over six times what Marsico had paid in 2002, demonstrating that few investments can match the returns of a good old Monet. Performance after performance by Monet at auction houses in London and New York has become commonplace. Yet, it's hard to imagine a starker contrast to Monet's financial struggles during his lifetime than what we see today. The tales of critical rejection faced by the Impressionist movement, and the resulting financial woes of its painters, are part of art history lore. While Monet did experience some early hardships  being rejected for the annual Paris Salon in 1868 and having to survive on potatoes that winter  he wasn't always a starving artist.

'We don't need any more of that kind of painting,' a juror sniffed in 1868, rejecting Monet's works for the Paris Salon. This rejection likely affected his sales. Yet, for much of his career, things were looking up for Monet. His diet, far from potatoes, often included delicacies such as foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Prigord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor raved about the exquisite cuisine at Monet's house. After turning 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was always flush with cash: a fortunate outcome for such a bon vivant. His wealth afforded him a fleet of cars, tailored tweed suits from Paris, and a team of gardeners to maintain his famous gardens. In this regard, he bears more resemblance to contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their high-profile dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multi-million dollar fortunes, than he does to artistic outliers such as Vincent van Gogh, who struggled financially. Monet's surname is almost a play on words with 'money,' a fact that headline writers and punsters appreciate. In truth, Monet was the Jazz Age's precursor to Koons because, in 1922, four years before his death, a sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist.

Monet has long been the preferred artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, Steve Wynn, and various Russian and Asian billionaires. His paintings serve as statements of both aesthetic taste and financial prowess, dazzling consumer goods that also offer lucrative returns. Much like they functioned during Monet's lifetime, his canvases today are more than just artwork  they are investments.

text: genes at the centre of mainstream biological thought. The discovery and progressive unravelling of the double helix seemed only to confirm their place there. The union of genetics with molecular biology has undoubtedly created a powerful new science. By unpicking the molecular interactions inside cells, we’ve been able to develop drugs to mimic or interfere with specific processes. The discovery of enzymes that synthesise and edit DNA laid the foundations for genetic engineering, which is usually discussed in terms of its commercial applications, but whose most common use has always been in pure research. In this way, advances in knowledge create the tools to advance knowledge, in a virtuous circle. Nevertheless, we appear to have come to a threshold. The more we know about the molecular processes, the less sense the gene-centric perspective makes. In the early days of genetics, scientists studying different animals would identify genes and correlate them with body-scale features. However, they had no way of knowing whether the genes they had identified in one species were the same ones that were known in another. Once technologies for reading DNA sequences were invented, it became possible for researchers to ‘read’ their genes and to notice when genes in two different species were so related they were effectively the same thing. This often produced surprises. The gene ‘Wingless’, for example, was identified by fruitfly geneticists as a blueprint for making wings (fly biologists name their genes after what happens if the gene is damaged). Cancer specialists working with mice had identified what they called Int-1 as a gene for mouse breast cancer. But these genes turned out to be effectively the same: they code for a protein now given the hybrid name Wnt. Properly analysed, Wnt is neither for making wings nor for breast cancer. It is for carrying signals between cells: the meaning of the signal depends on what cell, in what organism, is receiving it. This is just one of very many examples. The concept of ‘the gene for feature x’ is giving way to a much more complicated story. Think something like: ‘the gene for protein a, that interacts with proteins b, c and d to allow a cell to undertake process p, that allows that cell to co‑ordinate with other cells to make body feature x’. The very length of the above phrase, and the weakness of the blueprint metaphor, emphasises a conceptual distance that is opening up between the molecular-scale, mechanical function of genes and the interesting large-scale features of bodies. The genes matter – of course they do, because something has to build all these proteins. But the helix seems less and less appropriate as an icon for the all-important control systems that run life, especially at larger scales (cells, tissues, organisms, populations, ecosystems and so on). text:
In today's discourse, certain substances have historically been portrayed as the 'vices' of supposedly 'inferior' groups, branded as a 'plague' or 'contagion', capable of infecting society at large. Although such explicit beliefs may no longer be prevalent, these substances often retain an enigmatic and unfamiliar air. This is particularly true for psychoactive substances that are viewed as part of everyday life, like caffeine in the West, coca in the Andes, or ayahuasca in the Amazon. During the early 1900s, opium, morphine, and cocaine started to lose social acceptability, much like tobacco has in our contemporary era. Their use was increasingly viewed through the lens of medical risks, making users appear negligent or morally weak. In essence, these drugs transformed into what we now know as 'legal highs': technically not prohibited but existing only under-the-counter or among those in the know. Once their sale was officially outlawed during the years surrounding World War I, 'drugs' came to carry legal weight: a defined list of substances deemed not just medically harmful, but associated with criminal elements.

The banning of these substances sparked minimal public debate compared to the prohibition of alcohol in the U.S., given that the drug issue was then marginal and confined mainly to ethnic, bohemian, or criminal subcultures without significant public voice. The only organized resistance against this negative portrayal came from the pharmaceutical industry, concerned about damage to its legitimate trade due to unfortunate associations. For instance, what is now the American Pharmacists Association, backed by corporate giants like Johnson & Johnson, protested against terms such as 'drug evil', 'drug fiend', and 'drug habit'. They lobbied newspapers to specify the drugs in question as 'narcotics' or 'opiates.' However, the ambiguous and useful term 'drugs' proved too hard to replace with more precise words. It did not merely refer to specific chemicals but also conveyed a moral stance on their use by certain people and for specific purposes. This perspective was eventually embedded into legal frameworks meant to prohibit them.

The 1961 UN Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, the foundation of international drug laws, stands out among UN conventions due to its use of the word 'evil' to describe the problem it aims to address. Legislators hailed the 1961 Convention as the triumphant conclusion of a fifty-year struggle to ban drugs, a battle that had begun at the Opium Conference in The Hague in 1911. Looking back, however, 1961 marked the beginning of the disintegration of the consensus around the 'evils' of drugs. A daring postwar generation, the first to consume globally, was beginning to awaken to the realization that alcohol was not the world's only intoxicant. An underground network was starting to circulate news about hashish smoking in Morocco and LSD synthesis in Switzerland.

text:
In the late 18th century, a small town named Jena became a beacon for free thinkers and rebels. One professor mused, 'complete freedom to think, to teach and to write.' Compared to other places, censorship was more lenient here, and the range of topics that could be explored was vast. As Jena's most renowned resident, Friedrich Schiller, put it, 'The professors in Jena are almost entirely independent.' Intellectuals, writers, poets, and philosophers who had run afoul of authorities in their native lands were drawn to Jena by its openness and freedoms. Schiller himself had fled the Duchy of Wrttemberg after being arrested for his revolutionary play The Robbers (1781). On a good day, you might have seen more renowned writers, poets, and philosophers strolling through Jena's streets than in a larger city over an entire century.

Among the notable figures in Jena were the tall, gaunt Schiller, who wrote only with a drawer full of rotten apples on his desk; the obstinate philosopher Fichte, who centered himself in his work; and the young scientist Alexander von Humboldt, who predicted human-induced climate change for the first time. The brilliant Schlegel brothers, Friedrich and August Wilhelm, both writers and critics with pens as sharp as French guillotines, called Jena their home. So did the young philosopher Friedrich Schelling, who redefined the relationship between the individual and nature; G W F Hegel, who would become one of the most influential philosophers in Western history; and Caroline Michaelis-Bohmer-Schlegel-Schelling, a free-spirited woman who carried the names of her father and three husbands but lived life on her own terms.

The young poet Novalis, a former student from nearby Weienfels, often visited his friends in Jena. During the winter months, you might have spotted Germany's most celebrated poet, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, skating on the river, his belly held together by a flowery waistcoat. Though older and more famous, Goethe took on a protective role towards the younger generation. He was inspired, even rejuvenated, by their new ideas, while they revered him. These great minds attracted students from all over Germany and Europe to Jena. Starstruck at the sight of so many famous poets and philosophers seated together at concerts in Zur Rose tavern, they couldn't believe their eyes when it seemed like all of Germany's greatest minds gathered in one room at a party. Each of these intellects lived an extraordinary life, but the fact that they all converged in Jena during the same time is even more remarkable. That's why I've called them the 'Jena Set' in my book Magnificent Rebels (2022). In an age when most of Europe was still shackled by absolutism, the Jena Set was united by their passion for the free self.
label: ai

text:
KUWAIT CITY - The number of foreign workers in the energy sector within the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) states equates to the native population, according to a recent report. These overseas employees are estimated to contribute 6.2% to the combined GDP of the six GCC nations, which totals approximately $1.6 trillion, as per figures from the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank. In contrast, foreign workers in the United States and Britain send home only 0.7% and 0.8% of their respective GDPs, the report noted.

Saudi Arabia leads the list with an estimated 10 million expatriates remitting a staggering $44 billion, followed closely by the UAE with $29 billion in transfers. Remittances from Kuwait and Qatar amount to $12 billion and $9.5 billion, respectively, while smaller amounts are sent home by residents in Oman and Bahrain, according to the report.

The majority of Gulf expatriates hail from India, Egypt, the Philippines, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Indonesia, Sri Lanka, and Yemen. The high level of remittances can be attributed to the restrictive foreign ownership and investment policies implemented by GCC states, as well as the fact that citizenship is not offered to foreigners regardless of their length of residence.

The report encourages GCC states to stimulate investment from expatriates by providing specialized services and opening their markets to foreign residents, particularly in the real estate sector. This could help reduce the reliance on remittances and foster a more stable economic environment within these nations.

For me, God died with Anne Frank in Bergen-Belsen What about someone like me? Can I be so certain the other side is wrong? Like most reasonably sophisticated Christians, I take the Bible as a story of how a nomadic people came to recognise and refine their notions of their Creator. One can take the Old Testament in particular as an account of growth, from infancy through childhood to adulthood, until one has the loving God of the gospels: there are problems moving from the literal and the metaphorical and so on, but they can be worked out. On the other hand, I think certain things make the existence of God simply untenable. The very notion itself is confused – the God of the Christians is an uneasy amalgam of a Greek notion of the Divine, eternal and unchanging, and the Jewish God, personal and part of daily existence. What is more, I cannot reconcile the existence of God with evil. For me, God died with Anne Frank in Bergen-Belsen. Finally, I think all the stuff about faith is simply self-deception. On the other hand, I can see why some choose to believe. Why is there something rather than nothing? I am content to say I don’t know, but others are not, and when they say there must be a reason, I think they have a fair point. Equally, I am happy to accept that consciousness, sentience just happens, even though I cannot explain it – how can a computer made of meat think? But if the believer can make sense of consciousness only by invoking a deity, I cannot stop them from doing so, nor do I very much want to. And if someone says, sincerely, that the only way they can make sense of great evil is via religion, I don’t dismiss them as stupid or insincere. A process theologian, following Alfred North Whitehead, might argue that God has emptied Himself of His power – ‘kenosis’ – so He can suffer with us. Only by seeing a grieving God at the side of Anne Frank as she lay dying can we live and make sense of this life. This explanation might be incorrect – I think it is – but I am not going to say that someone who believes it is morally wrong. To be perfectly candid, I am groping my way forward here. TOKYO, JAPAN  A divergence in fiscal policy and currency strategies is poised to shape the discourse among G7 advanced economies, as prominent economist Robert Shiller leads an informal symposium prior to the official G7 meeting slated for this Friday. The symposium's attendees concur that instead of relying on traditional, short-term measures tailored to each country's unique circumstances, a more comprehensive approach focusing on sustainable growth strategies may be preferred by the G7 finance leaders. Despite concerns about potential risks to the global economic outlook, agreement on specific actions to stimulate flagging worldwide growth remains elusive.

In a press conference held Thursday, Bank of Japan Governor Haruhiko Kuroda expressed optimism about the forthcoming exchange of ideas, stating, "I anticipate open discussions among participants regarding how to attain price stability and growth using monetary, fiscal, and structural policies that cater to each country's particular requirements."

The impending British referendum on whether to exit the European Union (Brexit) will undoubtedly dominate Friday's G7 session centered around the global economy.

Given the potential consequences of a Brexit vote in June, the G7 finance leaders are expected to delve into this topic during their talks on Saturday. In addition to exploring ways to bolster cyber security as financial transactions grow increasingly interconnected, sources close to the group's deliberations suggest that there is a renewed sense among G7 leaders that swift action is necessary in response to this growing concern.

Following a cyber heist that targeted a Bangladesh central bank account in February, SWIFT  the global financial network utilized by banks for daily transfers of billions of dollars  has issued warnings about various cyber incidents involving fraudulent messages sent through its system. Consequently, the need for enhanced cybersecurity measures will be a focal point of discussions among G7 leaders during this weekend's meeting.

text:
"In an interview with The Guardian back in 2002, philosopher Bernard Williams mused on the recurring theme of authenticity and self-expression that permeated his work. Authenticity was a cultural ideal during Williams' time (born in 1929, passed away in 2003), but it has only grown more influential since then. Who among us can resist the call to be true to oneself?

Williams delved into the power and allure of this idea, and his work continues to help us understand it today. Yet, as he was well aware, authenticity comes with its own risks. In his essay 'Moral Luck' (1976), Williams examines Paul Gauguin's decision to leave Paris for Tahiti in pursuit of greatness as a painter. This move, on the surface, seemed incredibly selfish, as Gauguin abandoned his wife and children without a second thought.

While many might see Gauguin's actions as morally unjustifiable, Williams suggests that his eventual success as an artist could be seen as a form of moral luck  a justification that may not resonate with everyone but could make sense to Gauguin himself and possibly others. 'I was right,' Gauguin might have said to himself, 'I knew I had it in me.'

Williams paints Gauguin as a complex character torn between family obligations and artistic ambition. However, it's important to note that Williams' portrayal of Gauguin is not necessarily historically accurate. Instead, Gauguin serves as a useful thought experiment to explore the interplay between authenticity, achievement, and luck in justification.

It's also worth mentioning that Williams doesn't delve into the harm caused by Gauguin or the exploitation of his Polynesian subjects featured prominently in his most famous paintings. This is a topic I will address further on. For now, let's focus on the familiar tale of self-justification in pursuit of art, often told through male perspectives  both in real life and fiction."

text: infinite cosmos. We’re going to need the wondrous creativity of every single human being to knock this universe in to shape. I wonder, what will we create? We are Homo Faber, ‘Humankind the Creator’. God did not create us in his image, we created god in our image. We might only be an insignificant species orbiting an insignificant star in an infinite and impassive universe. But we have, perhaps uniquely, the power of creation. Why, then, are we trapped on this ball of rock, repeating the same patterns of self-destructive behaviour, instead of fulfilling our creative potential? We are caught in a consumer culture that works against our innate creativity. The economic crisis of 2008 might have heralded the collapse of that culture. The consumerist economic model — itself a set of ad hoc compromises following the death of the industrial model — has reached the end of its useful existence. It evolved for a world where technology placed creativity in the hands of the few and television communicated their message to the masses, in what the entrepreneur Seth Godin has called the TV-industrial complex. Today, the internet has decentralised communications, and computers the size of an iPhone place vast creative resources in the hands of broad swathes of the population. The ongoing financial crisis is a symptom of an economy that has fallen behind its own technological capacities. Notice how many of the new voices that are emerging through social media make creativity their central concern The instinctive response of our leaders is to reconstruct our consumer culture. The generation holding the reigns of society are trained to think of us as passive consumers. It might take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech, not to the world’s consumers, but to its creators. Yet a creator culture is emerging from the ground up, driven by creators themselves. The crowd-funding platform Kickstarter is only three years old and last year it significantly outfunded the US National Endowment for the Arts. It has allowed artists and entrepreneurs of all kinds to sidestep traditional forms of investment. Crowd-funded creativity is not driven by the commercial imperative of business or the political priorities of government, but by the creative passions of the crowd. ‘Maker’ culture resurrects the spirit of craftsmanship and combines it with technologies, such as 3D printing, that promise to do for manufacturing what the internet did for communication. The principles of ‘open source’ are now being applied far beyond the software development community, invigorating academic and scientific research, politics and government, the media and education. Notice how many of the new voices that are emerging through social media — the cultural curator Maria Popova, an ‘interestingness hunter-gatherer’ who started the Brain Pickings blog; the New York-based Big Think project to sift the ‘best thinking on the planet’; many TED talks — make creativity their central concern. He latched on, drinking what I had until then thought of as my birth-daughter’s milk. It surprised me at the time how natural – that complicated word for things we think of as being outside of culture – to allow a hungry but unrelated creature to feed from my body. I have thought of him as a kind of son ever since. It is tempting to speculate that the original mother-daughter vision of Caritas Romana gives us a glimpse of a kinship system defined by milk lines rather than the blood lines of agnatic, or paternal, kinship. This tale that harks back to older goddess-centred cults and religions reminds me that Caritas Romana is not the only Roman tale of a life saved by the milk of its not-mother, the best-known being that of Romulus and Remus, the founders of Rome: twin sons of the god Mars and the mortal Rhea Silvia, who were suckled by a she-wolf. There are also lactation tales that imbue the recipient with divine powers, demonstrating ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. One is writ so large that it has given the most spectacular feature of the night sky its name: the Milky Way. Tintoretto’s The Origin of the Milky Way (c1575) shows the infant Heracles being surreptitiously latched on to the goddess Hera while she sleeps. Heracles, the son of a mortal woman, apparently sucked so hard he woke the goddess who dashed him from her breast. Hera’s divine milk, which afforded him the immortality of the gods, then sprayed across the heavens and formed the Milky Way. The tradition of adult breastfeeding as a cure is evidence of the ancient belief in the magical power of breastmilk The Origin of the Milky Way (1575) by Tintoretto. Courtesy the National Gallery, LondonThe abundance of milk is present in images of phantasmagorical plenitude and eroticism. Suckling nymphs and multibreasted mermaids and goddesses abound, portraying an extravagant delight in this extralinguistic world of flesh and bodily satisfaction. Images like Giulio Romano’s fantastically tailed mer-mother and her mer-babies give the slip to the realities of sexual reproduction and the constraints of family and of species. text: range of competing and complementary technologies of optical display that proliferated in the late Victorian marketplace for spectacle and sensation, and were designed to instruct people in when and how to believe the evidence of the eye. Still, it’s interesting to note that illusions were a common feature of early cinema, too, George Méliès’s films of disembodied heads being a case in point. That said, if ‘animated photographs’ challenged Victorian viewers to look through the illusion and speculate about how it was managed, cinema increasingly didn’t. Dorothy’s unmasking of the wizard in The Wonderful Wizard of Oz (published in 1900, when the culture of illusion was reaching its climax, and filmed in 1939) dramatises just this difference. Victorian illusionists needed their audiences to know that what they were seeing was an illusion — otherwise there was nothing to be admired and no lesson to be learnt. By contrast, moviemakers, like the wizard of Oz himself, simply want their audiences to take the illusion for reality. If they start wondering about the mechanism of deception, they’ve lost the plot. As participants in the cinematic experience we’re meant to surf the illusion, not dive in. We know, of course, just as the Victorians did, that the illusion is there, but we respond to the seduction in a different way. Illusions are still useful tools. Philosophers use them to talk about the nature of knowledge. Neuroscientists use them to understand just how much work the brain does to turn visual data (the philosophers’ ‘seeing’) into meaningful pictures of the world (‘seeing as’). And just as the fascination of Victorian men of science with illusions generated more and better ways of fooling the eye, modern neuroscientific insights provide new mechanisms of deception, too. We are now very good indeed at producing illusions. We live in an environment that is increasingly made up of them. We use them to talk to each other (think of video conferencing), to carry out mundane tasks (virtual receptionists), and as agents of seduction, to sell products (holographic advertising). Illusions are all around us. Sometimes, they’re just old tricks masquerading as new: there are companies out there selling holographic projection technologies that on closer inspection turn out to be versions of Pepper’s Ghost — an illusion of an illusion, if you like. We’re quite adept at understanding how to deal with the different kinds of illusions that make up our world and to discriminate between them. Rather rarely do we dive beneath their surfaces to interrogate the mechanism of deception, though. But, as The Wizard of Oz ought to remind us, failing to pull back the curtain on the illusions surrounding us, at least occasionally, can lead to our trusting entirely the wrong kind of authority.

text:
Concentration is key to honing my focus, and focusing is crucial for success in chess. As my passion for the game started to wane, so did a piece of me fade away. You see, without concentration, we can't experience the "flow" state - a vital part of chess play that psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi coined and popularized. Flow is characterized by intense focus, loss of self-awareness, clear feedback from the environment, and an altered sense of time. These experiences are incredibly rewarding, but they only occur when our skills meet the challenge at hand - too little challenge, we get bored; too much, we feel overwhelmed. Chess is a great way to access flow, but it has its limitations as a guiding principle for life. Primarily, it describes a state of consciousness rather than a method to achieve it. Ultimately, flow is not a virtue, but a source of pleasure. While being in a flow state is desirable, promoting it might lead to undesirable characteristics - think an atomized society obsessed with gaming and virtual reality.

As a young player, I used various methods to maintain my focus, such as taking long walks before games or listening to favorite music tracks. These techniques worked because the purpose of concentration was clear. However, in early December 2008, in a hotel room in Palma de Mallorca, I remember feeling strangely detached from myself while trying to prepare for a game. As American football coaches have said: "The will to win is not as important as the will to prepare to win." And it seemed like I had lost that drive. It felt like the foundation of my motivation - to concentrate on chess - was crumbling, and with it, a piece of myself. The will to maintain the identity that fueled my competitive spirit had vanished, and I knew it was time to shift my focus from the game itself to what it represented.

Chess is a unique battleground where systems meet psychology, and today's world needs a better understanding of this complex encounter. To make good moves in chess, you need to see the whole board with all its shifting dynamics. But you also need to understand your own mind - its power and limitations. The personal is political, and vice versa. In the early 21st century, the position we find ourselves in includes a growing ecological crisis (as material-intensive economic growth remains the world's dominant strategy), the challenge of avoiding mass unemployment in an age of rising AI, and protecting truth itself. These are complex challenges that require focus, determination, and a clear understanding of our own minds - as well as the systems we inhabit.

A choice about a way to live presented itself to Berger shortly after he made Ways of Seeing. He was in his late 40s and had achieved an international level of fame. The invitations started coming in. He could have taken a position at a museum or university. He could have entered a world of sinecures and fellowships, residencies and agents, conferences and airports. He turned down almost all of this. The reasons were historical as well as personal, and might relate, however indirectly, to our own contemporary impasse: our inability to see more than one generation into the future, the dissolving legitimacy of the metropolitan and academic elite, the seeming incapacity to move beyond a politics of negativity and despair. Just as we are hitting the limits of critique as a culture, Berger was hitting them as a writer – and a person. With Ways of Seeing (and his Booker-winning novel G.), he had reached a tipping point that was also a midlife crisis and a fork in the road. ‘I can be only by destroying,’ Lionel Trilling once wrote of a certain modern attitude, ‘I can know myself only by what I shatter.’ But where is there to go when the demolition is complete? There is a photograph of Berger from the 1973 Frankfurt Book Fair. Taken by Jean Mohr, a lifelong friend, it shows a middle-aged writer, exhausted and detached, lying on the floor as others walk past him in a blur. What was Berger thinking about? What was he longing for? It was at this fair that Berger met a young American, Beverly Bancroft, then an assistant at Penguin Books. Within a year, they were married. Two years later, they had a son. Soon they moved to a small farming village in the foothills of the Alps. The chalet they rented lacked central heating and running water. The outhouse was across the driveway. The question, he once said, was of ‘continually learning to be embedded in life’ It would be easy to romanticise Berger’s third act as a rural storyteller. Even while haymaking, he was still a renowned writer with famous friends. But it would be just as easy to cynically write it off. Throughout the neoliberal era, most intellectuals have lived in a social world that is urban, cosmopolitan, cutthroat and status-oriented. Berger went someplace very different. He remained politically committed though his conception of the political shifted and enlarged, absorbing a broader sense of history and experience. The question, he once said, was of ‘continually learning to be embedded in life’. PRETORIA  Dean Elgar's dismissal was a freakish event, but debutant Stephen Cook remained unbeaten on 46 at lunch on day one of the fourth and final Test between South Africa and England at Centurion Park. Hashim Amla was batting on 34 at the break, having shared a flourishing 72-run second wicket partnership with Cook after Elgar's departure for 20.

England's James Taylor displayed excellent anticipation and quick thinking when Elgar's sharp shot hit him in the leg, causing the ball to become lodged around his calf. Taylor managed to pluck it before it touched the ground, denying Elgar a lengthy TV umpire review. The unfortunate dismissal came after Elgar danced down the wicket to Moeen Ali and will likely leave him feeling exceedingly unlucky.

It was Taylor's third extraordinary catch in the last two Tests, further cementing his reputation as a formidable presence at the most dangerous position on the field. Cook made an auspicious start to his Test career, hitting four off the first ball of the game, which James Anderson's opening delivery veered down leg side. This set the tone for the opening session, with England struggling to find consistent lines as they attempted to build upon their 0-run lead in the series.

England made one change to their team for this match, replacing the injured Steven Finn with seamer Chris Woakes, who played in the first Test. (Reuters)

text:
Title: Embracing Diversity for Revolution: Lessons from Deleuze and Guattari's Schizoanalysis

In pursuit of political transformation, Deleuze and Guattari proposed a three-step practice called schizoanalysis. This process begins by identifying desires that deviate from the capitalist norm, then pushing these to their extremes to free them from capitalistic constraints, and finally, uniting these diverse desires to ignite a 'molecular revolution.' But what does this term really mean? For Deleuze and Guattari, it's impossible to predict the exact form such a revolution would take. Instead, they advocated for a politics of experimentation, encouraging exploration of alternative desire organizations through unconventional means, such as Antonin Artaud's 'theatre of cruelty' or William S Burroughs' drug-induced adventures.

Deleuze and Guattari viewed a wide range of individuals as potential participants in this endeavor. They rejected the traditional Marxist notion that the working class alone holds the key to change. Instead, they aimed to unite all marginalized groupsthose oppressed by patriarchy (women), racism (people of color), and heteronormativity (now known as the LGBT community)under a shared umbrella. They argued that these 'minoritarians' were all victims of the same machinery: despotic, imperial capitalism. Only by joining forces could they bring about an anti-capitalist revolution.

The philosophical image of the individual is rooted in the seemingly autonomous figure of the white male subject. To eliminate this specter, Deleuze and Guattari suggested a process of 'becoming-woman' and 'becoming-minoritarian.' This provocative work, Anti-Oedipus, stirred controversy. Lacan was incensed, banning any discussion of the text in his seminars. Although many on the political Left sympathized with Deleuze and Guattari's goals, they criticized their audacity and unorthodoxy. Despiteor perhaps because ofthese criticisms, Anti-Oedipus became an instant bestseller, garnering a two-page review in Le Monde.

Their writing style and collaboration were as revolutionary as their ideas. Deleuze and Guattari's books don't speak with a single voice; instead, they resonate with multiple voices that defy easy categorization into either author. 'Since each of us was several,' they said in A Thousand Plateaus (1980), the sequel to Anti-Oedipus, 'there was already quite a crowd.' This collective spirit was later echoed by Guattari: 'We are very different... I'm more attracted to adventurous things; let's call it a conceptual commando who likes to visit foreign lands. Gilles, on the other hand, is a philosophical heavyweight, he has a whole bibliographic administration.'

Their collaborative approach aimed to combat the stifling individuality that can lead to sterility and death. A Thousand Plateaus spans 15 chapters on a wide range of topics, from geology to linguistics. Their aim was not just theoretical but practical: to spark a revolution by uniting diverse voices in pursuit of a common goalto free ourselves from the shackles of capitalism and create a world where desire thrives.

MANILA  Gold prices edged higher today, reaching a three-month high, as increased volatility in other assets benefitted the precious metal and could lead to further gains this year, with global central banks potentially forced to ease monetary policy further to spur economic growth.

With interest rates already near zero, analysts anticipate that central banks may move either towards zero or negative rates, as Japan and certain European countries have done in an attempt to force banks to lend more. At the time of writing, spot gold stood at $1,126.55 per ounce, a level last seen on November 3. Confidence in gold continues to grow, as evidenced by the rise in holdings of SPDR Gold Trust, the world's largest gold-backed exchange-traded fund (ETF), which reached 21.9 million ounces on Monday, its highest level since November 3.

Global manufacturing expansion remained weak at the start of 2016, with a slight acceleration offset by a contraction in emerging economies. In China, a gauge of factory activity dropped to its lowest level since mid-2012.

Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Stanley Fischer warned that if recent volatility in financial markets persists and signals a slowdown in the global economy, it could negatively impact the U.S. economy. Gold is often viewed as an asset of choice during uncertain times, and posted its best monthly jump in a year in January, with gains of over 6 percent so far this year, after falling 10.4 percent last year.

Meir expects only one U.S. interest rate hike this year, given weaker macroeconomic numbers out of the U.S., which could suggest that the Fed's December move was an outlier, forced onto a central bank that had promised to do so for much of the past year.

text:
For years, the Kindle design team aimed to create a device embodying the Platonic ideal of universal reading containers. However, my recent experience has led me to question this pursuit. If an overflowing pile of unread books on the bedside table symbolizes good intentions left unfulfilled, then a list of digital books on a Kindle represents fleeting intentions never realized. But something unexpected transpired over the past two years: I lost faith in the convenience of e-books.

I came to this realization only a few months ago when auditing my library  both physical and digital. While physical books, particularly works of literary fiction, continue to draw me in like a magnet, my interest in digital books has waned. Whether it's exploring bookstores in New York or receiving thoughtful gifts from friends, I find myself acquiring more physical books than ever before. The allure of the curated front tables at McNally Jackson or Three Lives and Company is too strong to resist.

The irony is that I've never consumed more digital content than I do today. From news articles to blog posts, I spend countless hours reading on my smartphone. Short to mid-length content seems tailor-made for the size, resolution, and usage scenarios of mobile devices, with many online publications offering beautifully designed typography and layouts that adapt seamlessly across different screens. Plus, phones make it effortless to share articles with others. The affinity between our smartphones and short-to-mid-length content is one reason why venture capitalists have invested hundreds of millions of dollars into digital publishers like Vox, Vice, and Buzzfeed.

So what accounts for my unconscious shift back to print? A book, once purchased by a reader, follows a familiar routine: it's read, underlined, dog-eared, scuffed, and  most importantly  reread. Reading a book just once leaves you with a superficial understanding; revisiting it over time forges a deeper connection. The bond between a reader and a book is not measured in minutes or hours but in months and years.

As a consumer of digital books, I am content, but as a reader, I feel let down. All the convenience aspects of the Kindle experience  an extensive catalog, immediate distribution, reasonable prices (albeit slightly less so now with updated contracts)  are well-executed. It's easy to forget that Amazon doesn't just frustrate publishers; they also power a significant chunk of the internet, providing hosting and servers for many leading companies worldwide. This business alone contributes nearly $2 billion to their bottom line, making it no surprise that they have built a seamless, efficient system.

However, the emotional aspect of reading is absent in the digital realm. The tactile experience of turning pages, feeling the weight of a book in my hands, and seeing the physical progression of my reading journey just can't be replicated on a screen  no matter how well designed it might be. As much as I appreciate the convenience offered by e-books, nothing compares to the connection I feel with a real, tangible book. And for now, that's where my heart lies.

text:
Around 4200 years ago, a region we now call southern Iraq was wracked by rebellions. The once-independent Sumerian city-states had been unified under one rule by the legendary king Sargon of Akkad. During what historians now refer to as the Old Akkadian period, Sargon and his successors transformed the cities they conquered in countless ways: old nobles were demoted, new men took power, old enemies were vanquished, and new standards of governance were imposed. The Sumerian world expanded and became wealthier, yet it also grew more unstable. Dissatisfaction with this new empire simmered, sparking a continuous stream of uprisings as the cities sought to regain their independence. One such rebellion is immortalized in a captivating poem called 'The Exaltation of Inana'. Besides being a literary masterpiece in its own right, 'The Exaltation' is unique because it was attributed to an author whose existence can be traced historically - Enheduanna, the high priestess of Ur and Sargon's daughter. According to 'The Exaltation', she was exiled due to one of the many rebellions that plagued the Old Akkadian Empire. Tablets inscribed with The Exaltation of Inana in three parts. Old Babylonian period, c1750 BCE. Courtesy of the Yale Babylonian Collection

It's unclear whether the poem was penned by the historical Enheduanna herself, as a reinterpretation of real events, or by a later poet writing in her name. What is certain, though, is that the poem conveys an authentic sense of what it would have felt like to live through such tumultuous times, whether as a personal account or a cultural echo preserved for future generations. That's one reason why Enheduanna's works - comprising five poems, including 'The Exaltation' - continue to resonate with us, millennia after their creation. As the world becomes more and more unstable today, we can learn much from this ancient priestess. These poems don't just chronicle a historical upheaval; they go one step further by turning that instability into a philosophical insight, a chance to contemplate what life truly is. Compressed within their enigmatic verses lies the seed of an ancient philosophy of change. If the poems aim to transform radical change from a temporary political event into a timeless principle, they do so primarily by extolling the goddess Inana, better known in Babylonian and Assyrian times as Ishtar. Two of Enheduanna's works, 'The Exaltation' and 'The Hymn to Inana', are dedicated to elevating Inana to a position of supremacy.

text:
The Stoics, in their pursuit of a life free from pain, identified two crucial functions of practical reason. On one hand lies the indirect function, where careful planning and thoughtful decision-making help the Stoic navigate the least painful path through life. In contrast, there's the direct function, where instead of removing or avoiding life's obstacles, the Stoic learns to reframe these challenges in a way that alters their emotional impact. This is a change in perspective, while the other is a change in action.

What sets Stoicism apart in ethics, as Schopenhauer points out, lies in its proposed method of mental transformation. The Stoic first realizes that painful feelings of deprivation do not arise from lacking something but rather from the desire to have it and not having it. To escape these painful feelings completely, we must address our desires themselves. Moreover, the larger our aspirations and the higher our expectations for what we want to achieve, the more devastating the disappointment when we fall short. If we cannot help but yearn for some things, then we should at least ensure those desires remain reasonable and achievable. Schopenhauer warns us to be wary of expecting too much happiness in the future; such expectations are likely unrealistic. 'Every intense pleasure,' he says, 'is an illusion.'

So, the Stoic strives for ataraxia, a state of inner peace and tranquility amidst life's storms. Schopenhauer suggests that accepting the inevitability of suffering can help us achieve this state if we internalize it as truth. Pain and suffering hurt more when we view them as accidents or avoidable, but in reality, they are universal and unavoidable. If we can accept this, perhaps we will worry less about encountering suffering, or at least worry about it as we do other inevitable aspects of life, such as aging (for most of us) and death.

We should resist the belief that the world owes us happiness, for it sets us up for disappointment. If we believe we are entitled to happiness, we will inevitably feel resentment towards the obstacles that hinder our perceived right to it. Some people, Schopenhauer observes, externalize this resentment by blaming external circumstances or others for their misery.

text:
Pondering the vast, mysterious cosmos, it strikes me that we humans have a remarkable task ahead - shaping this universe to our own design. But what will we create? As Homo Faber, or 'Humankind the Creator', perhaps we've created gods in our image, not the other way around. We might be just another small species orbiting an average star, lost amidst the infinite, indifferent universe. Yet, we possess a unique ability - creation. Why then are we stuck in a rut, repeating destructive patterns instead of unlocking our creative potential?

It's a consumer culture that stifles our creativity. The financial crisis of 2008 may have been the first crack in this system. The consumer-based economic model - an improvised solution following the demise of industrialization - has reached its expiration date. It thrived when technology limited creative expression to a privileged few, and television delivered their message to the masses - what Seth Godin calls the 'TV-industrial complex'.

But today, the internet has democratized communication, and smartphones carry powerful creative tools. Our economy is grappling with an identity crisis, struggling to keep pace with its own technological advancements. Observe the surge of new voices emerging on social media, centered around creativity.

Our leaders' instinctive reaction is to rebuild our consumer culture. They view us as passive consumers rather than creators. It may take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech not to the worlds consumers, but to its creators. Yet, a creator-centric culture is emerging organically, fueled by the creators themselves.

Platforms like Kickstarter, just three years old, significantly outfunded the US National Endowment for the Arts last year. They empower artists and entrepreneurs to circumvent traditional investment routes. Crowd-funded creativity is not driven by commercial interests or political agendas, but by the creative passions of the crowd.

text:
In contrast to certain contemporary thinkers like Jason Stanley, Latour posits that these movements aren't just a surface-level echo of early 20th-century fascism. Instead, they embody a distinct political landscape built on climate change denial. This new order sees wealthy elites striving to establish gated communities and escape routes through deregulation and disenfranchisement. Simultaneously, they yearn for fleeting fulfillment in luxuries that seem ultimately hollow: superyachts, brief forays into space or the deep sea, or purchasing entire islands. By manipulating and undermining democracy, they aim to generate more resources for themselves by deregulation. Understanding (to some degree) that this isn't sustainable, they retreat into increasingly detached fantasies such as TESCREAL - an ideological blend of transhumanism, extropianism, singularitarianism, cosmism, rationalism, effective altruism, and longtermism. This vision, promoted by philosophers at Oxford University like Nick Bostrom, Hilary Greaves, and William MacAskill, paints a future where humanity evolves into a posthuman state (through liberal eugenics and AI), colonizes the accessible universe, and depletes our cosmic endowment of resources to produce astronomical amounts of 'value.' The happiness of these future posthumans, largely digital entities, justifies ignoring present-day issues. As Greaves and MacAskill put it, "when evaluating actions, we can often initially focus primarily on the further-future effects, with short-run effects acting as little more than tie-breakers." The TESCREAL world offers limited space for human diversity: the rich tapestry of experiences found in communities like Travellers and Roma, Indigenous societies, and more.

Why do the wealthy choose to actively deny the climate crisis rather than address it? According to philosopher Beth Lord, drawing on Spinoza, they're gripped by negative emotions. Typically, our emotions guide us towards what benefits us and away from harm. We experience three fundamental affects: joy, sadness, and desire. Desire is an expression of the conatus - we pursue things that bring us joy and avoid those that cause sadness. Overall, this helps with self-preservation. However, due to the intricate nature of our emotions, it's possible to be misguided in them and desire things that don't truly benefit us. Seeking prestige, fame, and wealth may seem like it will help us realize ourselves, but we become ensnared by these desires instead. While this mindset is prevalent among the wealthy elite, it's not exclusive to them. The ethicist Eugene Chislenko argues that we might all be climate change deniers in some sense - not that we literally deny its existence, but that we fail to act upon its urgency due to personal desires and fears.

And yet, here we are, caught in a consumer culture that stifles our inherent creativity. The financial crisis of 2008 could have been the harbinger of this culture's downfall. Our leaders instinctively seek to rebuild our consumer culture. They see us as passive consumers, a mentality they have been trained to instill. It might take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech not to the world's consumers but to its creators. Each branch slowly drifts apart to give rise to a new species. Some branches dry up in extinction. Others split repeatedly, generating the 30 million branches that exist today. In the process, Pomiankowski reveals that metaphors not only shape the way we see things, but also carry story. In thinking through some of the ways that our relationship to metaphor might be changing, especially in educational settings, we consulted a study by Emily Weinstein and her research team at Harvard, published in 2014. They set out to study a possible decline in creativity among high-school students by comparing both visual artworks and creative writing collected between 1990-95, and again between 2006-11. Examining the style, content and form of adolescent art-making, the team hoped to understand the potential ‘generational shift’ between pre- and post-internet creativity. It turned out that there were observable gains in the sophistication and complexity of visual artwork, but when it came to the creative-writing endeavours of the two groups, the researchers found a ‘significant increase in young authors’ adherence to conventional writing practices related to genre, and a trend toward more formulaic narrative style’. The team cited standardised testing as a likely source of this lack of creativity, as well as changing modes of written communication that create ‘a multitude of opportunities for casual, text-based communication’ – in other words, for literalism, abbreviation and emojis standing in for words and feelings. With visual arts, by contrast, greater exposure to visual media, and the ‘expansive mental repositories of visual imagery’ informed and inspired student work. Of course, quantifying creativity is problematic, even with thoughtfully constructed controls, but it is provocative to consider what the authors saw as ‘a significant increase in and adherence to strict realism’, and how this might relate to a turn away from metaphoric thinking. Kyung Hee Kim, a psychologist at William and Mary College in Virginia, believes that creative thinking is ‘declining over all Americans of all ages’. text:
Born in Algeria and immersed in a French education system that glorified France - the 'over there', as he referred to it - Said Hocine Derrida, a philosopher who would grapple with the complexities of religion and secularism until his death from pancreatic cancer. On the day he received his diagnosis, he conversed with Mustapha Chrif, a professor of philosophy and Islamic studies at the University of Algiers. They discussed the intricate dance between faith and reason, a debate Derrida admitted he could not solve but felt compelled to continue exploring. 'If we knew what to do,' he mused, 'then there would be no real responsibility.' This sentiment of accountability in an increasingly complex world has echoed through French thought. Since Derrida's generation, French philosophy has wrestled with the age-old questions of traditional philosophy, often challenging conventional wisdom and the ascription of knowledge to a neutral white consciousness or transcendental realm. However, some philosophers have argued that this human-centric approach risks distancing us entirely from the 'real world', a concern amplified in the face of ecological crises.

Bruno Latour's evolving arguments provide insight here. A prolific writer on the constructed nature of scientific theories and the political influences on research, Latour has recently advocated for a realistic stance. In his article 'Why Has Critique Run Out of Steam?' (2004), he urged us to cultivate a realist attitude, suggesting that acknowledging something as constructed does not mean it should be deconstructed but rather treated with care and caution. Other thinkers have gone further, such as Quentin Meillassoux, whose work 'After Finitude' (2008) was hailed by his teacher Alain Badiou as opening a new path in the history of philosophy. Whether this is a new direction or not remains debatable, but Meillassoux's contributions fall under what John Mullarkey terms - with due caution - 'Post-Continental Philosophy'. These philosophers engage openly with scientific disciplines like mathematics, biology, and neuroscience in their pursuit of the 'real'. The challenge lies in finding a balance between understanding our human constructs and respecting the world as it truly exists.
