The potential for reduced US production has been noted by market analysts, who suggest that this could provide some support to oil prices. However, the global oil market remains burdened by a persistent surplus of more than 1 million barrels per day, exceeding daily demand levels.

Analysts have cautioned that recent price lows earlier this year were largely driven by speculative trading and financial hedging strategies among producers rather than fundamental changes in the supply-demand balance. text: Conventional wisdom depicts moral struggle as an internal conflict between a higher moral self and an untamed dark side. This picture pervades popular imagination: the angel and the devil on either shoulder, the ‘two wolf’ parable, the Ego and the Id, the ‘true self’ and the ‘false self’. It resonates with religious traditions that place us between angels and animals in a Great Chain of Being, leaving us torn between higher and lower, spirit and body, good and evil, the demands of conscience and the lure of sin. This view also calls to mind a philosophical tradition from Plato to Immanuel Kant that often presents life’s major moral struggles as a kind of combat between the requirements of duty and the dangers of desire. The self is fragmented and must struggle for wholeness by casting out or silencing its evil components, refusing to give immoral intentions a foothold in thought and deed. A good deal of moral theory, therefore, tends to assume that there’s a morally right answer about what one ought to do in any given circumstance. Any difficulty in doing the right thing results from (evil, selfish) resistance, not from the fact that one cannot do all the good or valuable things that one is called upon to do. However, this familiar view ignores the fact that, in many cases, the problem is not how best to override or silence one’s dark side, but how to cope with having too many good or morally neutral demands on your limited time, energy or resources. In other words, the key issue in many cases is not whether to be moral at all – but rather how best to distribute your moral resources in conditions of scarcity and conflict. Coping well with this latter kind of moral challenge requires very different ways of thinking about moral agency and how to lead good lives. There are (at least) three different classes of goods that regularly give rise to incommensurable but competing legitimate moral claims, each revealed through a different practical stance that we adopt towards the world as we try to figure out what to do and who to be. On this picture, each agent is indeed fragmented, but this fragmentation is not best understood as an internal conflict between ‘good’ and ‘evil’ selves. Instead, moral conflict should be understood in terms of competing dimensions of the good – not all of which can be accommodated in any given moment. What are these three basic normative domains or classes of value? It can be helpful to think of these in terms of the traditional literary distinction between the first-, second- and third-person perspectives. A novel written from the first-person perspective provides access to the protagonist’s struggles from the inside; the reader says ‘I’ along with her. In the second-person perspective, the focus is on the other person: the ‘you’ takes centre stage.

MANILA  Gold prices edged higher on Tuesday, benefiting from increased volatility in other assets as global central banks may be compelled to ease monetary policy further this year in an effort to boost economic growth. With interest rates already near zero, the only viable option is for central banks to move either towards zero or negative rates, as Japan and certain European countries have already done in a desperate attempt to force banks to lend more freely, according to Edward Meir, analyst at INTL FCStone. Whatever the case, this should be constructive for gold prices.

Spot gold touched $1,130.11 an ounce, its strongest level since Nov. 3, and was trading down 0.2 percent at $1,126.55 by 0226 GMT. A break above $1,136 could potentially lift gold towards $1,157, a level last reached in late October, according to technical analysts at ScotiaMocatta. U.S. gold for April delivery was flat at $1,127.30 an ounce.

Reflecting growing confidence in gold, holdings of the SPDR Gold Trust, the world's largest gold-backed exchange-traded fund, reached 21.9 million ounces on Monday, the highest level since Nov. 3.

Global manufacturing expansion showed a slight improvement at the start of 2016, but remained weak overall. Faster growth in developed markets was unable to offset a contraction in emerging economies. In China, a gauge of factory activity fell to its lowest level since mid-2012.

The U.S. economy could be negatively impacted if recent volatility in financial markets persists and indicates a slowdown in the global economy, according to Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Stanley Fischer. Gold is often seen as an asset of choice during times of uncertainty, and it posted its best monthly gain in a year in January, rising more than 6 percent so far this year after falling 10.4 percent last year.

The Fed's statement last week that it will closely monitor the global economy and financial markets lifted gold prices to near $1,130, as it underscored expectations that U.S. policymakers may take a more cautious approach in raising interest rates this year. Meir predicts only one U.S. rate hike for 2016.

Spot platinum fell 1 percent to $860.85 an ounce, while palladium was steady at $500.16 and silver remained unchanged at $14.31.

text:
Reintegration: A Complex Process for Former Rebels in Northern Uganda

Reintegrating former rebels into society was a delicate process. These individuals were often viewed as perpetrators of violent acts, yet they were also victims - many had been abducted during their childhoods and forced to join the rebellion. For civilians, reintegration meant transforming these 'animalistic' ex-combatants into peaceful, law-abiding citizens. The process was referred to as 'reforming' them by teaching them hygiene, social skills, and other life lessons that would help them leave behind their former ways of life in the bush (the Lum). This transformation was commonly referred to as 'repairing their minds'.

The reality of this reintegration process was that these brutalized, traumatized individuals needed to be remade into members of society. They had transgressed the boundaries of humanity and needed guidance, discipline, or counseling to become productive citizens once more. However, it wasn't always clear why former rebels felt this way about themselves, as they often saw their experiences within the LRA differently.

Yes, they had killed people with machetes and beaten them to death with logs - but sometimes, they didn't have access to guns or other seemingly 'humane' weapons used by state armies. Yes, they were forced to fight against their will - but some found purpose in the spiritual and political aspects of the rebellion. Many regretted being torn away from their lives in the Lum and being returned to their villages, where they faced challenges like unemployment, alcoholism, and infidelity.

Yes, they had lived in the wilderness for years and sometimes began to identify as gorillas engaged in 'gorilla warfare.' However, identifying as animals wasn't a fall from grace but rather an elevation to a higher status of power and ferocity. Most found the reintegration process confusing. They saw their time in the LRA as a meaningful period filled with close friendships and camaraderie. They considered each other family, united by a spirit of togetherness that transcended the bonds of blood relations. Some even claimed that their relationships within the LRA were stronger than those they had with civilians after leaving the rebellion.

So why did civilians treat them as violent, dirty beasts who needed to be civilized? Many former rebels questioned this treatment. 'We too are human beings,' some asserted. They didn't want to be seen as brutal killers or traumatized victims. Yet, their claims to humanity felt a bit disingenuous to me. After all, they had also identified as gorillas and sometimes dwelled in the elevated, superhuman realm of the sacred, protected by holy spirits. Their claims to humanity were a way to avoid being stigmatized as outcasts. But society couldn't recognize their unique experiences, beliefs, and ways of life because they often transcended the human realm, entering into divine and animal realms. The struggles of these former rebels highlight the complexities and contradictions inherent in the process of reintegration - a process that requires understanding, empathy, and patience.

From New York to Pennsylvania, Minnesota, and back to New York, Joe Lobdell moved around quite a bit. They worked in various jobs along the way, but their gender identity - being seen as 'assigned female' - often led them to be pushed out of towns. This happened twice, first in Pennsylvania while working as a singing teacher, and then again in Minnesota where they were a jack-of-all-trades.

Lobdell's family and neighbors began questioning their sanity, citing their gender identity and marriage to a woman as proof. This led Lobdell to wind up in the poor house in Delhi, New York, where they met Marie Louise Perry in 1860 or 1861. They spent nearly 20 years together.

In 1871, their relationship became a national news story when an Overseer of the Poor wrote an article detailing his encounter with them: 'Joe Lobdell and Wife  Their History, &c.' Other newspapers picked up the story, and The New York Times noted Lobdell's masculinity, attributing it to their hard life, saying 'the wild life she has led, and the hardships she has endured, have driven every feminine feature from her face.'

The press saw Lobdell's gender identity as something shaped by external forces - social and economic. But why did a person like Lobdell lead this kind of life? Their family and neighbors began citing their gender identity and marriage to a woman as evidence of insanity. One neighbor declared Lobdell was insane because 'she frequently claims that she is a man and has a wife.'

In many cases of female husbands, members of their own community were understanding and sympathetic towards them. But the situation with Lobdell was different. Here we see their neighbors and community members turning against them, describing them harshly before a judge who held their life (freedom) and death (forced institutionalization) in his hands. Whether at the behest of Lobdell's brother John, who really wanted them institutionalized, or from their own negative experiences, the neighbors told the judge what he needed to hear to order Lobdell institutionalized against their will.

Accusations of insanity were never made towards Wilson or Guelph, who were deemed deceitful, immoral, and odd, but also resourceful, bold, and even charming. Some ambiguity surrounded views of Wilson and Guelph because the source of their transgression was unclear. Were they motivated by the desire to move easily from one country to another, as both did? Were they seeking more lucrative work denied to women? Were they escaping someone and chasing another? Were they lonely? Any number of reasons could explain why a person assigned female at birth would lead an unconventional life like Lobdell.

text:
I'd like to discuss a peculiar perspective I refer to as 'pro-extinctionist views', which has surprisingly garnered acceptance among several philosophers. Before we delve deeper, it's essential to clarify this stance. Proponents of this view would agree that the prospect of humanity's catastrophic end is appalling and unfortunate, and they believe efforts should be made to prevent such an outcome. No rational person would wish for billions to perish. However, pro-extinctionists propose that the state of Extinct  ceasing to exist  would be preferable to the alternative, Extant  continuing to exist. Why? There are numerous reasons put forth. One argument is that by no longer existing, we could prevent future human suffering from occurring, which might be less detrimental or more beneficial than our current state.

The idea can be approached in various ways. The first angle revolves around the possibility of an abundance of future human suffering. According to cosmologist Carl Sagan, who supported a further-loss view in his writings, if humanity persists on Earth for another 10 million years, there could potentially be over 500 trillion people. This number skyrockets when considering space colonization  philosopher Nick Bostrom estimated that as many as 1 followed by 23 zeros biological humans per century within the Virgo Supercluster in 2003, although the figure rises to 1038 if we factor in digital people living in virtual-reality computer simulations. Further calculations in Bostrom's book Superintelligence (2014) suggested that over 1058 digital people could exist across the universe as a whole. These estimates were driven by the further-loss view, which values the nonexistence of all these future people and the value they might have created as a significant opportunity cost of going extinct.

However, one could argue the opposite: even if these future people lead lives that are ultimately worthwhile, the suffering they will inevitably endure during their lives could still amount to an immense, absolute toll. Yet, if we were to go extinct, none of this suffering would occur. The second consideration is based on the premise  which I find persuasive  that there are certain forms of suffering that no amount of happiness can offset. Consider a hypothetical scenario where you must undergo a painful surgical procedure that leaves you bedridden for a month. You opt for the surgery, recover, and go on to live another 50 joyful years. The surgery and recovery process might have been excruciating  something you wouldn't wish to relive  but you might still contend that it was 'worth it'. In other words, the decades of happiness you enjoyed post-surgery counterbalanced the pain you experienced. But now ponder some of the most horrific events in our world: child abuse, genocides, ethnic cleansings, massacres; pain that no amount of happiness could ever compensate for. If we were to go extinct, none of this suffering would take place.

In essence, pro-extinctionists argue that the potential for alleviating future human suffering and the possibility of eliminating certain uncompensatable forms of suffering are reasons enough to embrace the prospect of going extinct. This is a complex and controversial argument, but one worth considering as we reflect on our role in this world and the future of humanity.

Here, too, nostalgia functions as a response to trauma – the disturbing moral ambiguity of the present – but ‘yesterday’, instead of referring to the singer’s personally remembered past, now evokes the distant past of her grandpa’s generation, which she depends on him to confirm. Empirical research on the effects of golden age thinking – that is, collective historical nostalgia – is still in its infancy. The little that exists has focused on nationalist nostalgia, and the results are sobering. In a 2014 study, participants played a game in which they were presented with the opportunity to punish a transgression against an in-group member by sacrificing money. The in-group was defined by nationality – in this case, Irish. When participants were primed with collective personal nostalgia – ‘recall a past event experienced with fellow Irish-people’ – it led individuals to sacrifice more money ‘in order to punish a transgression perpetrated against an in-group member’. Another study from 2014 led by the Dutch psychologist Anouk Smeekes, published as ‘Longing for the country’s good old days’ found a significant correlation between collective historical nostalgia for ‘the way it was’ in the Netherlands and opposition to the rights of Muslim immigrants. A follow-up study suggested that national nostalgia fuelled out-group prejudice via a sense of ethnic national identity. ‘You see this nostalgic discourse in Western Europe, or with the rise of Donald Trump,’ says Smeekes, explaining the inspiration for her work. ‘They say: “There were glory days in our national past, when everyone was safe, and then all the changes came, one of which is immigration, and if you vote for me I will return us to the glorious past and give back the country to its original people.”’ This new research demonstrates how, in the crucible of collective historical nostalgia, personal selflessness can become in-group selfishness, a phenomenon described by theologian Reinhold Niebuhr in his 1932 classic Moral Man and Immoral Society. text:
In the realm of academic inquiry, many fields touch on what we might call 'morality'. Among these, normative ethics stands out as one of the least detached from everyday moral concerns. Rather than dissecting how people and societies perceive morality, normative ethicists strive to understand what constitutes straightforward goodness or wickedness.

The philosophical subfield of meta-ethics, on the other hand, takes a step back to consider the nature of these inquiries. It questions whether there are definitive answers to moral dilemmas, or if ethics is more of an illusion or personal opinion. Over the past decade, my work has primarily revolved around this latter area. I firmly believe that there are facts about right and wrong, and that some of these truths transcend individual perspectives - they're 'stance-independent'. To put it plainly, causing immense pain to others is morally wrong not because we disapprove, but because it inflicts harm.

Now, not everyone shares this viewpoint. Some are confused, conflating 'objective' with 'universal', 'innate', or 'rule-bound'. But many skeptics have more profound doubts about moral objectivity. They argue that for morality to be objective, it must rest on a solid foundation - one they believe is missing. Others try to prove that such a foundation exists.

In my opinion, both sides of this debate are off the mark. Moral truths are objective, but they don't necessitate or accommodate a foundation. They simply exist, floating alongside the realm of values without any support from elsewhere. Some parts can be explained by others, but the entire web or network of good and evil is brute - raw, undeniable reality.

If this perspective seems strange or even unworthy of consideration, you're not alone. I once felt the same way. The goal of this essay, based on my book 'Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System' (2022), is to nudge you towards seeing the world as I now see it. Let's start by defining what we mean by a 'foundation'. We can gain clarity by considering whether a moral theory like utilitarianism could serve as one. Utilitarianism suggests that actions are right only if they promote overall wellbeing. So, does this make utilitarianism a foundation?

As you delve deeper into the world of ethics, I invite you to challenge your preconceptions and engage with these ideas, just as I have over the past years.

text:
Hey there, folks! I'm just a regular guy who happens to be a professor at Yale University, but let me tell you, I'm not one of those genius types. When I told my four grown kids that I was going to teach a new course on genius, they thought it was the funniest thing they'd ever heard! "You? You're no genius! You're more of a plodder." And they were right.

So how did I end up teaching a successful course on genius at Yale and even writing an Amazon Book of the Year selection, The Hidden Habits of Genius (2020)? Well, I guess I must have taken Nikola Tesla's advice to heart: "The boldness of ignorance."

I started my professional life trying to be a concert pianist during the Cold War era. Back then, the U.S. was in a race with the Soviet Union to excel at everything. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a young pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition, which was like the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Bobby Fischer from Brooklyn defeated Boris Spassky in chess.

Seeing my interest in music and being tall with big hands, my mom thought I'd be the next Van Cliburn. Although we weren't rich, my parents managed to get me a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, D.C. Soon, I was off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music where every opportunity was thrown at me. And I worked hard: by the time I was 21, I had put in around 15,000 hours of focused practice (Mozart only needed 6,000 to become a master composer and performer).

But within two years, I realized that I wouldn't make a dime as a concert pianist. I had everything going for me except one thing: I lacked musical talent. No special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no perfect pitch  all things crucial to a professional performer. "If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach"  that's the mantra of conservatoires like the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists?

My intuition was to find a larger arena in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn how to become a college professor and a researcher of music history  a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I landed a job at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the "three Bs": Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms. But it was Mozart who truly captivated me, especially after the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a while, the entire world seemed obsessed with this funny, passionate composer.

text: How should governments respond to the prostitution trade? It’s a question laced with deep-rooted contests – over power, money and the pursuit of equality. But as organisations and advocates increasingly line up behind opposing answers, it’s a question that is becoming difficult to ignore. The fault lines are clear: recognise prostitution as work or as abuse; either see it as compatible with feminism or irredeemably sexist. Accept it, or work to end it. One policy prescription currently gathering weight as the ‘progressive’ response to the sex trade is full decriminalisation. Championed by institutions such as UNAIDS, Amnesty International, and the billionaire financier George Soros’s Open Society Foundations, full decriminalisation involves removing all laws that specifically restrict prostitution or third-party profiteering from it. The main push for this laissez-faire approach has come from the Global Network of Sex Work Projects (NSWP) – a group whose support was cited in Amnesty’s draft prostitution policy, and whose vice president Alejandra Gil was sentenced last year to 15 years in prison for sex trafficking. The NSWP’s rationale is that being paid to have sex is regular service work, and that the best way of promoting the safety of those involved is to leave the prostitution trade entirely to its own devices. In discussions about prostitution laws, a distinction is often made between ‘full decriminalisation’ and ‘legalisation’, with the latter defined (by the NSWP) as ‘the introduction of laws that aim to impose state regulation and control sex work’. This is something that the NSWP vehemently rejects, opposing ‘all forms of criminalisation and other legal oppression of sex work’ as well as limits on ‘how, when and where sex work happens’. New Zealand is commonly the go-to case study of a fully decriminalised prostitution regime, repeatedly invoked as an exemplar by the Open Society Foundations in their report ‘Ten Reasons to Decriminalize Sex Work’ (2012). It’s worth noting, however, that New Zealand doesn’t actually meet the criteria of a fully decriminalised regime. There, local authorities can impose limits on prostitution advertising as well as where brothels are located, while women who sell sex are breaking the law if they fail to adopt safer sex practices. The crucial point here is that regardless of which label is applied, legalised and so-called decriminalised prostitution regimes share some basic properties – properties that beg the question: do all those calling for full decriminalisation of the sex trade in the name of human rights seriously realise what they are asking for? Do they realise, for instance, that brothels become legitimate businesses? In New Zealand, which adopted its so-called decriminalised prostitution regime in 2003, brothels with four or fewer people selling sex from them don’t even have to apply for a licence before opening. Brothel-keepers operating larger outfits have to get a certificate, although, consistent with the hands-off ethos advocated by champions of full decriminalisation, there is no ongoing inspection

text:
JOHANNESBURG  South Africa's sports minister, Fikile Mbalula, announced Monday that the country's national cricket and rugby federations would not be allowed to host or bid for international tournaments for at least a year, due to their inadequate efforts to increase representation of black players. The Athletics South Africa (ASA), Cricket South Africa (CSA), Netball South Africa (NSA) and South African Rugby (SARU) were all affected by the minister's decision, which came as a significant setback for the latter's plans to bid for the 2023 Rugby World Cup.

The bidding process commences next month. The minister's decision was prompted by a report revealing that four out of the five major sports in South Africa had failed to create sufficient opportunities for black players. The soccer federation, however, was the exception, having met its transformation targets, according to Mbalula.

Mbalula warned that he would reassess this decision once the results of the 2016-17 Transformation Barometer are considered, likely at the end of next year. His move is an attempt to encourage more black participation in South Africa's most popular sports, over two decades since the end of apartheid. Despite the passage of time, whites still constitute a substantial majority in athletics, cricket, netball, and rugby teams.

"In light of this, I have resolved to revoke the hosting and bidding privileges of ASA, CSA, NSA, and SARU for major and mega international tournaments," Mbalula stated.

text:
ISLAMABAD: After gaining approval from the National Assembly, the upper house, the Senate, passed the Gas Infrastructure Development Cess (GIDC) bill 2015 on Wednesday amid protests from opposition members. During the ensuing debate, opposition party members criticized the government for introducing the bill in the house without first presenting it to the Council of Common Interest (CCI) for consultation with the provinces. Senator Tahir Hussain Mashhadi of MQM argued that the bill should have been presented in the CCI, stating that any tax on natural resources cannot be imposed without provincial consultation. Senators Nauman Wazir and Saleem Mandviwala advocated for a mechanism to finance gas infrastructure development, while also acknowledging the need to address opposition concerns.

Opposition Leader Aitzaz Ahsan and several other senators, including Usman Saifullah, Mohsin Aziz, Shahi Syed, Saeed Ghani, Usman Kakar, Sassui Paleejo, Robina Adnan, Naseema Ehsan, and Mohsin Leghari voiced their opposition to the bill during the discussion. Members belonging to Muttahida Qaumi Movement (MQM), Pakistan Tehreek Insaf (PTI), Awami National Party (ANP) and Balochistan National Awami Party walked out in protest from the House.

Earlier, Minister for Petroleum and Natural Resources Shahid Khaqan Abbasi, in his concluding remarks, explained that the National Assembly had already passed the bill and that the government needed Rs1000 billion annually to maintain and improve gas infrastructure within the country. He noted that Rs100 billion would be collected annually through this levy, with the funds allocated for gas infrastructure development. Abbasi also highlighted several major projects, such as the IP gas pipeline project, Turkmenistan-Pakistan-India (TAPI) pipeline project, LNG import projects, and LPG supply enhancement projects in both public and private sectors, which necessitated the creation of this new cess. He warned that without the necessary infrastructure for these projects, the government would be compelled to import more expensive liquefied fuels, potentially leading to liquidated damages or payments.

Currently, neither the federal government nor any gas utility has sufficient funding to implement these crucial projects. To address this issue, Abbasi suggested forming a committee to identify and rectify any anomalies in the bill, which would then present recommendations to make the bill more beneficial. He also emphasized that it is the current government's intention to provide gas to every district of Balochistan. APP/Geo News

We brought our drinks with us and were pleased when they offered glasses and an opener without any hassle (although I noticed the website doesn't mention anything about outside beverages). All in all, we had a great time and kept our bill fairly low thanks to the Entertainment coupon. If not for that, we may never have discovered this gem! text: a camera. Portraiture had become democratised – and smilified. From the turn of the 21st century, iPhone photography and social media confirmed the preferred individual expression of social identity was through smiling. The technologies also worked to erode barriers with global smiling cultures that had formerly been less reflective of Western practices. One in every five of the more than 500 million Twitter messages sent each day, it has been calculated, contains an emoji. It is the lingua franca of the globalised mass culture of the electronic age. The most utilised of all the 3,000-plus available emojis is the ‘smile with tears of joy’, an upgraded version of the original smiley. In 2019, the long march of the modern (Western) smile received a severe jolt, with the appearance of COVID-19. Suddenly, that expression retreated behind a surgical mask. It is true that the perceptive among us may have been aware that a genuine and sincere smile causes a detectable crinkling of muscles around the eyes. But then we are not all so perceptive. And who smiles only sincerely anyway? The emoji registered the hit. Though the ‘smile with tears of joy’ maintained the top spot in global usage, another emoji surged dramatically: the surgically masked face. The popularity of the masked-face emoji became so intense that when, in November 2020, Apple released its annual additions to the range, it was thought wise to tweak the mask emoji, adding colour to the cheeks and a meaningful creasing around the eyes so as to give the appearance of smiling under the mask. The smile, it seemed, was fighting back. And indeed it looks unlikely to lose its iconic cultural value and global appeal. A receding pandemic status across the world gives everyone something to smile about.

text:
In various academic realms, one could argue that the pursuit of morality is a central focus. Among these, normative ethics within philosophy stands out as it approaches the study of morality in a manner less detached from its essence. Rather than examining how people perceive and discuss morality, normative ethicists strive to uncover what constitutes moral goodness or badness fundamentally.

Meta-ethics, another philosophical branch, takes a more introspective view on the nature of the inquiries carried out by normative ethicists. It grapples with questions such as whether there exist absolute answers concerning good and evil, or if morality is, instead, an illusion or subjective matter. Over the past decade, much of my work has been dedicated to meta-ethics. I am convinced that moral right and wrong are not mere constructs of our perspectives but have inherent truths. These truths can be considered as 'stance-independent', implying that their moral wrongness is not contingent upon our disapproval; rather, actions like torture are immoral because they inflict immense suffering on people.

I believe that these objective moral truths can be grasped, and some individuals possess a greater aptitude for understanding them than others. If you'd prefer to label these individuals as 'moral experts', I have no objection. Of course, not everyone shares my viewpoint, and there are various reasons for this. Some misunderstand the concept of objectivity, equating it with cultural universality or innateness or adherence to a few unwavering principles. However, many people's doubts about moral objectivity stem from more profound concerns.

One common misgiving revolves around the idea that for morality to have any basis, it must rest upon a foundation that, in their opinion, remains elusive. Others are eager to demonstrate that such a foundation or ultimate ground for morality exists. In my view, both sides of this debate miss the mark. Morality is objective, but it does not necessitate nor accommodate a foundation. It simply exists, along with the evaluative realm more broadly, unsupported by anything else. Portions of it can be rationalized through other components, but the entirety of the moral spectrum is largely brute  raw and unfiltered.

You might find this perspective strange or even deserving of rejection outright. I once held similar reservations. The goal of this essay, inspired by my book "Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System" (2022), is to prompt you to consider this facet of the world as I now do. To clarify, what exactly is a 'foundation'? We can better understand what a foundation entails by questioning whether a moral theory like utilitarianism could serve as one. Utilitarianism proposes that actions are right only if they promote overall wellbeing. So, does this make it a foundation for morality? Not quite  at least not in the traditional sense of a foundation providing a solid ground upon which other ideas can be built.

We know, for example, that many well-known variants that are clearly associated with very serious human disease are the normal state in other species. All this might seem confusing: genes are molecules and hence fundamental causal agents of life, yet their effects are highly probabilistic and very hard to pin down or predict. As we have tried to explain, although genetics and evolutionary research are often very technical, the issues are actually reasonably simple. That’s fortunate, because an understanding of how life and evolution work as an orderly collection of uncertainties can lead us to a better sense of what is ‘inherent’ in our nature, and why. In this light, we can return to the intriguing topic of behavior and, particularly, of free will. What we know about life undermines the explanatory power of molecular reductionism: that is, the attempt to use genetic variants to predict not only physical traits but also higher-level phenomena – such as the ability to do calculus, or write poetry – which seem to ‘emerge’ magically out of nowhere. For scientists attempting to understand life’s complexity, this might be the winter of our discontent, but Richard III’s soliloquy was written in Shakespeare’s hand — not his genome. Complex organisation arises from webs of interaction among causal factors. Even if individual factors cannot be held responsible for particular developments, complex phenomena such as people, skills, skulls, languages, and even football teams clearly do exist, and have a material rather than any mystical or immaterial basis. text:
Brea's collection of biota from the Late Pleistocene is truly astonishing, Harris shared with me. 'We have over 600 different species of animals and plants from the final stage of the last Ice Age, and there's more diversity preserved here than any other deposit of similar age,' he said. The sheer number of some species is astounding. 'If you want to study living wolves,' Harris explained, 'you'd be lucky to find 20 in a museum. Here, we have over 4,000 dire wolves.'

As the camel chews and starts to digest its meal of shrubby plants, carbon and nitrogen isotopes from those plants become integrated into its tissues and bones. Unlike charismatic megafauna like mammoths or saber-toothed cats, many of the 600 species extracted from La Brea are still around today. 'Most animals smaller than a coyote are still living,' Harris explained, 'but they're not found in this part of the world.' For instance, a specific type of snail found trapped in the asphalt is now only found in cooler, wetter habitats at elevations above 5,000 feet in California and Arizona.

Thanks to La Brea's natural asphalt, we have an incredibly detailed understanding of the plants and animals that existed during that time. 'Asphalt acts as a great preservative,' Harris says. In most cases, bones trapped in asphalt are not even mineralized, and remain the original bone of the animal. Although they still count as fossils, they're not like the hard stone dinosaur bones you might be used to seeing. When a museum volunteer uncovers a Smilodon skull, they're actually touching the stained bone of the big cat.

Early 20th century paleontologists would boil the bones in kerosene to remove the tar and paid little attention to the microfossils found in the sludge that came off the skeletal parts. Today, researchers at La Brea take great care to extract every fossil they can. Not only are the bones carefully collected and documented so that the entire bonebed can be reconstructed from notes, but the matrix surrounding those bones is also labeled to record its association with a specific grid square. This matrix is then treated with biodiesel to reduce pounds of sediment and asphalt into a siftable slurry that yields small fossils. These are some of the most crucial pieces of evidence in the collection. Sabercats and mammoths may grab visitors' attention, but the microfossils provide a much more detailed picture of the ancient environment.

Even when bones make it to the lab, volunteers spend a significant amount of time picking out the tiny fossils that have been preserved inside skull cavities or in the matrix clinging to bones. 'They keep you chained to your chair until you identify all the little bones,' Herb Schiff, a friend and Page Museum volunteer, told me while showing off a dire wolf skull. A hassle for volunteers perhaps, but a treasure trove for researchers. Despite its challenges, La Brea remains an invaluable resource for understanding our planet's past.

MELBOURNE - The Australian Open experienced a significant upset on Tuesday as Fernando Verdasco triumphed over Rafael Nadal in straight sets (7-6(3), 6-4, 6-4). This defeat marked Nadal's earliest grand slam exit since 2009 and his first at Melbourne Park.

Nadal, the 2009 champion, found himself in a 2-set deficit against Verdasco who had just returned from a cross-season training camp. However, this early elimination will do little to diminish the prevailing belief that Nadal, with 14 final appearances in any of the grand slams last season, is still without a major trophy since 2004.

Verdasco displayed an impressive comeback, recovering from the brink by delivering a game laden with 90 winners and 91 unforced errors. His performance served as a partial revenge for his loss in the epic five-set final against Nadal in the 2009 tournament, which remains one of the longest matches recorded at the Australian Open.

At 32 years old, Verdasco demonstrated exceptional serving skills throughout each tiebreak, particularly in the second set to keep the match alive. However, he stumbled early in the fifth set, succumbing to a break. Fortune often plays a part in Nadal's defeats, and this was evident when Verdasco swung wildly at a second serve with his racquet frame, sending the ball wide.

Apologetically raising his hands, Verdasco followed up the unintentional error by acing Nadal to take the lead. He then amplified his aggression on Nadal's serve, a move that eventually forced errors from the more fancied opponent. The pressure proved too much for Nadal who, despite reaching six grand slam finals last year, struggled under the weight of expectations.

"This year was a completely different story," Nadal admitted. "I had been playing and practicing well, so it's tough when you work so hard and you arrive at such an important event only to exit early."

The notion of "ability" became a silent mantra, a twist on Marx's adage, 'to each according to their needs.' In the early 20th century, intelligence tests were championed as evidence that each person possessed an inherent, relatively fixed intellectual capacity. This idea was often misused during the growing eugenics movement to justify widespread racial discrimination. Terman, for instance, argued in 1916 that low IQ scores among Mexican and African American families were 'very, very common.' He further suggested that these children should be segregated into separate classes because they struggled with abstractions but could be made efficient workers. From his perspective, low IQ scores were the simple result of an objective deficiency. We now know how misguided that assumption was. Today, many psychologists view IQ and aptitude tests as culturally biased to some extent - favoring abilities valued in Western culture and potentially privileging those raised in that environment. Despite efforts to make these tests fairer for non-dominant cultures, inequities persist.

As social Darwinism took hold in America after the First World War, the government began devising its own chilling solution to the 'grave problem' Terman had warned about. In 1927, the US Supreme Court ruled in favor of compulsory sterilization of the 'feeble-minded' for the sake of public welfare. Over the next four decades, US states sterilized thousands of people with low IQ scores; a disproportionate number of victims were nonwhite. In later years, although the eugenic roots of aptitude tests would fade from view, the ranking of test-takers based on perceived social value continued unabated.

I didn't fully understand until much later in life that I was a direct beneficiary of the hierarchical system Terman worked so hard to establish. As a child with above-average intelligence test scores, I was often praised, petted, even loved, in ways many of my peers were not. I received certificates for high scores on tests I hadn't studied for. From fifth grade onwards, my aptitude test scores opened doors to academic summer programs run by the Johns Hopkins Center for Talented Youth, where I explored subjects like logic and creative writing that were rarely taught in school. I thrived in the company of similarly gifted classmates.

But as I looked around, I began to wonder. What about my peers who weren't 'gifted'? Why did they not get the same opportunities or attention as me? As I grew older and more aware, I realized that the label 'gifted' came with an unspoken implication: I was somehow superior. But is intelligence really a matter of superiority or inferiority, or is it simply a different way of seeing the world? These questions still linger in my mind today.

text:
In the realm of learning and brainstorming, we wanted to celebrate not just the creative sparks of our students but also the underdogs, the novices, and everyone in between who might slip through the cracks. So, we introduced 'mini-c', a concept that encompasses those personal epiphanies and spontaneous moments of inspiration that often bring a smile to your face or make you pause for thought. It could be a fleeting daydream, a whimsical thought, or an unexpected 'aha' moment with potential far-reaching implications. Sometimes we share these ideas with others; sometimes we keep them to ourselves. But they hold significance for us - however small it may be - and that makes them worthwhile.

Mini-c is unique in that it should feel fresh and meaningful to you, rather than being strictly novel or task-appropriate as conventionally defined. It's about discovering something new for yourself, even if others might not find it particularly groundbreaking. A person could experience dozens of mini-c moments each day. For instance, someone might come up with catchy lyrics to a popular tune to describe their current situation (like, "Im leaving... in my Kia. Don't think I'll try to use the heat" to the tune of 'Leaving on a Jet Plane'). Others may conjure up spontaneous puns, figure out how to improvise when ingredients run short in a recipe, doodle caricatures of colleagues during video calls, or jam out jazzy variations on familiar melodies. As novices and students begin learning a creative skill, they'll likely start here as they develop their craft. Similarly, those who replicate existing work with their own personal touch will also find themselves in this space. Think about someone sketching a copy of a masterpiece in an art museum or telling a joke with their unique twist. All of these moments fall under the mini-c umbrella and serve a purpose: they can provide a momentary distraction, ignite further creative thinking, or mark the first step on the road to becoming an expert creator. A simple doodle might evolve into a complete piece of art. An improvised tool could become a basic prototype.

Regrettably, many overlook mini-c - and may not even realize it as creativity in the first place. Why does this matter? If you view these everyday behaviors as creative acts, you'll be more inclined to practice creativity yourself. You can't succeed if you don't try, and everything begins with a tiny spark of mini-c. If you don't embrace those fleeting whimsies or trust those small insights, you may miss out on the chance to tap into your full creative potential.

Many of the metaphors humans seem to favour are based on spatial images, and so they lend themselves well to the imagistic medium of gesture. The best-studied example of gestural metaphors are those used to represent time. People all over the world think of time as having concrete spatial properties. And they gesture accordingly, as though events had a certain ‘length’ or were located in different places. English speakers, for example, often imagine sequences either as a line stretching across their bodies – with past events to the left and future ones to the right – or as a path they are walking on – with past events behind them and future ones ahead. These particular metaphorical models, however, are not universal. Speakers of Aymara in the Andes point to a future that is not ahead but behind them. Mandarin speakers locate next week below last week. When Yupno speakers gesture about time, as my collaborators and I have documented, they locate the past downhill and the future uphill, no matter which way they are facing. A complete account of gestural universals and diversity would have to consider a number of other phenomena, too – how people use their hands and bodies to greet each other, to represent numbers, to ask questions, to beckon or dismiss, and to negotiate the conversational floor. Certainly, there is still a lot of work to be done to further document such behaviours. But a reasonable guess is that the punchline would be the same: gestures around the world exhibit constrained diversity. This, after all, is the primary moral that can be extracted from the best-studied workhorses of gestural communication. On the one hand, several general methods of gesturing – signalling ‘yes’ and ‘no’, pointing, depicting, conjuring metaphors in the air – appear to be universally used and in constrained ways. On the other, when we dig into the details of precisely how such methods are deployed, we find unexpected texture. This recurring pattern of constrained diversity should perhaps be unsurprising. People everywhere have the same bodily equipment to work with – our hands, arms and heads are basically the same, and they flex, bend and rotate in the same basic ways. We also have the same kinds of minds, which means we tend to notice and care about the same sorts of things. And we all have to solve similar communication problems – problems such as orienting attention, correcting misunderstandings, and posing questions. This novel, "Infinite Jest," feels incompatible with human life as we know it. It embodies an unsettling and pervasive sense of evil, not just a trait but the very essence of conscious existence. It's like a poison seeping into the most fundamental layers of oneself  a cellular and spiritual nausea. It's hard to put into words, but it's a kind of double bind where any action or inaction we associate with human agency feels not just unpleasant, but downright horrifying. No description I've encountered has managed to capture the excruciating agony I experienced quite as vividly and directly as this book did.

The book's most famous lines revolve around suicidality and the inescapable logic that comes with it. It compares the decision of a person considering suicide to that of someone trapped in a burning building deciding whether to jump. Don't be fooled by those who leap from burning windows, the book suggests. Their fear of falling is just as great as ours would be, standing there idly looking out. The terror isn't about wanting to fall; it's about the flames. And those on the sidewalk, shouting and urging you not to jump, can never truly understand the terror beyond the fall. They haven't experienced the flames themselves.

The suicidal person, then, is not misguided but rather facing choices that are unimaginable to those who don't share their experience of being slowly consumed by those flames. Reading this book was like looking into a mirror at that moment, reflecting back my feelings so accurately and directly. It validated my unique sense of this 'mental illness,' not as a mistake or miscalculation on my part, but as something happening to me  a force within me, a billowing shape, as the book often describes it, to which all my dread and despair were just rational responses.

But finishing the book marked the beginning of my struggle to hold onto this self-understanding  and thus, my grasp on life itself. It was only a few days later that I attempted suicide. I believe reading "Infinite Jest" had been keeping me alive up until then. So, when he came to the realization...

This essay is about how the novel "Infinite Jest" resonated with the author's feelings of despair and suicidality, validating their experiences, but also potentially triggering a suicide attempt shortly after finishing the book. The metaphor of being trapped in a burning building is used to explain the terror of suicide and the seemingly unimaginable choices faced by those experiencing it. The essay concludes with the author's reflection on how the book had been keeping them alive, but that their grip on this understanding began to slip shortly after finishing it.

text:
In the backdrop of decolonization, a pressing need arose to reshape and redefine British identity, not along the paternalistic lines of the ruling class as Orwell did during fascism, but grounded in the communal values of the working class. E.P Thompson's book embodied this shift, although it was subtly influenced by the tides of decolonization. The Age of Revolution, his subject matter, was stirred by rebellion abroad. Post-WWII anticolonial rebellion, too, left deep fissures in British thought and activism. Thompson penned his book under the looming shadow of disillusionment with the West following anticolonial strife. Shortly after co-founding the New Left Review in 1960, he departed in a huff as theoretically-inclined editors took over the reins. He was particularly irked by the journal's embrace of 'Third Worldism', which held the West accountable for its complicity with colonialism. In his resignation letter in 1963, the year The Making of the English Working Class was published, he wrote, "The entire 'West' stands impeached by its complicity with colonialism."

Affirming the shared interests of peoples across the alleged West-Third World divide, Thompson engaged with Frantz Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth (1961), which advised strongly against mimicking a West whose culture and institutions were evidently morally and practically bankrupt. Though understanding its context made it 'not only comprehensible but inevitable', Thompson vehemently disputed the notion that the 'West' had 'nothing to offer'. His endeavor in his work was to resurrect alternative 'English' values - radical, humanistic ones - as a defiant act of redemption. "For us," he stated, "the 'European game' can never be over": "If 'our' tradition has failed... then it is for us to fix it, not to hastily conclude that 'the humanist values discovered in the West are corrupted beyond repair'."

He sought to uncover a different England whose values didn't inexorably lead to empire. This radical English legacy was also his response to his adversaries' derisive caricature of an 'English ideology' - inveterate traditionalism, empiricism, and parochialism that they alleged kept the working class subdued and the intelligentsia confined within ivory towers, unlike in 'Other Countries'. Thompson delved into 'The Peculiarities of the English', as he titled his 1965 essay on this debate, partly to defend their radical heritage, the 'revolutionary inheritance' that was 'the tradition of dissent'. We know that these values were molded through interaction with other traditions, as Thompson's own values had been. He knew this too, criticizing his opponents for drawing 'hermetic divisions between national cultures which are quite unreal.' But since England's redemption was at stake in this debate, it was his focus.

The stakes for recovering a Britain that was both distinct and egalitarian were high. The debate was not about denying the faults of the West but reimagining a future where its failures could be remedied, not discarded. Thompson sought to find a path forward without completely abandoning the progressive values that emerged from the West. His work was a plea for a nuanced understanding of history and culture, one that recognized the complexities and contradictions inherent in every society.

text:
In the midst of crafting my novel, D.H. Lawrence's words resonated deeply with me and mirrored aspects of my life like an echo. I wasn't seeking guidance from him nor was I hunting for self-help remedies. Lawrence was simply a catalyst, stirring thought and provoking reflection. He never conformed to societal expectations; instead, he scrutinized his own thoughts under a harsh, discerning light, challenging the status quo and questioning the implications of opposing viewpoints. This intrinsic quality was evident in his keen observation of people and nature alike - children, adults, animals, and even the world around them.

In 'Education of the People,' penned in 1918, Lawrence proposed an unconventional approach to child-rearing. He suggested that babies should be taken away from their modern mothers and given to rather indifferent women who wouldn't bother with them too much. Although this advice may seem extreme or even dismissible at first glance, I believe there's a kernel of truth in it. Lawrence wasn't advocating for neglectful parenting; instead, he cautioned against overwhelming our children with affection.

Lawrence's ideas on democracy and race were also contentious. He was an outspoken critic of both, expressing views that we might find distasteful today. However, I believe it is essential to separate the profound wisdom found within his novels from the less nuanced, single-minded opinions expressed in his essays. In many instances, Lawrence pushed his ideas to their comic extremes, but this methodology, in my opinion, offered value. He was a writer who embraced intellectual play, allowing multiple thoughts to coexist within his mind and permitting every position to converse with its opposite.

In our current divisive age, where expressing ambivalence or contradiction feels increasingly challenging, Lawrence's unorthodox parenting advice offers food for thought. I believe that parents who demand an emotional response at every turn, transforming every disagreement into an emotional battlefield, inadvertently rob their children of privacy and the blank curiosity needed to explore the world independently.

Lawrence's counsel on excessive love resonates with me. It feels true that parents who overindulge in affection may inadvertently stifle their children's ability to forge their own paths, fostering dependence rather than independence. While I don't necessarily agree with Lawrence's extremes, his advice serves as a timely reminder that love, like any powerful force, should be wielded carefully and sparingly if we wish to raise confident, independent human beings.

Thinkers, writers and poets in trouble with the authorities in their home states came to Jena, drawn by the openness and relative freedoms. Schiller himself had arrived after he had been arrested for his revolutionary play The Robbers (1781) in his home state, the Duchy of Württemberg. On a lucky day at the end of the 18th century, you might have seen more famous writers, poets and philosophers in Jena’s streets than in a larger city in an entire century. There was the tall, gaunt-looking Schiller (who could only write with a drawer full of rotten apples in his desk), the stubborn philosopher Fichte, who put the self at the centre of his work, and the young scientist Alexander von Humboldt – the first to predict harmful human-induced climate change. The brilliant Schlegel brothers, Friedrich and August Wilhelm, both of them writers and critics with pens as sharp as the French guillotines, lived in Jena, as did the young philosopher Friedrich Schelling, who redefined the relationship between the individual and nature, and G W F Hegel, who would become one of the most influential philosophers in the Western world. In an age of absolutism, the Jena Set were united by an obsession with the free self Also in Jena was the formidable and free-spirited Caroline Michaelis-Böhmer-Schlegel-Schelling. She carried the names of her father and three husbands, but she was fiercely independent and had no intention of living according to social conventions. The young poet Novalis, who had studied in Jena, regularly visited his friends there from his family estate in nearby Weißenfels. In the winter months, you might have glimpsed Germany’s most celebrated poet, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, as he skated on the river – his bulging belly buttoned together with a flowery waistcoat. Older and more famous, Goethe became something like a benevolent godfather to the younger generation. He was inspired, even rejuvenated, by their new and radical ideas, and they, in turn, worshipped him. These great thinkers attracted students from across Germany and Europe to Jena. There's a vast void - which he referred to as the 'Promethean gap' - between our capacity for self-destruction and our ability to truly grasp the immense scale of catastrophic extinction. This aligns with a psychological phenomenon called 'psychic numbing', a term coined by psychologist Paul Slovic, describing our inability to fully appreciate the loss of life as it becomes larger. The importance of saving one life is significant when it's the first, but its significance decreases slightly as more lives are saved. So, psychologically, the value we place on saving one life diminishes against the backdrop of a larger threat - we may not feel much different, or value the difference, between saving 87 lives and 88, when presented separately.

Now, imagine if the number wasn't 87 or 88 deaths but 8 billion - the total human population on Earth today. As a 1947 Washington Post article quoted Joseph Stalin as saying, "if only one man dies of hunger, that is a tragedy. If millions die, thats only statistics," which is often condensed to: "A single death is a tragedy, a million deaths are a statistic." The point being, an extinction-causing catastrophe would be unfathomably devastating, something our minds struggle to process, intellectually or emotionally. Yet, simply acknowledging this reality can help us better gauge its severity and consequences.

In my view, the horror of such catastrophes - events with the highest possible death toll - is reason enough to prioritize, as a species, projects aimed at minimizing such risks. The concept of going extinct is more profound and carries more profound implications than one might initially think. But I also believe that being extinct would be deeply regrettable for many reasons.

As Mary Shelley wrote in her novel 'The Last Man' (1826) - one of the first books to grapple with existential ethics - without humanity, there would be no more poetry, philosophy, art, music, theatre, laughter, knowledge, and science. The loss would be profoundly sad. I find myself particularly moved by the thought that our extinction would mark the end of the transgenerational pursuit of scientific understanding. It would be a tragic waste if humanity had emerged, gazed upon the Universe with puzzlement and wonder, pondered the question of why there is something rather than nothing, and then vanished before discovering the answer.

Perhaps the answer is unknowable, but even discovering that fact could provide some intellectual satisfaction and psychological closure - a 'eureka' moment that relieves and validates our prior confusion. There would be no more love, but there would also be no more heartbreak. This sentiment pulls at my heartstrings, and I am particularly affected by the thought that our extinction would bring to an abrupt end the quest for understanding that we, as a species, have embarked upon. It would be a monumental shame if humanity had appeared, marveled at the Universe, wrestled with questions of existence, and then disappeared before finding answers.

MUMBAI: Industry officials predict that Pakistan's cotton imports will remain close to record levels as the country trimms its cultivation area for the crop.

A supply shortage in Pakistan, coupled with drought-affected output in India - the world's largest producer - could potentially drive up global cotton prices, which currently hover around 11 cents per pound.

Saleem Saleh, acting secretary general of the All Pakistan Textile Mills Association (APTMA), explained, "Punjab has reduced its cultivation area." Unpredictable weather events, such as late-year floods and poor rainfall in recent months, have left many farmers reluctant to grow cotton, hampering Pakistan's attempts to boost domestic production.

Shahzad Ali Khan, chairman of the Pakistan Cotton Ginners Association, elaborated, "Farmers are finding other crops more profitable." The country's cotton output fell by a third to 9.7 million bales in 2015/16, forcing it to import a record 4 million bales that year, up from 1.2 million the previous year, according to APTMA. Pakistan's annual consumption is approximately 15 million bales.

Even for the crop season starting August 1, weather has not been favorable and the crop in Punjab has already suffered due to poor rainfall in May and June, Khan added. Cotton sowing in Pakistan typically begins in April, with harvesting commencing in July. While Pakistan has set a production target of 14.1 million bales for the new season, industry officials suggest that output will fall short, and the next few weeks' rainfall will be crucial in determining yields.

"Import requirements are rising due to increasing local consumption, but production remains stagnant," Saleh said. "This year's imports surged due to crop failure, and we expect next year's imports to remain around this year's level - the actual number depends on production."

Typically, lower output in Pakistan presents an export opportunity for neighboring India, which shipped out approximately 6.5 million bales during this season, with Pakistan importing nearly 2 million. Dhiren Sheth, president of the Cotton Association of India, noted, "India has a freight advantage over other suppliers, making it a preferred choice for buyers in Pakistan whenever they initiate imports."

However, India's recent contract to import 20,000 bales from Pakistan suggests that supply at the top producer is also running low.

In the Caribbean nation of Barbados, renowned West Indian cricket journalist, commentator, and writer Tony Cozier passed away at the age of 75 following a bout with illness. He was admitted to hospital on May 3 for tests related to infections affecting his neck and legs.

Cozier's love affair with cricket began as a teenager back in 1958, despite never having played the sport himself. Over the years, he would become one of its most esteemed voices, a distinction that was far from surprising considering his passion for the game. His final article, published on these pages on May 1, serves as a poignant farewell.

The International Cricket Council (ICC) expressed their sorrow and shared their condolences via Twitter: "Deeply saddened to hear of the passing of Tony Cozier today. One of the truly great voices of cricket. A huge loss for the cricket community."

Starting his commentary career during Australia's tour of the West Indies in 1965, Cozier went on to carve out a successful media career that spanned continents. His accomplishments included work in radio and television, authorship of numerous books, contributions to various print and digital publications, and editing of the West Indies Cricket Annual between 1970 and 1991. In 1978, Cozier published the highly acclaimed book, "The West Indies: 50 Years of Test Cricket." In recognition of his services to the game, the MCC awarded him life membership in 2011. It was estimated that Cozier had watched an impressive 266 Test matches during his nearly half-century-long career covering cricket.

Wisden reported in 2003 that he was among the most dedicated observers of Test cricket anywhere.

It's crucial to maintain a balanced perspective when discussing the plight of Latinx children detained at borders. While they share some basic humanity with their white, middle-class American counterparts, we must not oversimplify or equate their experiences. The children in cages are extraordinary beings who have braved treacherous journeys to flee from the harsh realities of capitalism and empire that have shaped them. They've endured unimaginable hardships, and many of their peers didn't survive the journey.

It's important to acknowledge these differences rather than trying to force a false sense of sameness upon them. Material comforts like a bar of soap or a flu shot don't undo the injustices they've faced. Similarly, asserting their essential similarity to privileged youth growing up within the heart of global empire is misleading.

The abstract concept of humanity is meant to help us empathize with people in vastly different circumstances, but it can also lead us astray by making us believe we are more alike than we really are. This misunderstanding can do more harm than good. After all, the idea of universal humanity has often been used to justify oppression rather than liberation for non-Western communities.

Perhaps it's time to reconsider the term 'humanity.' Instead, let's call for specific justice and recognition for those who have been marginalized by historical and contemporary structures of imperialism, racism, colonialism, and capitalism. We should strive for a more precise language that reflects our genuine desire for emancipation and liberation, rather than relying on a term that has been historically tainted with oppression.

At one point, Michelangelo worked on his ceiling masterpiece behind a partition in order to hide it from Raphael. The latter, no shrinking wallflower himself, managed to arrange a view of it and later, in his fresco The School of Athens, incorporated a seated figure taken straight from Michelangelo’s work. Thanks to these machinations, the rivalry between the two giants became one of the most famous in the annals of art. It wasn’t until the establishment of science societies in the late 16th century that major scientific rivalries reared their head. Perhaps the most notable early outbreak was the fierce war between Newton and Leibniz, each of whom claimed to be the first to invent calculus – today widely considered to have been developed independently by each of them. The feud caused such a rift between the English and European mathematics communities that, for more than a century, almost no scientific knowledge was exchanged between them. In the early 18th century, Newton balked at nothing in his campaign for priority over the invention of calculus: in 1712, the Royal Society of London published a document granting Newton ownership of the invention and discrediting Leibniz. The paper, however, should be taken with an exceptionally large grain of salt, since Newton, who was president of the society at the time, personally appointed all the committee members and even wrote large parts of the document himself. The two colossi of mathematics never met in person and it is not clear that Leibniz was ever exposed to Newton’s work. One can only imagine how a productive exchange of their ideas, disputed over a public platform, could have enhanced the introduction of calculus and the scientific developments that followed. ‘The aim of argument, or of discussion, should not be victory but progress,’ said the French 19th-century essayist Joseph Joubert. Once the new societies and their publications made information more accessible, rivalry between scientists, research institutes and even states began to drive new discoveries. Journalistic interest in the drama enabled more public exposure to science. In one notable case, the dispute between Thomas Huxley and Richard Owen, two of the leading biologists in 19th‑century Britain, shined an important spotlight on Charles Darwin’s theory of evolution, little‑known to the general public at the time. The current winter lacks one essential element - humility from us humans. What we're missing is a profound understanding of our planet beyond just a magical creature or a temporary thrill. Snowy owls are messengers hailing from places most of us will never set foot on. Yes, the world has become smaller and more accessible in many ways. From the safety of our homes, we can explore the rugged peaks of Ben Nevis, mingle with elephants across the vast plains of the Serengeti, or drift along the Colorado River beneath the vibrant cliffs of the Grand Canyon.

Yet, despite this new age of mass experience, the cinematic is no match for the real thing. It's strange that during Earth's sixth mass extinction, we're more connected than ever before, but fewer of us will witness the majesty of beluga whales breaching in freezing Arctic waters or caribou migrating across the endless tundra. Polar bears won't grace our neighborhoods with a visit this winter or any other. Instead, we have snowy owls to remind us.

From other wild places, we won't receive such messengers. Once-thriving tallgrass prairies that made the American Great Plains vibrant with ornate wildflowers and grasses as tall as we are, have been swallowed by plows and pavement. The regal fritillary butterfly, a creature so captivating it could make you consider quitting your job, selling your home, and becoming a lepidopterist, will never leave its dwindling habitats to visit the rusted cities of the Rust Belt.

No ornate butterfly fish, as radiant as a snowy owl's eyes, will migrate from the coral reefs to our Midwestern towns to remind us of the biodiversity we're losing. Perhaps the snowy owl can serve as a stand-in for these other earthbound emissaries. Whether we observe them through binoculars or on screens, their eyes may offer glimpses into distant worlds - from the Highlands to the Serengeti, from the Arctic to the Ocean. Or at least, they might inspire humility in us.

Over a century ago, Aldo Leopold found his humility in another predator. During his early years as a forest ranger, the great conservationist and philosopher rarely passed by without feeling a sense of respect for the natural world. Perhaps the snowy owl can help us rediscover that same sense of wonder and responsibility for our planet today.

The fluidity of relationships between various aspects of life has always been intriguing, especially for those who yearn to transcend the divide between imagination and reality. This desire can be traced back to Andr Breton's lecture, given several years before he visited Saint-Alban. The artists Breton referred to were indeed the surrealists, including his friend Max Ernst. However, I believe that description could just as well apply to Forestier, whose work showcased a unique blend of freedom and unity that Breton seemed to appreciate.

I suspect that Breton also saw in him a hint of a broader mission, one that he himself championed through the surrealist movement: a rebellion against an absurd order based on inequality, striving for the complete emancipation of humanity. While the concentration of distressed and disabled individuals in secure institutions may no longer be as popular an issue as it once was, the reality that fosters apathy towards their plight remains very much alive.

This reality can be observed in a media landscape that provides a platform for comments like, "Two-thirds of COVID-19 fatalities would have probably died within a year anyway." It becomes evident when acts of violence against disabled people are euphemistically called 'mercy killings.' And it surfaces when lawyers justify the death of an individual by claiming they died due to 'underlying health conditions.'

Many readers may find it unsettling to label this prevailing reality in Europe and the US as 'fascism.' I used to be one of them, preferring to view fascism as something vanquished 80 years ago in Europe, or at least dormant until the actions of a few fringe elements. Others may understand fascism as an enduring threat, but perceive it as a distinct political ideology, primarily advocated by white supremacists and ethno-nationalists who have gained prominence over the past decade.

However, the concept that fascism should be viewed as something more extensive and insidious  something that's not just out there but also present within our own societies  has a rich history. In her 1940 essay on Homer's Iliad, Simone Weil observed the casual disregard of the strong towards the weak and reflected on the use of force throughout human history: "Exercised to its extreme, it turns a man into an object in the most literal sense  it makes a corpse out of him while he is still alive."

In the aftermath of the war, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno were clear that fascism had persisted in the combination of bureaucracy and capitalism that defined the post-war world, a system that "relieves its people of the burden of moral feelings" and is "deeply in accord with pure reason... [as it] treats human beings as things."

This essay attempts to shed light on these insidious aspects of our reality, not to label everything we dislike as 'fascism,' but to encourage a more thoughtful, empathetic, and critical approach towards the world around us.

We remember them more vividly and they play a larger role in shaping our lives. Farewells, accidents, bad parenting, financial losses and even a random snide comment take up most of our psychic space, leaving little room for compliments or pleasant experiences to help us along life’s challenging path. The staggering human ability to adapt ensures that joy over a salary hike will abate within months, leaving only a benchmark for future raises. We feel pain, but not the absence of it. Hundreds of scientific studies from around the world confirm our negativity bias: while a good day has no lasting effect on the following day, a bad day carries over. We process negative data faster and more thoroughly than positive data, and they affect us longer. Socially, we invest more in avoiding a bad reputation than in building a good one. Emotionally, we go to greater lengths to avoid a bad mood than to experience a good one. Pessimists tend to assess their health more accurately than optimists. In our era of political correctness, negative remarks stand out and seem more authentic. People – even babies as young as six months old – are quick to spot an angry face in a crowd, but slower to pick out a happy one; in fact, no matter how many smiles we see in that crowd, we will always spot the angry face first. The machinery by which we recognise facial emotion, located in a brain region called the amygdala, reflects our nature as a whole: two-thirds of neurons in the amygdala are geared toward bad news, immediately responding and storing it in our long-term memory, points out neuropsychologist Rick Hanson, Senior Fellow of the Greater Good Science Center at University of California, Berkeley. This is what causes the ‘fight or flight’ reflex – a survival instinct based on our ability to use memory to quickly assess threats. Good news, by comparison, takes 12 whole seconds to travel from temporary to long-term memory. Our ancient ancestors were better off jumping away from every stick that looked like a snake than carefully examining it before deciding what to do. Our gloomy bent finds its way into spoken language, with almost two thirds of English words conveying the negative side of things. In the vocabulary we use to describe people, this figure rises to a staggering 74 per cent. And English is not alone. Aside from Dutch, all other languages lean toward the bleak. A non-believer might be just as morally good as the believer, but prefer to contribute through taxes than through voluntary donations. One interesting finding, judged from internet downloads, is that US porn consumption is highest in Mormon-saturated Utah. Whatever the side effects, then, it’s not clear whether we’d be better off if we were all made to believe in God, or if we were made not to believe in God.
