text:
Reflecting on trauma and a fragmented identity, there's no better tune than The Judds' 1986 hit 'Grandpa (Tell Me 'bout the Good Ol' Days)':

"Grandpa, tell me 'bout the good old days,
Sometimes it feels like this world's gone crazy.
Grandpa, take me back to yesterday,
Where the line between right and wrong didn't seem so hazy."

In this song, nostalgia serves as a coping mechanism for dealing with the moral ambiguity of the present - but 'yesterday', instead of referring to her own past memories, now refers to the distant past of her grandpa's generation. She relies on him to provide clarity and assurance.

Research on the effects of golden age thinking is still in its early stages, with most studies focusing on nationalist nostalgia. A 2014 study found that participants who were primed with collective personal nostalgia - 'recall a past event experienced with fellow countrymen' - were more likely to sacrifice their own money to punish transgressions against in-group members. Another study led by Dutch psychologist Anouk Smeekes, published as 'Longing for the countrys good old days', discovered a significant correlation between collective historical nostalgia for 'the way it was' in the Netherlands and opposition to the rights of Muslim immigrants. A follow-up study suggested that national nostalgia fueled out-group prejudice by reinforcing a sense of ethnic national identity.

"You see this nostalgic discourse in Western Europe, or with the rise of Donald Trump," says Smeekes, explaining her inspiration for the research. "They say: 'There were glory days in our national past, when everyone was safe, and then all the changes came, one of which is immigration, and if you vote for me I will return us to the glorious past and give back the country to its original people.'"

This new research reveals how collective historical nostalgia can transform selflessness into in-group selfishness - a phenomenon described by theologian Reinhold Niebuhr in his 1932 classic Moral Man and Immoral Society. Individuals are naturally self-interested, argues Niebuhr, but they also possess the ability to 'consider interests other than their own'. In contrast, groups consolidate the egoism of their members at the expense of out-group empathy.

"The frustrations of the average man, who can never realize the power and glory which his imagination sets as the ideal, makes him the more willing tool and victim of the imperial ambitions of his group," writes Niebuhr. "His frustrated individual ambitions gain a measure of satisfaction in the power and aggrandisement of his nation."

In essence, collective nostalgia can lead to a dangerous distortion of reality, where individuals sacrifice their empathy for others to fuel their own sense of identity and belonging within their group.

And yet nobody down on the sidewalk, looking up and yelling ‘Don’t!’ and ‘Hang on!’, can understand the jump. Not really. text:
Title: A Personal Reflection on Philosophy: A Challenge to Wittgenstein

I've been grappling with philosophy lately, and I must say, it's a tricky beast. It's essential we don't dismiss it as mere nonsense, Wittgenstein; instead, we should take it seriously and acknowledge its potential to clarify our thoughts and actions. After all, the very core of philosophy is not that it's nonsensical, but that if it is, we need to address that, not pretend it's some important nonsense!

One of the most frustrating positions I encounter in philosophical discussions is reminiscent of a child saying they can't say 'breakfast'. When asked why, they respond with a shrug and a 'can't.' As Bertrand Russell famously quipped, 'But what we can't say, we can't say, and we can't whistle it either.' This is one of two objections I have to Wittgenstein's view that philosophy is nonsense and should be discarded.

Firstly, if Wittgenstein argues for a specific view on the nature of meaning, his argument itself becomes questionable, as it then becomes unclear what he's actually saying. We do understand Wittgenstein's philosophical argument, but it's not a ladder we can toss away once climbed, as he suggested in the Tractatus.

Secondly, this kind of philosophy leaves us intellectually impoverished. If, like Wittgenstein, we believe that philosophy is about arranging the propositions of science and everyday life into a logical system with basic terms and definitions, it's hard to see how this contributes much at all. In his notes from 1929, Wittgenstein himself acknowledged this: 'The standardization of beer colors is not philosophy, but in a sense, it is an improvement in notation and a clarification of thought.'

Both the Vienna Circle and Wittgenstein face these objections, although they did share one thing with him - an incredulity toward the idea that there's something inexpressible yet important. Ramsey, however, was starting to develop his own approach, his rebellion against Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle. He summed it up perfectly: 'We cannot really picture the world as disconnected selves; the selves we know are in the world.'

Philosophy arises from misunderstanding the logic of our language, but the logic of our language is not what Wittgenstein thought. The mental images we construct aren't pictures of facts. If a proposition is a picture of the world, disconnected from any reference to the self whose picture it is, we become vulnerable to skepticism or solipsism. How can we bridge the gap between ourselves and that world? How can we make claims about that world at all?

Wittgenstein's 'world' contains no thought. If we want to understand the world, we must not ignore the subjective side. Carnap, too, seems to have made this mistake. In the ordinary sense, solipsism suggests that only one's own mind is real; Wittgenstein and Carnap both seem to imply this in their philosophies. But Ramsey thought differently: 'What we cant do, we cant do, and its no good trying.'

In conclusion, philosophy should not be dismissed as nonsense. Instead, we must challenge its ideas and strive for a more holistic understanding that includes the subjective side of our human experience. Only then will we bridge the gap between ourselves and the world around us.

For Derrida, the idea of the secret is intimately bound up with the idea of ‘confession’, be it political, personal or religious. Confession need not be forced, of course. In fact, it can be an act of love. If I confess to you my ‘deepest secrets’, I am – in commonsense belief – ‘telling you who I really am’, or rather showing you who I really am, by performing a kind of revelation that makes me vulnerable and open, and inviting a reciprocal openness. I trust/love you so much that I have no secrets. More prosaically, it can also be an act of profound friendship – another of Derrida’s themes – and of bonding. When someone says: ‘Do you want to know a secret?’ we feel not only the thrill of the illicit, but a sense of being entered into a community, even if it is one of only two people. We share secrets. In each of these examples, confessions forced or otherwise, the tacit understanding is that the confession is indeed an act of revealing the self. In Nineteen Eighty-Four, Winston exposes his secret and destroys his ‘self’. If I confess to you my secrets, you enter into my most intimate selfhood, we share it. Thus, we have a commonsense – and legalistic – belief that there is a direct relationship between our secrets and our selves. This is also, traditionally, a religious belief, and the connection between religion and secrets is a powerful and enduring one. The Catholic rite of confession is an exemplary model of this. To the priest, I reveal my secrets, and therefore my true self, the self only God otherwise has access to. If I keep secrets from the confessional, the self I offer up to God and the self that God knows to be true are misaligned. I betray God because I withhold information from His representative/proxy on Earth. Thus, Catholic guilt – the guilt of non-confession, the guilt of the unmentioned transgressions in both thought and act. For Derrida, as the philosopher Agata Bielik-Robson points out in her excellent book Derrida’s Marrano Passover (2022), it was his interest in one particular religious group that opened up his own dialogue with the idea of secrets – a group so secret that it forgot that it disguised its own existence even to itself. The courts have been relatively unresponsive to medical expertise, even as the fields of psychiatry and psychology have added to our understanding of mental illness. The 16th-century Dutch physician Johann Weyer sought to challenge the Saxon Code of 1572 on its treatment of insanity, complaining that it didn’t reflect the realities of mental illness. The judge retorted: ‘Weyer is not a lawyer, but a physician – consequently, his view on the relationship between mental disease and transgressions of the written law are of no moment.’ Nearly 400 years later, in 1950, the schism between legal definitions of insanity and the psychiatric realities of mental illness continued; the US Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter noted ‘the pathological processes which give rise to the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity’. In the ensuing almost 40 years, Toole writes, the ‘legal definition of exculpatory mental disease in the context of criminal liability has remained largely static, sheltered from the immediate influence of medical theory and advancements’. The current legal definition of insanity has no psychiatric correlate; it is a pure creature of law, owing nothing to psychiatry and the sciences of brain and behaviour. As the psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote in Mind, Medicine and Man (1943): Except for totally deteriorated, drooling, hopeless psychotics of long standing, and congenital idiots – who seldom commit murder or have the opportunity to commit murder – the great majority and perhaps all murderers know what they are doing, the nature and quality of their act, and the consequences thereof, and they are therefore legally sane, regardless of the opinion of any psychiatrist. The lack of a psychiatric correlate makes it look like psychiatrists and psychologists are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective; the fact is, mental health experts often can’t agree on whether a defendant fits into the legal definition of insanity, even though they agree about the defendant’s diagnosis and degree of psychiatric impairment. As the forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School said in 1998: ‘Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defence, not about mental illness.’ In ‘On Responsibility’ (1996), the federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard wrote: With contemporary psychiatric and psychological data, the legitimate experts, who must testify about mental conditions, are unable to make accurate and scientific determinations fit into the rigid legal definitions the law imposes … Trials in which a jury must decide upon the accused’s guilt based upon the contradictory testimony of experts result in ‘battles of experts’ that virtually assure arbitrary outcomes. The Scottsdale location, on the other hand, is larger, so you shouldn't have to deal with unbearable queues.

So, if you're looking for a meal fit for a king or queen, swing by Lo Lo's and enjoy some good ol' 'Winner Winner Chicken Dinner', whether it's for breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Trust me; your taste buds will thank you!

Wow, what a mouth-watering BBQ experience at Whole Hog Heaven! Despite the slightly sketchy neighborhood and the 30-minute wait, the meal was absolutely worth it. My boyfriend is a BBQ enthusiast, so I left the ordering up to him. He went for the Whole Hog Heaven platter  four meat choices plus three sides. After much deliberation, he chose St. Louis and Baby Back Ribs, BBQ Chicken, and Brisket, accompanied by mac & cheese, coleslaw, and beans. I was pretty famished too, and the tantalizing aroma of BBQ wafting from the kitchen only fueled my anticipation.

The meal didn't disappoint one bit. The ribs were tender and juicy, practically falling off the bone with every bite. The chicken was perfectly cooked, and the brisket was so tender it melted in our mouths. All three sides complemented the meat wonderfully, but I must say that the coleslaw was my favorite  especially when paired with the brisket.

To make this meal even more unforgettable, we were served homemade, warm cornbread with whipped butter before our entrees arrived and complimentary mini donuts after our meal (yes, you read that right!). It's not every day you get a taste of BBQ heaven like this.

That being said, there was a group of rowdy girls who seemed to be attached to the club next door that occasionally invaded the bathroom. While it didn't ruin the experience entirely, it did add a bit of an annoyance factor. All in all, I would give 5 stars if not for that minor inconvenience. Highly recommended  we'll definitely be back!

I stumbled upon this hidden gem through Yelp, and let me tell you, it was an absolute treat! From the cozy ambiance to the exceptional service, every moment spent here was pure joy. The restaurant is small but charming, with wooden accents and mini chandeliers that add a touch of elegance to each table setting.

Our server, Gina, was simply delightful. She recommended two exquisite cocktails for us to start  the Bohemian Sidecar and the Cucumber Vesper. Both were a delightful surprise! After savoring our drinks, we moved on to the appetizers. The steak tartare with potato chips and a fried egg was heavenly, and the mussels in saffron and white wine sauce were equally amazing. If only they had given us more bread to soak up that delicious sauce!

As for the main course, my partner enjoyed the pork shoulder while I devoured the stuffed chicken with pome puree and mushrooms. The slight char on the mushrooms added a depth of flavor that was simply divine. I have to admit, I still believe mine was the better dish  but we both agreed that both entrees were incredibly reasonably priced at $15 and $17.

To top it all off, we indulged in dessert and after-dinner drinks. The souffle with creme anglaise and the berries with creme fraiche were heavenly, while the chocolate martini and pear tini were the perfect way to end our meal. I can't even begin to describe how incredible this place is  it's an experience that truly sets it apart from any other restaurant in Scottsdale. If you haven't been yet, go now! text:
To truly focus and immerse oneself is crucial, especially when it comes to chess. As my passion for the game waned, I felt a part of myself fading away too. If we can't concentrate, we miss out on the state of consciousness - known as "flow" - that is integral to the chess experience. Flow was introduced and popularized by Hungarian-American psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, and it's characterized by deep absorption, loss of self-awareness, goal-oriented feedback from the environment, and altered perception of time. Experiences in flow are extremely rewarding, but only when our skills match the challenge level; too little challenge and we get bored, too much and we feel anxious. Chess offers a wonderful opportunity to achieve flow, yet it's important to remember that flow is more about the quality of consciousness rather than a method for achieving it. Flow is not virtue, but pleasure. While experiencing flow is desirable, promoting it might lead to undesirable consequences; a society of sophisticated hedonists addicted to gaming and suffering from virtual reality sickness is not far-fetched.

Concentration, on the other hand, prompts an awareness of mood, a commitment to meaning, and an appreciation for process. As a young player, I relied on various methods to strengthen my focus, such as taking long walks before games or listening to favorite music tracks. These strategies worked because the purpose behind my concentration was clear. However, in December 2008, in a nondescript hotel room in Palma, Mallorca, I remember struggling to prepare for a game while feeling disconnected from myself. As American football coaches often say, "The will to win is not as important as the will to prepare to win." I realized that I had lost this drive. It was as if the foundation of my motivation had crumbled, and along with it, a piece of myself. The underlying motivation for my concentration was gone, and I knew that it was time to focus on what the game represented rather than the game itself.

Chess is a unique platform where system meets psychology, and we need to better understand the dynamics of this intersection in today's world. To make good moves in chess, you need to see the entire board in all its dynamic complexity. But you also need to understand your own mind, its strengths, and limitations; the personal is indeed political, and vice versa. In the early 21st century, we face a complex situation that includes a worsening ecological crisis, the challenge of mass unemployment due to AI advancements, and protecting truth in an era of misinformation. We need to find ways to navigate these challenges with clarity and focus, just as a chess player needs to see both the game and their own mind with equal precision.
